Emma Grede's Rule Of Thirds For Navigating Leadership Challenges

Forbes(2.1M) ·26:54 ·2.9K views · 2026-04-16
Emma Grede (Cofounder and CEO, Good American)
True self-leadership requires replacing "manifestation" with a concrete business plan for your life, de-stigmatizing money conversations to gain power, and using the "Rule of Thirds" to maintain momentum through the inevitable difficulties of entrepreneurship.
TL;DR True self-leadership requires replacing "manifestation" with a concrete business plan for your life, de-stigmatizing money conversations to gain power, and using the "Rule of Thirds" to maintain momentum through the inevitable difficulties of entrepreneurship. Concrete...

TL;DR

True self-leadership requires replacing "manifestation" with a concrete business plan for your life, de-stigmatizing money conversations to gain power, and using the "Rule of Thirds" to maintain momentum through the inevitable difficulties of entrepreneurship.

The Framework for Self-Leadership

  • Concrete Vision vs. Manifestation: Grede distinguishes between passive vision boards and holding a concrete, actionable vision for work and life.
      • Life Planning: Individuals should create a "business plan for themselves," mapping out specific goals and values by decade (30s, 40s, 50s).
  • Cultural Conditioning: Women are often conditioned to avoid the very behaviors—such as discussing wealth or being "ambitious"—that lead to opportunity.
      • Self-Awareness: The goal is to "get out of your own way" and ensure your internal narrative isn't your biggest enemy.
  • Managing Emotions: Leadership requires managing fear, anger, and guilt (specifically "mom guilt") rather than letting them dictate decision-making.
      • Emotions as Signals: Fear and imposter syndrome should be viewed as signals to lean in and investigate what a situation is trying to teach you.

The Rule of Thirds in Business

  • Contextualizing Difficulty: Grede uses a "Rule of Thirds" to level-set expectations for entrepreneurship and leadership:
      • One-third of the time: You are happy and things are going well.
      • One-third of the time: You feel indifferent or "okay."
      • One-third of the time: The experience is "shitty," difficult, or sad.
  • Resilience through Momentum: Understanding this ratio prevents leaders from quitting when things get hard. A "bad" third is not a signal to stop, but a signal that growth and resilience are being built on the other side of the challenge.

Advocating for Professional and Financial Worth

  • The Money Stigma: Grede argues that money and power are inextricably linked; therefore, women must de-stigmatize money talk to achieve political and economic influence.
      • Transparency: Successful women often avoid discussing exact numbers due to "ambition" having a negative connotation for women compared to men.
  • Practical Advocacy:
      • Benchmarks: Advocacy begins with education—knowing the comps, benchmarks, and the specific context of your company (startup vs. legacy).
      • The Audacity to Ask: Women should explicitly ask superiors: "What does it take for me to get to [X level]?"
      • The "Walk Away" Factor: True advocacy requires the willingness to leave a position if the feedback or compensation does not align with your worth.

AI and the Future of Small Business

  • Agentic AI: Grede predicts a transformation for "mom-and-pop" shops as they integrate AI to scale small teams (2-5 people) into "incredible companies."
  • The Human Touch: While AI should handle prediction, planning, and efficiency, Grede maintains that product-based businesses must preserve human creativity and design.
  • The Competitive Edge: Employees who become the "AI implementers" within their specific divisions will become indispensable to their organizations.

Leadership Authenticity and Failure

  • The Fallible CEO: Grede rejects the idea of the "untouchable" CEO. She advocates for admitting mistakes and "failing fast" to help teams learn more quickly.
  • Success is Non-Linear: Grede highlights that her career is a "long succession of things that were way less successful."
      • Decoupling Failure from Identity: She advises entrepreneurs to view a failed business as a "terrible situation" rather than a personal indictment of their fitness to lead.
"You can do deeply meaningful and impactful work and care about money... We have to put money at the center of our plans because money and power are inextricably linked." — Emma Grede
"When you’re doing something difficult or you’re chasing a dream... you will be happy about a third of the time. The other third you're indifferent. And the other third is really difficult. Once you contextualize that, you go, 'Oh, this is just one of the bad moments.'" — Emma Grede
"You are the most important business that you will ever run. No sooner would you start something tomorrow without a plan than you should do that for yourself. Create a plan for your life and own it." — Emma Grede
So much information out there about manifestation and creating vision boards, and that's very, very different from holding a concrete vision for yourself, for your work, and for your life. Welcome. I'm Maneet Ahuja, Editor-at-Large at Forbes and founder of Iconoclast. We're here at the Nasdaq MarketSite, and I'm thrilled to be joined now by the incomparable Emma Grede. As co-founder and CEO of Good American, founding partner of Skims, podcast host, and now author, Emma is a trailblazer in the world of fashion and entrepreneurship. In her new book, "Start With Yourself: A New Vision for Work and Life," Emma details how her early life experiences as the child of a working-class single mother in East London molded her path into becoming one of America's richest self-made women according to Forbes. Emma, thank you so much for joining us. Thank you for having me. You know, so really your book spoke to me on so many different levels. So, it's—it's hard to know where to start, but we're going to—we're going to find our spot. So, in addition to opening up about your upbringing and how you defied the odds to become this serial entrepreneur, you really positioned "Start With Yourself" to be a framework to help people understand the right mindset for success, according to you. So tell us a little bit about that and how the concept of the book came together and why now? Well, I really think about this book as, yes, it's a business book, but it's about self-leadership more than anything else. And I wanted to create something, not just a book for people to read, but a book for people to use. Because wherever I go, all over the country, it doesn't matter if I'm walking down the street, having lunch with my kids, or I've come off the stage at a conference, people want to know the same things. They want to know how I've done what I've done. But they don't want to know for, you know, to know more about me, they want to know how they can do it for themselves. And I think that where we are in the culture right now, there are so many kind of prevailing old thoughts that exist for women. And there's so much that we create for ourselves, that I really wanted to say, look, there is no one-size-fits-all prescriptive way to be successful, but you really have to understand that there's so much that you can do for yourself. There's so much that I have done when I think about the way that I've come into work and how long it's taken. And that is really not what, you know, you see on social media. That's really not what you get from any of the articles. So I wanted to be really honest about my journey and what it's taken for me to actually get to this point in my life. And that is such a service that you've done for the readers or people that don't fully know your story. Even as a woman, I identified very much with your hustle mentality and your hard work ethic. But when you see somebody that you aspire to be like, right? And so I love, you know, your podcast is called "Aspire," which we'll get to as well. But you don't necessarily know how they put the building blocks in place or if they had the same scenarios as you. And you've had so many obstacles that you've had to overcome. You actually—it—it's almost liberating that people get to see your roadmap as a way to say, okay, you can have this in your way and you can find a way around it. And so much of that boils down to mindset and going inward. So I want to double down on that a little bit. You emphasize self-awareness and personal responsibility, which are cornerstones for—for your framework. How do you define starting with yourself in a world where there are so many determining outside forces at play? Well, I love that you asked the question that way because I do think that as women specifically, we are actually culturally conditioned to avoid the exact behaviors that lead to opportunity, to wealth, and to the way you behave in leadership. And so, you know, what I've understood and what I know is there's not one way to be, but what you have to do is get out of your own way. You have to make sure that your biggest enemy is like not living, you know, in your own head. And so, I think that mindset is a huge piece of this, but it has to be coupled with a lot of hard work. Ambition has to find you working. And so, I think that what we have to make sure is that the prevailing thoughts, the things that are in culture—like perhaps that you're going to be able to network your way into an amazing situation or if you're not invited to the right things—those things are really unhelpful old thoughts. For example, you know, it being kind of considered a bit crass for women to talk about money. Those things are really unhelpful old thoughts. And we have to find new thoughts to replace them with. And that's what I'm trying to talk about in the book, like how we can get out of our own way. You know, the book is really based around this idea of managing your emotions because again— I love that! And—and I think it's really important, right? Because you can't separate the two for many, many women. We're coming from a place of immense fear. We're coming from a place of having and sitting with a lot of guilt, especially for working parents. And so I'm not sitting here pretending that I haven't been fearful in my businesses and in my careers at all, or that I don't have mom guilt. I'm talking about how you manage that stuff. Right. And it's so important that we hold a vision for ourselves. And I think again, there's so much information out there about manifestation and creating vision boards, and that's very, very different from holding a concrete vision for yourself, for your work, and for your life. And I just want to touch on what you said when you said manage your emotions. One of the things I loved when I was going through the book is, you know, I think that that is somewhat an underappreciated skill, right? And then you actually broke it down into different emotions. You had one on fear, one on anger, one on—I think—guilt, right? Guilt! My favorite one of all of them! Right. No, I have a three-month-old. I—you know, so we can—we can all relate to various aspects of that. but I want to shift also to your Rule of Thirds. You—happy a third, okay a third, and shitty a third. To put it bluntly. To put it bluntly. That's a bit of a departure from the aspirational messaging most women in business put out. Talk to us about that framework and how it's been so helpful in level-setting on your journey. Yeah, I think it's really important that we are able to really contextualize what it means to move in leadership. And when you start a business, like, it's not that easy. It's not always fun. And so I really thought about this idea of the Rule of Thirds as something that helps me contextualize my everyday. Because when you're doing something difficult or you're chasing a dream or you're going after something that you really want, you will be happy about a third of the time. And the other third of the time you're going to feel indifferent. And the other third is really difficult. And to say shitty is the right thing because you feel bad, you'll feel sad, you'll have days where you're really, really down. But actually, once you start looking at everything, you go, "Oh!" Well, you know what? You're able to contextualize: "this is just one of the bad moments." Because life is really long and it happens in seasons. And I think that what happens is that we can sometimes get put off if things aren't working well, if the business doesn't turn out as we expected, if we don't get that promotion. That is not a signal to stop trying. That's a signal that something great is on the other side. So when I feel fear, when I'm, you know, in my head, I actually look and think: "what is this trying to teach me?" And the Rule of Thirds is a really great way to just think about your life and the fact that it isn't supposed to be great and easy all the time. The question to ask yourself is, "what is this thing here to teach me? What can I learn? Actually, which part of me can grow and what muscles can I flex because I'm having this really hard time?" And so every time I'm in one of those spots, I'm like, "Do you know what? The other thing's working. I'm becoming resilient here. Like, something else is at play." And that's really helpful too, because what you're also, like, teaching your brain and these mental exercises, the anticipation and the expectation that every day is not going to be a bright day, but you're still putting one foot in front of the other and you're getting along with your journey. Yeah, and I actually even talk about that, momentum is key. At the end of the day, you have to find yourself always in motion. And just getting out of your head and not allowing your emotions to rule your decision-making is one of the most important things we can do for ourselves. And again, as women, that's so key to who we are. It's also our superpower. So harnessing that can really kind of turbocharge where we can go in business, and you're really a key evidence of that. I hope so. Yeah. One of the things you spoke about earlier and you talk about a lot in the book is money. And money is a topic women refuse to discuss. You name it potentially the biggest obstacle to women's success. Why do you think even the most successful women still don't like to talk about money openly? You referenced a couple situations in your book as well. Yeah, well, I feel like, you know, I'm so lucky because I get to interview the most successful women in the world. I get to interview founders that are brand new on my podcast "Aspire with Emma Grede". And what's so interesting is how seldomly people will talk about the numbers. How seldomly they will actually get concrete with what it took. Oh, we know that at Forbes! Right! And it's not always about smoke and mirrors. Sometimes there's just a level of embarrassment. And because I interview a lot of people that I really admire, they will say to me, "Emma, you know, I just don't want to touch on that. I just don't want people to think about me differently." Because when we think and we conjure up an idea of what it means to be an ambitious man, we see this idea of strength and we see this idea of somebody so fantastic. But when we say ambitious women, it's a very different connotation. Right. It's a double-edged sword. It's a double-edged sword. It's like, "Mm, she's ambitious," right? It's looked on negative, aggressive, or she's—exactly. And so the minute we start talking about money, it gets really complicated for women. And I want women to understand that you can do deeply meaningful and impactful work and care about money. That you can care about money and a lot of other things too. But we have to put money at the center of our plans because at the end of the day, we are desperate right now, not just in the workplace, but politically, economically, for more women in positions of power. That is a non-negotiable. Right. And money and power are inextricably linked. So understanding what you're worth, taking the stigma out of talking about money is an absolute imperative for women right now. We really need to have those conversations. And I hear my own husband having those conversations with ease every day. He's talking to his friends about, you know, what they're investing in or, you know, the latest crypto something or how they structured a deal. We need to also have those conversations because we need to be in the mix. We need to have that type of deal flow. We want to be the people that are getting paid what we're worth. And so I just really want to de-stigmatize any conversation about money because we've got to be having them. That's so valuable. This is such valuable advice. And one of the things that you reference that, you know, in social settings talking about money, but you also reference women being paid what they worth. So what's your practical guidance for women in the workplace to better advocate for themselves in those scenarios? Well, I think just in general, it's about really understanding your worth, right? And what I mean by that is you have to understand the context in which you work. Do you work at a startup? Are you in like a—you know—an old company, like a legacy business that give like really, you know, like minuscule pay increases all the time? What is the working environment? How much, you know, what are the benchmarks and the comps look like? So I really think the place to start is with education. You need to know the most about your space, about what you're paid, and to be able to decipher if you're being paid adequately. But the most important thing is to be willing to move away from that if you're not getting what you need. The other thing that I think is really interesting is how seldomly people actually do advocate for themselves. That they do actually come to their superiors and say, "I need X, Y, and Z," or "What does it take for me to get to here?" Because again, oftentimes we can be having those conversations with ourselves. Right. In your head. It requires some audacity if you want to be paid what you're worth. So you have to have those conversations and ask the pertinent questions so that you can understand: "Is this something I can get here? Do I need to go elsewhere for that? And if so, what does it take?" And then be willing to take the feedback on the other side. Right. And be willing to leave if—if need be, right? And you've gotta—you've gotta walk away. You've gotta look for the feedback. Mm-hmm. One of the other things that we've spoken to you about and that is not a new question, but people really resonate with is imposter syndrome. And you have an interesting take on it as well. You think it can be an advantage. I personally think we all identify with imposter syndrome. Talk to us a little bit about that. Well, you know, the reason it can be an advantage is because when you start to fear a little bit like out of your depth, again, I think that these things are signals. This is a signal about what you should be doing. It's a signal about— Just like you say fear is, right? Exactly. And I think that you have to lean into that. What—what is that telling you? What is it that you're so afraid of? And so whenever I get those feelings, I try to dig really deep and understand, like, "what is this feeling actually telling me?" The truth is, I mean, does anybody really know exactly what they're doing? We're all working in so much uncharted territory right now. If not you, then who? I really think imposter syndrome is something that we have to put to bed for ourselves first, because oftentimes it's something that you have put in place about yourself and nobody else is thinking about you in the same way that you're thinking about yourself. So you're so transparent with how you're feeling. How do you reconcile that as a CEO? You're supposed to project certainty. But on the flip side, it's so refreshing that you're very open about imposter syndrome yourself. I'm curious from your perspective how that factors in. I don't think people want CEOs that are, you know, distant and untouchable. I think that being human, being fallible, being somebody that admits mistakes, being somebody that creates the conditions for mistakes to happen is actually a real sign of leadership. My team will get things done faster because we learn and fail fast. And I'm the first person to put my hands up and say, "I got this thing wrong. Here's what we need to learn from that situation." And so my experience is that, you know, I come to work as a whole person. I'm not a classically trained CEO in that way, but it's—it's worked out pretty well for me so far. Oh, well I'll say that's for sure. And on that point, you've built multiple successful ventures including Good American and your role as a founding partner in Skims. Looking back, what was the defining moment when you realized you could turn your ideas into a thriving business? Well, this is a really good point because there's no single moment. There's nothing that ever tells you "this is for sure a success." And what I really like to do because everybody is so, you know, excited to talk about the successes that they forget everything that came before that. All the things that you did that perhaps didn't work out. All the times that you failed and all the times that you were miserable. And my life has been and my career has been a long succession of things that were way less successful, that nobody else was talking about. And so what I try to do in the book is be honest about the mistakes, to be honest about what it's taken to get to this place where you finally have a success. What I never allowed any of those failures to do though was to tell me that I wasn't fit to be a leader. That I wasn't fit to create my own thing or to be a CEO. Because again, for oftentimes for women, we can take that feedback as a reason for not doing something. Men start businesses and fail in business all the time. They get up, they dust themselves off, and they try again. And we need to take some of that mentality into what we're doing and really take the learnings, understand why something didn't work, but don't equate it with you. It's not personal, right? It's like you figure out why something didn't work and you learn and you do better next time. It's not "I did something terrible", it's "this situation was terrible" and you move on. I love that self-talk. You literally laid it out the one-by-one step. My—That's the conversation I've had with myself many times. Talk us through that self-talk, right? Because we're all in those positions where then it leads to self-doubt and you're like, "am I really doing the right thing? Maybe this is a sign of the opposite, that I should be moving in a different direction." So it's great to hear that if you have conviction in your idea and you're passionate about it, the failure is not a sign to give up. Damn right. Yeah. So AI is a huge topic and something that you've spoken about is agentic AI in entrepreneurship. Talk to us a little bit about what you see or predict, in the face of how AI can shift the landscape as an entrepreneur. You know what? I'm so excited about AI right now, and it's one of my obsessions because we have been working on integrating AI into our businesses for the last, I don't know, two, two and a half years, something like that. I think that what you're going to see is millions and millions of small American businesses figuring out how to utilize AI. And it's going to take—it's going to turn those businesses, those mom-and-pop shops, those businesses that have two, three, four, five people into like incredible companies when they figure out how they can utilize it to their benefit. I'm really excited. I know that there's so much trepidation, but I think that for individuals, if you can become the person within your company that takes your division and figures out how you can implement AI. You know, it's not going to replace us all as humans. I think within our companies, we are really looking at what requires a human touch. I'm in a product-based business, so there's certain elements of creativity and design where we will never ever allow that to be something that machines are doing. There has to be a human touch. But there are other places where we're like, absolutely, almost everything else should, you know, should be touching and utilizing AI in some ways. But what I'm most excited about is what it's going to do for small businesses across this country, and I can't wait to be in a position where I can actually help really make that happen. And I love one of the stories in your book about day one in Good American you sold I think a million dollars, right? And by your— Oh yeah! Best day, worst day ever. Best day ever, but it was also the worst day ever. And then by what was it, 11 o'clock, there was all of this doubt and uncertainty. So who knows, maybe AI could help with—predicting? It could have—it could have certainly helped me with some prediction and planning. That—that I didn't know then and I'm not sure I've completely figured out now. So I want to touch on your podcast "Aspire". You've had everyone on that show, and I'm a subscriber, from former First Lady Michelle Obama to Netflix CEO Ted Sarandos, to Cardi B. Talk to us about why you decided to start "Aspire" and in tandem now with— Sorry, I'm—I'm laughing because—what? I don't know that Michelle and Ted and Cardi have ever been in like a sentence like that, but that's great! Well, they have been on your—yeah, they have! So that is a new achievement for—for all of them. Both—yeah! For all three! Yeah. So I'm sure you have such a wide array and it—it's not dissimilar to Forbes, right? We are so fortunate to interview such a wide array of innovators, disruptors, changemakers like yourself. So talk to us about some of the key lessons that you learned and why you decided to start that podcast now, like not like you have enough to do, right? Right, with four kids, multiple companies, now a book, book tour. It felt so necessary to me because, you know, I think that talent is one of those things that's fairly evenly distributed. Opportunity isn't. And so when you come from a place like I do, East London, I didn't finish high school, I dropped out and I found my way into a really great position. But there was so much that happened along the way. And so what I wanted to do with my podcast "Aspire" is really lift the lid on what it takes for people to be successful. And you know, you read these interviews and people say, "You know, and when I went out and I raised 10 million bucks," and I'm like, "Whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa. First of all, how do you make a soft drink?" Right? And then we can start to break down like how you raised 10 million bucks. You talking about Poppi? It's like—and a—you—you really want to get under the covers. Yes, Allison, she's the most incredible. But that—that's what I think, you know, it's almost like I want to break it down for people and understand what the commonalities are, what the habits are, and what people really need to do to be successful. Because this country is full of so many incredible entrepreneurs and people with brilliant ideas and they just need to know how to get started. And so if I can just, you know, open up the conversations and again, we've had the most incredible people on that podcast. And what comes out when you really dare to ask the questions is just like such useful and brilliant information. And I love having the conversations. And what I really love is you're democratizing access to the world's most brilliant minds, right? And just like your personal story and so many of the people that I've been fortunate to profile for Forbes, like Mellody Hobson and others, growing up you don't have necessarily those role models in front of you. I know I certainly didn't either. But where do you find them? You find them in books, you find them out in the media, in the ether, in the universe. And so what you're doing is just helping also push that forward with the content you're putting out. I think so, and you know, really simple things. Like, we did an episode recently on how to interview. It was just one of our best episodes because whoever teaches you that? If you're really good at your job, you actually don't do that many interviews even in your life, right? And so you get pulled into situations and so you get these opportunities and how do you make the most of that opportunity? And even things like that, learning how to negotiate for yourself, learning what to do when you get like a really impressive bonus check. How do you even create the conditions for you to think about your first investment? So we're really trying to give people opportunities to make the most of any situation they've got in their work and in their life. Mm-hmm. So I know we're almost out of time. I have a couple quick questions. So you already had a massive platform, but you gained even more recognition as the first Black woman investor on the show "Shark Tank". How did that experience shape your view on investments and entrepreneurship? You referenced all the small businesses. Our economy is run on small business in America, especially for underrepresented founders, right? I'm sure there was an extra layer of some interesting perspectives that came out of those conversations. Yeah, well, I think when you grow up like I did, the—the feeling that I could be part of somebody's journey who perhaps wouldn't get traditional investment is super appealing. And so when those founders come on and they've put their heart and their soul and sometimes like their life savings and their mortgage, it's—you know—it's like they've done everything that they could to become successful. And then that type of visibility coupled with the mentorship of a shark, like, it's—it's like gold, you know? I enjoyed doing that show so much and I just, you know, I made amazing incredible relationships out of it. I sat next to Mark so I really used that relationship! I was like, "I'm going to talk to him the whole day when we're not filming." Right! which was so wonderful and useful. But, you know, just—I—I think that that is so much the spirit of America, right? That you can change your own circumstances, that you can do incredible things. But we all need people. And I know in my own business the network of people that I've built around me that enable me to do what I do has been the difference between things being a little bit successful and really successful. And so my point of view is that I'd always—I always want to be that for someone else. And I feel like I'm really lucky. I have one big success story from "Shark Tank" which, you know, is incredible, but you really do it to actually help these founders and, you know, it's your own money, so you've really got to believe in what you're doing. You really gotta put your money where your mouth is. Literally got to put your money where your mouth is. So I want to circle back to the book because we're nearly out of time. How do you hope "Start With Yourself" will impact the next generation of entrepreneurs and business leaders? Well, you know, I think that this book is really personal, but it is so applicable. You know, it was so easy for me to write about building a brand and a business, about what it takes to have a great career and leadership and money and tradeoffs. But to have that kind of interwoven with family. You know, I'm a mother of four. It has to go in there. To have the emotional piece in there and the vision piece in there and to speak about what has actually made the biggest difference in my life and my career was, I think, something that you don't see in the majority of business books that are out there. There are so many incredible business books, not nearly enough written by women, and not enough written by high school dropouts that are moms of four. There are lots and lots of different ways to be successful. And so all I can hope is that somebody reads this and feels inspired to do something for themselves. And I imagine that, you know, one day like a million Emmas running around, people that on paper didn't look like they were going to start something great, but they do. And they take that spark of inspiration and they quite literally start with themselves and do something incredible. Well, I found the alchemy of the book to be special, to your point. It was part memoir, part self-help, part personal story, part business brand book guide framework for how to change your own life. So last question: if you could distill the most important lesson from the book into a single sentence, what would it be and why? I would honestly say it would be to have a vision for yourself. And I don't want that to be conflated with vision boards or manifestation. But it's to have a very clear idea of what is important to you. What type of person do you want to be? What are your values? What are your principles? Because once you understand that and you anchor your work into that, it changes everything. When you know exactly what you're aiming for, you get to decide what you're saying yes to, what you're saying no to. You get to figure out— You map out each of your decades, right? I'm sorry. I map out—and—no, and—and I love that you do because the mapping of the decades is like: "what do I want from my 30s? What do I want from my 40s? What am I aiming for when I'm 50 years old?" And when you get crystal clear about that, you make different decisions. Different decisions. So I think that in the same way that when we go into businesses and we have mission, vision, values, I want you to have that for yourself. For what type of parent you're going to be, for what type of person you're going to be, both inside the workplace and out. And I think if everybody created that for themselves, you would—it's almost like you take control of your life. It's like a business plan for yourself. Because at the end of the day, like, you are the most important business that you will ever run. Like, if you think about yourself like that, you would plan it. You would—no sooner would you go and just like start something tomorrow without a plan than you should do that for yourself. It's like create a plan for yourself and your life and own it. Start with yourself. Well, on that note, we're out of time. Emma Grede, thank you so much for joining us. I'm Maneet Ahuja at the Nasdaq MarketSite.

We’re entering the Most Disruptive Era in Human History

What's Uptin?(116.0K) ·41:34 ·14 views · 2026-04-16
Reid Hoffman (Co-founder, LinkedIn) Uptin Saiidi (Host, What's Uptin?)
Reid Hoffman argues that we are entering a transformative era where AI serves as a "general-purpose technology" to amplify human capability, rather than a bubble.
TL;DR Reid Hoffman argues that we are entering a transformative era where AI serves as a "general-purpose technology" to amplify human capability, rather than a bubble. While he acknowledges significant labor market transitions and a shifting geopolitical order, he maintains...

TL;DR

Reid Hoffman argues that we are entering a transformative era where AI serves as a "general-purpose technology" to amplify human capability, rather than a bubble. While he acknowledges significant labor market transitions and a shifting geopolitical order, he maintains that "steering" toward positive outcomes—such as AI-driven cancer cures and universal medical assistants—is the only way to navigate this disruption.

The AI Economy: Beyond the Bubble

  • The "No Bubble" Verdict: Hoffman categorically denies we are in an AI bubble. He argues the technology is already transforming industries by providing massive cost savings.
      • Example: Legal tasks that previously cost $5,000 in Silicon Valley can now be performed in 10 minutes via a $20/month AI subscription.
  • Workforce Transformation: Hoffman dismisses the "fixed amount of work" fallacy. While 10-20% layoffs may occur as companies realize they "mishired" or found efficiencies, he believes the "other half" of workers will start new companies or join growth-stage startups.
      • The "Superagency" Concept: Professionals will not be replaced by AI, but by other humans who use AI effectively.
      • Government Role: Instead of focusing solely on Universal Basic Income (UBI), governments should prioritize "workforce transformation" and nimble, network-based education.
  • Regulation Philosophy: Hoffman advocates for regulation only when "bad regulation is better than no regulation," specifically regarding catastrophic risks like bio-terrorism or cybersecurity. For everything else, he suggests "monitoring and auditing" rather than pre-predicting problems.

Healthcare and the Human Quotient (EQ)

  • Emotional Intelligence (EQ): Through his company Inflection AI, Hoffman focuses on "EQ" rather than just "IQ." He believes the most useful AI assistants must establish trust by being in tune with a user's emotional tone and personal needs.
  • Curing Cancer with Manas: Hoffman co-founded Manas with Siddhartha Mukherjee to use AI for identifying therapeutic molecules.
      • Progress: Though in early stages, the company has already identified potentially interesting molecules for drug development.
  • The "Universal Doctor": Hoffman’s immediate goal is for every human to have a high-quality medical assistant on their smartphone—one that is better than the average GP and available 24/7.
  • Longevity: While optimistic about AI’s role in health, he is skeptical of immediate "quick unlocks" for living to 140, noting that cellular rejuvenation remains a significantly harder scientific path than reducing child mortality or treating heart disease.

The Future of Work and LinkedIn

  • The LinkedIn Mission: Unlike other social platforms, LinkedIn is designed for "time-saving, not time-spending." It prioritizes utility and civility over entertainment and "freedom of yell."
  • Video and Influencers: LinkedIn is integrating short-form video to help leaders and coaches establish "human connection" and tangibility, though Hoffman jokes he doesn't expect to see "business rain dances" or TikTok-style trends.
  • Maximizing "Serendipity":
      • Profile Utility: A robust profile takes 10 minutes to fill out but acts as a passive "digital library" that helps people find you months later.
      • Introduction Power: Use the network to find "two-degree" connections for warm introductions, which significantly increases the likelihood of career "magic."

Education and Geopolitical Shifts

  • College ROI: Hoffman believes college is still worth it for the network and the "launch pad," but warns against $300,000 debt for degrees with low returns.
  • The New Curriculum: He advises students to focus on "computational thinking" rather than memorizing coding syntax (like Java or Python), as AI will handle the bulk of technical execution.
      • Critical Skills: Teamwork, sales, wisdom, and compassion will become higher-value human "moats" as AI automates IQ-heavy tasks.
  • Geopolitical Realignment: Hoffman is critical of current US trade policies, specifically tariffs.
      • The End of an Era: He claims the current administration has effectively ended the post-WWII American world order.
      • China’s Advantage: By using "mob boss" tactics like protection money and tariffs, the US is driving allies (Europe, Canada) to collaborate more closely with China, which presents itself as a collaborative business partner.

Strategic Advice for AI Interaction

  • Avoid "Copy-Paste" Dishonesty: Hoffman views pasting raw ChatGPT output as a violation of trust.
  • The "Touch-Up" Rule: Use AI to generate drafts or ideas, but always "touch it up" to ensure it reflects your unique voice.
  • Moment of Trust: Before posting, ask: "Is this good enough that I would hold my head high if my friends had to sit and listen to it?"
"The future is what we make it. We can steer. The way you get to a positive future is not by avoiding bad futures, but by steering towards positive ones." — Reid Hoffman
"LinkedIn is boring? Well, we’re not trying to be exciting, we’re trying to be useful." — Reid Hoffman
"We should regulate when bad regulation is better than no regulation... Why do you have regulation in the financial system? Because the financial system breaking is a serious problem for society." — Reid Hoffman
"This current administration has ended the post-World War II American world order. I don't think it's reversible; it will take a lot of work to reconstruct." — Reid Hoffman
Bill Gates, Sam Altman, Ray Dalio all kind of cautioning about an AI bubble. Do you think we're in an AI bubble? I already see two or three things where today AI transforms industries. You know, in Silicon Valley, probably would have historically cost you $5,000 in legal fees. You do that in 10 minutes of interacting with an AI that goes along with your subscription of $20 a month. What keeps you up at night? Probably the biggest thing is... Reid Hoffman co-founded LinkedIn. He was an early investor in Facebook, Airbnb and he's one of the most connected people in Silicon Valley. Today he's putting everything he has on AI. But not just to change the way we work, but to even change how long we live. Is this time different? It is different insofar as it's a new, much more general-purpose technology than earlier technologies. Are we in an AI bubble? Will humans still work in 10 or 20 years? And where should we be investing right now? This current administration has ended the post-World War II American world order. Do you think the damage can be reversed? This is What's Uptin? with Reid Hoffman. Reid Hoffman, thank you so much for sitting down with me. My pleasure. So I want to start with LinkedIn, naturally. I was at GTC and Nvidia this week and I met a girl, she has 350,000 followers on LinkedIn, from Germany, no Instagram, no YouTube, she's a LinkedIn influencer. This is like become a thing. Did you ever imagine this happening, like the LinkedIn influencers? No, I think it's one of the things we learned during the path in terms of what happened. We thought there would be the sharing of business intelligence and content because part of the thing about navigating the modern world professional is like, well, what should I be learning, who should I be talking to, what are the career changes, what are the industry changes? We knew that sharing, like the collaborative intelligence of the network, would be useful. The notion of having influencers and having that be a follower concept, that was something we learned by looking at the rest of the social web. Mm, and seeing it eventually come to LinkedIn. Yeah. And part of the and part of the theory on LinkedIn is to say, well, there's a bunch of things that are very useful within the social web. There's things where you go, "Hey, that's entertaining, talking about sports, social games, other kinds of things." But what are the things that help people with their work? What are things that help people with their careers? And that's what we do at LinkedIn. Now, when we see something out on the social web and say, "Actually, there's a version of that that would be helpful for work," then we do it. Talk about video because, you know, 2020 brought the emergence of TikTok, YouTube brought YouTube Shorts, Instagram Reels, etc, etc. LinkedIn brought its own version of short-form vertical video. Anecdotally, it was like really big for maybe six months and then it stopped being big and now kind of a lot of people are wondering, is it is it going to be... I mean, do you see that as a big part of LinkedIn going forward? Well, I think, for sure, part of what we have to do is we have to figure out what the version is that works for the network. And the reason why I think it's for sure is because the same notion where you have like LinkedIn influencers, you have either business leaders or experts or content leaders or coaches or any of these things who are establishing a presence and talking to a set of people and having that content be useful for people navigating their careers and work. The same reason that like a post or an essay is useful, video is also useful. You see the person... Now, I don't think we'll be seeing TikTok dances. I think that would be a bit of a surprise. I don't know what a business rain dance looks like, for example. But, I do think, you'll see, the reason it's like, "Oh, do I find you compelling as a potential leader in understanding about, you know, a transformation like AI or something else?" Actually, in fact, seeing you, interacting with you, getting that sense, video is part of what brings that tangibility for human connection. And so I think that that will persist. What version of it, we continue to iterate on to say what works for the network. Okay. What do you think it was about LinkedIn that made it successful when other apps, you know, many other apps failed? So, well, that's decades of work. But, I think part of it was staying very true to the mission, right? So the mission is we try to be time-saving, not time-spending. We try to be what are the things that matter to you in terms of what you're doing at work. So for many, many years, people were like, "Well, LinkedIn's boring." It's like, "Well, we're not trying to be exciting, we're trying to be useful," right? So... It's it's almost counterintuitive to other platforms which, you know, inherently are consuming more of our time because they're they're addictive, right? Yes. And and our thing is, no, no, it's the how do you get the most bang for your buck in the shortest time frame? It doesn't mean that we say, you know, zero time frame, but it's kind of like the question of what are the things that are most helpful to you and you go, "Good. I'm I've got the things I need right now. I'm moving on. It's great." Because part of being kind of work-focused is like you'd rather work eight hours than 10 hours if you can get the same stuff done. Same thing in terms of kind of use of LinkedIn. And so that focus, and then also being like for example, like one of the things is like LinkedIn, one of the principles is civility. We don't permit you to be like hostile, right, to people. That's just not the right environment. And if you say, "Well, freedom of speech," like great, go yell on whatever social platform of your choice is. That's not what we do here. Yeah, I was going to say, I never see that kind of content on LinkedIn. I'm not sure if it's because people are representing their professional selves or if LinkedIn is doing a good job of suppressing it or... Short answer is both. Okay. There you go. Let's talk about some of your AI companies. You have Inflection, Manas, tell me about tell me about them. I mean, what what, you know, you're you have a lot of options of how you want to spend your time. What brought these into into into light? So Inflection first, because Mustafa Suleyman and Karén Simonyan and I had the idea that chatbots were going to be important. This was pre-ChatGPT. So ChatGPT came out while we were building. Part of the idea with Inflection, which is, you know, this kind of great still-going company, not as big as ChatGPT because they got out there first and that's great. But the but like part of the thing is is you what you do as an entrepreneur is you picture the future. And part of picturing the future is we will all have multiple AI systems that we're close to that help us navigate our life. There'll be things obviously in a LinkedIn context, like work-life and so forth, but there's also like travel assistant or life assistant or everything else. And one of the things we realized was that everyone in the AI space was focusing on IQ. And IQ is only one important element of that kind of life assistant. EQ is also important. Like how do we establish trust isn't because, "Well, you seem to be smart." There's a lot of smart people you don't really trust. It's because you seem to be in tune with me. You have an understanding of who I am, what I want, and it's and it's much more natural to interact with you if the emotional tone is right. So we were the first out at the substantive EQ portion. And now, of course, there's other quotients that have been added to this too. There's, you know, some with Inflection but like AQ, like can I act on your behalf? Action quotient. SQ, am I socially intelligent within the group? Right, there's a stack of these things going. That was Inflection, Inflection was the first one. Manas is partially like what are the really important things about AI that are beyond chatbots, beyond coding assistants, beyond work, you know, kind of work process automation, that could make a massive difference in human life? And health is obviously one of them. Now, one part of it is you'll have a medical assistant who can say, "Hey, you know, I've got this rash on my on my hand, you know. Should I be alarmed? Should I do anything? What should I do about it? Take picture and so forth." But like if you can cure critical diseases that are killers, like cancer or other things, that makes a massive difference to all people, young, old, rich country, poor country, etc, etc. And so Siddhartha Mukherjee and I co-founded Manas because AI is the right technology that gives us the first real shot we have of curing something that is as difficult as cancer. How is it going? Oh, it's going well. It's early days. These take years to do. We've built some pretty interesting fundamental technology about how do you kind of identify targets, because one of the things that's weird about cancer is they say "cancer," you're like there's thousands of cancers with this kind of description of it. So what are the targets that are the different kind of things that are doable, which molecules might be a therapeutic in this? And even in this earliest prototyping work, we've actually already discovered some potentially interesting therapeutic molecules. So it's very early, right? So it's kind of like there's this thing about, you know, you file like this drug development form and you kind of say, "Hey, we're going to look at this drug," and so we've done that. And that's just prototyping the the AI technology that we have are in the process of building. You have Bill Gates, Sam Altman, Ray Dalio all kind of cautioning about an AI bubble. It's the talk of so many, you know, headlines right now. Do you think we're in an AI bubble? Categorically no. I think that the look, I think to some degree bubble is kind of an expectation question. So are there some financings of billions of dollars into AI companies that will fail? Yes. That's not a bubble. A bubble is when like the promise of an industry has overly grown and the whole thing falls down. The the reason why I think categorically no is I already see two or three things where today AI transforms industries. There's obviously all the coding stuff which will be very intense this year and the coding will then spread to others. But like for example, if anyone hasn't tried to say, "Hey, there's this I'm going to use, you know, Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini as my legal assistant," try it. Doesn't mean that there aren't room for lawyers and so forth, but like here you go, "Oh, I'd like to I'd like to offer you a term sheet or something." You can literally go in and say, "Here's the things that I think should be in the term sheet. Can you provide me a draft term sheet?" You look at it and go, "Oh, add in some of these clauses and withdraw those." Get another version. That, you know, in Silicon Valley, probably would have historically cost you $5,000 in legal fees. You do that in 10 minutes of interacting with an AI that goes along with your subscription of $20 a month. Well, so on that note, you know, I have a few friends been laid off within AI or tech companies within the past year. You also have, you know, tens of thousands of layoffs happening at the Amazons, Salesforces. What do you say to people who say that, you know, these jobs are not going to come back? And some people are saying we could hit 10% unemployment, 20% unemployment. So, look, I I think the transition period is always difficult. So I don't want to undersell the difficulty. And so the very first thing for a lot of companies is go, "Oh, I can save 10% of my cost with a 10% workforce reduction because I've got this kind of productivity and all the rest." But by the way, the reason why companies grow employees is not because they're always looking to trim employees. They're looking to have like kind of high revenue and business impact per employee. And they hire more employees as they can have more high business impact. So it's not like the the usual way that people chart this is they think there's a fixed amount of work and, oh boy, now the work's going to be done with 80 people rather than 100 people, so 20 people are going to get laid off. And it's like there's no such thing as a fixed amount of work. There's, for example, when you say even if this company says, "Hey, I only need half the engineers that I had before," by the way, it's going to take them years to get there, just be clear. And the other, you know, the kind of the layoffs à la Square, you know, Block and everything else, those are all "we mishired." There's nothing about AI in that right now. I mean, there's nothing that shows that. But if you say, "Hey, I I'm going to have half," what do what do the other half do? Well, the other half go join other companies, the other half go join other startups and so forth, like company start... Or start their own companies, right? Yes, exactly. So that but it doesn't undercut the transition difficulties. So there are transition difficulties and nearly for sure all of us are going into those transition difficulties. And my general recommendation to people is start learning to use AI because how are you on the right side of that? Because as companies are adjusting, they're like, "We need people to help us with AI." Great, that can be you. Be one of them. Yeah. You you seem to be in a like an advocate for using AI to be superhuman and I I think a couple people say this now, but it's like you you won't get replaced by AI, you'll get replaced by someone who does AI really well or who knows AI really well... And who's working with AI. And who's working with AI. Yeah. How about though, like long term? Let's say 10 years from now. Should governments be experimenting with universal basic income? I mean, do these because that's the whole point of generative AI, right? It's like you you know I love the analogy of the the elevator, right, or the car didn't replace the this, right, the jobs came back or it took half of America to do farming for the other half. I get all of that. But then when you bring up the generative nature of AI, all of a sudden you're like, "Wait a minute, is this time different?" Well, it is different insofar as it's a new, much more general-purpose technology than earlier technologies. So and it affects not just one industry but like all industries. And we're in a network-connected environment, so it affects them faster and broader. So all of these things make it new. And what's more, as a jet-tech technology, it's the first beginning where you go, you know, for example, cranes did replace human workers and what they're doing, but it was kind of a very specific focused way of doing that. Here, with agents, you can replace human workers, say customer service agents, with just AI, and there will be a bunch of replacement where that agentic technology plays that way. So it is different: speed, size of impact, etc. But what I think is not different is that like it's the you never can say fully like, "Well, what's the job, what are the new jobs that are going to get created?" And you never say that, that gets evolved out through like you don't know, right? So and they go, "Well, but maybe this time there isn't new ones." It's like, okay, there's some chance and we'll have to navigate the episodes, but I would be willing to take a fairly substantive bet there will be new jobs. Now the transition's still really difficult. So we said, "What should governments be doing? Should they be doing UBI? Should they be doing other things?" Actually, in fact, what they should be doing is workforce transformation, right? And and many different experimental things to see which ones, because, you know, when you tend to talk to government people they say, "Great, we'll set up the community college, we'll have community colleges that have AI education." You're like, "It's not going to be that simple," right? So you have to be dynamic and trying with a number of different things and, you know, some of the projects I support like Opportunity@Work and others are trying to figure out that kind of a nimbleness of network to be adaptive to this and that's where I think we need to do. Now, just as a last note on this, what you want to be doing is monitoring what's actually happening, not trying to build for what you theoretically think will happen, because just about everybody who theoretically thinks they know what'll happen are only right by accident, whether you're an expert, a government person, an economist. We don't really know. And a lot of people find that scary, but by the way, and I appreciate that, I understand, but by the way, in that scary, like as an entrepreneur, I look at that as an interesting opportunity because those transformations, it's like when we brought in electricity in the society, everyone thought it was really scary. Can you imagine the modern world without electricity? The opportunity there is huge. Transition very difficult. So in that case, I mean, you hear people from Silicon Valley once in a while come out with this doom and gloom prediction and they're dropping out of the board or whatever. I mean, do you you're obviously much more optimistic than that, but but what makes you optimistic on that? Well, so a couple things. One, history. Like the doom and gloom that you've heard, you know, like by the way, if you went back and looked at the discourse around the printing press, it's very similar to the discourse around AI. "Oh, it's going to collapse society, people are going to have a whole bunch of misinformation, they're going to... the social infrastructure is going to collapse," and so forth. And by the way, human beings in disruptive change do create problems. So we have the printing press, 90 years of religious war, right? So, you know, we humans have to navigate well. So that's one, history is that. Two is I'm a very strong believer that the future is what we make it, right? And so we can steer. Like I don't go, "Oh, things are going to be bad! Aaah!" I go, "Oh, steer well," right? Like like like engage, do things. And it doesn't mean that there aren't, you know, potholes and, universe you know, traffic accidents and other kinds of things as you're going. But staying in the game and steering towards a much more positive future is how you get there. The way you get to a positive future is not by avoiding bad futures, but by steering towards positive ones. And to what role has government regulation helped or prevented where you think AI should be heading towards this transformation of many industries? So I think primarily what you want to be doing when you have even as a amazing... like this massive tsunami of AI technology, you want to be doing two things. One, prevent things that are genuinely like just no exceptions catastrophic. So you go bioterrorism, cybersecurity. You say, "Okay, we should try to prevent that stuff and in preventing that, even if we have bad or clumsy regulation that has some other unforeseen costs, that's worth preventing those things." That's the top thing because like a general proxy I use is you should regulate when bad regulation is better than no regulation. Right? So why do you have regulation in the financial system? Because even though it impedes the development of new financial system technology, the financial system breaking is a serious problem for society. So bad regulation's better than no regulation. The second thing you should be doing is monitoring. As opposed to saying, "Well, I'm insightful and I think that we're going to have a problem with people who are working in the customer service agents, you know, customer service industry." Yes, probably. So I'm going to pre-predict what all the transitions should happen. Like well, no one can do that effectively, including that person who thinks that. So what you should do when you say "I have a worry, a specific worry," whether it's, you know, job transitions, misinformation, other kinds of things, say, "Well, how do we measure that? How do we monitor that?" So what should regulation be doing? It should be saying generate that measurement, generate that kind of like, "Here's the thing I want you to be measuring and I want you to be reporting." And what's more, if I'm really concerned about it and I don't think you'll necessarily be reporting honestly, I want you to have your auditors report it, right? So the third party, that's why we have auditors, is saying, "Yes, this is what's happening on this concern in our in our measurement thing." So that you can say, "Oh, look, this is really growing, we have a problem here, now let's start addressing it," as opposed to pre-guessing every problem. Is college still worth it? Yes, but for strange reasons. the strange reason is a combination of the network of people that you meet at college and the fact that you spend some time getting ready on the launch pad for society. There are many things that are broken on college. It's it's a question around, for example, the you know the degree system, what you're learning. Like for example, one of the classic things is most universities are saying "no AI" because AI is cheating. You're like, "Everyone's going to be using AI for everything that they're doing, including by the way graduate students and professors." You're adopting an idiotic thing. It's like saying "no driving on the highway, you just have to run," right? Like it's like this doesn't work. So so there's a bunch of transformation that's going to need to happen, but it is still useful. Now the problem is is our college system has kind of gotten to a place where it's it's like it should be had at any price and everything has an ROI to it. And while, for example, a liberal education is a very good thing to get, it's not again at any price. So if you have graduates who are, you know, this is a little bit of an extreme statement, but like they're graduating with an urban studies degree with $300,000 in debt, that's a very bad challenge to start from. So then what would you major in, or what would you advise someone to major in? So, well generally speaking, again hard to predict the future. There'll be more and more software transformation. So you say, "Well, CS." Well, CS sort of. You want computational thinking, as is demonstrated this year, you don't really need to learn coding per se. That's like a lot of CS majors tend to be "I learned how to, you know, write in Java" or "I learned how to write in C++" or whatever, Rust, a whole bunch of different frameworks, Python. What you actually need to be is thinking about how software amplifies your work, whether your work is I'm a historian or your work is I'm a journalist or your work is, you know, I'm a financial analyst or anything else. So you should be learning that and you should also be learning the kind of what are the set of skills that would be important for the future. Now one part of it is human skills would be more important. So like for example, we know that AI will be later in how are we doing, like for example, a sales thing of you and I interacting together. We will be... that's not a thing that AI will be anytime soon in. So like how do we work together as a team? So you want to be learning all of those kinds of things too. But like still thinking, still, LLMs are terrible at math, right? You know, so yes. You know, so it's like there's a whole stack of stuff you should be learning, but it's not like, you know, I don't know if you, you know, dating my age, but like one of the things was there's this old film called The Graduate and they say "plastics." It's like there isn't a specific kind of thing where you go, "Just learn this." Yeah. It seems, you know, I saw seven or eight AI CEOs on stage yesterday, you know, some many of them billionaires, but seeing them interact like was really interesting because the you're talking about social skills. I mean I was really shocked the way they interacted and the way they were kind of introverted and... I mean I shouldn't be shocked because it's nothing new necessarily to Silicon Valley, but it almost made me it almost made me a little scared. Like is this if these people are running the next wave of the transformation of every single industry, you know, is there going to be enough empathy or, you know, the human side of of understanding what they're actually building. So how does someone... I mean there's a lot of genius people out there that are total nerds or introverts. Like what should they be doing? So well, it's there's still a lot of room for people who are on the spectrum and people building stuff, as we see a lot of great technologists building the current AI wave. That's of course part of the reason why at Inflection we did EQ, because it's actually really important. And it is the thing that, you know, part of what I've been trying to whether it's writing Superagency or other things is to kind of try to bring a certain humanism back to the construction of new technology, new technology businesses, and how how do people think about it, what are the things they do? So for example, a classic one is, you know, in you know kind of my book before Superagency, Impromptu was like it's amplification intelligence. How do we think about AI is amplifying human capabilities? It doesn't mean that we won't also do replacement, but put as much energy or more into how does that make us better in terms of as humans, in terms of what we're doing on a full spectrum? Not just IQ, IQ of course super important, but like wisdom, right, or even compassion as ways that you're operating. And I think, you know, the way that you do that is you add that into the market, you get that in a market demand, you get that into the general discourse, you go on podcasts and talk about it, and that's part of how the industry starts responding to that more. Yeah. you know, when you think about college, I almost wonder if the whole model needs to be rethought because my dad, you know, studied civil engineering, got his PhD, was in the same office for 40 years, right? Using what he learned in his PhD, I mean he's doing research and all of that, but you know versus me in my 20s, I think I had seven different jobs, right? And so it almost begs the question of now when you think about what's going to happen even what's happening right now, what you're learning in the university in every industry is going to be changing, you know, every few years. Have you thought about that? I mean have you thought like do you think that we'll see the sort of evolution of what it means to even go to university, whether it's more certificates or, you know, I don't know. Well, for sure, I think that the look, so one of the things that Opportunity@Work does is say there's a whole bunch of jobs and skills that a university degree is not the actual... it says nothing about your capability of doing it. It's not positive, it's not negative, it's just irrelevant. So and by the way that means that lots of people, like say for example you didn't have the economics to go to the super expensive universities, you were working to put your family or yourself, you know, through, but you still have the skills, you still have the talent. And and so we both want to make an ability to construct all of those to to learn those skills and deploy them, and also an employment environment that allows that encourages and allows this to happen because we want talent to get there. And that's part of the reason why like projects like Opportunity@Work, Tear the Paper Ceiling, you know, what are stars? Like people who are like highly talented people who do not have college credentials that can work on things. All of those things are an important part of the future. Last year you expressed concerns that US tariffs and spending cuts could inadvertently help China's tech industry and damage America's competitiveness in AI. I I've been to China multiple times. I've been to Tencent and Alibaba. I went to the Shanghai Auto Show last year and saw crazy, you know, Chinese EV companies that you and I, well you've probably heard of but most people have never heard of. Where do you stand on that now? I mean when it comes to US, China, and AI. So, you know, look, as an as an American, I'm very like I want the US society to prosper and kind of continue to have a great influence in the world order because I believe in kind of a lot of our fundamental American values. Now that being said, the various things that the current administration's been doing, tariffs being one of them, are kind of causing the rest of like even former friends to no longer like us. And you can see Europe, Canada, other places that are formerly like us being their closest partner, they're now like, "Well, you're treating us badly so we'll take a look at who is our best trading partners." And if it's China is our best trading partners, great. China's not coming in and saying, you know, "Do this for us" or "pay this additional tariff" and so forth. They just say, "Hey, great, we'd love to do business with you." That's how you do business, right? You don't you do business collaboratively. You do business not by ordering people around, not by trying to play like a mob boss with protection money, you know, etc. You do it by collaborating. So I think we have actually already have done serious damage to not only like your average American citizen doesn't understand, it's like look, if if the ability for a bunch of US industries to to including the tech industry to sell overseas diminishes, then less money flows into the US. And if less money flows into the US, there's less money everywhere. It's less money for the schools, it's less money for taxes, it's less money... like there's all kinds of things where that gets to less money for. And that's the reason why something that they may think is abstract like trading partnerships is actually necessary for the health of their communities and the health of their families. Do you think the damage can be reversed or we have to wait till a future presidency for that? So I think this current administration has ended the post-World War II American world order. So I don't think it's reversible. I think there's a reconstructible, but it will take a lot of work to do that. Hmm, interesting. I want to speak about, you know, a little bit about Reid and the advice that Reid would give. So let's start with LinkedIn. I mean what would your advice be to grow on LinkedIn? So on LinkedIn, well, it's a little bit like also AI, which is start using it, experimenting. What people don't realize is if you do just a fair a like a little bit, it can actually really benefit you. Now say for example the most basic, fill out a profile, have some real information in your profile. You go, "I did that, nothing happened." Yes, because when you do that, it's like filling out a thing where when people are searching do they find you? Right? And who knows, maybe they find you tomorrow, maybe they find you six months from now. But it's so little work to do. Like filling out a relatively robust LinkedIn profile is under 10 minutes, right? It's relatively straightforward. So that's one thing to do. Another thing is when you connect to people who and this is one of the things people are like, "Well, who should you connect to?" It's like, well, no, connect to people who know you and trust you, right? And it could be by the way you know your friend's uncle met you and liked you and so forth and, you know, you connect with them. Great, connect with them. Because then when you look through like okay, who would be people that would be useful for me to talk to about what I might be doing with my career, my jobs, and so forth? And you start doing searches at two degree and you realize your friend's uncle knows somebody at this company that you're interested in about and you can ping them and say, "Hey, do you mind if you get me connected so I could just talk to them and see if there's a good fit?" And and so sure, that your friend's uncle's, you know, colleague may not know you specifically, but because you come in through an introduction that's "Oh, bright, hard-working, solid, smart, etc. Great." Then I may not be the right person, but this other person at the company is the right person to talk to because I'm introducing, you'll do that. So that kind of engagement starts creating serendipity. And what you wanted to have is you want to increase your serendipity of something that could be magical that would help you in the career. And those would be very first things to do. Yeah, absolutely. I always when I was a I worked at MTV News actually back in the day and I was like, you know, I was covering Justin Bieber and stuff that I didn't enjoy so I said, "You know what? I want to work in in business news." And so I started every time I would meet someone at a party or a networking event or whatever, I would say, "Yeah, I like my job, but I'm looking to do this." And it was amazing how many times people would say, you know, "Look me up on LinkedIn, see how many, you know, if I have someone you want to meet." You know, so it was kind of like this digital library of of of a database that I could kind of be like, "Hey, do you actually know this person?" When it comes to posting though, I want to talk about ChatGPT. There's this thing that I've noticed where and people are now starting to create memes and make fun of the the LinkedIn posts where it's like, and I'm not talking about the "I'm thrilled to announce," like that's been there for a while, but the the em dash, the you know, "It's not it's not challenge, it's redirection," you know. And so there's these instant things where you're scrolling ChatGPT... I mean, sorry, you're scrolling LinkedIn and you can say like, "This guy used ChatGPT, not just used, but probably just copy and pasted a ChatGPT prompt." And then again and again and again. And I almost find myself and some other people I know, it's almost like our unique selling point as LinkedIn creators is not even opening ChatGPT and like, "Let me just write this from my own voice to ensure 100% no one assumes it's it's AI." So I mean in terms of creation, you have your podcast, your books, I mean how do you think about that, differentiating the AI-sounding content from Reid? Well, I think the most central thing, now you're asking a person as you know who created a digital twin of themselves, who co-wrote the first book with GPT, right, Impromptu. So, now I think it's important to be transparent. So I think going to ChatGPT, Copilot, whatever, and producing something and then just pasting it in, I think that's kind of dishonest. And it isn't being... that's terrible, but it's like part of what you want to be is when you're establishing trust and brand as people like trust, "Like no no, I think this is worth your reading." Like I looked at it, I touched it, etc. Now, I have no objection to someone producing a you know Claude or Copilot or Gemini thing and then saying, "Okay, I'm looking through," and then like touching it up to something that they think is really good. Because it's like, "Here, I recommend this to you." It's actually a value to your audience, it's a value to you, it saved you time, it enhanced your... But what you should do is say, "What I'm posting, I am taking a moment of trust. Like am I am I taking your attention with some care that I really recommend that you pay attention to this? I I own this in terms of what I'm talking to you." And so I think that's really important. And that's how I think people should approach it. So it's not that I actually think one should use AI tools, but, you should see is like, is this good enough that this is what I would want people to be reading that I produced when all of my friends had to sit there and listen to it and would I hold my head high versus go, "Oh, oops, sorry about that," you know. Like that's how you should look at it when you're doing posts. Mm-hmm. What do you think about today's new tech talent? The new graduates coming out of Stanford. What do you make of them? Well look, I think every generation is smart. I think that the, you know, part of what I think is this is Generation AI. So I think that part of what they are is just like each each new generation has some is kind of native in the current tech. I think that's something that's super important, I look forward to learning more from them about this. And so and you know, look, I have some sympathy of like, "Wow, this is a lot more chaotic than when I read," you know, kind of graduated. Which you know and and by the way that means that lots of people, like say for example you didn't have the economics to go to the super expensive universities, you were working to put your family or yourself, you know, through, but you still have the skills, you still have the talent. And and so we both want to make an ability to construct all of those to to learn those skills and deploy them, and also an employment environment that allows that encourages and allows this to happen because we want talent to get there. And that's part of the reason why like projects like Opportunity@Work, Tear the Paper Ceiling, you know, what are stars? Like people who are like highly talented people who do not have college credentials that can work on things. All of those things are an important part of the future. Chaotic in terms of funding and meetings and the way things are moving just faster. Yeah, exactly. But like what's what's what's a good thing to do with your career? It used to be kind of like much more simply tracked and now it's like it's a very chaotic melee. But you're optimistic, it seems, in the talent in the next generation. And yes, absolutely. No no, I think anybody anybody who goes, "Oh, this generation has problem X," and even if a generation does have problem X, well that's what the other generation said about you. So you know, come on. That's true. They're always judging each other. Yeah, that's true. The last question I want to ask you is what keeps you up at night? Probably the biggest thing is, I mean there's a whole set of things and it's not just world chaos, you know, war in the Middle East, you know. I mean it just goes on and on and on. It's a lot right now. Yes, right. But probably the biggest one is let's not fumble the opportunity about how magical AI can be for us. And we can, there's all kinds of ways we can fumble it. And part of the reason I'm being very positive is not because there won't be traffic accidents and, you know, things going wrong. Of course there will be. There was things going wrong with every launch of technology, including electricity and all the rest. But like the possibility, like for example, like it's really a question of which year do we all have a medical assistant on our smartphone? It could be this year.
