That's better than today's average GP. And think of what that makes the difference. Like relatively few people have access to a doctor and almost nobody at a moment's notice. That's massively transformative to human life. That's the kind of thing I don't want to fumble. Do you think we will like there's a lot I mean I've been reading articles there's a lot of like longevity research happening right now. Do you do you see a time where we're living to 140, 150? I think we will be figuring out how to live longer and longer. I don't think it's kind of the quick unlock. Because the principal ways that we've expanded our average lifespans, which we've done a lot of low-hanging fruit on, is eliminate various forms of child mortality. That really helps with age. improve basic nutrition and a bunch of other things. You know, that helps a lot. Measure basic things that kind of kill a bunch of us, heart attacks, that kind of thing, and try to do that. Now it starts getting into the hard part of it, right? Which is, okay, so we're all in a version of we're all dying from cancer, it's just the question is does something else get us first? And so what are the things that we can do to kind of create that cellular rejuvenation? And I think that's going to be a hard path. I think AI will help with that, I think other things, but I don't think it's a, you know, some people are like, "No no, we're all going to be living to 100 you know, in you know naturally in a couple years." Like I don't think the science is there yet. Reid, thank you so much. This was a really insightful conversation. I learned a lot and I really appreciate your time. Thanks for listening to this episode of What's Uptin? where I bring you inside the minds driving tech, money, and power. Follow along on YouTube, Spotify, Apple, or wherever you get your podcasts. You can also follow my personal account on Instagram @uptin, would love to connect with you.

Commissioners Set the Course | Material Matters with SEC Chairman Paul Atkins

U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission(22.2K) ·27:40 ·1.2K views · 2026-04-16
Paul Atkins (Chairman, SEC)
The SEC is pivoting back to its "core mandate" of disclosure-based regulation and capital formation, prioritizing the establishment of a clear, "fit for purpose" regulatory framework for digital assets to ensure the United States remains the global leader in financial innovation.
TL;DR The SEC is pivoting back to its "core mandate" of disclosure-based regulation and capital formation, prioritizing the establishment of a clear, "fit for purpose" regulatory framework for digital assets to ensure the United States remains the global leader in financial...

TL;DR

The SEC is pivoting back to its "core mandate" of disclosure-based regulation and capital formation, prioritizing the establishment of a clear, "fit for purpose" regulatory framework for digital assets to ensure the United States remains the global leader in financial innovation.

Restoring the SEC’s Core Mission

The leadership team emphasizes a return to the agency’s "tripartite mission": protecting investors, maintaining fair and efficient markets, and facilitating capital formation.

  • Critique of Recent Policy: Commissioner Mark Uyeda characterized the last four years as a "complete deviation" from the SEC's historical role.
      • Regulatory Overreach: The agency moved toward being a "business conduct regulator," micromanaging areas such as share repurchases, cybersecurity, supply chain management, and DEI at the board level.
      • Focus on Non-Financial Disclosures: Uyeda specifically cited greenhouse gas emissions and resource extraction as being "outside the lane" of traditional securities regulation.
  • A "Disclosure-First" Approach: The leadership intends to move away from prescriptive mandates and return to a model that relies on disclosure, allowing investors—rather than the government—to judge the merits of business decisions.

Revitalizing Public Markets and Capital Formation

A major priority for the Commission is addressing the decline in the number of public companies, which has dropped by nearly 50% over the last 30 years.

  • Making IPOs "Great Again": The SEC aims to shift the perception of the IPO from a mere "liquidity event" for early investors to a viable capital-raising tool for growth-stage companies.
  • Democratizing Opportunity: Leadership emphasized that public markets allow the general public to participate in wealth creation that is currently often restricted to private markets.
  • Portfolio Diversification: Commissioner Uyeda argued that the SEC should not block high-risk investments for "protection," noting that even high-risk assets can be beneficial when part of a diversified portfolio.
  • The Entrepreneurial Spirit: Chairman Atkins and Commissioner Uyeda framed financial regulation as a tool to sustain the American tradition of free enterprise and minimal government interference in trade.

Digital Assets and "Project Crypto"

Commissioner Hester Peirce is leading "Project Crypto," an initiative designed to move away from "regulation by enforcement" toward a durable, understandable framework.

  • Technological Utility: Peirce highlighted that cryptography solves the "double spending" problem, allowing for the secure transfer of value over the internet without traditional intermediaries.
  • Benefits of On-Chain Finance:
      • Disintermediation: Reducing reliance on third parties who may mishandle or lose assets.
      • Programmability: Using smart contracts to automate regulatory requirements, collateral payouts, and restrictions efficiently.
  • Global Competitiveness: The Commission is aligned with the goal of making the U.S. the "crypto capital of the world" by providing the legal clarity needed to keep innovators from moving offshore.
  • Urgency and Durability: While there is a push to move quickly to provide clarity, Peirce stressed the need for "future-proof" and "principles-based" rules that can survive technological shifts.

Inter-Agency Cooperation and Investor Education

The Commission is fostering a more collaborative relationship with other regulators and focusing on "meeting investors where they are."

  • SEC-CFTC Alignment: With former SEC staffer Michael Selig now chairing the CFTC, the agencies intend to coordinate closely to avoid redundant resource spending and clearly allocate jurisdiction over crypto assets.
  • Legislative Role: The Commissioners acknowledged that Congress must ultimately play a role in allocating authority between the two agencies to provide a permanent framework.
  • Investor Protection through Education:
      • Building Skepticism: Education efforts are focusing on empowering investors to ask questions and recognize "too good to be true" offers.
      • Early Intervention: Commissioner Peirce advocated for starting financial education at the elementary school level to normalize financial literacy before individuals begin managing their own capital.

Notable Quotes

"In the last four years... we became instead of a regulator with our tripartite mission... more of a business conduct regulator and even beyond that. We were starting to somewhat micromanage whether you issue dividends, engage in share repurchases, what your cybersecurity looked like... we were so far out of our lane." — Mark Uyeda, SEC Commissioner
"Innovation is what makes the financial markets resilient; it's what ensures that they serve people's actual needs. We need to have financial regulation that is open to innovators." — Hester Peirce, SEC Commissioner
"The president has often said that he wants to make America the crypto capital of the world. We do want to make this the place where people want to innovate, whether it's in crypto or something else. If we can encourage people to build here, our investors will benefit, our markets will benefit." — Paul Atkins, SEC Chairman
When you look at the history of America, it is about opportunity, it is about free enterprise. So that's what I view our job is, making sure that we can still sustain that entrepreneurial spirit in this country. But in the last four years, it was a complete deviation. The one interesting thing is, you know, the president has often said that he wants to make America the crypto capital of the world. We do want to make this the place where people want to innovate, whether it's in crypto or something else. If we can encourage people to build here, our investors will benefit, our markets will benefit. This is a very important inflection point, I think, in the American markets. I’m Paul Atkins, Chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission, speaking to you from our headquarters here in Washington, D.C. The SEC's mission has a profound impact on the American financial system and the businesses, market participants, investors, and innovators who make it the most dynamic market in the world. And yet, for many people, the SEC itself remains something of a mystery. I believe that it's time to change that. And that's why I'm excited to launch a new podcast here at the SEC, which will feature exclusive and hopefully insightful conversations with policymakers from inside this building, leading experts outside it, and friends and colleagues who are as informed as they are engaging. For our very first episode, I couldn't think of a better place to start than right here at the SEC with two colleagues who help to drive our mission forward every day. I'm delighted to sit down with SEC Commissioners Mark Uyeda and Hester Peirce, and I hope that you will enjoy both discussions. Commissioner Uyeda, it's great to be with you here today and to chat a little bit about SEC matters and your role here at the SEC and some of your background. So you basically grew up here at the SEC, which, proud to say, has created a really great colleague. From your journey from staff member to Commissioner, maybe you can describe some of the events that helped shape your outlook on your job and why you are doing your job here at the SEC. Well, I think it just seems like I grew up here, and it's true; I've been here for nearly 20 years. But before that, I had a whole career for nearly 11 years in private practice and as a state securities regulator. And in that role as outside counsel to many publicly traded companies, having to deal with all their compliance requirements. I remember dealing with the corporate executives, their forms three, fours, and fives under Section 16a, and all of that good stuff—the 10-Ks, the 10-Qs. Form 8-K was much shorter at the time, back in the mid-'90s. And then going on after that to work for two and a half years as a state securities regulator. I think I'm one of the few SEC Commissioners coming here to the SEC after service as a state securities regulator, certainly in the past several decades. But do you remember when we first met? It was back in 2005. I was the chief advisor to the California Corporations Commissioner, and you were the keynote speaker at the SEC's annual small business forum, and it was combined with a meeting of the then-convened Advisory Committee on Smaller Public Companies. And that is something that has always really stuck with me—thinking about how do we have these smaller public companies access capital formation? You asked about things that have really influenced me during my time as a corporate securities lawyer. I think of the dot-com boom of the 1990s. I think of the accounting scandals of the early 2000s, Enron, HealthSouth, Global Crossing, and the like. Sarbanes-Oxley, the creation of PCAOB, the financial crisis, Dodd-Frank, but then the JOBS Act. So all of those things and taking all those experiences is how it gives me a prism to think about what we're currently doing today. Well, so you served as Acting Chairman from January of last year, 2025, to April, last April. And so you inherited an agency that had really diverted, I would say, from its core mandate. So thank you very much for your leadership in helping get it back on track and dealing with some of the real outstanding issues that needed to be addressed. So let's maybe do a two-part question here. First, what was the impact on capital markets of, say, the last several years of Commission action? And then second, you know, how does that inform you and the most pressing priorities for the year ahead? I think, you know, the nearly 30 years I've been a corporate and securities lawyer—actually I should say over 30 years—and then nearly 20 years here at the SEC, it's given me a pretty good baseline as to what the SEC typically does regardless of who is the president. And I can tell you the last four years were a complete outlier to anything I've seen during that over three decades of time as a corporate and securities lawyer. And it was unfortunate because I think we really went off track. We became, instead of a regulator with our tripartite mission of protecting investors, maintaining fair, orderly, and efficient markets, and facilitating capital formation, more of a business conduct regulator and even beyond that. All of a sudden, we were starting to somewhat micromanage whether you issue dividends or engage in share repurchases, what your cybersecurity looked like, your supply chain management, human capital resources, the makeup of your employee workforce, DEI at the board level, and not to mention greenhouse gas emissions, environmental issues, resource extraction, your mine safety record, and all of these things, how you paid your executives. We're supposed to be a disclosure agency, and that is something that we have hewed to regardless of whether this was during the Clinton administration in the '90s, George W. Bush, or President Obama. But in the last four years, it was a complete deviation. And one has to wonder, well, why? I think when going back to when you were first here as a Commissioner, we were facing Sarbanes-Oxley and this big congressional mandate to take action. There was absolutely no such legislative mandate to undertake any of these things. So the way I looked at it as someone who's a nearly 20-year veteran of this agency, it was really important to get back to our normal course of action, how we have thought about things. I realize there can be different policy perspectives, but you know, if we're playing a football game, this is kind of a bit like asking if the ball is going to be positioned more towards the left hash mark or the right hash mark. Unfortunately, in the last four years, I think we weren't even in the stadium; we were outside, we were so far out of our lane. So you know, that's what I'm thinking about: getting us back to that core mission and making sure that we don't forget that part of our mission is to facilitate capital formation. Well, speaking of capital formation, today we have about half the number of public companies as we had 30 years ago. So why is that? And how can we help make IPOs great again? Well, I'm a firm believer in both the public and the private markets. They need—it's so important that they coexist together, and they both can thrive together. One feeds off the other. What we need to do though, I think, is make sure that the IPO still has—is used as a capital-raising opportunity for a lot of companies. One of my big fears is many times the IPO is now considered a liquidity event for employees and for early-stage investors and venture capital funds. Very, very important participants in the capital raising ecosystem. But one of the things that we saw back, certainly back in the '90s, was a lot of companies using the public markets to raise capital. What that meant is that everyone, any person in the public, could invest in those companies. And I think that's something that's often overlooked. When I look at the history of the SEC, one thing that we perhaps don't take as much into consideration is diversification. You can have the highest-risk security in your portfolio, but if it's part of a broadly diversified set of positions, the overall effect on that portfolio is not that significant, and in fact in many cases, it can present a superior risk-return opportunity by having that diversity. Talking going back in time to the 1990s, there were a lot of dot-com companies that failed. But there were a number of them that succeeded. And it's certainly not for us at the SEC to tell investors, "no, you cannot invest in this opportunity for your own protection." That's not our role. Well, yeah, I think that's an important thing to remember, I agree with that. So, you know, in the time we have left here, maybe we can turn to the future. So this is the 250th year since the Declaration of Independence. So we're commemorating that throughout the country. And so soon the SEC—I mean we've just passed the 90th year of the SEC back here in 2024 and looking forward towards the first 100 years in 2034. So where do you see the SEC today with respect to this history and what else do you think that we need to do to ensure that America has leadership in the financial services in the years ahead? I remember when I was the Acting Chairman during that brief three-month period, we had a series of executive orders that affected the agency. And I remember my staff going through each of them, and one of them was an executive order for how each agency should think about celebrating the 250th anniversary of this country's founding. I said, this is very important for the SEC. We weren't around during the early parts of this country's history. But when you look at the history of America, it is about opportunity, it is about free enterprise, it's about freedom, it's about the pursuit of happiness, which in many ways I think is the ability to pursue a trade with minimal government interference. Now there is an important role for government for things like safety and other issues, but it was not a society where you had to ask permission for the government to start a business or to engage in a trade. And if you look at the state of the world in 1776, where the United States was relative to many countries in Europe, we weren't as advanced. But that freedom, that liberty, creating those opportunities for people to pursue products, goods, and services for a profit, that is what helped instill this entrepreneurial spirit, and that has propelled us so far beyond all of our other competitors in 1776 to where we are today. But we got to be very much on guard because of all the success America's had in terms of economic growth, innovation, and new technology, that can breed complacency. Just like I'm sure many of the European countries who were much more advanced than the United States in 1776 thought that they were at the top of the world during their era. Well, there's always a competitor willing to try to knock you off. So that's what I view our job is, making sure that we can still sustain that entrepreneurial spirit in this country. And if we can, I can't wait to see what happens when we celebrate our 500th-year anniversary. Of course I won't be around, but you never know. But you know I think look where we came in the first 250 years and looking forward to the next 250 years of what we can yet accomplish. Yeah, it's a big task ahead of us, and so we have a lot of work to do here in the next few years. So I'm glad you're on board and look forward to continuing to work with you. Thank you very much, Commissioner Uyeda; those are great observations and a real challenge for us going forward. So thank you very much for joining us today, and I'm sure the viewers will be very interested to hear what you have had to say. I look forward to chatting with our other colleagues along the same line. So thank you very much for being here. Well, thanks for having me on your inaugural show. Commissioner Peirce, I look forward to spending the next few minutes with you discussing some of the salient issues here at the SEC and some of your background and all of that. So... Mr. Chairman, it's a delight to be here. So super. Well alright, so let's get going. And so first of all, maybe we can discuss a little bit about your background here not just at the SEC but elsewhere. But you and I started working together back in the aughts, so around about 2003 or so, I recall. And so maybe you can discuss some of that and your work elsewhere on the Hill and at other places. Yeah, well I came to your office from the Division of Investment Management and had been an admirer of the way you thought about issues and was really honored to be able to work in your office and get a broader sense of what the Commission does. That led me pretty naturally to go work on the Hill and learn a bit about the legislative process. And that was during the time when the financial crisis happened and Dodd-Frank was developed in response to that. So after that I went off to work in a think tank and think and write about regulation, financial regulation specifically, and worked with a lot of economists in doing that. One of the emphases during the time in your office was cost-benefit analysis, and that was something that I wanted to think more about in an academic setting. So I did that and then had the opportunity to come back as a Commissioner. Well you did such a great job, you know, both here in the Commission and outside and especially at your think tank where you did a lot of work on the Dodd-Frank Act and all that. Any particular issues that you think are still relevant from what you worked on now and that you've been able to address while you've been here? Over the last—you're now in your almost fifth year, about it. Even more. Even more. So I think one of the things that—one of the lessons that anyone who was in the financial regulatory world and lived through the financial crisis comes to think about is what could we have done differently and what can we do to make sure that we don't contribute to a future crisis. And I do think regulation had a role to play. You can actually drive decision-making with regulation in a bad way, and so we want to avoid doing that. I think some of the response to the financial crisis was to sort of reactively try to pull more decision-making into the government and take it away from the private sector where incentives are really important. And so I think what we can do as regulators is think about how we can return incentives to the financial sector, to the private markets, and make sure that the people who are making the decisions then bear the consequences of those decisions. Yeah, well so and one area now that is really top on our list to try to get right with respect to regulation is the whole digital asset area, crypto assets. So maybe—and you are obviously the head of our crypto task force, now Project Crypto, which is wonderful. You've done an excellent job; you have I think a lot to be proud of to help change the direction of the SEC vis-à-vis that particular area of financial services. So I was wondering maybe what you could do is explain a little bit about what is crypto and why is it so important for continued strength of America in capital markets? Well I will tell you that it's a lot easier to do a good job on crypto in this environment where you're very supportive of us trying to get to a regulatory framework that's understandable, that's fit for purpose. Then we can I think better address the problems that come up in crypto but also really open up opportunities for people to innovate in this area. And that was really how I was looking at this issue from the get-go. We need to have financial regulation that is open to innovators because innovation is what makes the financial markets resilient; it's what ensures that they serve people's actual needs. And so I think with respect to crypto specifically, crypto solves the double spending problem. You used to be able to send data over the internet, but you couldn't send value because I could send you value and then I could send the same value to someone else and say, "oh look I paid you both." Cryptography solves that problem. It enables you to send money and say I'm sending it only to you and I can't send it to anyone else. And so on top of that there have been built a whole set of assets, and some of those fall within our space either because you're tokenizing actual securities or because you're taking something that's a token and you're selling it in a way that is a securities transaction. And so what we're trying to do now is build a framework for both of those things and also trying to help regulated entities that are serving their customers and clients to figure out how they can help them with these assets. So what's the difference between an online, on-chain payments versus otherwise? So now I can give my money to PayPal and then send it, or Zelle or whatever, send it to someone else. So what's the difference with an on-chain sort of approach? The beauty of the technology is that it allows you to disintermediate, so that peers can transact with one another directly or through the intermediation of technology. And that's really powerful in our markets because intermediaries have sometimes been the source of problems, right? Either they walk away with your money or they're careless with your money or your assets. And again, this is not to say that we're going to live in a world without intermediaries, but I think there's power in eliminating intermediaries where they're not necessary and building a framework that allows you to do some things in an automated fashion. You can through the power of smart contracts, you can program assets, which is very powerful. You can build regulatory requirements right in or other kinds of restrictions you want to build in. You can programmatically say when collateral has to be paid out, so things happen automatically. And so there's real power to the ability to do those kinds of things very efficiently and automatically. Again, there will still be intermediaries but their roles may change as this technology takes hold. Right. Well so I agree completely with that. And the one interesting thing is, you know, the president has often said that he wants to make America the crypto capital of the world, and so we've been working hard about that. You got started before I got here after inauguration till I arrived in April. So you built up at that time a really good crypto task force; you ran one of your recruits, Michael Selig, now has left us and now he's the chairman at the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, which I think is very exciting. It portends really close working relationship between the two agencies for the first time in decades and decades. So anyway, I think that's a lot to look forward to. So what do you think should be at the top of our list to address some of the risks that are inherent as we undertake rulemaking in this rapidly changing environment with respect to crypto assets? I think the cooperation itself is very helpful because we want to make sure that we're not spending resources to address the same problem. So we're allocating responsibility, and I think that that will help. Right now there hasn't been a regulatory framework around the spot trading of crypto assets, and that's something that CFTC will be working on. But I think we have a role to play where things are within our jurisdiction related to securities and it's just the same mission that we have with respect to anything else: protecting investors, facilitating capital formation, and fostering fair, orderly, and efficient markets. And I think having the close cooperation with the CFTC ensures that we're monitoring markets which are very interrelated with one another and then thinking about where it makes sense for products to be regulated, who the primary regulator should be, and I think then the market can develop in an orderly fashion. And a little bit with Congress as well. I mean obviously they have a role to play. Absolutely. Absolutely. Yes, and they have to allocate authority between the two agencies, sure. Yeah. And so anyway, so now this is a very important inflection point, I think, in the American markets. So we have a lot of opportunities ahead of us, so it really is a historic time. So what do you think the opportunities are ahead and is there any cost of our maybe taking our time doing this? Do you know—is there urgency here? How do you look at the current situation? Well I think we do want to make this the place where people want to innovate, whether it's in crypto or something else. And in order to do that we have to send the message to people that we will work with you when there are ambiguities about how the law applies to your particular facts and circumstances. There have been a lot of ambiguities in connection with crypto which is a new technology that does things in new ways. And so I think laying that framework will make people comfortable to innovate here not only with respect to crypto but with respect to other things because they realize that at the SEC we're willing to deal with those hard questions. We're willing to work with people through those hard questions. And I think then if we can encourage people to build here, our investors will benefit, our markets will benefit. Of course we have to pay attention to difficult and terrible frauds, and we have to try to clamp down on those. But having a good regulatory structure in place is going to be helpful to us as well to identify where the bad activity is and to go after that bad activity and not to spend our enforcement resources where our regulatory resources could have done the job. So I think there's a lot of promise, I'm very excited about what the possibilities are. And of course we have to approach all of this carefully, which is why we do want to work fast, but we also want to work in a way that is principles-based and durable so that as the technology changes it's able to keep up with those changes. Exactly. And we need to future-proof things so we don't have backsliding in the future. So then one thing that undergirds a lot of this though is investor education and that people know what they're investing in and have the tools to do that. Both of us have been very focused on this and you as well. So, you know, why is this so crucial to focus on investor ed and can you speak to some of the efforts and initiatives that have been going on? Yeah, I mean the fraudsters are really able to reach investors very easily these days. They're using all kinds of channels, they're very creative, and I think we need to be creative as well in reaching investors. I don't think the message has really changed. A lot of the problems that we see in the crypto space are very similar to problems that we've seen in other places. But it's really giving people the—empowering them to ask questions, to be skeptical, to say this looks too good to be true, and to say, "I don't need to make a decision today." If someone's pushing me to make a decision today, I can wait and think about it. There will always be other opportunities. Just to exercise skepticism when people are telling you to communicate with them in a channel that looks very informal, and we see a lot of that going on now. So one thing is just to build that skepticism, but the second really is to meet people where they are, to meet people where they're meeting scammers. We want them to meet us, and we want to be able to direct them to our resources which can really arm them. And then of course I think we need to start financial education much younger in the United States. I want elementary school kids to be immersed in it so that by the time they're managing their own money they're very comfortable and they're not scared to find the help they need and they're not scared to ask the questions they need to ask. Yeah, amen to that. So that's a charge that we have to push forward and with as much effort as we can bring to bear. So anyway, so Commissioner Peirce, thank you very much. Our time is up but thank you very much for joining me today and look forward to doing some more discussion in the future. Thank you. Thank you.

Crypto Fed Chair, Polymarket Bots, and the Faketoshi Movie

The Breakdown(19.7K) ·28:33 ·2.3K views · 2026-04-16
Kevin Warsh (Fed Chair nominee)
Trump’s potential Federal Reserve Chair pick, Kevin Warsh, has disclosed a sprawling investment portfolio worth up to $209 million, including stakes in over 30 crypto projects, while Hollywood prepares a high-profile thriller that risks rehabilitating the "Faketoshi" narrative.
TL;DR Trump’s potential Federal Reserve Chair pick, Kevin Warsh, has disclosed a sprawling investment portfolio worth up to $209 million, including stakes in over 30 crypto projects, while Hollywood prepares a high-profile thriller that risks rehabilitating the "Faketoshi"...

TL;DR

Trump’s potential Federal Reserve Chair pick, Kevin Warsh, has disclosed a sprawling investment portfolio worth up to $209 million, including stakes in over 30 crypto projects, while Hollywood prepares a high-profile thriller that risks rehabilitating the "Faketoshi" narrative.

Kevin Warsh’s Pro-Crypto Financial Disclosures

President Trump’s pick to lead the Federal Reserve, Kevin Warsh, recently filed financial disclosures revealing deep ties to the digital asset industry.

  • Financial Profile: Warsh, a former Fed governor and Morgan Stanley banker, disclosed assets valued between $131 million and $209 million.
      • Consulting Fees: He earned over $10 million in consulting fees last year, much of it through his work with investment legend Stanley Druckenmiller.
  • Crypto Holdings: Approximately 10% of Warsh’s material holdings are linked to blockchain and Web3 projects.
      • High-Profile Stakes: Investments include the Bitcoin Lightning Network, Polymarket, Solana, Optimism (ETH L2), and SpaceX.
      • Institutional Exposure: He holds interests in Polychain Capital, the Brave browser, and decentralized finance protocols like Compound and DYDX.
  • "Degen" Investments: Canellis noted that some of Warsh’s holdings are surprisingly high-risk or controversial.
      • Deso (BitClout): An investment in a project whose founder was charged with fraud by the SEC.
      • Blast: A yield-generating Ethereum Layer 2 often described as "degen-forward."
      • Friends With Benefits: A social DAO that rose to prominence during the 2021 NFT boom.

The "Nothing Ever Happens" Polymarket Bot

The rise of algorithmic trading on prediction markets has birthed new strategies, specifically a bot developed by Sterling Crispin that bets against volatility and "hype" narratives.

  • The Strategy: The bot is built on the premise that "nothing ever happens." Crispin’s data suggests that 73.4% of all Polymarket outcomes resolve as "No."
  • Operational Logic:
      • Filters: The bot targets binary (Yes/No) markets that are not sports-related, not macroeconomic, and expire within three months.
      • Execution: It buys "No" votes only when the market implies a 35% or greater chance of an event occurring, assuming the crowd is overestimating the likelihood of change.
      • Risk Management: The bot uses only 2% of available cash per trade to maintain a "naive" but diversified exposure.
  • Market Efficiency: Canellis warns that if such bots become common, they will quickly price in the "nothing ever happens" edge, eliminating the profit potential for latecomers.

Hollywood’s High-Stakes Bitcoin Thriller

A new film titled *Bitcoin*, directed by Doug Liman (*The Bourne Identity*), is entering production with a star-studded cast including Gal Gadot and Casey Affleck.

  • Controversial Synopsis: The film follows a man’s quest to prove he created Bitcoin—a plot clearly mirroring the saga of Craig Wright.
      • "Faketoshi" Context: Despite the British High Court ruling that Wright is not Satoshi Nakamoto and committed fraud, the film’s synopsis paints the protagonist as a man whose life is "in peril" for his claims.
  • Industry Concerns:
      • Misinformation: Canellis expressed concern that a blockbuster film might "muddy the waters" and paint Wright as an anti-hero to a general audience.
      • Funding Rumors: Reports suggest the film may be partially funded by Calvin Ayre, a long-time associate and financier of Craig Wright.
      • Comparison: Unlike the 2019 "cringe watch" film *Crypto* starring Kurt Russell, this production has significant Hollywood backing and could shape the public’s foundational understanding of Bitcoin.

***

Notable Quotes

"It’s very curious that as sophisticated as Kevin Warsh is, he did pick up a lemon like Deso... a lot of people have pointed out how 'degen-forward' his crypto investments are, and it does seem to be the case." — David Canellis
"The 'nothing ever happens' crowd would say that none of [the hype] is really going to happen, so you might as well sell all the volatility." — David Canellis
"I just really wish [the movie *Bitcoin*] was not being made because it is only going to muddy the waters... I worry about what's going to happen when you have a blockbuster film essentially painting Craig Wright as an anti-hero." — David Canellis

And then we kind of show you like a shortened version of that interview on the Tuesday to complement the video essay. And then on the Thursday, we release the full interview so you can kind of go deeper on the conversation. But over time, we're just kind of tweaking the format, making sure that the content is super engaging and interesting for the audience to consume. So what we're going to be doing, at least for the next few weeks, is I'm going to try doing these solo episodes. If you missed that, please go back and check it out if you're interested to know who I am and why the Breakdown has a different host all of a sudden. Obviously, you're missing that in the content that you're consuming, at least from us. So this is going to be my first attempt at doing that, and we're also going to release this as a video episode, too. I'm going to be talking about what I'm seeing on my screen as well, so if you feel like watching the video version of this, please do follow along. But okay, so let's jump into it. This week, I've got three interesting things that I'm looking at, at least. The first is actually this story about Kevin Warsh, so let me share my screen here. This story came out a day or two ago, actually yesterday. This is from the Wall Street Journal, so Kevin Warsh, President Trump's choice to lead the Fed, has actually disclosed a sprawling set of financial interests worth well more than a hundred million dollars. This includes stakes in Elon Musk's SpaceX and, funny enough, prediction market firm Polymarket. And actually, it's not just Polymarket on the crypto side; it's actually like 30-plus crypto and blockchain Web3 investments that Kevin Warsh actually holds. If you're not familiar with Kevin Warsh, they do a little bit of a profile on him down here. He began his career at Morgan Stanley and then worked as an economic aide to President George W. Bush before serving as a Fed governor from 2006 to 2011. So he is a long-time banker, long-time financier. He was at the investment firm of Stanley Druckenmiller for a while and earned more than 10 million dollars in consulting fees last year. So he's a big-time banker, big-time TradFi guy, and he's apparently well deep in crypto and a bunch of other stuff. Actually, in here is a bunch of investments that he could not disclose, apparently due to non-disclosure agreements, and he said that he would probably divest some of those if he was indeed nominated to lead the Federal Reserve at the end of the day. But let's just—I thought it would be cool to just walk through some of these crypto and blockchain investments that Kevin Warsh has disclosed. These are just in alphabetical order, so I'm going to share—I've pulled the actual filings and kind of condensed them into a spreadsheet. So these are in alphabetical order and, I mean, there's some really good stuff in here. Like, he's an investor in the Lightning Network, which is interesting, the Bitcoin Lightning Network. There's also—he is an investor in Optimism, the Ethereum L2, and an investor in Solana. It doesn't say if he owns the liquid tokens for all of these crypto and blockchain stuff; there's actually no value associated with the disclosures, it's just that he does have a material investment in them. Let's see if in the WSJ report it says what the minimum disclosure amount is. The holdings are complex, WSJ reports, and the disclosure only specifies their values within large ranges, so determining Warsh's precise net worth isn't possible. All told, the disclosure signals that Warsh's assets might be valued between 131 million and more than 209 million. So, I mean, some of the disclosures are like under a thousand dollars, so it's not exactly clear how big his holdings are in each of these things. It's worth pointing that out, but there is also—what else is in here? There's Polymarket, Solana, and Tenderly, which is an Ethereum developer platform. So there is some really good stuff in here, but there's also some rather questionable projects. Deso is probably the most questionable in here; that was originally BitClout, which was founded by this fellow Nader Al-Naji. Maybe you're familiar with him; he was actually doing algorithmic stablecoins way back when and then came back and tried to get into decentralized social and bonding curves for creator coins and stuff like that. The SEC actually charged this fellow with fraud a couple of years ago for defrauding investors of around three million dollars. But that was last cycle, but it's just curious—it's interesting that as sophisticated as Kevin Warsh is, he did pick up a lemon like Deso. There's also Friends With Benefits; I mean, Friends With Benefits was a big deal back in the day. Nowhere near as fraudulent as Deso, obviously, but that's a curious one as well. What else is here? I mean Polychain, the VC investment firm, that's an interesting one. dYdX, Compound, Brave, the Brave browser—that's very crypto-native, that's another good one for him to hold. But it's just curious that there is such a range here of projects with different qualities, so that was really cool to see from Kevin Warsh. Blast, which is the yield-generating Ethereum layer 2, there's a lot of incentives to pick up Blast when that first came out, but it is quite degen. A lot of people have pointed out how degen-forward Kevin Warsh's crypto investments are, and it does seem to be the case. If you scroll down here, I'm showing off the rest of his disclosures; all up, how many is there? There's hundreds. So, you know, the crypto part of his disclosures at least make up about 10% of his material holdings, which is fairly decent. I would say it would be nice to see if he was holding liquid tokens or equity investments in these firms. I know Friends With Benefits was wound down or it was acquired by a private equity firm, I believe that's correct—please don't quote me on that—but yeah, it would be curious to know if these are NFTs or if these are liquid coins that he's holding. Dapper Labs was also a big NFT play in 2021 as well, so he's been doing this for years, obviously. So big up Kevin Warsh, and let's see if we can get a degenerate leading the Federal Reserve. Get started today at nexo.com/breakdown. As always, investments in blockchain technology involve risk; terms and conditions apply. Do your own research. Okay, so what is the next thing that I'm looking at? The next thing I'm looking at is actually this—I mean, Polymarket trading bots are so big right now. And I think that, you know, algo traders and all that kind of thing have been a cottage industry for the longest time, but it's very interesting to see that now Polymarket bots have become kind of the next evolution of the algo trading—I don't want to call it an industry, but the algo trader corner of the internet. And for those who aren't really familiar, I mean, algo traders are like—you can come up with a—I mean, LLMs have kind of changed this a little bit, but you can design a bot that will go out and trade for you and read signals and try and make money for you based on particular strategies. And you know, anyone can make an algorithm and try and sell it; they normally come with courses on Forex and stuff like that. And I'm sure if you're scrolling through Twitter, you would see a lot of Polymarket bots these days from people who have cooked up all kinds of different strategies. It's very difficult to know which ones are scams and which ones are just trying to fleece you, or they might be malware or phishing, that kind of stuff as well. But this one from Sterling Crispin I thought was really cool: a Polymarket bot that automatically buys 'No' for every non-sports market and holds to resolution. It's essentially boiling down the theory that nothing ever happens into a potentially profitable Polymarket bot. And you know, background on the 'nothing ever happens' theory—I mean, I think we're all watching and doomscrolling and seeing the narratives play out in real time that say the whole world is going to change. And you know, Trump is going to drop nukes on Iran, or AI is going to make everyone jobless, or there's going to be no jobs in five years, it's only going to be LLM engineers or something like that. But the 'nothing ever happens' crowd would say that, well, none of that is really going to happen, so you might as well sell all the volatility. So this is essentially doing exactly that. Sterling Crispin, he's crunched the numbers and he found that 73.4% of all Polymarket markets resolve as 'No'. So why would you bother trying to predict the future or make predictions when you could just buy the 'No' votes and make a profit? So how this works—I mean, you can flick through the GitHub; you could plug GitHub into ChatGPT or Claude to try and figure out exactly how it works. But basically, what it does is that it pulls all the markets from Polymarket and keeps only markets that resolve binary, so either 'Yes' or 'No'. They can't be sports-related because obviously that would skew the probability a little bit, because crazy stuff can happen in sports. They need to be standalone, so they can't be a Polymarket that is relative to another Polymarket that would resolve; they have to be just a standalone market. And they have to expire within the next three months or so. But also, I mean, that excludes stuff like crypto markets, macroeconomic stuff, finance keywords; it gets rid of those too. So it's very black-and-white markets that aren't subjective and aren't related to what the market would say. It excludes stuff where the market has a vote in what the outcome is going to be, if that makes sense. So no highly driven, highly narrative-driven or efficient markets is basically the idea. So what it does is it essentially only buys when the market is implying that there is a 35% chance or more that the event does happen. If nothing ever happens and the Polymarket is showing that there is a 35% chance or more probability that that is going to happen, then it assumes that probability is too high because nothing ever happens, and then it buys the 'No' vote and holds it until it resolves. Which I find rather compelling. It uses—there are some guardrails here for the bot. It uses only around 2% of the available cash per trade. So the whole idea is to have very small bets over on any Polymarket that fits these conditions. And I suppose it would also make sense that the bot has to buy the 'No' votes as soon as possible, because if there were a lot of bots operating on Polymarket that were doing the same thing, then effectively it would be pricing in the fact that nothing ever happens already. You would essentially get no real edge if there are a lot of bots like this operating on Polymarket. So the bot would need to get in on the ground floor. There is also some logic about handling liquidity in the markets and making sure that it is getting the right price for things and stuff like that. But what I find really cool about this is that it's—I mean, I think that we're all a little bit contrarian deep down. I think if you're into crypto and Bitcoin, you instinctively want to go the other way than what the whole world is telling you is the safe bet, is the nice thing. And I think that at the moment, there is so much—I don't want to say doomism—but there's just so much hype and velocity around the power of AI and what the world will look like in three to five years. And we're all getting older, we're all getting more advanced in our careers, we're all having children, we're all starting families and building up our own businesses. So there is a lot of pressure to stay ahead of what the normies are doing, but the contrarian in me would like to think that really nothing ever happens. And something I find curious is that this is somewhat of a—it's like the opposite of the Black Swan theory, almost. It's like an anti-Taleb trade, this 'nothing ever happens' stuff, because you know, black swans are real, that is true, but they are rare. So my instinct here is that this Polymarket bot, the 'nothing ever happens' bot, would actually do quite well as long as they were making quite small bets on things. And what's curious in here in the comments—Ryder Ripps, who was embroiled in a legal dispute with Bored Apes and Yuga Labs from memory, he asks, 'Have you made any money with it?' And Sterling Crispin says, 'No, I have a bunch of other bots going through that are profitable; this one is kind of just for the lulz, as they say.' 'It has basically zero risk management; it's a very naive bot.' Which, you know, I suppose that you can tweak that to make it better, but I would put this in the camp of like, hey, that's quite a novel trading bot that probably warrants a little bit more investigation. But I found this particularly interesting. And yeah, as I said, if you're ever fooling around with Polymarket bots that you download on GitHub or find on Twitter, please just do your own research and vet those things very thoroughly. But hey, I live in a jurisdiction that I cannot appropriately access Polymarket, based in the Netherlands. But if you are fooling around with Polymarket bots, I would love to hear from you because I'm very interested in agentic trading and all that kind of thing. But it is very much a wild west out there in terms of the trustability of some of these things, especially when it's handling your money, so just be careful. Okay, so the next one is quite brutal. I'm sure that you have been following along with the fallout of the New York Times story that supposedly definitively proved that Adam Back, Blockstream CEO and cypherpunk legend and Bitcoin celebrity, Adam Back is actually Satoshi Nakamoto. Which is like a very old theory that keeps getting trotted out every now and then. So this is already in the zeitgeist of like, 'Hey, who is Satoshi?' and 'Maybe it is Adam Back.' And at the same time, we have Hollywood dredging up the Craig Wright saga all over again in order to make a film. And it's just—it's just a little bit insane. So this is from Deadline: 'Gal Gadot and Isla Fisher join Casey Affleck and Pete Davidson in Doug Liman thriller Bitcoin as pic heads to Cannes market.' I mean, oof. So Doug Liman—I wasn't super familiar with who Doug Liman was because I just didn't recognize his name, but he's a film director and producer. Let's look at his filmography. He directed the first Bourne Identity, he directed Mr. & Mrs. Smith, Jumper, Edge of Tomorrow—also the executive producer on that—American Made, the Road House remake; he directed that. So 'Bitcoin' is his next film. And I mean, it was originally meant to be titled 'Killing Satoshi,' but now it's officially titled 'Bitcoin'. This is how Deadline described the synopsis—oh, it's just quoting the synopsis, sorry: 'Bitcoin is the true story of one man's quest to prove that he is in fact the creator of Bitcoin. A claim that puts his life in peril and sets off a global firestorm and a wild high-stakes race between the who's who of tech billionaires and world leaders with the fate of the world's entire financial system hanging in the balance, all leading to the question: Why do the world's most powerful people want to erase one man and why would they spend hundreds of millions of dollars to do it?' If you're ready to approach digital assets with a more structured wealth strategy, visit nexo.com/breakdown to get started. As always, investments in blockchain technology involve risk; terms and conditions apply. Do your own research. And I mean, Jesus. Like, I mean, to Deadline's credit, they do kind of give us this blurb about the whole Craig Wright saga, you know, but it's like—even the language here, I find a little bit wishy-washy. 'The claims have been regarded as false by many.' 'False by many in the cryptocurrency community and in 2024 the British High Court ruled that Wright is not Nakamoto and must stop saying he is so.' I'm very curious how much money Craig Wright was able to sell his naming rights for this project. It is a very curious project in general. I mean, yeah, like, I just can't believe that we're still doing this. I really thought that the whole Craig Wright saga was over when the British High Court ruled that he needs to stop claiming and that his claims were fraudulent. Something I found interesting in the comments that is worth looking into: 'It was confirmed in prior leaks the movie was partially funded by Calvin Ayre. The writer is also Calvin Ayre affiliated.' And I haven't—take this with a grain of salt because it is just in the comment section—but you know, 'Made by those with sunk cost fallacy who fell for Craig Wright's giant con job and now can't let it go.' I mean, I haven't fact-checked that, but I'm inclined to at least sympathize with that take because, yeah, I mean BSV was just a nightmare and, you know, a lot of people were completely captured by Craig Wright's story. But I will say that I am—this would have to be the most high-profile—this would have to be the crypto movie that has the most high-profile actors in at least my memory. Because there was also Crypto, the Crypto movie starring Kurt Russell actually in 2019. And it's—if you missed that film, it's worth going back and having a look because I just found that movie in particular quite fascinating because the main character was an anti-money laundering analyst at a bank who got caught up in a crypto conspiracy involving Kurt Russell's farm and all this stuff. It was a great cringe watch, but this film at least looks to be a little bit more high-quality in terms of the actual plot. But just based on what we know about the reality of what happened, I just really wish that it was not being made because it is only going to muddy the waters, I feel. And I mean, if the New York Times can put out with confidence a report—you know, and to their credit, the journalist who wrote the New York Times piece is widely considered one of the best investigative journalists in the world. If the New York Times can put that out and people can be sold on the idea that Adam Back is really Satoshi, even though there are all these signs pointing to the fact that actually Adam Back is not Satoshi and they were quite different people, I just worry about what's going to happen out in the real world when you have a blockbuster Hollywood film essentially—I mean, from what I interpret of it—it is painting Craig Wright as something of an anti-hero. And this was always the defense as well. I remember back in the day, five or six years ago when I was really kind of like a beat journalist writing about crypto all the time, you would see major tech websites—I think it was ZDNet—would put out quite BSV-aligned narratives about developments on that network and the things that Craig Wright and nChain were doing. I was frustrated by the fact that quite surface-level analysis was being done on what BSV was doing and the claims that Craig Wright was making. I would even DM the reporters that would do that and ask them, 'Why is it that you entertain this project in the way and you don't show skepticism about what they're doing?' And they would tell me, it's like, 'Well, Craig Wright has been super stubborn about his claims despite all of the public pushback and despite all the lawsuits and everything, and he still claims to be Satoshi.' So that seems to me to be compelling, and I worry that this film is essentially the same thing as that. And it's quite a roundabout way for Craig Wright to still be pushing his narrative into the public. So I mean, I will watch Bitcoin when it comes out; I just wish that it wasn't being made, and I imagine that you all feel something similar. So that's about it for this solo episode. I hope you enjoyed it; it was a little bit rough in parts, maybe, but we'll try it again next week and see if we can get it happening smoother. So for now, take care of yourselves—to borrow a phrase from NLW—and we'll see you next time. Bye. Blockworks. The Breakdown.

FULL HEARING: U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer Grilled On Trump's Tariffs, Trade Policies

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M) ·81:47 ·1.4K views · 2026-04-16
Jamieson Greer (U.S. Trade Representative)
United States Trade Representative Jamieson Greer defended a $95 million FY2027 budget request, arguing that the administration’s "reciprocal" tariff policy is successfully reducing trade deficits and fueling a manufacturing renaissance, while critic...
TL;DR United States Trade Representative Jamieson Greer defended a $95 million FY2027 budget request, arguing that the administration’s "reciprocal" tariff policy is successfully reducing trade deficits and fueling a manufacturing renaissance, while critics characterized the...

TL;DR

United States Trade Representative Jamieson Greer defended a $95 million FY2027 budget request, arguing that the administration’s "reciprocal" tariff policy is successfully reducing trade deficits and fueling a manufacturing renaissance, while critics characterized the policies as an illegal and "chaotic" burden that has cost the average American household $1,700 annually.

FY2027 Budget Request and Operational Priorities

  • Funding Request: USTR is seeking $95 million for fiscal year 2027, an 8% increase over FY2026.
      • Personnel Investment: The increase is primarily intended to hire 20–25 additional negotiators, economists, and attorneys to enforce new trade agreements and conduct Section 301 investigations.
      • Infrastructure: A portion of the funding ($1 million) is allocated to consolidate USTR’s office footprint to reduce long-term leasing costs.
  • Workload Expansion: Ambassador Greer noted that USTR concluded a record nine trade agreements in the past year, requiring more staff for monitoring and enforcement to ensure foreign partners meet commitments on labor, environment, and intellectual property.

Defense of Trump’s Trade Agenda and Economic Outcomes

  • Deficit and Export Performance: Ambassador Greer reported a 24% decrease in the U.S. goods trade deficit between April 2025 and February 2026.
      • China Trade: The deficit with China specifically fell by over 30% ($130 billion) in one year.
      • Record Exports: U.S. exports reached historic highs of over $300 billion monthly in early 2026.
  • Manufacturing and Reshoring: The administration credits tariffs for driving significant domestic investment.
      • Automotive: Stellantis announced a $13 billion investment to return production lines from Canada and Mexico to the U.S.
      • Appliances: Whirlpool invested $60 million in its Ohio facility, adding 150 jobs.
  • Reciprocal Trade Agreements: USTR has secured new deals with partners including Indonesia, the UK, Japan, and Ecuador, focusing on lowering foreign tariffs and eliminating non-tariff barriers (e.g., meat marketing terms and FDA approval recognition).

Congressional Criticism: Costs, Chaos, and Job Losses

  • Economic Burden on Families: Ranking Member Meng and Representative DeLauro challenged the administration’s "pro-worker" narrative, citing data that tariffs have acted as an effective tax increase of $1,700 on the average American household (and up to $4,200 in New York).
  • Manufacturing Declines: Critics pointed to Bureau of Labor Statistics data showing the U.S. lost over 80,000 manufacturing jobs in 2025, with the goods trade deficit in manufactured items rising by $62 billion compared to 2024.
  • Policy Volatility: Ranking Member Meng criticized the administration’s "pay-to-play" system and lack of transparency, noting that tariff rates often changed via social media without formal application processes for small businesses.
  • Legal Fallout: Following a Supreme Court ruling that IEEPA tariffs were illegal, the government owes approximately $166 billion in refunds plus interest.
      • Refund Controversy: Members criticized the "CAPE" portal application process, arguing that refunds should be automatic and paid with interest to all affected businesses.

Geopolitical Strategy: China and USMCA

  • China and Fentanyl: USTR is using tariff leverage to pressure China to control fentanyl precursor chemicals. Ambassador Greer confirmed that fentanyl deaths have declined over the past year and the issue remains a top priority for upcoming presidential meetings.
  • USMCA Renegotiation: A mandatory six-year review is approaching. USTR priorities include:
      • Rules of Origin: Strengthening requirements to prevent "transshipment" of Chinese goods through Mexico.
      • Steel Enforcement: Advocating for "melt and pour" standards to ensure steel is actually produced within North America.
  • Critical Minerals: USTR is developing a "plurilateral agreement" with allies to create a protected market for critical minerals, establishing price floors to prevent China from "dumping" and breaking the market for domestic miners.

Notable Quotes

"President Trump’s trade policy is working for the American people... We have never exported so much from the United States as we are now." — Ambassador Jamieson Greer, U.S. Trade Representative
"What we have seen from this administration has been an unlawful tariff and trade policy that is chaotic, not transparent, ripe for corruption... and deeply harmful to the American people." — Rep. Grace Meng, Ranking Member
"A nation that offshores its industrial capacity really offshores its deterrent capacity... If we can’t pour our own steel or make our own chips, we are at the mercy of those who do." — Rep. Barry Moore
"The administration stole from American businesses and consumers in an illegal way with these tariffs. The money must be returned... in full, with interest." — Rep. Madeleine Dean
I would like to welcome everyone to this morning's hearing and the committee's first budget hearing of the fiscal year 2027 cycle. Thank you to our subcommittee members and to Ambassador Greer for being here today. USTR's fiscal 27 budget request totals 95 million, an increase of 8% from fiscal 26. The budget request includes funding for increased personnel investments to enable USTR to protect national security, address unfair trade practices, and open foreign markets for US goods. Your role as the President's principal trade advisor and negotiator on trade issues is vital to the success of President Trump's trade agenda. The fiscal year 27 budget request for the office of the United States Trade Representative comes at an important time. The administration has announced trade deals with many of our key trading partners and is actively working on renegotiating the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement with our neighbors. Just last month, USTR also launched various Section 301 tariff investigations aimed at addressing unfair trade practices relating to forced labor and excess manufacturing capacity by over 60 nations. USTR has taken on increased focus and responsibility, and Congress has worked to meet your needs by providing targeted funding increases. USTR must ensure that rival nations like the People's Republic of China are not able to take advantage of Americans by engaging in unfair trade practices. We must also not allow China to export deadly fentanyl precursor chemicals, flooding North America and poisoning our children. I look forward to hearing how the President's budget request for USTR furthers our shared goals of responsibly managing taxpayer dollars while expanding market access for domestic producers, protecting national security, and combating unfair trade practices, particularly by geopolitical foes like China. Let me now recognize the ranking member of the subcommittee, Ms. Meng. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and I join you in welcoming Ambassador Greer for his first appearance before our subcommittee. This opportunity to discuss this administration's trade policies, which have created chaos, uncertainty, and high costs for American families and businesses is quite timely. I also look forward to hearing from you about your fiscal year 27 budget request. I support trade policies that protect American workers, consumers, and businesses from unfair trade practices, promote access to global markets, strengthen trust and collaboration with our allies, and bolster our national security. When smartly targeted and applied, tariffs can be a useful tool in our tool belt to address unfair trade practices, and Congress has provided the President with limited tariff authorities. But what we have seen from this administration has been an unlawful tariff and trade policy that is chaotic, not transparent, ripe for corruption, damaging to our closest allies, and deeply harmful to the American people and businesses it claims to protect. Tariff rates changed countless times in a single year. Policies were announced on social media and reversed just days later. American businesses were unable to make basic decisions about what to buy because the rules could change overnight. In February, the Supreme Court ruled that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act does not authorize the President to impose tariffs. That left the government owing over 166 billion, with interest growing by the millions per day to the American businesses that paid those illegal duties. Unfortunately, the damage of the illegal tariff regime has already been felt by American families who have paid more for clothes, groceries, and other items. Businesses have faced higher material costs and retaliatory tariffs on their own exports. The administration did not come to Congress and seek lawful tariff authority. Instead, it reached for the next untested statute. Within hours of the Supreme Court ruling, the President signed a proclamation invoking Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 to impose a 10% global tariff. The next day, he announced on social media that the rate would increase to 15%. No formal policy has been issued. These tariffs expire this summer and are already facing legal challenges. At the same time, the administration has indicated it will pursue dozens of new investigations under Section 301 and 232 to impose yet another set of tariffs. The result for Americans has been chaos, uncertainty, and a higher cost of living. According to one independent analysis, 90% of the economic burden of tariffs fell on US companies and consumers in 2025. This has resulted in an effective tax increase of nearly $1,700 on the average American household. This administration told us that the tariffs would boost American industry, but they have not. In 2025, the trade deficit in goods hit a record high and over 80,000 manufacturing jobs were lost. After getting a boost from historic investments under the Biden administration, spending on US factory construction has fallen every month since the beginning of President Trump's second term in office. In New York, those costs have hit us even harder. According to an analysis by the Manhattan Chamber of Commerce, New York households face an estimated $4,200 in additional annual costs due to tariffs. In the middle of a housing crisis, they have added around 11,000 to the cost of building each new single-family home. In the second quarter of 2025, as tariffs ramped up, the city saw a net loss of approximately 4,900 businesses, the weakest quarter for business formation in five years. The state comptroller reported just weeks ago that exports to Canada, our state's most significant trading partner, declined by $3.8 billion. As the chair of the Congressional Asian Pacific American Caucus, I have been sounding the alarm on this issue since the beginning. There are more than 3 million Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islander owned small businesses across this country, and many of them rely on imports of goods that cannot be produced domestically, yet are still subject to tariffs. I have seen the impacts of these tariffs firsthand in my own district in Queens. Just last month, I led my colleagues in local and state government on a tour of Flushing, Queens, where we heard that costs for small businesses are through the roof. What makes all of this worse is the two-tiered pay-to-play system this administration has created. The wealthy and well-connected are cutting deals for themselves, while Main Street businesses like those in Flushing are left out and pay the price. Tariff exemptions and favorable rate adjustments are granted behind closed doors with no formal criteria, no application process, and no way for the public to weigh in. Meanwhile, small businesses across New York City are absorbing approximately $4.5 billion annually in added tariff costs. They don't have lobbyists in Washington to help them make their case for relief, and they shouldn't be treated as collateral damage while the President uses tariffs as leverage to reward friends and extract favors. The prices of groceries, gas, clothing, and homes are skyrocketing for American families. Small businesses have been forced to raise prices or close their doors. Construction costs are rising at a time when we desperately need much more housing construction. The chaos of an unpredictable murky tariff regime continues. I look forward to your testimony and to what I hope is a candid discussion. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you, Ms. Meng. I would like now to recognize our witness, Ambassador Greer, for an opening statement. Without objection, your written statement will be entered into the record. I would ask that you keep your statement hopefully to five minutes so we can have additional time for questions. Gentleman's recognized. Thank you Chairman Rogers and Ranking Member Meng for having me today. I'm glad we can gather for this hearing today. I'm looking forward to our conversation. And before discussing the activities of the Office of the United States Trade Representative in detail and the resources my office needs to continue our good work in 2027, I want to make one overarching point, and that is that President Trump's trade policy is working for the American people. The US goods trade deficit has decreased by 24% between April 2025 when tariffs first went into place through February 2026 compared to the same period a year earlier. So a 24% decrease. It's gone down by over 30% with China in particular, so we're diversifying away from that, which is a positive. US exports reached 302 billion in January 2026 and nearly 315 billion in February 2026, the highest monthly export figures in US history. So when I hear people say that somehow our exports are affected, it's for the positive. We've never exported so much from the United States as we are now. Food and agricultural exports rose by double digits in 2025. Manufacturing labor productivity registered its highest annual increase in 15 years, and manufacturing workers' wages are up by an average of $1,800. We produced more steel than Japan last year for the first time in a long time, and orders for capital equipment that go into factories are surging, all because President Trump decided to take trade seriously. And as you know and has been discussed, last April President Trump imposed reciprocal tariffs in response to the national emergency caused by our large and persistent trade deficit of 1.2 trillion, which had increased by 40% over the previous four years. The result is that we had lost and continued to lose the manufacturing capacity that supports our workers, fuels US innovation, and safeguards our economic and national security. And these tariffs have been instrumental in creating a level playing field for American workers and creating incentives to build, grow, and produce more in the United States at a higher wage for our workers. Last week, during a swing through the heart of American manufacturing in Michigan and Ohio, I was encouraged to see the results of a $13 billion investment by the owners of the Dodge and Jeep brands and the return of auto production lines to America from overseas. A $60 million expansion by Whirlpool, creating an additional 150 jobs in Clyde, Ohio. And highly automated facilities producing drones, tooling and dies, and solar panels. I can't account for New York State policies and taxes that drive away business because all of these other developments are being spurred by President Trump's tariffs. And alongside the tariff program, the President has also secured deals with numerous trading partners to address the unfair practices that contributed to our trade deficit. And these new trade deals cover over half the world's population. We have nine agreements on reciprocal trade with countries like Malaysia, Cambodia, El Salvador, Guatemala, Argentina, Bangladesh, Taiwan, Indonesia, and Ecuador. And they represent real meaningful new market access for American exports, the kind that American farmers and manufacturers were promised that the World Trade Organization would achieve but did not. We've also inked deals with the United Kingdom, the European Union, Japan, and South Korea, and we're looking forward to finalizing even more deals in the weeks and months ahead. And these agreements have achieved long-standing US trade priorities, many that have festered for decades, including the lowering of foreign tariffs on most US exports and elimination of unfair non-tariff barriers. For example, eight countries have exempted American meat and cheese products from geographical indication rules that prevented the use of common marketing terms. 11 countries have agreed to accept US automotive emission and safety standards. Nine countries have agreed to accept US FDA approvals for new treatment and medicines. We've also received strong commitments from our trading partners to enhance intellectual property protection, provide a level playing field for US companies with respect to their domestic labor and environmental production standards, and improve coordination on economic security. The President's trade program continues despite the Supreme Court's decision in February. Within hours, the President reimposed tariffs using Section 122. And a few weeks ago, we signed another agreement on reciprocal trade with Ecuador. So the program continues. And as we continue to address our trading partner's unfair practices, we are using our investigatory powers granted to us by Congress to investigate forced labor in other countries and structural excess capacity that lead to global imbalances. In President Trump's first term, this tool, Section 301, was used to great effect to establish a stable and durable approach to trade that has received bipartisan support. As you know, the Section 301 tariffs on China for forced technology are still in effect today. Not only did President Biden keep those tariffs in place but even expanded them, showing bipartisan support for this tool. So with all of this new work, USTR needs to have the resources to meet the need. The USTR team is dedicated to helping American blue-collar workers, American farmers, and families in the communities they support. But we can only meet those obligations we have to Americans if we have the resources that we need. Whether it's negotiating new agreements or enforcing agreements, we need to be able to have the personnel to do that. Last year, this committee heard our call for more resources and generously increased USTR's appropriation. And we're spending that money now to hire new negotiators, economists, attorneys, and other subject matter experts. And these help strengthen the agency for the benefit of the American people. And so this year, President Trump has requested that USTR's budget be increased to 95 million, a 7 million dollar total increase over FY 2026. This will enable us to do the hiring we need to secure a level playing field, secure new market access for American farmers and workers, re-industrialize our country, and fully achieve all the wins we've gained through our agreements on reciprocal trade. I think our shared interest in doing what's best for America should allow us to continue to meaningfully collaborate as we work together, and I approach this hearing in that spirit. Thank you for your consideration of the President's budget request, and I look forward to your questions. Thank you, Ambassador Greer. We will now proceed under the five-minute rule for questions. And I'll begin by recognizing myself. President Trump has made trade one of his core policies during his second term in office, and you and your office are very busy carrying out this strategically important agenda. As a result, Congress provided a funding increase of $14 million in fiscal 26 to match this increase in workload. Please share with us today why USTR requested an additional $7 million in fiscal 27. Thank you, Chairman. I want to express again gratitude for the committee for extending our appropriation last year. We are finally now, after having many employees who left the agency during the Biden administration, we're finally getting up to full strength. But over the last year we concluded nine additional trade agreements, that's the most we've ever done in a single year at USTR. And what's important to us going forward is to be able not only to conclude more agreements but to be able to enforce these agreements. And these agreements are very important because they open up markets overseas. We have countries that have lowered their tariffs for us. We've been asking for decades, they finally have done it. But on top of that we have countries who have made commitments on forced labor, environmental regulation, intellectual property, non-tariff barriers. And if we don't have the personnel to monitor and enforce these agreements, we will not fully realize all of the benefits. We're at the beginning of a real resurgence in exports and manufacturing and we want to make sure we have the personnel to deliver for the American people. Ambassador, as you know, the growing economic and technological threat posed by the Chinese Communist Party is a bipartisan concern. Can you explain how the USTR budget request strengthens the nation's ability to counter China's unfair trade practices? Certainly. So with respect to China, we expect that with these additional funds, a portion of that will go to ensuring we have appropriate staff in our policy team who know these issues with respect to China. Their economy is different than ours. They use extreme subsidies. They control their private sector in a way we don't. They use measures, they constantly issue new measures and we need to be able to have the resources to understand those measures and respond to them. We would also use this funding for enforcement resources.
