So as we're working with the Chinese, we're trying to have a constructive relationship with them and we want to be able to trade in non-sensitive areas with them in a balanced way, but we also need to make sure that we have enforcement available where if the Chinese violate any deals we make or engage in other unfair trade practices, we have the negotiators, the subject matter experts, the economists, and the attorneys we need to conduct investigations and make sure that we're defending American interests. How do you expect to advance the administration's efforts to counter China during President Trump's meeting with the Chinese President next month? So about one month ago, I with the Secretary of the Treasury met with our counterparts to discuss outcomes for the meetings with President Xi and President Trump next month. We expect that that meeting will go smoothly. We expect that we will prolong stability in our trade and economic relationship with the Chinese. On the one hand, we want to make sure we can continue to receive the rare earth minerals that most of the world is dependent upon China for. And at the same time we also want to be able to ensure that the Chinese are buying things from us that Americans are good at making. This means agricultural products, it means airplanes, it means medical devices. There are certain things we're fine to import from China as well. And so we want to have an outcome geared toward having balanced trade with China and a managed trade relationship with them to avoid surprises, avoid escalation, and ensure that the trading relationship is for the benefit of Americans. Ambassador Greer, China has long supplied the precursor chemicals that are used to produce fentanyl that ends up in the United States and killing tens of thousands of our young people. What is USTR doing to ensure that China stops supplying these chemicals that are killing Americans? Well last year the President imposed tariffs on China to stop these chemicals and of course we had a bipartisan view or others tried to remove this and eliminate this leverage against the Chinese. All that being said, whenever we speak with the Chinese, we bring staff with us from the National Security Council and other agencies to engage with the Chinese and ask them to control not only fentanyl but the precursor drugs. And if we have information available on specific companies or shipments, we'll share that with the Chinese. And so there is incremental progress. Fortunately we've had fentanyl deaths decline over the past year which is good but there is a lot more to be done and certainly the Chinese have a lot more they can do on that front. Late last year the US closed the so-called "de minimis" loophole that allowed shipments of low dollar value to enter the US with minimal inspection. How has closing the de minimis loophole helped combat the importation of these precursor chemicals? So we have found in the past that other countries, China, Mexico, and others have used the loophole under de minimis, which typically has very little scrutiny at the border, to send shipments of fentanyl and precursor drugs and also counterfeit goods and other dangerous goods that aren't certified or regulated. Closing down the de minimis loophole has been very important for that. We're continuing to analyze that and monitor that but we think it's been a very positive development. How is USTR and the Trump administration ensuring that China is following through and that these efforts are working to stop the flow of fentanyl into our country? Again Chairman, whenever we meet with the Chinese, which we do so regularly both at my level and at staff and deputy levels, we raise this issue and we assess their progress on controlling items. And they have in fact controlled additional fentanyl precursors and we've seen action on that front. There is much more to be done and we're happy to have and use leverage with authorities. If Congress wants to give us additional authorities on tariffs to combat fentanyl, we're happy to take that and use it. It's been opposed by others in Congress but we will continue to raise it with the Chinese to fight this battle. Will that be on the agenda when the President goes to China? It will be, yes sir. Thank you. Ms. Meng. Thank you Mr. Chairman. Mr. Ambassador, this administration initially said that there would be no exceptions to these tariffs, but has since issued a long list of exceptions across multiple executive orders and annexes with no formal public process. Even without a formal exclusion process through which companies can request tariff exclusions for certain products, it looks like the administration has effectively granted tariff exclusions for certain products, companies, and categories. I was wondering if there was ever a formal notice or public comment period for any of these exclusion decisions. So Ranking Member, thank you. So at the beginning of this term when the President sought to impose tariffs, he made a decision that he did not want to have broad exclusion and exemption programs. He felt it created, it undermined the program to some degree. And so to the extent that the President has adjusted product coverage from time to time, it's been in response to analyses done by economists within the administration. You know one example is last November after concluding negotiations with many countries, the President decided that he should remove tariffs from certain goods that either cannot be naturally grown here, things like bananas or coffee, or things that can't naturally be extracted here. He also made a decision that, you know, certain items that might be covered by a later Section 232 investigation, which is a different tariff authority controlled by the Commerce Department, that those should be separate issues. And so they shouldn't necessarily be part of the reciprocal tariff program. So to the extent that the coverage was adjusted, it was for kind of broad-based principles like that, things we literally can't grow or extract from the ground or things that are going to be covered by other tariff programs. Thank you for that. Do you think that there should have been a more public or formal process for stakeholders, people in the industry, businesses and industries that would directly be impacted? So we have had, you know, hundreds if not thousands of meetings with stakeholders who have an interest. And certainly I take your point. You know a big company with lobbyists, you know, it's they have more resources to come in and talk about it. We also talk to trade associations who represent a broader group. And we do talk to small business, and often we talk to congressional offices where small businesses feel like they have a larger voice. With the Section 301 investigations we're doing, these do in fact have by law a public process. And so we just closed yesterday our comment process that was very public, federal register notice went out, everyone had weeks, and so now everyone in my office is going to start going through these. There will be further federal register notices out there where folks can comment. So I agree, I think it's a great policy to have. I appreciate your comments about the 301 process. I was asking more specifically about the IEEPA and now Section 122. And if you have information or records on data and some of the trends of the conversations that the administration has had with stakeholders, it would be really helpful. I think a lot of us, and I can speak for both sides of the aisle, I think are concerned about a lot of our small businesses, small manufacturers that are seeing their cost spike and deciding, you know, do they eat the cost or do they put it onto the customers, our consumers, our constituents, and American families because of these global tariffs under the IEEPA part and now Section 122. But I just want to make the case to the government that a product, if someone wants to make a case to the government that a product that is critical to their business should be excluded, what process would a small business or manufacturer in our district go through? So right now with respect to Section 122, that legal authority has a fairly narrow authority with respect to exceptions. It gives the President a lot of discretion within that, which is fortunate, but the idea is for a global tariff, we've imposed a global tariff there. With Section 301, which my office runs, and Section 232, which the Commerce Department runs, both of those processes have Federal Register notice opportunities. And I've been encouraging people who have a view on product coverage for tariffs to comment, to put in their comments. There's both a public comment process if they're concerned about confidentiality, there's a way to bracket and provide confidential information as well. So we're trying to make it as simple as possible for anyone in the country who wants to comment. They can do so, they can do it confidentially, they can do it publicly, they can do it, you know, through a lobbyist or just themselves and send a letter. And again, you know, I'm just looking out for our small businesses and our manufacturers who for almost a whole year were paying these IEEPA tariffs with no way to seek relief. They don't have fancy lobbyists in Washington advocating for their concerns and the high costs that either them or our constituents and American families were impacted by. And look I just, you know, has the administration considered simply sending Congress a proposal requesting tariff authority that it wants through a normal legislative process? So so far Ranking Member, the President has used tariff authorities provided by Congress. So those are in place. I know that there have been many bills proposed by different members of the House, including that would actually impose a global tariff and higher tariffs on countries that pose a bigger problem. We would be supportive of many of those. So there are proposals out there. We're happy to talk to anybody and everybody about them. I know Congresswoman Van Duyne has something, Representative Golden from the Democratic side had something. These are things where I think we would be supportive and we're happy to discuss them. Okay. I'll yield back. Mr. Strong. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Chairman Rogers, Ranking Member Meng, for holding this important hearing. Ambassador Greer, it's great to be with you again and I thank you for being here today and for your work in advancing a trade agenda that puts American workers and business first. America is back. My district in Alabama's fifth district is home to over 400 companies working on advanced technologies tied to national defense, space, and homeland security, along with a large agriculture presence. Ambassador Greer, can you speak to what USTR is doing to strengthen export control enforcement? Certainly. And I'm always glad to hear the list of industries you have in your district because it matches up really well with a lot of the foreign market access that we're getting in our trade agreements and so that's why we've seen record numbers of exports here in the United States. With respect to export control enforcement, it's operationally that's part of the Commerce Department. I know they're very diligently working at that. In our agreements that we are carrying out, we are asking our trading partners for the first time in any trade agreement to align with us on export controls and export control enforcement. Our view is that if you're going to trade favorably with the United States, you should also take our economic and national security concerns into account. And so we are having a lot of commitments from our trading partners that they will respect our export controls. Thank you. What additional personnel, resources, or authorities would help ensure that the rules we put in place are being enforced effectively on the ground? Well, in terms of resources, the President's budget proposal for USTR gets at that to some degree. We've asked for an additional 7 million with the vast bulk of that just going to salaries for new civil servants to supplement the folks I have now who are working really hard to enforce these agreements. So that would be very helpful. In terms of other authorities, some of which I just mentioned to the Ranking Member, there are members of Congress who have suggested implementing a 10% border adjustment tax or something like that, or having higher taxes based on a country's trade surplus with us. I welcome those kinds of things. I think that can help us get the deficit down, re-shore manufacturing, and allow enforcement. Thank you. North Alabama farmers, along with farmers across this country, continue to face low commodity prices while competing against heavily subsidized foreign producers. Cotton prices today are approximately the same price per pound as they were in the 1970s under President Jimmy Carter. We need to stop importing foreign cotton from other countries. Ambassador Greer, what actions is USTR taking to expand market access for American agriculture and push back against countries that impose unfair barriers on US beef, poultry, and row crop? I would give, I've mentioned the agreements on reciprocal trade we've concluded over the past year a few times. I'd give Indonesia as an example. This is a country, this is the fourth largest country in the world by population. And for the past few years they put all kinds of barriers to our ag and we had an ag trade deficit with Indonesia. We have now required them to remove their tariffs and non-tariff barriers on ag and we're starting to see that go the other way. Thank you. In my last 15 seconds I want to commend you for your efforts to stop this import and bringing in fentanyl to kill a generation of Americans. You look at fentanyl poisonings they're down by more than 54% since this administration took office. You deserve a lot of credit for that. Mr. Chairman, I yield back. Thank you. We're pleased to welcome to the hearing the ranking member of the full committee, Ms. DeLauro. Thank you very much Mr. Chairman and ranking member. This is I think the fourth hearing this morning so it's just kind of I appreciate the opportunity to be here. And I want to say welcome to you, Ambassador Greer. It's great to see you again. And I thank you for being here and engaging with us. On the campaign trail and in office, President Trump made a lot of promises about trade policy about putting more money in our pockets, creating millions and millions of new manufacturing jobs. It was the most pro-American manufacturing and jobs policy. Jobs and factories, these are all quotes. Jobs and factories will come roaring back into our country. There's a reality here which is different. The tariffs have exacerbated the cost of living crisis. American families paid more than $1,700 each in tariff costs last year. Things are only getting worse as the war of choice with Iran continues to drive up the cost of gas, groceries, everyday goods. Question of manufacturing and jobs. US trade deficit in manufactured goods was $62 billion higher in 2025 compared to 2024. Not exactly the manufacturing renaissance that was promised. So Ambassador Greer, how many manufacturing jobs do you know that the United States lost per day during the President's first year in office? So the last two years of the Biden administration, 230,000 manufacturing jobs were lost. In the first quarter of this year we have a net positive on manufacturing jobs. After 40 years of offshoring, it takes more than one year to get them all back. Well, but let me tell you what the Bureau of Labor Statistics has said. Okay, I have it right here. US manufacturing lost more than 100 jobs per day from February 2025 to February 2026. That's more than 88,000 in total. Not a trade policy that delivers for working people. So let me if I can move on. Another reason for the decline in manufacturing is that incentives, you spoke about offshoring, the incentives for offshoring in our trade deals continue to operate. The USMCA, which we both worked on together, the President touted this as this was incredible, was the most important quote most important trade deal that we've made so far. Since USMCA entered into force, offshoring has continued. Agricultural equipment, automobiles, dishwashers, tires. Companies across the country are moving or threatening to move production to Mexico to take advantage of USMCA's rules and Mexico's relatively lower wages and labor protections. Mexican manufacturing wages remain roughly 40% lower than their Chinese counterparts, 88% lower than those in the United States. I believe you and I agree on USMCA that it's not just a review, that there needs to be a renegotiation. You've indicated that while not every issue with USMCA will be resolved by July 1, ongoing discussions with Mexico and the discussions with Canada will focus on priority issues. So what issues are you prioritizing for discussion with those countries? What are the top items for discussion, the sticking points, and what about closing the loopholes that incentivize offshoring or allow China to take advantage of the deal's benefits? That must be at the top of your list. But let's talk about your priorities. It is, and I agree with you, Representative DeLauro. I think you and I are aligned on so many of these issues and the President is, right? The President is very focused on outcomes. And when we did USMCA, which we did together, we were excited about a lot of things. We've done the rapid response mechanism which we have continued to use. It was a floor and not a ceiling and rapid response was critical. That's right and we've continued to use it. But we look at outcomes and the outcomes indeed there's continued offshoring toward Mexico. I'm going to Mexico next week and I'm going to speak with my counterpart and I have already told him and we've already had conversations about changing the rules of origin which is kind of a granular topic. This is how we avoid the transshipment through Mexico and it's how we make it tougher. And I will say it's also important for us to have outside to have a tariff level that incentivizes compliance with those tougher rules. Because if our outside tariff level is low, people will just say well I don't care about the rules I'll just bring something in from Vietnam. That's why I'm trying to have higher tariffs on the rest of the world so that I can actually incentivize manufacturing in the US. Well, look I just say overall and you won't agree, I believe that the President's trade policy is worsening the cost of living crisis. We've raised prices, we've not fulfilled the promises on jobs and manufacturing that he promised in the first days in office. There's an opportunity, opportunity you know to turn that around including again through the USMCA renegotiation. I'm looking forward to staying in touch on that process and looking at what are the kinds of of incentives or proposals that we can make in order to look at how we can renegotiate this and make it work and have it be a worker-centric trade agreement, which is where our trade agreements should be. I thank you and I thank you Mr. Chairman. Thank you the gentlelady. We have a series of votes on the floor which will require us to recess the hearing until the last vote in the series of votes on the floor. So the subcommittee will now stand in recess subject to the call of the chair. The plan will be to reconvene after the last floor vote in the series of votes on the floor. We will proceed with questions from Mr. Alford. Thank you Dean and Ranking Member Meng. Thank you so much for holding this important hearing. US Trade Representative's work has real consequences for America's farmers, our manufacturers, and small businesses. A strong pro-America trade agenda like this administration's focuses on strict enforcement and better deals, helps our producers compete globally, and strengthens our supply chains here at home. In Missouri's fourth congressional district, we see the benefits of the President's pro-America trade policies each and every day. When our beef, pork, soybeans, manufactured goods, and small business exports face unfair barriers or delayed access, the impact hits our communities immediately. We need USTR fully engaged in breaking down those barriers, enforcing commitments, and expanding opportunities abroad. Today I want to understand how this budget request supports stronger trade enforcement, faster market opening efforts, and a more resilient supply chain for American industry. This committee expects transparency with clear priorities, measurable results, and consistent communications with Congress. What I hope to learn today is how USTR plans to deliver for American workers, farmers, manufacturers, and small businesses. Ambassador, thank you for being here today. I really want to focus on the ag sector in particular in Missouri. It's home to more than 80,000 farms, the second highest number of farms of any state in the nation, second to Texas. We have 16,000 farms in my district alone. Our farmers, producers, and rural manufacturers continue to face significant pressure from rising input costs. And I want to make this clear, I don't like looking in the rearview mirror all the time, but a lot of those input costs were due to the war on fossil fuels from the previous administration. Farmers are trying to catch up. Demand continues to soar. How is USTR prioritizing trade actions that directly impact the cost and availability of key ag inputs such as fertilizers, chemicals, and feedstocks? Thank you, Congressman. And we're again always glad to hear about the robust agricultural activity. You know we've been as you know we've been opening markets around the world and we're cutting down that ag trade deficit that grew up during the Biden administration. When it comes to the input cost, you know, we think about things like fertilizer, right? Just yesterday I was in a meeting with Secretary Rollins, Secretary Lutnick, and a handful of others, and we met with folks all along the supply chain. Fertilizer manufacturers, traders, you know state ag commissioners, etc., to talk about this very issue. You know the President's quite focused on it. On the one hand, the good news is we have a lot of domestic production of fertilizer right here in the United States. There are certain elements like potash that come in from Canada. We don't have a lot of potash here. That was never subject to any kind of tariff or anything like that. You know our sense is that, you know, the trade policy is a long-term policy, it's about re-shoring, it's about manufacturing. But there are certain markets where we just don't get involved. Oil and gas, certain hydrocarbons, you know, LNG is at the heart of some fertilizer manufacturing. Potash we don't have tariffs, other fertilizer we don't have tariffs on that. China continues to dominate key supply chains for inputs like fertilizer, feedstock, and industrial components. How is USTR using tariff tools and trade enforcement to reduce US dependency on China and strengthen domestic production? Yeah thank you. So as I in my opening comments I mentioned that the President's tariff program again which really extends back to his first term when he first imposed tariffs on China. President Biden kept them in place. Our trade deficit in goods with China, manufactured goods and everything else, it went down by $130 billion last year. In one year. That's a pretty incredible decrease. And at the same time it was going down with other countries too. So we pay attention to transshipment and all that, but overall we're going in the right direction, we're diversifying from China, and we're supporting domestic manufacturing and protecting with tariffs where we need to. How much stronger are we now than we were two years ago when it comes to trade in general? You know I know some small business owners, this de minimis doing away with that has impacted their business but overall the American economy, how much better off are we? Well first of all, we've had wages go up. So when we think about trade, we think about exports. And I've already mentioned today January and February of this year were the highest exports in US history, over 300 billion dollars each month. So we're winning on that front. Wages, especially manufacturing wages, went up by 5% last year. And this is for like non-supervisory, this is for people on the line working. We've seen manufacturing productivity go up. And in the first quarter of this year, we saw manufacturing jobs be positive in the quarter for the first time since 2023. So all of those trends are going in the right direction. Thank you, Ambassador, and I yield back. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Ivey. Thank you, Mr. Chair, Ranking Member. Ambassador, thank you for being here today. I want to ask you about IEEPA tariffs in the wake of the Learning Resources decision by the Supreme Court. The issue seems to be that I think 166 billion or so was collected with respect to the tariffs and after the court declared them illegal the issue became well how will the money be refunded to the people who paid the tariffs. And there's sort of two sets of issues that I wanted to touch on. One in particular is what happens to regular people who paid money in the form of higher costs or higher prices. Is there some kind of process that's going to be put in place or that is in place for them to get a refund. The Department of Homeland Security, I guess it was CBP, put in a program that starts on Monday and that's I think it's called CAPE. And the idea there is that entities that are the importer of record or the licensed customs broker can file a request with respect to the entity or the department and seek a declaration for repayment. And I think about 2,000 companies or so have sought that already and there's going to be a process where they can try and recover the funds that they put in. I haven't seen what's going to happen with respect to regular people. And as you move down the supply chain, as smaller business, I'm going to focus on individual customers here, but smaller businesses are facing sort of similar issues too, some in my district have raised this with me. So is there going to be a process in place or is there one already that will help to make sure that people at the other end of the supply chain, not just the big corporations and the like at the front end of it, but the regular people are going to have a chance to get some sort of refund for money they paid in the form of the tariffs that the Supreme Court said are now illegal. Sure, thanks Congressman. So again, the tariffs were imposed to protect American jobs and reverse the offshoring that happened for decades. This money was in the general treasury, it was the taxpayer's money that was brought in. It was assessed on foreign goods made by foreign workers who worked for foreign companies, that's where the money came from. And then we had a bunch of Democrat Attorneys General who sued, and in their suit Congressman, this is relevant because they asked for the money just to go back to the companies. So the Democrat Attorneys General asked for this and they're getting what they asked for. CBP has set up a program like you mentioned and they'll send the money back to the people who paid it. If I might, I appreciate that comment, but during the course of the federal litigation, the Trump administration stated that if the tariffs were found to be illegal, they would refund the money. So there was a stipulations made in multiple cases as this litigation rose up by the administration for the repayment. So what I'm not seeing though is a way to get the money back to, as I stated in the predicate to the question, whether it's smaller businesses on the supply chain or regular customers. And I'll add this too. Even if it's the case that state AGs only asked for that particular remedy, that doesn't block the administration from trying to find ways to refund the money for people. For example there's you guys got a letter from Governor Pritzker and he proposed $1,700 liquidated damage amount. He only spoke about the people of Illinois but that's one possibility. There's two bills that are pending here in the House now, Mr. Cuellar, Ms. Crockett. The Crockett bill proposes putting together, the administration putting together a process to through the Department of Treasury for repayment. Mr. Cuellar does something roughly similar but his would focus more on targeting lower income people who made the payment, so I think there's a cutoff at $400,000. I'm not wedded to any of those, I'm agnostic on it, but I guess the question is is the administration going to do anything to make sure that regular people who paid higher prices are going to get some money back. Because if you don't, what'll happen is the corporations are going to get sort of double compensation, which I think is an unjust enrichment. They'll get the benefit of having charged the higher prices at the front end, and they'll get the benefit of the refund through the process that you all have set up. So is there anything that's going to be done by the administration to try and address that? I see I'm short on time here. The one thing I'd say is Governor Pritzker's Attorney General asked for the money to go to the corporations, so he gets what he lays in the bed that he made. We have asked companies that they should give if they get a refund they should give it to their workers as bonuses. It was money in the general treasury, it was going toward the re-industrialization of America, and the Democrat Attorney General said no we want it to go back to the companies who import foreign goods made by foreign workers, like that's the law. I yield back. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Cline. Thank you Mr. Greer for being here. I want to talk about apples and then Korea and if we've got time EU. US apple growers spent two decades of time and money to develop a year-round apple market in India. In 2018, US apples were 53% of the apples imported into India. India's 50% tariff on apples has greatly hindered US growers' efforts to rebuild the market. After several years of retaliation, US apples are now only 8.5% of imports. Apples from Iran, Turkey, and Afghanistan are nearly 50%. Competitors in New Zealand and the EU will soon enjoy preferential treatment under trade agreements with India and further chisel away the market US growers built. US apple growers need a better deal. The references to India reducing tariffs for US agricultural products in the framework agreement announced in February was positive. As an agreement on reciprocal trade is finalized with India, how is USTR securing significant tariff reductions for US apples? Thank you. So we've been working with the Indians for over a year. We have an initial joint statement, general terms of agreement with India. I met with their ambassador this week as well to try to bring that agreement to a conclusion. We have discussed apples many times. I've personally raised it at my level, so certainly our staff talk about it, but I've personally raised it with my counterpart. This is something that they're considering. Nothing's done till it's done in these negotiations but it's something that we've made a priority. We want to be able to make sure that to the extent India's going to be importing apples, we want them also we want to be able to play in that space. We're not trying to undercut, you know, Indian apple industry or anything like that, but if they're going to import it they should be importing it from America too. Agreed. Moving on, the 2026 National Trade Estimate Report identified Korea's digital trade policies, including the KFTC's enforcement campaign and the Fairness Act as significant barriers to US companies. Research by the Compete America Foundation estimates that Korea's policies could cost the US economy $525 billion over the next decade with American households losing nearly $4,000 each. What specific enforcement mechanisms is USTR using to hold Korea to its commitments under the joint fact sheet? And as you know, the Korean government continues its transparent discrimination against US digital companies. At what point does continued Korean defiance trigger formal trade actions? Thank you. As you note, we have a joint fact sheet with Korea where they specifically agreed and committed not to use these types of laws or rules in a discriminatory basis. And so this is something where we're holding their feet to the fire. We have tools, you know Section 301 is a tool that I've talked about today and some of you have discussed. This is something we can use if we need to. Again I've had conversations with my counterpart in Korea, I've spoken with the Prime Minister of Korea about this issue. I know there are a lot of views domestically about big tech companies and regulation and that kind of thing. What I want is a situation where Congress gets to decide how American tech companies are controlled and not foreign jurisdictions. So we are very attentive to any suggestion of discrimination by foreign countries against our companies. They need to be leaning on them pretty hard. Their proposed Fairness Act sets market thresholds that Chinese platforms like TikTok, Temu, and Alibaba fall below, effectively exempting them from the regulations entirely while American companies are bearing the full weight of the fines and operational mandates. To put it in perspective, a quick glimpse at the new 1260H list of Chinese military companies included some of these firms like Alibaba. Does the administration view Korea's discriminatory treatment as a national security concern and, if so, is that concern being incorporated into how you're approaching enforcement? So if they were to follow through with this and apply these laws in a discriminatory way, we would take action. Until now, they have been willing to speak to us and negotiate with us because they are concerned about this. And we are prepared to take action if we need to because it's not like there's some other company waiting to step into the wings other than some of these Chinese companies you're talking about. The EU is another area where we see discriminatory action being taken against US companies. The Digital Markets Act, Digital Networks Act, Cloud and AI Development Act, Digital Services Taxes, EU Space Act, and other policies all disproportionately target US companies. How does your budget prioritize comprehensive action against this entire suite of discriminatory measures? What enforcement mechanisms will USTR deploy to protect American technology leadership against European regulatory overreach? So we have an office dedicated to services investment. And this is an office where we have been going through and inventorying all of these unfair and discriminatory laws and practices that you're talking about. If I'm to take action on these, again, here is a—I keep saying Section 301. Every time I say Section 301, you have to think that's a policy person, that's one or two lawyers, and that's a political person in the front office. Every time I say 301, that's more people. I'm happy to do it, but I do have to have the resources to hire those people. Thank you, yield back. Ms. Dean. Thank you, Chairman Rogers, thank you Ranking Member Meng, and thank you Ambassador for being here today in front of us. I'm Madeleine Dean. I serve on both the Appropriations and the Foreign Affairs Committees and I am co-founder of the Fentanyl Prevention Caucus. And so I know that you play a very pivotal role in economic policies including tariffs, which we are talking a lot about today. So first let me say something that I agree with the President on and I congratulate the President for doing, which is eliminating the de minimis loophole. I say that because eliminating the de minimis loophole is good for US businesses, it is good for individual consumers, and of course it is good in the fight against the transport of fentanyl. So thank the President for that; I have said it openly before. Ambassador Greer, I read with interest in your testimony, quote, "Our most important trade agreement is our Constitution." Yet a few paragraphs later you state, "The President's trade program continues despite the Supreme Court's decision in February relating to IEEPA tariffs." That's puzzling to me. That's ironic. It's a paradox. You ground your trade work in our Constitution. You emphasize that that is what the administration relies upon. And yet the Supreme Court found the President's tariffs to be unconstitutional, to be illegal. So I want to say to you, I want you to live up to the order that the Supreme Court said. The moneys must be returned to those who paid them. You'll see some difference of opinion here, but it's very clear the moneys were illegally gotten by this government, by this administration. They are illegally sitting in Treasury and they need to go back to those who paid them in full with interest. So let me examine that with you. I noted that you spoke a lot about trade deficits and trade surplus; we heard the President do all of that. It reminds me of the last time we had a trade surplus, as you well know, was in the Great Depression. To say the least, that's not a good thing. It was in part as a result of isolationist policies of the 1920s. I'd hate to have history repeat itself. The administration stole from American businesses and consumers in an illegal way with the tariffs. The money must be returned. Will you commit to making sure the money is returned in full without request from those who it was taken from, automatically to those from whom it was taken? Will you commit to work with Treasury, Customs, and Border to return in full automatically? Yes or no? Yes or no? So congresswoman, anything the court tells us to do, we'll do it right. And so then are you going to hide behind the court didn't speak to whether we have to return the money or not? So as I was just speaking with Mr. Ivey, Customs and Border Protection has a program, right. We don't just do a willy-nilly grab and just anyone comes and we give them money. They come in through the CBP program that's set up. It's a portal that opens on Monday. I want to interrupt you. Stop me because I'm aware of the program. Well, that's how we give the money back. No. Here's how you collected the money. I took a look at this. This is an entry summary for an importer, a small importer—a form 7501. I'm very familiar with it. I think you're right. Of course I'm right. It is 7. Very good. Then you know on 7501 IEEPA tariffs are listed here as clear as day to the penny, to the product and to the penny. There should be no application process. Return, hit the button, return it to those who paid it. Will you commit to saying that the program needs to be changed and it should be automatic? Will you commit to that? Yes or no? Well, I don't control customs, so I can't commit. Would you commit as part of the administration and as the ambassador of trade? Yes or no? So there's a manufacturer ID number on that form, and if you take that and you go to the portal and you put it in and you tell them I'm the manufacturer, CBP can look at it and verify. I'm going to reclaim my time. I mean this is just—in terms of paying interest on these moneys wrongly, illegally, and unconstitutionally gotten by this administration, interest is owed on it. Do you agree with that? If there's interest owed on it by statute, it gets paid. That's right. If there is interest—or is there interest? I'll defer to CBP, I don't have the specific—do you know what the interest rate is? I think it's 6%. I don't think that's true. Okay what is it? I think it's 4.5. I'll take 6. I'll take 6 since it was illegally gotten. But the IRS corporate overpayment rate is 4.5, so we can check on that. Oh, you know what, no you're right. Overpayments exceeding $10,000 go to 6%. You're absolutely right. I missed that. Will you commit to making sure that interest is paid on all of these refunds, which should be automatic? If there is an issue with this, or your constituent has an issue with this, I'm happy to talk to CBP about it. We shouldn't have to do it by constituent. For the record, may I enter something for the record? The gentlelady will respond. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. By the opinion of the United States Court of International Trade, the order written by Richard Eaton, these duties must now be refunded with interest. The clock is ticking and further interest is accumulating every day to the tune of approximately 650 million dollars a month. Mr. Cline. Thank you, I yield. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and thank you, Ambassador Greer, for testifying before us today. And again, you are right. For far too long the United States has been taken advantage of by unfair trade practices around the world. These persistent trade imbalances have driven manufacturing overseas, weakened critical supply chains, and left our defense industrial base even more dependent on foreign suppliers. To restore both free and fair trade, President Trump has utilized tariffs on more than 60 countries, putting America first and ensuring US manufacturing is no longer shortchanged by foreign competitors. Already President Trump has delivered major results, securing billion-dollar trade deals with our allies and partners, bringing thousands of jobs back to our shores, and driving billions in new manufacturing investment. Despite the Supreme Court's decision earlier this year, President Trump remains committed to re-shoring American manufacturing. So my question is, President Trump has already announced historic private sector investment in American manufacturing here at home. In your view, have the administration's tariff policies been successful and have you seen significant evidence of companies re-shoring investment back to the United States? Yes, thank you. And I mentioned this earlier, just last week I was in Michigan and Ohio and I went to a variety of factories from very small businesses to large multinationals. One of these is Stellantis, which is the company that owns Jeep and Dodge. And due to the President's tariffs on autos that he imposed last year, they announced a $13 billion investment in the United States across the upper Midwest. Some of that included bringing back product lines from both Canada and Mexico to the United States. So that's one example. We also announced there was an announcement of a $60 million investment in the Whirlpool factory in Clyde, Ohio, which is the world's largest and oldest washing machine factory. And that has another 150 jobs. So these are just examples. The pharmaceutical companies, they're pouring foundations and building superstructures in North Carolina, Indiana, and Southern California across the country. And when we ask them why, they say tariff. They don't always love to put it in their filings with the SEC, but that's why. Well thank you, and I've been to Clyde, Ohio too, so great place. Were you named after it? No, it wasn't named after me. In your fiscal year 2027 budget request, there is an increase of $7 million or an 8% increase above the fiscal year 2026 enacted level for the Office of the US Trade Representative. Now I'm a businessman and I believe in return on investment, so that needs to be positive and not negative; otherwise, why are we investing. So can you explain specifically how this additional funding will translate into tangible outcomes and to positive ROI, particularly in terms of securing greater investment in expanding economic opportunities here in the United States? Certainly. So every time we consider making a trade deal—which again, the way we get the trade deals are by having tariffs—we have to have the leverage to get the deals. Otherwise, these countries will continue to treat us as they have for decades. And so when we have some level of tariff, you bring some money into the Treasury and that's real revenue that can be used for programs. Second, when we get the trade deals, every time we do these deals we do an assessment of what we think the market opportunity is. And so from Indonesia to India to the UK to Argentina, it's hundreds of billions of dollars in opportunities. And as I've mentioned earlier, we've seen that actually start to happen with record-breaking exports in the last couple of months. Okay, but with the increase of $7 million, all right, I mean how do you monitor these trade agreements to ensure that the folks are holding to what they agreed to? Or is that what this is necessary for? That's certainly part of it because we have to monitor it. One of the problems we've had over the past decades is my agency typically only has 200 to 250 people at any given time. And we believe that with this money we can have another 20 to 25 people. Because we have more trade agreements now after a year and more commitments, I need more people to monitor it and I need more people to enforce it. There's also one million dollars I'm asking for which is actually to consolidate our footprint from buildings to get rid of another lease. So I'm trying to save money for you in the long term if I consolidate this year. So I'm trying to be as respectful as I can with the taxpayer's money and bring opportunities for American workers and farmers. All right, one last question before my time runs out. Given our reliance on adversarial nations like communist China for critical materials and technology, how is your office contributing to trade strategies that reduce these dependencies, especially for the critical materials? So we are right now preparing text, which we'll share with Congress before we circulate it to partners, for a plurilateral agreement on trade in critical minerals. We want to create a situation because China has broken the market for critical minerals and this is not the first time they've tried to use rare earths to control other countries' behavior. Leverage. Exactly right. And so what we are doing with trading partners who have critical minerals or who have manufacturing is we want to create an agreement where among the parties to the agreement, we have a market in critical minerals among us and we try to trade at market-based prices. But if you're not in the agreement and you want to trade with one of these parties, you have to do it at a certain level; you have to have a price floor. Because in the past, the Chinese have just dumped into our market and then we have a mine that can't operate anymore because it doesn't make economic sense. And so we're trying to do an agreement with a select group to protect and create a market because it's been broken in the past. Thank you, Ambassador Greer, you're doing a great job. And I yield back, Mr. Chairman. Gentleman yields back. Mr. Mrvan. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you, Ranking Member. Mr. Greer, thank you for your testimony today. Indiana's first congressional district is home to the nation's largest concentration of steel production. American steel makers play a critical role in the defense, energy, automotive construction, and industrial manufacturing industries. Therefore, ensuring that domestic producers can compete globally on a level playing field is an economic and national security imperative. To that end, I appreciate the work that you have done to enhance Section 232 steel tariffs and I hope to continue to work with the administration as co-chair of the Steel Caucus to strengthen our domestic steel industry. On the USMCA, one of the largest looming conversations in the steel industry is the review of the USMCA slated for this summer. While the initial adoption of the USMCA in 2020 made strides with strengthening certain rules of origin provisions that have benefited domestic steel makers, trade policies in Canada and Mexico have allowed for a surge of global steel overcapacity in the North America steel markets. The mandatory six-year review of the USMCA presents an opportunity for the USTR to encourage Canada and Mexico to take actionable steps to stem the tide of non-North American steel imports that distort US markets and harm American steel producers and fabricators. What conversations are you having with Canada and Mexico counterparts—Mexican counterparts—regarding the USMCA? And what steps do you plan in order to curb transshipment of heavily subsidized steel from other parts of the world, particularly China? Thank you, Congressman, and we appreciate your support on this. I look forward to working with you and the rest of the Steel Caucus on something of just critical national security importance. Over the past year I've had a lot of conversations with my counterparts in Canada and Mexico and I've been clear with them that we cannot really have free trade with those countries in steel unless they are controlling their own borders with respect to steel, to get at the transshipment issue you're talking about. For me, the gold standard for rules of origin for steel is "melt and pour," which means we want it to be melted and poured in these countries. Right. So that's my view; that's my expectation. When you say "my view," you mean also the administration? Yes sir, that is the President of the United States and the Secretary of Commerce, who runs the 232. This is our view. Because we brought in steel from another country for the White House expansion. So I just want to make sure we're on the same page. Well, we don't prohibit imports, but we do have a tariff on them. Right? I'm not—I just want to make sure that you speak consistently, so please go on. Yeah, we don't prohibit it. People can still import, but if they're going to—if it's going to be foreign steel—it's going to be paid for. It's a 50% tariff. It's the highest tariff any President's ever put on steel using Section 232. So I'm happy to work with you on that. I'm having conversations with the Canadians and the Mexicans about rules of origin on this issue. Okay, thank you for that. Northwest Indiana is the largest steel producer in the country—national security—along with 12 to 14,000 steel workers and tradesmen who are in that industry. It's the lifeblood of my economy, so it's something I take very seriously. So the next step: national security farmers. With the tariffs and what has happened to the farming industry, I represent a lot of farmers. I specifically want to ask the question that China's soybean market has been closed. That market was filled by Argentina. The administration sent $40 billion—or 40 million or 40 billion dollars—to Argentina while the farmers are suffering here because of the tariffs and the closing of the market. I'm a believer of E15 and making sure that we do what we can to open those markets. But also with the closing of the Strait of Hormuz, nitrates and fertilizers are increasing by 30-40% consistently, and growing season is starting now. And so the administration and you specifically have talked about lowering costs while grocery costs are rising, which is directly related to the input costs of our farmers. Please tell me what you would say to the farmers who believe that it was a slap in the face to give $40 billion to Argentina, who took over a market that was their market. Thank you, Congressman. With respect to Argentina, again, this is something China does. Anytime we try to defend or take care of unfair trading practices, China tries to squeeze us. And they always use our farmers as a target. That's why we don't want them to be super dependent. They get it from Brazil, they get it from Argentina. So it was purposely—the tariffs purposely took the soybean market away from the American farmers because you don't want them to have that market? I don't understand. What happens is when we try to take care of unfair trade practices by the Chinese who are a national security threat, the Chinese response is often to target American farmers. Correct. Right? I think we agree on this. And then the Chinese start importing from Brazil or Argentina or other places. With respect to the 40 billion, that wasn't a gift. I didn't call it a gift. Well, you said you gave it to them, so you give a gift. You're being presumptive. The United States of America made a return on that. Very good. So you know, if you don't want us to make a return on our money we don't have to, but I think it's better that we—give them a return on what? When you give a country a loan from a fund that's set up by Congress to help float countries. So the 40 billion to Argentina was a loan? So Congress—they control the purse, right? And you set up by statute funds at the Treasury Department that they can use to float countries. That's what it's for. So Congress or the executive gave the 40 billion? No, I'm sorry, returned 40 billion dollars to Argentina. Because we're losing track that the farmers—you didn't answer the question. The administration gave 40 billion dollars to a country that took their market. What do you say to the farmers who feel like it's a slap in the face that one market was closed and you enhanced the country that took their market away? We have a 25 million metric ton commitment from the Chinese every year for the next three years to buy soybeans. 25 million metric tons a year. That's what we have from the Chinese; that's what they committed to do. That's what we've negotiated. Are they currently doing that? And they don't buy it all at once; they do it over the year. Because why would the farmers say that the market is closed if you have a commitment? We do have a commitment. But it's not being fulfilled because of the tariffs. Well, let's talk about the data. If you look at—they had a commitment from our last meetings from the last quarter of 2025 and for the season and they filled that, and we have 25 million metric tons. I'm going to see the Chinese next month with the President of the United States and we're going to follow up on this to make sure they fulfill it. Know that my farmers conclude. Conclude. I thank you for the extra time. Thank you. Mr. Moore. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, and Ambassador Greer, thank you so much for being here. In the 19th and 20th centuries, America built our industrial might on the foundation of really a protectionist trade policy, abundant energy, and union knowledge and skills. Tariffs created really the conditions for domestic production to thrive, while unions ensured that the resulting prosperity reached the workers on the factory floor. Carrying on that tradition, the administration's reindustrialization efforts for the United States through tariffs have been vital in reorienting the American economy to manufacturing. Manufacturing jobs are high-quality jobs that provide a stable lifestyle and allow families and communities to flourish. Can you explain how a robust protectionist policy, coupled with unleashing American energy dominance and tapping into unique skills of American workers and unions, will serve to unleash a golden age of manufacturing here in the United States? Thank you. So starting really in earnest at the end of the Cold War, there was a view in Washington and Wall Street, I would say, that we should open our markets, we should lower our barriers to foreign goods, services, capital, and labor. And that in doing that, you know, we would grow and it would all be shared and that kind of thing. We now have 35-40 years of data and we know that was wrong. We lost five million factory jobs, we lost 70,000 factories because people wanted to lower all of our protections. They wanted to be dependent on countries like China; you know, they wanted us to just go backwards in the face of unfair trade practices. They didn't want us to do anything. You know, if we wanted to put tariffs on China, you know, President Bush for a hot minute had tariffs on steel from China. And then everyone said no, no, no, we can't, you know, we can't close our markets to China; we can't deal with Chinese retaliation, right? So we're not going to give up on this, right? We are committed to this. So it sometimes can be challenging. The Chinese will retaliate, but we're going to deal with this. We're not just going to lay down and let the Chinese have their way with the American economy. So we have to be able to reverse the script and that's what we've been doing. We've been putting up tariffs to protect our manufacturing and, by the way, our ag producers, because our ag producers have had a trade deficit for the past few years under Biden. That's finally being cut down. And at the same time, we're opening foreign markets. That's why we are exporting more than America has ever exported in its entire history of 250 years. 300 billion dollars—over 300 billion dollars—in each of January and February. So we flipped the script. Instead of having our market just wildly open and facing closed markets overseas, we've done a moderate control of our own imports to protect domestic manufacturing and agriculture. You know, not having tariffs on everything—we have a lot of things that aren't covered by tariffs. But importantly, things are covered by tariffs and we've opened markets overseas, and that's what's creating the turnaround for us. Well, and this has been touched on a little bit, but I just want to put a finer point on it. And I really believe that a nation that offshores its industrial capacity really offshores its deterrent capacity. And this is true both for the defense industrial base, but also our industrial base more broadly. If we can't pour our own steel and produce the active ingredients for pharmaceuticals or make our own chips, we are at the mercy of those who do. Ambassador, can you speak a little to the national security imperative of bringing our industrial supply chain back to the United States and why that is so critical? Well, think about the pandemic in recent history. It wasn't a war, but there were supply chain snarls and logistics challenges. And for a time, we couldn't get enough semiconductor chips that we use in cars, so we couldn't make cars. The same types of chips go into satellites and weapon systems and things like that. What about pharmaceuticals? During the pandemic there were a lot of countries that locked down the export of pharmaceuticals. So we have fundamental advanced manufacturing and basic manufacturing like steel and other things that we have to have here. We have to—are we going to import? Of course we're going to import; we're not—it's not autarky we're talking about. But we have to have resilient supply chains where we can provide for ourselves. If there's a pandemic, if there's a war, if something else happens, we have to be able to have those resilient supply chains—not "just-in-time" supply chains like we like to have, but ones that actually provide for the security of the American people. Well thank you, Ambassador Greer, and please continue the great work. I yield back, Mr. Chairman. Gentleman yields back. That concludes today's hearing. I want to thank our witness, Ambassador Greer, for being here today. Thank you, sir. Without objection, members may have seven days to submit additional questions for the record. Subcommittee stands adjourned.

Episode 16: Building AI for Life Sciences

OpenAI(1.9M) ·44:26 ·6.9K views · 2026-04-16
Joy Jiao (Research Lead, OpenAI) Yunyun Wang (Product Lead, OpenAI)
OpenAI is developing a specialized Life Sciences model series and scientific infrastructure to accelerate drug discovery and automate manual lab workflows, while implementing biosecurity "differentiated access" to ensure high-capability tools remain in the hands of verified researchers.
TL;DR OpenAI is developing a specialized Life Sciences model series and scientific infrastructure to accelerate drug discovery and automate manual lab workflows, while implementing biosecurity "differentiated access" to ensure high-capability tools remain in the hands of...

TL;DR

OpenAI is developing a specialized Life Sciences model series and scientific infrastructure to accelerate drug discovery and automate manual lab workflows, while implementing biosecurity "differentiated access" to ensure high-capability tools remain in the hands of verified researchers.

Specialized AI for Scientific Workflows

  • Life Sciences Model Series: OpenAI is building biochemistry-focused models anchored on complex research workflows, specifically targeting genomics, protein understanding, and early-stage drug discovery.
  • The Life Sciences Research Plugin: This tool provides over 50 "skills" or templatized workflows, allowing scientists to perform one-click deployments for tasks like cross-evidence paper searches, pathway analysis, and reproducibility checks.
  • Codex as Scientific Infrastructure: Beyond text, researchers are using Codex to manage remote dev boxes, monitor logs, and build custom data visualization UIs (e.g., interactive spinning protein models) to share insights.
  • Tool Integration: The models act as "computational biologists" by autonomously running and tweaking open-source protein structure prediction algorithms and other biological design tools.

Autonomous Labs and the "Pipetting" Bottleneck

  • Ginko Bioworks Collaboration: In a landmark experiment, an early version of a frontier model (referenced as GPT-5 in the transcript) designed experiments that successfully produced a non-zero amount of protein in an autonomous wet lab setting.
  • Automation of Manual Labor: A primary goal is the elimination of "pipetting"—the manual transfer of liquids that limits the pace of science to the speed of human hands.
      • Protocol Translation: Models are being used to translate traditional biological protocols into code that robotic platforms can execute.
  • Shift to Compute Bottlenecks: Joy Jiao and Yunyun Wang envision a future where scientific progress is no longer limited by human labor or "thinking time," but rather by the availability of compute to run parallel sub-agents on discovery tasks.

Biosecurity and Responsible Deployment

  • Information Hazards: OpenAI identifies biosecurity as a critical "dual-use" risk. Because the steps for benign research and harmful pathogen synthesis often look identical, general-access models sometimes "soft refuse" legitimate requests to err on the side of safety.
  • Differentiated Access: To empower professionals without compromising safety, OpenAI is moving toward a model of "differentiated access."
      • Verified Identities: High-level capabilities are reserved for researchers at legitimate institutions (pharma, academia) where reagents and cell lines are already strictly regulated.
      • Enterprise-Grade Controls: This allows experts to bypass the limitations placed on general-access traffic while maintaining a high security bar.
  • Evals and Testing: Model performance is measured through "virtual cell" work—predicting outcomes of unseen biological perturbations—and real-world wet lab validation through research partnerships.

The 10-Year Vision: Autonomous Research Institutes

  • 6–12 Month Outlook: The team aims for AI to assist in patenting new discoveries and reaching a milestone where AI designs a novel drug or identifies a cure for an "orphan" disease.
  • Autonomous Science: In a decade, the vision includes "Autonomous Research Institutes"—robotic labs hooked to AI that constantly run experiments, design new materials, and solve personalized medicine challenges.
  • Societal Defenses: AI systems could autonomously sample wastewater and air to detect emerging pathogens (e.g., new influenza strains) and suggest medical countermeasures in real-time.
  • Democratization: AI aims to lower the "memorization" barrier of biology, allowing researchers to focus on high-level creativity and hypothesis setting rather than rote learning and manual labor.
"When we first announced the team's formation on Slack, I think one of the taglines was to scale test-time compute to cure all disease."

— Joy Jiao, Research Lead

"I think the dream is to have a lot of the basic foundations of scientific workflows happen on Codex... to pretty much be able to do everything that is possible to do on a computer."

— Joy Jiao, Research Lead

"The skepticism is really healthy and should be welcomed. I think it's just really great to see people get really excited... the potential for this technology is so great if we get it right."

— Yunyun Wang, Product Lead

"It’s really like the democratizing science aspect and putting like really capable expert-level knowledge in the hands of a greater amount of people."

— Yunyun Wang, Product Lead

Hello, I'm Andrew Mayne, and this is the OpenAI podcast. On today's episode, we're talking with research lead Joy Jiao and product lead Yunyun Wang about OpenAI for Life Sciences. We'll explore what new models are making possible in biology and medicine, and what it takes to deploy the most advanced capabilities responsibly. This allows you to kind of reach new levels of difficulty and discovery that we didn't think was even possible before, putting like really capable, expert-level knowledge in the hands of a greater amount of people. One of the taglines was to scale test-time compute to cure all disease. So that is like our team tagline. We started off with just a basic API, and then we had ChatGPT, which was more conversational and was really good for text. As code became a capability, it went through basic code models and then Codex. Now that you're getting more scientists in the life sciences working on these systems, does that mean things have to evolve to help with the way researchers might work with these tools? Yeah, we're really excited to build and deploy the life sciences model series. So this is a new biochemistry-focused model series that's really anchored on these very complex life science research workflows. And we're focused on adding new mechanistic understanding, starting with genomics understanding and protein understanding. We are really focused on early discovery use cases because we feel like that's like one of the core bottlenecks. Greater thinking time, greater compute, and really leveraging more capable AI models can help meaningfully scale some of these research barriers. And I think there's also like a model orchestration piece of actually how to embed this into workflows. It's been really great first off having all these different product surfaces to deploy to. We're seeing a lot of really great literature synthesis workflows happening on ChatGPT. And these models really push the frontier of like long-trajectory agentic workflows, and we're really able to empower that on Codex. And more on the model orchestration piece is that I think for enterprise use cases, there's like this reproducibility and repeatability element. And we are trying to overcome this by working on like some of the life sciences research plugins that we're shipping for very specific translational bio users. So the life sciences research plugin has over 50 skills, which are essentially templatized repeatable workflows. Whether you need to do some sort of cross-evidence match and search across various different papers, or do pathway analysis—something that's repeatable that you often do—we can have like almost like a one-click deploy option by using our life sciences plugins on top. And that's also how we're kind of seeing the balance between scaling for very specialized purposes, something we're hoping to get into is maybe clinical purposes, but also make it still very general use for all foundational biology. I think the models can get quite far by using tools. For example, we can use open-source protein structure prediction algorithms inside our research stack. In this case, the model is acting kind of like a regular computational biologist. You would kind of go run these tools on a computer, you would look at the output, and you would tweak the input a little bit. So I think that is something our models can already do. I do think what will make the models even more powerful is to start to turn them more into kind of a biochemistry expert. And I think with this kind of intuition and expertise, you can use these tools even more intelligently and get at the right answer more quickly. How did you get your interest in life sciences? My original background was actually in life sciences. So I've always been interested in biology as a kid. I got my PhD in systems biology around a decade ago from Harvard. I found academia to be very interesting, but the pace was a little bit more slow-moving than I would have liked. And I think just the experience of kind of having to physically be in the lab and transferring small amounts of liquid from one tube to another, I think I wanted something a little bit faster pace where I felt like I was more in direct control of my own velocity. So I went from that to software and then ended up here at OpenAI. And so this is kind of like a full circle moment for me where I'm starting to look at biology again and looking at how to accelerate my previous self with AI. So yeah, I'm really excited to see what progress AI can make in the space. So you're like, yeah, this is too slow. Let me go off in AI and speed it up so I can get back into it. Yeah, except, you know, from this end, I don't really ever want to touch a pipette or anything again, so I would prefer for robots to do it for me. Yeah, we joke about that a lot. A lot of our motivation for this is we can automate pipetting and never have to do that again. Well, that's what's interesting. I was looking at what you all did with Ginko Bioworks and the idea of taking GPT-5 and taking an AI system and then working with a robotic lab and how it was able to speed things up. Could you tell us a little bit about that? Yeah, the Ginko work is interesting because I think when it started, I think it was like July of last year, 2024. And at that point, GPT-5 had just finished training. We were really not sure if the models could do any kind of biology. We didn't really have that much biology in our training data. It was mostly math and computer science, which I think makes sense because these things have verifiable solutions and this is usually not the case in biology unless you can go and do the experiment in the lab, right? So when we started the collaboration with Ginko, it was really: can the model do any biology at all? Can it design experiments that actually make reactants, like make the product that we want? So it was actually quite surprising, I think, when GPT-5 designed the first set of experiments with Ginko and the results came back and we were like, oh, we made a non-zero amount of protein. That was actually quite surprising. And then I think progressing from that point in time, which was just roughly six months ago to now, where it actually just feels quite obvious that our models can accelerate science, is actually just really surprising. And it really shows like the art of the possible, I think. I think before that experiment led by Joy on the Ginko team was conducted, I think we really didn't know for ourselves. And I always say like we kind of learned that for ourselves when we engage in these experiments, and we have a few more in the works with others. And I think that is like the type of acceleration that we're looking for. Ingesting high-throughput experimental data is really difficult. It's very compute-intensive, and I think for a lot of these scientific workflows, the true bottleneck for the speed and progress of scientific acceleration is at almost human bottlenecks. And I think the future that me and Joy see is that it's no longer human bottlenecks, but rather maybe compute bottlenecks. And we're really able to deploy many sub-agents doing parallel orchestration to divide and conquer all these tasks, and the researcher can now spend their time on really analyzing and interpreting the most meaningful insights coming out of that. So Yunyun, how did you get into this? Yeah, I think reflecting back, I've actually been working on biology research in some shape or form for a majority of my time here at OpenAI. So I first started on working on bio-risk mitigations and a lot of our bio-defense initiatives. And so I feel like coming to now working on the life sciences research side gives me just appreciation for how difficult this problem is and tackling it from both sides. And my initial entry point into wet lab research was actually through doing a lot of infectious disease and virology work. So I think I've always had the interest in bio-security in that way. So this just feels like a really great moment right now to work on it, especially when our models are getting more capable at beneficial use and just general life sciences. How long has OpenAI been focused on life sciences? Yeah, I would say it was really the way we design our capability evals that show us that this is possible. So it's been I think for at least two years now that we have worked on a lot of our early research experiments. And now with the Ginko autonomous wet lab model in the loop experiments, I think we have a few more research partners in this space that we're really excited about. I think I can't actually name everyone right now, but there's a lot of stuff kind of in the chemical design, protein design, and enzyme design space that I think is very AI-native and a lot of people are interested in. So understanding how the world works, understanding how chemicals react, understanding how cells interact, how pathways inside cells interact, all the way to: can we accelerate drug discovery? So given a disease, can a model help scientists understand the mechanism? Can we, once given a target, actually design a drug against that target? Can we even accelerate the FDA approval process? So I think there's a role for AI to play kind of at every step of this pipeline and yeah, I think there's a lot of AI possible in everything. I've been to some of those cutting-edge labs, and on the outside you have this impression of it, but then you walk in there and you literally see somebody with a row of petri dishes, a row of samples, and just some grad student going click, click, click. And I'm like, oh, this is the pace of science. But yeah, exactly, you're like: enough of this, I've got to go speed this up. But we forget that's often the pace of science—it's just how fast the human hands can move through that, and with a tool like that, it's kind of exciting. When you start using these tools to maybe think about new pathways for treatments or just evaluate, you also introduce the idea that these could be used for things that maybe are less desirable. Bio-weapons is something that comes up a lot, the fact that if an AI can figure out how to do a code exploit, it might be able to figure out how to do a gene exploit. How are you addressing that? Yeah, that's a great question, and I think it is just probably one of the most severe risks that we're currently really tracking for rising AI capabilities. Our first approach to that was really thinking about how do we assess for information hazards? At what point does a model now maybe give the final step in a synthesis of a dangerous pathogen? And what we've found is that the precursor steps to that really look very benign and it's really hard to distinguish between... So another way to put it is like the same steps that a beneficial, like a legitimate actor might take, looks very similar to the ones that a dangerous actor might take. Yes, exactly. So I still think that we made the right call for really taking a very risk-averse approach to that. But now I'm really excited about differentiated access and responsible deployment as really a core pillar of all of our safeguards work. And really understanding that there are different user segments. And I almost feel like the future we're going towards is something like models as professions, similar to how models have different personalities and sometimes you want to invoke the right one depending on the type of workflow you're looking at. So I think how this translates is similar to how biologists working on therapeutics and their research, they require access to datasets that are often very tightly controlled, or they require access to just expert-level—they all have PhDs and have expert-level biology knowledge. How does that translate over to models? I think that's why we have to kind of similarly take the same training approach but also the same security approach and deploying that in a way where we can have those very heightened enterprise-grade controls in place. So you just mentioned safeguards, can you explain how that applies here, where you would need them, and why you would need them? Yeah, so we very thoughtfully design new safeguards for pretty much all of our models across very different risk areas. But I think when it came to bio, this was the first dual-use risk that is both also a capability risk, so it very much correlates with how we as capabilities improve, the risk correlates. And I think that's why when our first approach—when there was no precedent for a lot of this work—we were the first to really activate these high safeguards when we saw that significant reasoning jump in our model capabilities. We really wanted to make sure that we get it right, and I think the best way to get it right is to incrementally deploy. Yeah, I think it's really a fine line between having a very capable model that's capable of accelerating benign science and beneficial science, versus a model that could be used by a bad actor. And I think the safest model here would be a model that just had no capability, right? And you... It's not very good. Yeah, it was not very good but it was very safe. And then on the other hand, if you had a model that is basically an oracle of the physical world—it basically knows everything about every experiment—that model could fall into the wrong hands and do potentially very bad things. Because someone can go and say, hey, design a new novel pandemic potential pathogen, and the model can just go and do that autonomously. So I think we need to kind of figure out where we draw the line in between the two and kind of think about who gets access to a potentially very capable model and who doesn't. And what we found in what we call general access traffic is that it's very difficult to figure out what a user's actual intentions are just from reading a prompt. And I think as an example of this, let's say someone says, hey, help me clone a gene. The model might not even be given what the gene is, but it can come up with a protocol for it. And so this gene could just be something like green fluorescent protein, or it could be a toxin, and there's basically no way to figure that out from the context of the conversation. And so this becomes a very difficult problem in production. And basically, I think like Yunyun said, we decided to kind of err on the side of safety here and basically say that, okay, if we think that there is a potential for misuse, we either have the model kind of soft refuse the user, in which case it tends to say things like, "Sorry, I can't really help you with that but I can give you a high-level overview of this protocol instead." And this unfortunately very, very much annoys our professional scientists, understandably. And then we also kind of have multiple layers of mitigation on top of that. But I think really to unlock the full capabilities of our models, we need this differentiated access. And what this means is we know who the user actually is—they are a professional working at a legitimate research institution or a pharma company.
