And because of the regulations around these institutions, we know that for example all the reagents are being tracked, all the cell lines that they're using are being tracked. And so this gives us confidence that this is a legitimate user and not a random person in the basement doing who knows what. And that allows us to give them basically more capabilities than we are able to provide to the general access traffic. What can you do right now if you are working with a model, you're working with it within a laboratory? What would you say the capability is at this moment? So I think people use the models for very different things. I've talked to people in the Baker Lab recently on how they've been using our models on Codex, and sometimes it's as simple as, hey, can you write a spreadsheet for me? I want to just minimize the number of pipetting steps that I have to make. And this hits me very hard because I had done this thing by hand in grad school. So that's like a very simple just mathematical software operation. And then there's much harder tasks, like can you design an enzyme for me with all these biological design tools? So I think there's a huge range of sophistication. Yeah, and something I'm very excited about is how we can use our models to be a more powerful discriminator in testing and assessing new novel ideas. And I think something that I've been noticing as a trend with a lot of our research partners and also the users of our models is that models for scientific research and tasks almost require a different persona or a different prompting style. So we often feel like a model that is much more scrutinizing or a skeptic at good ideas is very similar to how human scientists would go assess originality and feasibility. It's really helping understand, out of all the new papers and new publications out there that push the frontier of a lot of these hypotheses, what are the ones that are really feasible and valid for testing that's going to help lead to new breakthroughs? So and then translating this to something like disease target screening and selection—the potentials for these drug targets are endless, but it's really narrowing down that aperture and I feel like that's where the assistance comes. This is extremely difficult work to do at scale and having a model that can empower and accelerate that process, I think, is kind of like one of the immediate impacts we're hoping to see by responsibly deploying this model to those users. It seems like it's a very interesting trajectory. You went from GPT-3 on the API and GPT-3.5, then you get ChatGPT, and now we have ChatGPT apps, and now we have Codex, and it sounds like these things just—the number of things you can do with this continues to grow. How would you see this building? Do you see this as basically just becoming a complete infrastructure for every kind of inquiry you might want to pursue? Yeah, I think the dream is to have a lot of the basic foundations of scientific workflows happen on Codex. And I think the goal is to have Codex to pretty much be able to do everything that is possible to do on a computer. Of course, we also want to extend beyond that with hooking it up to robotics and so forth. But I think right now, we already do things, for example, if we have a bunch of different dev boxes on our remote on our laptop, we can actually say, "Hey Codex, go and run this code on all these different dev boxes that are all remote," and then Codex can do that. We can say, "Monitor this for me," and I can kind of go away and do something else, and the Codex is just like there watching all the logs for you. It can build a lot of fit-for-purpose software for analyzing specific pieces of data, for visualizing data. For example, if we have experimental biology data that we're sending each other on the team, what I've noticed recently is instead of sending the raw data, we've started sending HTML files or just these beautiful UIs that Codex has built with spinning proteins. It actually just changes the way that we share with each other and collaborate. Yeah, when we first started mapping out how users and organizations might adopt this, I think we envisioned that each scientist would get their personal assistant or their co-worker, and this is a way that they can help scale their collective output. And then the next paradigm of that would be scaling up whole research institutions where a whole program team can actually deploy a workforce of various agents and they can all do parallel task delegation, kind of mimicking a lot of these existing patterns, and we can figure out the pieces of how they can all collectively work together to solve larger tasks. It's interesting because OpenAI shared the need for compute. And I think that sometimes we just sort of think like, okay, so I can have more conversations and stuff. But when you're talking about the idea of building these tools to become entire platforms for scientific exploration, it sounds like the compute advantage is really critical. Yeah, I think there's two different axes we can think about how we are scaling compute. The one that I think everyone's familiar with is just getting bigger models. And I think as we went from, you know, GPT-2 to 3, there was a huge size increase and there were just these amazing emergent properties from the model. And we thinking about, you know, when GPT-2 was released, we were all kind of collectively amazed that it was able to write a coherent article about unicorns and now we're in a completely different world, right? And a lot of that is driven by model architecture, yes, but also just the number of parameters in the model just allows it to achieve this incredible intelligence that we never thought was possible before. And then on the other axis, we have what we call test-time compute scaling. And this is when you are inferencing the model, when it's kind of spitting out tokens. And this is a thing that happened fairly recently when we call these reasoning models—it can think for a scalable amount of time, and this is variable depending on how difficult it thinks the problem is. But we can have the model think for days, or really there's ways to just kind of have it think forever about a problem. And this allows it to kind of reach new levels of difficulty and discovery that we didn't think was even possible before. When we think about data centers, we often just sort of think about it as generating cat pictures or doing text conversations. But I think that's really the helpful framework to look at it—that these are going to be systems for doing extremely long-term, big, complex processes of thinking about this. And to me, it just makes a lot more sense when you look at projects like Stargate saying we're going to be building a lot of compute. It's not just for what we're doing now, but it's going to be for things like that. When we had first announced the team's formation on Slack, I think one of the taglines was to scale test-time compute to cure all disease. So that was like our team tagline. That's our team model. That's ambitious. Yeah. I had a friend whose child was born with one of those orphan diseases, and she would do fundraisers, everything she could to try to support some researchers who were trying to find a cure for this, but just not enough time, not enough people. And you know, I'm hopeful that we're kind of in an age now where these kinds of tools are going to make that maybe a thing of the past. Yeah, I think we're already seeing the model help a lot in these cases, from things like drug repurposing. For example, a drug that's already been cleared by the FDA for use in one different indication, but from mechanistic understandings of how that drug works, the model has suggested in many different cases that maybe you can use this drug to temporarily ameliorate symptoms. We're also seeing a lot of advances in personalized medicine, so for example the design of CAR T-cells or other RNA-based treatments is very common. And I think yeah, we are actually very, very close to being able to scale this up in a really vast way with AI. I think just in the next year or two, I think we'll see very big changes here. Every researcher I know, when you ask them what they could use in their lab, they always say more hands, more people, more people doing this kind of work. And you hear some people talk about, well, is AI going to displace that? And I think no, it sounds like it's just a big accelerator for all the things that could be done. Yeah, I completely agree. I feel like when you think about lab automation for example, a lot of the bottleneck comes from actually being able to translate a protocol into something that can be run on the platform. And we've had partners tell us about how Codex has been helping them do this. And this is kind of fundamentally: I have a coding problem, I have understanding how a wet lab works. And then I think thinking about the data analysis piece, I feel like having our models kind of walk through a user who maybe doesn't understand the deepest understanding of statistics—they can still rigorously analyze the data that's coming in. The model can kind of help them probe different hypotheses, or it can suggest different statistical tests. It can point out potential issues and biases in the data. I think these are all ways of kind of uplifting individual scientists and helping them do better science. But I don't think we can ever fully replace the scientist in the loop. So you've been putting in the lab, you've figured out how to help with automation. Where do you think we're going to be six months from now, 12 months from now? Well, I would really love to get to the point where we can say that AI has designed a new drug or cured a disease. I don't know if that can happen in six months, but I would hope in the next few years that's going to happen. I think we're seeing signs of this happening kind of all over different stages of the pipeline. I think obviously earlier in the drug discovery process where you're looking at literature synthesis or the model is discovering new biology—for that to become a new drug on the market is going to be a very long process, probably like a decade. But I think there's ways that we can really speed up this process by starting at maybe the clinical trial stage or starting a little bit before then in the safety reviews or in the drug design phase. So I think yeah, basically that's what I'm the most excited about coming up in the next few years. Yeah, for me, I think I'm most excited about all the possibilities that our users, our scientists, can do on our platforms. So for one, I think a huge win would be if a researcher can patent a new finding or a new discovery on our platform using our models. And that's why we really focused on early discovery and starting with teaching the model the mechanistic understanding. So this is again trying to provide the most powerful tools through our life sciences models to these scientists so they can really accelerate the speed of their research. Do you think we'll get to a point where the models are going to get really good at basically predicting the cell or predicting the outcome? I think definitely yes. I think it depends on the complexity of a system. For example, one thing our models are already very good at is predicting the outcome of a chemical reaction. And I think as you increase in biochemical and biological complexity, some of the hardest things to predict is: given a drug, will this be toxic to a specific person or to a specific system? And I want to slowly work our way up to that, but that is definitely on a roadmap and something we want to do eventually. When we're looking at models that do things like language or math, it's pretty easy to put together evals for it—did it get the problem right or get it wrong? What do evals look like for models that are doing biology? Yeah, we have various different ways of evaluating model performance. A really nice way to do this is kind of with experimental data. Someone has already done the experiment and then you ask the model: can it predict the outcome of these experiments? So a lot of the virtual cell work basically looks like this, right? Someone has done single-cell RNA seq on millions of different cells, and then you feed this to a model and then you try to get it to predict an unseen perturbation. We can also do a lot with synthetic data, and this means that maybe you have generated a set of data and you put very specific characteristics in this data that could be bug-ons for the model. These are things that maybe a typical computational biologist might encounter day-to-day. This could be some weird bias in the data, it could be some QC thing that you have to do, or statistical correction. And because we generated the data ourselves, then we can actually go and test the model's capability as a computational biologist—like, does it catch all these different mistakes? So there's a lot of different ways we can be creative with evaluation. But that being said, I think wet lab is still kind of the final, real evaluation of the model, right? And as we like to say, nothing in biology is really real until you can prove it in the real world. And so we do have a lot of research collaborations where we try to do just that. Yeah, evals have really become more complex and sophisticated over time, and I think that's especially true for designing evals that can really capture both value creation and solving complex problems for life sciences. So I think we really try to focus on examples that are not toy problems but really capture, for example, the messiness of pre-processed data. And when we design these new evaluations, a starting point is often just trying to recreate an existing experiment. So it's something that has already a baseline, so we already know what the current state of the art looks like or what the current ground truth looks like. An evaluation I'm really excited about is looking at if our models can assess antibody binding predictions and looking at how that's been done for existing virus variants. And then once we have already done that baseline, we can push forward and say: can we do this with something that hasn't been done before? And I think that is like some of the precursor steps to de novo antibody design, maybe expanding the neutralization for new viral variants. And that's also on the path to new treatments and potentially developing new vaccines. What has been the reception in the life sciences, particularly at conferences or in the community with people you know? Have you seen a lot of willingness to embrace this, or skepticism, or people who just don't think it's helpful? I think it probably depends on what part of the country you're in. I feel like kind of being on the West Coast, everyone is pretty AI-pilled and so they really embrace these AI scientist agentic workflows, and they really kind of see the future for AI. When I'm at a conference on the East Coast, this changes a lot. I think people are generally a bit more skeptical, maybe there's a little bit more doubt around the AI capabilities. And yeah, I think it's just maybe like a cultural difference. I think most of the big AI labs are here and so we kind of have a firsthand experience of what the models are capable of and this kind of changes our perspective a little bit. How do you bridge that gap? How do you get more scientists to understand? Because it sounds like the more people contributing the better, because there are weaknesses and areas that need to be improved upon, and the more you get people who are maybe skeptical to figure out how to participate... I think there's a few different ways. The easiest way is by launching our models through different platforms like Chat or Codex. And I think just by showing individual scientists how useful this could be—maybe it's just making a serial dilution spreadsheet for someone who's pipetting, but that has real value, right? And I think you can kind of build up from there. I think coming from the other end, we do have these more deep research collaborations with labs for, for example, antibody design or enzyme design. And these sort of things are more industry-vetted in publications and then people will read and say, "Okay, an AI system did a lot of work, it has biological novelty, it's been proven out in the wet lab." And so I think that also lends credibility to the system. Yeah, I think the simple answer is you show by doing and you show by publishing and engaging with the scientific community. And I think the skepticism is really healthy and should be welcomed. I think it's just really great to see people get really excited because they are trying to disprove it maybe, because the potential for this technology is so great if we get it right and if we can actually really leverage its full capability. So I feel like the carefulness about how do we actually make this work for real problems is very much warranted. But yeah, I think when we publish, that just also shows the need for more rigorous evaluations that represent these life science workflows and research problems. So people can look at an eval and say, "Yes, I feel like now I have a hundred different ideas for how I can implement this into my lab and solve some of the current bottlenecks I'm facing." I actually think there's a certain amount of stress I've encountered from people who are worried that AI is really powerful but they don't know how to use it the right way. And so there is this general feeling of: I need more AI in my workflow, in my life, but they don't know where AI should come in. And I think part of the product vision is to just make it so simple that it just works. So you can just go to something like Codex and say, "Hey, I want to do whatever I'm doing today," and then Codex can figure out all the different pieces—the multi-agent workflows, the tool calling, all of that. And so, yeah, basically you don't have to stress about how to get uplift from AI and it just happens naturally. We do see those step changes every time these models become smarter and understand users better—you get more utility because then people go, "I don't have to spend a lot of time trying to prompt it or figure out all the tricks to it." If you were talking to somebody who was considering getting into the life sciences, maybe a high school student right now, what advice would you give them? I feel like when I was in high school—so I did the USA Biology Olympiad back when I was a high school student. And I think out of all the different Olympiads, I think biology was seen as kind of the most memorization-heavy one versus math, right? Where math is kind of more test-time compute scaling, whereas biology is more about memory and retrieval. I think my hope is that with AI having kind of learned all the relationships between all the different research pieces, it can really uplift human creativity and just make the process less memorization and more kind of helping people connect different fields of research together and just kind of furthering the frontiers of what people are able to explore in biology. So yeah, I feel like my advice to a high school student would be that maybe you don't have to go and memorize all the biology books, you could just do more exploration with AI. I think you can definitely read papers and just ask questions, and I think you can do both deeper dives and broader overviews this way. And I think just the way of learning really changes. I found that when I was in the lab, there was like a real solo, individual aspect of doing biology research compared to, for example, when I went to my first CS hackathon and there was some excitement about the collaborative nature when we first built our app together. So I feel like that's really the future I hope to see for early adopters and students using our models, and maybe using it in the Codex runtime because there is a collaborative nature to it too. I think, for example, sending your scripts or sending your conversations. Or maybe one day we all have our own co-scientist or agent and we can deploy our agent to now work with a teammate in that way. I think there's just new interactions and new modalities for us. So I would just encourage students to adopt early and just to also pioneer their own path for how they would like to use it. For me personally, I always actually kind of felt like I got into the wet lab a little bit too early and, like we mentioned earlier, I did not enjoy pipetting. That's the theme here—nobody likes pipetting. Yeah, there's a lot of very intense manual tasks involved. And so I hope that, you know, when our AI models can connect with physical devices, we can just make a lot of the learning curve more fun for students so that they can kind of learn with the models and then kind of maximize their time with the really interesting interaction spaces. So I've been working with a student—I like to help students come with projects—and one of them is we've taken Codex and he's connected it to a greenhouse and basically using it to get photos back and to look at and to evaluate. And I think it's been fun to see how he's been taking both AI technology and then something traditional like a greenhouse and combining the two of them, and basically building up the skill set of learning how to use the two of them. When you talk to your peers, you talk to people who are running labs or running experiments, researchers, what advice do you have for them? Because the problem I've seen is that a lot of them go, "That's great, I just don't have the time." But ultimately what we're trying to do is save them time. So do you have any kind of quick advice that you give them or any ways you try to maybe inspire them? Most people that I know in academia use AI in two main ways that I've seen. One is to talk to AI about an existing research paper or something and just kind of make sure that you're understanding things right away or kind of fact-checking. And this is personally what I really like to use AI for because you can ask it really dumb questions and you don't feel any judgment. It's actually just really wonderful for learning. And then I think people use it a lot for analyzing experimental results. And I think this comes back to the statistics piece that I mentioned before, where sometimes you don't know what the right way to analyze your data is, or there's just so many different interdisciplinary fields that your data might touch on—something in chemistry or something in a random niche field like protein biology. And the really nice thing is that the model can pull those different ways of data analysis in for you and kind of explore all of these different paths. I feel like both of those are pretty low-lift ways to try things out. So you could just throw a PDF file at AI and just be like, "Hey, help me understand this paper," and just have a natural conversation. Or you can boot up Codex and do some data analysis directly on your laptop. Yeah, I would say that you'd have to start with making sure it doesn't feel like work right away. So maybe it'll be easier when you're focusing on AI adoption to just work on a hobby project or a passion project. For me, for example, I actually started working on more literature synthesis tasks when I was doing creative writing projects, which are kind of just something that was not at all related to our day-to-day even though biology is a very creative space. I was just exploring that through a different medium. And I think that's actually when I started unlocking a lot of different ways to either prompt the model or to actually access different data sources. So I think that gave me a lot of pattern matching abilities for when I was trying to apply it. Because we're not going to get it right in the first try, and it is really hard. I feel like the progress and pace of this field moves so fast that every week or month there is a new, pretty exciting development that might change how we engage with models or AI systems. So I think it's just important to get started somewhere and I think another theme is the collaboration element. I feel like it's more powerful when you have a recommendation from somebody on your direct team who is doing the same day-to-day tasks as you. That happens a lot on our team as well where somebody will say, "Oh, I got Codex to now touch these three different internal databases that we weren't able to connect before." And the latent capabilities are just so vast that there's a lot that we just don't know until we can do it. So I think just having conversations with your friends, your labmates, your teammates will spark a lot of those creative juices and then help you with your own adoption. What does science look like 10 years from now? I think when we started this team, we had really ambitious targets. And one of those is—I think we do want to make meaningful strides towards, or even assist with, curing a disease. And I think there's just so many rare orphan diseases that don't really have the attention and the resources that they warrant because it's just such a difficult field to actually... for example, clinical research is so difficult to actually bring to patients and to market. So while 10 years feels like it's a really long timeline, I'm really excited about some of the progress that we can make and I think it's good to be carefully optimistic that we're going to see some of those breakthroughs pretty soon. Yeah, I think maybe this is a bit of a sci-fi vision that I have of the world that I really hope becomes reality, which is that you have these autonomous labs. They are just mostly robots, and you have them all hooked up to AI, and you just have autonomous research institutes that are constantly running and curing human disease. It's maybe making new materials, making new drugs, or solving personalized medicine. There's a lot of n-of-one or just ultra-rare diseases where people without vast monetary and research scientific resources can even begin to think about solving. But we can solve that with AI. And I think we can kind of almost break through the financial and regulatory and monetary constraints with this system. And I think that's the dream. And I think also even separately thinking kind of more about the bio-security side of things... These systems can be kind of constantly sampling our environment, right? It can be sampling wastewater, it can be sampling the air, and constantly detecting potential threats, or even just better predictions for the flu and getting better flu vaccines. But just generally, these different medical countermeasures I think should be happening autonomously in 10 years, and I think that's basically something I'm really excited about. The AI lab is exciting because I think if people really understand what it means, it's not that there aren't scientists. It's that there are more scientists but they sit at home and they go into Codex and say, "Can you go run this experiment for me?" Like you have a data center, you have a science center doing that. Right, exactly. Yeah, and I think I didn't talk about the scientist as the individual I was describing, but obviously there are people involved in here. And I think it's really kind of high-level direction setting from the humans where you're saying, "Here's a patient with this disease, here are some potential solutions or things that maybe you can look at." And I think the AI can then go off and explore different ideas, it can design experiments and then come back to the humans and say, "Here's what I found, what do you think we should do next?" And this can be kind of an academic discussion. It's a little bit similar to the way that people interact with Codex today where you say, "Here, go write a function or go write a piece of code," and then it writes it and says, "Here's the code," and then the person tells it the next thing to do. So I think it's a little bit similar to that kind of interaction but on a much grander scale and on a much longer time horizon. I think it's really the democratizing science aspect and putting really capable expert-level knowledge in the hands of a greater amount of people. And I think what that can mean for personalized medicine, for bolstering our societal defenses... there are just so many naturally occurring new variants every year, new influenza strains. So I think it's really just securing defenses and feeling like we actually have more agency to counter all of that. And I think I'm really excited about a lot of the medical countermeasure acceleration work as well. Well, it's very exciting. Thank you for sharing this with us. Thank you for having us. Yeah, thank you so much.

Meet The $9 Billion AI Company Reimagining Vibe Coding

Forbes(2.1M) ·31:55 ·742 views · 2026-04-16
Amjad Masad (Cofounder and CEO, Replit)
Replit is spearheading the "vibe coding" revolution, leveraging AI to automate software creation through natural language and visual interfaces, effectively removing the technical barriers that once bottlenecked entrepreneurship and global economic opportunity.
TL;DR Replit is spearheading the "vibe coding" revolution, leveraging AI to automate software creation through natural language and visual interfaces, effectively removing the technical barriers that once bottlenecked entrepreneurship and global economic opportunity. ...

TL;DR

Replit is spearheading the "vibe coding" revolution, leveraging AI to automate software creation through natural language and visual interfaces, effectively removing the technical barriers that once bottlenecked entrepreneurship and global economic opportunity.

The Evolution of "Vibe Coding"

  • Definition of Vibe Coding: A shift from manual syntax to natural language expression. Users describe a business idea (e.g., an e-commerce site), and the AI handles design, database configuration, and cloud deployment.
      • Time Compression: The transition from idea to revenue-generating business has been reduced from months to hours.
  • Agent 4 and Replit Canvas: The latest iteration of the platform introduces a digital whiteboard interface.
      • Human-Centric Design: Masad argues that linear text is a "straw" that limits creativity; the Canvas allows users to doodle, iterate, and chat with specific designs in a non-linear way.
      • Broadened Scope: Beyond software, users are applying vibe coding to fashion design, art, marketing videos, and slide decks.
  • The Physical Therapy Use Case: Masad highlighted a therapist who used Replit to build an app that digitizes patient motion tracking via photos—scaling her domain expertise into a "physical therapist in your pocket."

Strategic Pivot and Market Positioning

  • The 2024 Pivot: After a difficult 2023 involving layoffs and a stagnant business model, Masad pivoted Replit to be "AI-first." He declared a single goal: making programming 10x easier by automating code entirely.
  • Competitive Differentiation: Masad distinguishes Replit from "thin wrappers" like Anthropic’s Claude Code.
      • Integrated Infrastructure: Replit provides a full-stack package, including deployment, maintenance, security, and database management, rather than just a terminal-based chat interface.
      • Top-Down Control: To combat security risks, Replit hardcodes standards for database access and infrastructure rather than leaving critical configurations entirely to AI agents.
  • Financial Discipline: Despite a $9 billion valuation, the company maintains a "frugal" underdog culture. Masad remains "paranoid" about survival, avoiding flashy marketing like Super Bowl ads in favor of product-led growth.

Global Expansion and "Opportunity Equalization"

  • Mission of Equal Opportunity: Drawing from his childhood in Jordan, Masad views the internet and AI as tools to decouple wealth creation from geographic or socioeconomic status.
  • Key International Markets:
      • Japan: A high-growth region where the community has already authored books on "Vibe Coding with Replit" and formed dedicated user groups.
      • India: Partnering with Razorpay to help Indian developers monetize their apps and expand into the enterprise sector.
      • Middle East: Despite delays due to regional instability, Replit is targeting Saudi Arabia and Jordan to integrate vibe coding into early childhood education.

Personal History and Advocacy

  • Founding Roots: Masad, the son of a Palestinian refugee, credits his father's "pure intuition" in buying an early computer on debt as the catalyst for his career.
  • The "Bond Villain" Moniker: Y Combinator co-founder Paul Graham jokingly compared Masad to a Bond villain because his vision of automated coding threatens the traditional "code artistry" Graham has championed for decades.
  • Social Advocacy: Masad has been vocal about the Palestinian struggle and the Nakba. He noted that as a visible Palestinian in tech, he felt an obligation to counter misinformation and defend Palestinian rights, despite initially losing friends and opportunities for speaking out.

Notable Quotes

"Vibe coding is no longer just about coding and making software. It’s really a new way of creating with computers." — Amjad Masad
"Paul Graham is a code artist... for him I was sort of like this Bond villain character because I’m telling him everything that he loved and dedicated his career to is no longer relevant." — Amjad Masad
"I think the people who are going to be able to reskill and enter this world with excitement and passion, they're going to see expanded opportunity as opposed to shrinking opportunity." — Amjad Masad
Amjad, thanks so much for joining us. Thanks for having me. So, for the viewers at home, tell me about Replit and what your software does. So Replit is a vibe coding platform. You go to Replit, you have an idea for a piece of software. You don't need to know how to code. You can just express that idea, just write it down, similar to how you'd be talking to someone about it. For example, I want to build an e-commerce site to sell dog food online, whatever it is. It could be a sentence long, could be a paragraph long, could be a whole business, you know, booklet about your business. And then the AI might ask you clarification questions. And then the first thing the AI will do will generate some designs for you, see what you like. And then once you're happy with the designs, you can build out the entire business. You can build out a website, a web app, a mobile app, everything with a database, with a cloud all hooked in. And once you're happy, you can just click publish and now you're online. We've heard about people going from an idea to a business to revenue in a matter of hours. And so that used to take weeks or months and now it's compressed down to hours. And so with your new agent, Agent 4, you're trying to change the way people vibe code. Can you talk about this digital canvas that you've released and what that means for how people visually interact with vibe coding? Yeah, so if we look at, you know, how vibe coding came to be. Back in the years pre-AI, called pre-2022, we used to type all the code manually. And then we had like the Copilot era, with GitHub's Copilot where it can auto-complete some piece of code. We also had a product there as well. But around 2023, 2024, we figured that we're headed to a world where coding becomes something that is almost exclusively, if not exclusively, an AI does. So 2024, we launched the first vibe coding tool and Replit stopped being about typing code and became about communicating your ideas in natural language. And the term "vibe coding" was coined in January 2025. Now AI continues to progress and we think the modality, how people actually want to interact with AI, will change over time. When you walk around our office, you're going to see people when they're working with each other, they're whiteboarding, you know, they're expressing themselves with their hands. There's so many ways humans can express ideas. And vibe coding has sort of improved a little bit over code, but you're still, you have a universe of ideas that you have to kind of almost pull through a straw into a linear piece of text. So we're like, okay, can we introduce a way to collaborate with the agent that is more human? And that's how the canvas came to be. It was really inspired by a whiteboard. So you have an infinite canvas, you can doodle on it, you can generate designs, you can chat with one particular design about it, you can right-click, generate a bunch of variations. And we're seeing so many interesting use cases for it. Some people are using it to design fashion, other people are using it to design pieces of art, and of course the core use case of making software. But also marketing people are using it to generate marketing videos or slide decks and what have you. So it really expands not just the interaction with AI, but what can be made with vibe coding. And that's a big shift that I don't think us as a company but the whole world has realized that vibe coding is no longer just about coding and making software. It's really a new way of creating with computers. What is the coolest piece of software that you've seen somebody make using Replit? Well the other day I was talking to a physical therapist and she always wanted to use software in order to make her work a lot better. And with Replit she made, they made this thing where you can visualize, you take a few pictures of your clients and you can visualize their entire body. You can look at the different trigger points, you can look at the different problems that they have. You can test their range of motion, you can track it over time. At home, they can track their own kind of range of motion. You know, I had shoulder surgery, I worked with her. And what we do is we do different exercises and over time we're seeing whether my range of motion is improving. And now all of that is digitized and she also can scale herself. Her, all the domain expertise she built over many, many years can be scaled out and anyone can now have a physical therapist in their pocket. So the impact is amazing, but also visually the software is so compelling, because you can see a digital scan of yourself simply by taking photos of yourself, which is pretty cool. And how are you using Replit, you know, outside of work in your personal life? I use it with my kids a lot. It's like one of our favorite Saturday morning type of thing. You know, one of my theories about what is special about humans, I think we're all kind of trying to answer this question of why is, you know, what is our role in the age of automation? And my belief is that it is that of creativity, is that of being generative and having great ideas. And so I try to create that in my kids. And so we sit around thinking of games to build, thinking of apps to build. And it gets pretty absurd. It was like skeletons on the moon fighting dinosaurs. And we have a lot of fun with it. And then I start introducing concepts in computers and networking and things like that at a young age. So that's one, a lot of family stuff. Productivity, you know, I build a lot of productivity tools, like hook into my Gmail account, analyze how much time I'm spending on this or that, find opportunities in the mountain of emails and inbound that I get. Finally, personal health. You know, I like the quantified self type of apps and wearables and things like that. And it's really hard to make use of all of that data, but with AI and with building apps on Replit, I can now kind of measure progress over my health. And speaking of family and kids, you know, we've spoken about your childhood. You grew up in Amman, Jordan. What was your childhood like? It was very interesting. Computers have always been a fascination. I was very lucky that my father decided to buy a computer out of pure intuition that computers were going to be something useful in the future, and he took out actually debts in order to buy a computer. He was a, you know, government engineer, he was actually a refugee from Palestine. And so the fact that he made this investment, you know, that was a huge privilege for us. So I was the first person to have a computer in my family, in my school, in my neighborhood. And I got immediately I understood the promise of programming, that you can think up ideas and you can make them. One of the first pieces of software that I made is to teach my younger brother math. So I created this, you know, fill in the blanks, which number, and if he gets the number right, he gets a clap, if not, he gets a boo. And it worked so well that my brother now is an engineer at Replit. So it was a lot of fun. One challenging aspect of it is, you know, my father like a lot of, you know, Palestinian families kind of focus on education, so they put us in really good schools. But there was like a bit of a difference in the income level. So a lot of my friends there, you know, had PlayStations and Xbox and Air Jordans, everything that I really liked, but we couldn't afford that sort of lifestyle. And so we were kind of in the middle between our home life and our school life that had like such a big difference in socioeconomic status. And so that also motivated me to do entrepreneurship, to make money. And so I started building businesses when I was 13 years old. You spoke about your background as an immigrant. What was it like, you know, growing up outside of Silicon Valley so far away and what did you bring from that perspective to Replit? A few things. One is the internet is a huge opportunity equalizer. You know, one of the I think core values of the West, the thing that really made me attracted to America is the idea that there's equal opportunity. Doesn't matter who you are, where you are, your skin color, whatever it is. At least those are the ideals, right? And I felt like the internet is a potential to realize these ideals. And my story of, you know, being an immigrant and coming to the United States and building this multi-billion dollar business, I thought that could be available for a lot more people. So that's always driven me not just with Replit, but my previous career, you know, for example, at Codecademy taught millions of people how to code, at Facebook I built open-source developer tools that made programming a lot easier. So there was always this excitement about what the world could look like if access to opportunity was really global. One of Replit's big breaks was when you met Paul Graham, the co-founder of Y Combinator. How did you guys meet? Well it was funny, I always wanted to meet him. One weird thing about my life is that a lot of people that I really want to meet I end up meeting, which is always interesting to think about. But I applied to YC three or four times, you know, 2015, 16, 17, and we never even got an interview. And one day I get a message from Sam Altman on Twitter and he asked me to go meet him. I remember one weird thing about it was like he asked me to go meet him in the Mission in San Francisco instead of Mountain View Y Combinator. I was like "what is this office?" He was like "this thing called OpenAI." And when I went there we were talking and then he flipped his computer and said, "look, this is an email from Paul Graham asking about you and wanting you to do YC." And so we decided to do YC primarily at that point where we're like we were able to survive so we didn't really need to do YC but we did YC because we wanted access to Paul Graham. Then when he visited from the UK, we went for a walk. That was the first time Haya, my co-founder, and I met Paul Graham. And one cool story there is we were walking and suddenly we saw him running. Like, what's going on? And then he paused and we ran after him like, what do you do? Do you run with him? And he was looking up and he's like, "Oh, this plane is XYZ, blah blah blah." And we're like looking at him. He's like, "Well, you know, my kids and I like to know what kind of plane it is." And so he's always a huge nerd even in our first interaction. But I think the really cool thing about Paul Graham and Jessica is they're really good people and they've been supportive through many ups and downs and it transformed from just an investor relationship to more of a friendship. Who else have you met that you wanted to meet? Mark Andreessen I wanted to meet. I wanted to meet Joe Rogan, I was able to get on the show with Joe Rogan. But yeah, the royal family in Jordan, there's a lot of people throughout my life I'd be like, it'd be cool to meet this person, and then a lot of it just happens by happenstance. Paul Graham told this story about you where he said you reminded him of a Bond villain. Can you tell us that story? Yeah, so when Paul Graham visits from the UK, because that's his primary residence now, he comes and does office hours with YC companies. I like to invite him to our home. He's like a great family guy, he always likes to be around kids. And so whenever we invite him, he spends 75% of his time playing with our kids. And then I can drag him to my home office in the back, we have like a small ADU where it's my office and Haya's office. And I like to show him demos. And I think July 2024, the company actually wasn't doing well. We had just done a layoff. I had just decided that coding was done and we had to pivot the entire company to become an AI-first company and that everyone will be coding using natural language. And I showed him this demo. I was like, "okay, enter an idea for an app." And I think he entered an idea for a calculator or some color thing, I forgot what it was, simple app. Spent some time generating some code and then the app popped up. And he was visibly surprised and he was kind of playing with it. He's like, "oh, that's exactly what I wanted." And his instinct immediately to go look at the code and he started critiquing the code. I'm like, "don't look at the code, that doesn't matter, it's like machine code right now." And so I was so excited about it and for him it was slightly, I'll say offensive, because Paul is a code artist. He always talked about Lisp and the beauty of code and for him I was sort of like this Bond villain character. I'm telling him everything that he loved and dedicated his career to is no longer relevant. And so he came up with this Bond villain analogy. You talked about this, you know, there's a point where Replit kind of hit the bottom and you had to really think about what the company was going to do next. Can you talk about that difficult time in the company and how you got out of that? Yeah, so I developed this thesis around the idea that anyone could become a programmer, that creating software is a way to create wealth, to lift people out of poverty, to equalize opportunity back in Amman, Jordan, in the late 2000s when I started building what would become Replit. And I thought initially the way to do it is to make it so that code is easier to run and code is easier to teach. And so I wrote the first open-source coding in the browser tool. And that became infrastructure for a lot of learning companies that had started in the US around 2011. There was this MOOC revolution, massive online courses, and one of them was Codecademy and so they recruited me, they built on top of the open-source software that I built, and they brought me to the US on an O1 visa. And that was a really cool experience. We started hearing these stories that we hear now every day at Replit, which is "oh, I learned to code and I was able to build this business, I was able to gain financial freedom and get rid of student debt," or what have you, like life-transformative things. But it wasn't happening at a large enough scale. When I started Replit in 2016, it was kind of a reboot of this idea, but we had to solve much harder problems. And even in the master, I wrote a, I was inspired by Elon's master plan, and I wrote about how AI could impact coding. So even back then I started thinking about that. But it took a lot longer than we expected. We had to solve a lot of infrastructure problems so that it's a production-ready, secure, scalable system. But coding was still a bottleneck. You still had to learn a little bit of coding and the reality is most people just don't have the time or energy or focus to be able to learn how to code. And so 2023, we had grown the company based on my expectations. I made a mistake, I expected that AI was going to move faster than it actually moved. For most people in the world, AI is moving plenty fast, for me it wasn't fast enough. And the business was not performing well. So we had to bear down a little bit and really go focus on and invent the future of programming. And so I stood up in front of the company actually in our All Hands over there and I said, "we have one goal, is to make programming 10x easier and the way to do it is to automate code." And so we spent that summer in 2024 leading up to that demo with Paul Graham and leading up to September 2024 when we showed the world that it is possible to code without knowing how to code. What is your moonshot goal with Replit? Well I think the thing that I get most excited about is creating those life-transformative events for everyone. Equalizing opportunity. Like, so there's so many ways that can be done. One is you're inside a big company, you want to gain fast promotion, you want to make a huge impact, you can use Replit to drive revenue, to drive efficiency. You talk to some of our customers like our customers at Zillow, there's like a product manager there, his name is Mike Messenger, who made a huge impact on the business bottom line and at some point he was sitting in board meetings. He became a huge champion of Replit and became very well known in the company as the first vibe coder. He's no longer bottlenecked by engineering capacity, he can actually deliver his ideas. So you can change people's careers. Entrepreneurs, we have entrepreneurs all over the world who are generating more revenue and creating more wealth than they ever thought they were creating. We have, for example, one of our earliest examples of success is a company called Magic School. Magic School was started by a teacher and now it's a half a billion dollar company and he started on Replit. And the second-order effect of that is amazing because he built tools for teachers and those teachers are having AI tools that are created by a domain expert. I think part of the problem in Silicon Valley for a long time is that we were building the software for the entire world. Like we're not teachers, yet we're building the tools for teachers. Now as the opportunity decentralized, those domain experts are building for people like them. And so not only is it creating the opportunity, but also creating better software for the world as well. AI coding is so hot right now. The Goliath is really Anthropic with Claude Code. How do you compete with them and other players and how do you differentiate yourself? I think on product. You know, Claude Code is a fantastic piece of software, but it's a thin wrapper around Claude. It's in the terminal, doesn't even have UI, you need to learn how to use a terminal in order to use it. Engineers love it. But when it comes to creative, entrepreneurial people that want more, that want all the support to be able to create software, Replit is bringing the entire package. We're giving you deployment, we're giving you scalability, we're giving you maintenance, we're giving you security, we're giving you collaboration, we're giving you this infinite canvas, we're giving you parallel tasks, all of those features. There's no other software in the world, there's no other vibe coding platform in the world that has the amount of tools that we created for our customers. There's this thinking I think in AI right now that all that matters is the model, that the model's going to eat everything, this AGI type of thinking. I don't agree with it and I think that people will always want good tools as opposed to just submit a prompt and random things happen. We're seeing so many security issues right now because, I'm sure you're following the Dell story, where we were actually surprised like a lot of our competitors they came out of nowhere or like SOC 2 compliant. It was like how did you do that? So you know this company is you know there's a lot of problems there, but also turns out there's a lot of insecurity as well with their databases and all that. A lot of vibe coding platforms they let the agent figure out how to configure a database, how to... With Replit there's a lot of control that we apply, there's a lot of standards, there's a lot of taste that is not up to the AI. We apply top-down control that we hardcode in terms of like where's the database accessible from and so on and so forth. And I think that will always be the case. You don't want to always just hand it off to the agent and have the agent do whatever it wants and that's the sort of AGI complete picture. We're not there yet and even when we're there, you still need companies like ours to give you the best standards. Shifting gears a little bit, Replit is now worth 9 billion. It's made you a billionaire. How does that scale affect the way you're running the company now and your kind of newfound visibility or becoming even more visible? What does this kind of new normal for the company do for you? You know it's hard for me not to think of myself as an underdog. And I think it is not always a positive thing. I think I need to appreciate our place and my place in the world. But I think having been haven't grown up in the circumstances that I've grown up. Replit being 10 years as an underdog and all that is just inbuilt in me to not take any of that for granted and to fight every day waking up fighting for our survival. And I do think it, I think maybe it's a little exaggerated, I don't think Replit will go to zero, there's a risk of that going to zero tomorrow or anything like that, but that's my mindset. And Andy Grove from Intel wrote a book called "Only the Paranoid Survive." I didn't read the book, I just read the title and understood. It's like only the paranoid survive. And that's how I operate naturally. So I don't wake up in the morning like "we're a 9 billion dollar company." I wake up in the morning just thinking how do we service our customers better? What is coming down the pipeline in AI that could disrupt us or could change our business fundamentally or could allow us to disrupt the industry going forward. So it doesn't really factor that much into my thinking. I will say one thing is we need to learn how to spend money. I think Replit has been so frugal for a long time that you know a lot of our competitors are spending so much on marketing. There's been Super Bowl ads and all of that stuff. To get me to you know put in 10 million dollars for a Super Bowl ad, it's going to be very difficult. But with the kind of capital that we have it's important for us to invest in our future growth and I think that's the next big challenge. You mentioned where you come from. You have spoken about Palestine and did so before a lot of others in tech were doing it. Why did you want to do that? Well I think I understand the Palestinian struggle from my family and how you know our family originally from Haifa now in Israel were expelled in the 1948 Nakba. Travelled around Lebanon and Syria eventually settled down in Jordan. I saw my father and my uncles build up literally bootstrap from scratch and I saw a lot of other Palestinian families struggle, continue to this day struggle, after that initial displacement. And so I understand the Palestinian struggle and so when the world woke up to what was happening in Gaza and what was happening now in the West Bank as well, there was initially a lot of misinformation, there was a lot of propaganda. A lot of our colleagues in tech were very, were putting out a lot of harmful information, some of them were actually being very racist and very, and they were like attacking Palestinians as a group. You know we have a sitting senator, congressmen right now Randy Fine that are saying there's no innocent Palestinians or all of them are terrorists. So the climate around America around just the Palestinian identity is so negative. And as one of the more visible Palestinians, I thought I had the obligation and the role to be able to say something about what I know as the Palestinian culture and mindset and talk about the good things but also defend the rights of Palestinians. Also in tech there's a lot of bearings on what's happening in the world. You know a lot of people sell software to the military and to other places and the kind of software we create could have impact on people's lives. And so it's important that people understand that sort of struggle. And when I started talking about that, like you said there wasn't many, only Paul Graham perhaps the other big person. And it's not there was nothing coordinated between me and Paul Graham, Paul Graham has been talking about the Palestinian struggle since you can find tweets since 2013. Like I said Paul Graham is fundamentally decent and awesome person. A lot of people who can see the truth can tell there's a problem there. And as Americans our tax dollars are going towards a lot of this injustice. So it's important for us to be able to be truthful about what's happening there. And it there was a cost to that initially, where I lost friends, lost opportunities, but I thought it was more important to be on the side of truth and justice. And now things have shifted. Even the people that stopped talking to me are now talking to me again and they're saying "well, I see your perspective now." So I think things are headed in the right direction. There's been like a new poll showing the sympathy to the Palestinian people in America has grown a lot since October 7th. But I'm glad I said something when it wasn't popular. And you know with your new funding round you told me earlier that a big part of it you want to be international expansion in the Middle East and in Asia. Can you talk about what you see there? Yeah, I mean it's sad and it's disappointing because I think it is a very it was on a very encouraging trend what was happening in the Gulf. Like the leaders in the Gulf and I hope you know we get back to peace as soon as possible but the leaders in the Gulf were really focused on AI, were focused on uplifting their populations. We were actually talking to the leaders in Saudi Arabia and partnering on how do you make it so that people get exposed to vibe coding as early as possible, maybe in childhood, in school. There was a lot of startup activities and so I was seeing the Middle East as a place for growth, as a place where we're going to have an impact at the ground level. And obviously with the war that's happening there that is like I was supposed to be there next month, we cancelled the plans to be there, I was supposed to do big tour in the Middle East. But I'm hoping things go back to normal so we can continue our investment there. That being said, we're still selling our software there, we still have people on the ground there. We have people in Saudi Arabia and Jordan and other places, so we still have clients we maintain our relationships with there. We're just watching and seeing when it comes back to stability so that we can continue our expansion. With regards to Asia things are going really well in Japan. Japan is a fantastic place, like our community there they're really great when they got excited about Replit, they wrote a book called "Vibe Coding with Replit." There's like user group meetups there. We're used by one of the top banks there and they're helping us expand in Japan. There's a lot of activity in Singapore, India. Our team was just at the AI summit in India and we're seeing a lot of expansion opportunity there on enterprise. Razorpay is a partner of ours and they're going to help us expand in the developer market there. We're going to help Indian developers also monetize their apps using Razorpay. And obviously there's also Europe, we have amazing amazing logos in Europe and we're going to be investing a little more there as well. If you have one message to give the Forbes audience what would it be? Well I think look, I think there's a lot of anxiety when it comes to AI, what is it going to do to the job market, what is it going to do to our lives. And I think the best way to get ahead of that is to start using the tools. And we try to make Replit as easy as possible to use, so like downloading it on your phone, there's we have like a little phone app you can play with and create an app. But try investing in those things. You know I was talking to your filmmaker earlier about what he thinks about the future for his job is going to be like. And he was talking about all the different AI tools he was using and playing with. And I think the people who are going to be able to reskill and enter this world with excitement and passion, they're going to see expanded opportunity as opposed to shrinking opportunity. So I think approaching it with optimism is the right way to go.

Why Claude Feels Different (And What That Means for AI) | The a16z Show

a16z(266.0K) ·33:20 ·4.3K views · 2026-04-16
Erik Torenberg (General Partner, a16z) Anish Acharya (General Partner, a16z) signüll (Tech Commentator)
The AI industry is transitioning from building "delivery vehicles" for data to architecting digital "personalities" and artisan experiences; to gain public trust, the sector must move beyond chatbots toward ambient interfaces and focus on driving massive deflation in healthcare and education.
TL;DR The AI industry is transitioning from building "delivery vehicles" for data to architecting digital "personalities" and artisan experiences; to gain public trust, the sector must move beyond chatbots toward ambient interfaces and focus on driving massive deflation in...

TL;DR

The AI industry is transitioning from building "delivery vehicles" for data to architecting digital "personalities" and artisan experiences; to gain public trust, the sector must move beyond chatbots toward ambient interfaces and focus on driving massive deflation in healthcare and education.

The Evolution of AI Model Personalities

  • Artisan vs. Utilitarian Models: signüll observes a distinct cultural difference between models. Claude is described as "artisan" and "premium," possessing a "soul" and less sycophancy, whereas other models often feel more robotic or utilitarian.
  • Architecture Shift: In the Web 2.0 era, founders built delivery vehicles for human-generated payloads (e.g., Twitter, Digg). In the AI era, the model *is* the payload.
      • Personality Development: We are now moving into the "upper echelon" of human mind operations—developing digital personalities, which is a significantly more complex technical and cultural challenge than previous tech cycles.
  • Human-AI Connection: The pursuit of connection is a core human desire. Because AI does not get tired and offers ease of access, AI-driven relationships (friendships and romantic) are expected to scale significantly over the next decade.

The Future of AI Interfaces and "Ambient" Intelligence

  • The "Stone Age" of Usage: Despite high adoption rates, most users are only performing basic tasks. A primary challenge for labs like OpenAI is making the models’ advanced capabilities accessible to the average person.
  • Ambient AI Layers: The next evolution will move away from the "chatbot" paradigm toward "ethereal entities" that weave into daily life.
      • Predictive Intelligence: Interfaces may become unnecessary if AI acts as an ambient layer. This mirrors the original goal of "Google Now"—predicting a user's needs before they search.
      • Learning through Friction: Erik Torenberg notes that high-level learning often occurs through real-time debate and "fights" on the internet, suggesting AI interfaces should facilitate active learning and debate rather than passive consumption.

Solving the AI Sentiment Crisis (NPS)

  • The Popularity Gap: Anish Acharya highlights that AI is currently less popular in the US than agencies like ICE. To win public "hearts and minds," the industry must make essential services cheaper through actual deflation, not just disinflation.
  • Targeting Administrative Bloat:
      • Healthcare: Approximately 45% of healthcare costs are administrative. AI can aggressively target revenue cycle management and back-office overhead to lower costs.
      • Education: Productivity gains in teaching and the reduction of administrator-to-student ratios could make education cheaper every year.
  • Collective Action vs. Intelligence: Acharya distinguishes between "intelligence-bound" problems (which AI can solve) and "collective action" problems (like housing), where the barrier is political will rather than technology.

New Economic Models and Founder Archetypes

  • Democratizing Equity: signüll proposes that public sentiment would improve if "normal people" could own equity stakes in private AI labs. The current trend of companies staying private longer concentrates wealth in Silicon Valley and fuels the perception of "resource hoarding."
  • Archetypes of Great Founders:
      • The Technical Builder: Figures like Dario Amodei or Sam Altman who "will" technology into existence.
      • The Consumer Philosopher: Figures like Ev Williams or Kevin Rose who treat technology as a canvas for cultural expression.
  • Founder Advice: Founders should focus on problem spaces they are genuinely passionate about rather than just looking for "an AI use case." The "spark" must originate in the human brain before it becomes a prompt.

Notable Quotes

"We're moving towards a world that's highly abundant in everything that humans might actually need... The number one way you change the NPS of AI is you make important things cheap quickly." — Anish Acharya
"Ten years ago, if you said we were going to build personalities for computers, you would have been like, 'Wait, what?' Now, we're designing this upper echelon of how a human personality works." — signüll
"A lot of our problems in society—are they actually intelligence-bound or are they collective action problems? Housing has nothing to do with intelligence; it's entirely collective action." — Anish Acharya
"The real prompt is in your brain... without the spark in your existence and your inner self, nothing would happen." — signüll
It's funny how on the internet now, everybody can comment on everything. Every technology cycle to me is increasingly harder because you're probably going into a different part of how the human mind operates. Right now, we're like developing personality. That's insane. There's technology, there's culture which is collective, and then there's sort of our individual progress as a human species. Culture is changing, technology is improving. Where are we as people? I can't believe the scale at which we're at now. Like, it's absolutely unbelievable. I was at OpenAI, and we were discussing a bunch of things around how do you think about personality development of models. These are really technically hard problems. I think the number one challenge even OpenAI mentioned is: how do we make the power of the models more easily accessible and useful in terms of what they can do? I think this is happening with agents, but it still seems very primitive and very inaccessible to a lot of individuals. I think that the number one way you change the NPS of AI is you make important things cheap quickly. Here live with Signal, the great culture commentator of our time. You have opinions on everything from what's happening in AI, both as a consumer but also the industry, to what's happening in all the big tech companies, to what's happened in dating markets more broadly, to how the products should be developed. There's no commentator like you. There's no sort of... how do you sort of make sense of yourself on the internet in terms of how you thread these topics together? What sort of threads it all? I mean, I could have an opinion on Iran and dating at the same time. Within minutes of each other. Maybe even Iranian dating. Like, you're like, "By the way, how does it work there? Are they using Tinder?" Like, I don't know. I mean, there's probably several jokes about terror about love bombing and stuff. Oh my God. Sorry. All right. I apologize. Yeah, don't apologize. Okay. I have this weird tendency to where I like to add humor into things that are maybe not appropriate. Anyway, I think, look... It's funny how on the internet now, everybody can comment on everything. Yeah, there's a lot of people that have a great perspective in a singular dimension. You've got Ben Thompson writing about technology and markets, and if you want to have an analysis of an earnings report of Microsoft, I mean, who better in the world than Ben Thompson? I mean, you got Packy who writes about deep tech and crazy, I think 40-page papers about industries and things. They are really fun to read, really great things. What I notice is like, I've been in technology for such a long time since I was a kid and I'm particularly fascinated with culture in general as well. I think that intersection of tech and culture is so fascinating to me. And I like to relate everything back to computer science and how I've learned about the world through computer science and computers in general. I think all my tweets and things are really, in essence, relating the idea of life to technology and culture to technology in maybe an interesting way. This is how my mind works. I think my writing is all a reflection of the prompts that happen in my brain that get translated somehow into words that are in the right order, so that other people can interpret and therefore have a reaction to them. Maybe it generates a little bit of hate online or a little bit of love, and I think that's actually quite beautiful that we're all able to do that. That's the most poetic thing I've ever heard. I do have a Shakespeare profile photo. Exactly. And, you know, I think that's been great. I grew up playing a game called SimCity, and in SimCity you can increase the simulation speeds. There's a button that allows you to increase the simulation of the city, and the cars move faster and the people move faster and the disasters happen faster and everything is just increased. And I feel like in the recent maybe... obviously in the last 20 years with respect to iPhone and whatnot, but man, in the last two, three, four years, holy shit. Who the hell hit the 100x speed? Like it feels ridiculous. Like if I talk to somebody about something that happened last month, it feels like it happened like 10 years ago. Like Nicolas Maduro got pulled from Venezuela and brought to America in the most amazing outfit. And people forgot about this. It's just incredibly fascinating the way that the world is moving so fast and technology is accelerating that. I mean, if it's the fuel that is empowering that engine, then I don't know what we did, but it's moving really, really fast. And then do you think if I was to almost separate the three ideas... there's technology, there's culture which is collective, and then there's sort of however you measure our individual progress as a human species. Culture's changing, technology's improving. Where are we as people? Are we more spiritually mature, less spiritually mature than we were 50 years ago, 500 years ago, 1,000 years ago? Or are we just basically, you know, Neanderthals with iPhones? I think generally, technology should help us understand ourselves in a better way such that we are able to have intellectual, spiritual potential growth as a species as a collective and the individual, I suspect. I firmly believe that I'm like pro-technology helping achieve that. I love using AI to be able to understand myself. Like, if I say something, it's like, "Wait, am I... does this make sense? Is this more interesting? How do I personally think about it? What am I missing?" Those are really things that help me grow intellectually, spiritually, personally, in relationships, all of those things. So inherently, I mean, if I understand your question correctly, it's more like these things are really helping us experience ourselves much better. And I think that probably is the greatest achievement you can possibly have. I mean, we're tool builders and every tool that we've ever built has helped us progress as a human species or individually, whether it's art or the wheel or whatever. And I can't believe the scale at which we're at now, right? Like it's absolutely unbelievable, and I think what's shocking to me is that the collective has not caught on yet. Do you think the norms around AI relationships with boyfriend, girlfriend, or just best friendships are going to be something in the next five to ten years that are extreme to a degree that seems unimaginable right now for the average person? When you have ease of access and reward structures around it, and a human desire paired with it that's deep, I think you get some interesting outcomes. The desire and the pursuit of connection is an incredible and important element of any human's existence, and the idea that AI can help you facilitate that with depth and scale and ever-ending... like AI doesn't get tired. What else is surprising you most about how people are interacting with AI, particularly the different models? What are you observing as you're looking at the landscape? Well, first of all, I don't think most people are utilizing them for anything beyond the basics. It's fascinating to me that every single time we're focused on really advanced capability demonstration. Like holy crap, this is a PhD researcher or whatever. Yet I don't think most people are utilizing it in such a fashion. I think most people are utilizing it for very, very basic tasks. And so I think we're in like the Stone Ages of how people view and perceive and use these things, even though there's a billion people utilizing them, but they're not utilizing them to the full capabilities. I think the number one challenge that even OpenAI mentioned is: how do we make this stuff, the power of the models, more easily accessible and useful for individuals? I don't know how that will be or what that will look like, but it's certainly something that I love to think about personally. I don't know where we'll end up, but I view it more as an art than a science at the moment. But it's cool. It's a fun time to exist as a technologist. A lot of people are wondering what to even work on in the age of sort of the big labs. Like what to start, how to think about what could be a real company versus something that they'll end up doing, or what's just worth doing? How have you thought about it or how do you advise people to think about that? A lot of AI today is very much the big labs kind of dominating consumer territory, whether it's OpenAI and Anthropic. And then tons and tons of people trying to find a business use case for it in various verticals. It's interesting. I personally focus on what I am passionate about. Like I was at a demo day. There's a lot of individuals who I thought clearly found the idea through AI for what to work on. And it's fascinating, I was like, "Are you really interested in real estate?" Like I don't know. "Do you want to spend your time working on this and is this an interesting problem?" I don't think about it from a technology perspective, like screw AI. I don't care about... like what area are you interested in? What thing drives you? If I were an investor, I think that's probably the only thing that matters is: are you going to keep going into this problem space if you're not that interested? Look, we're having a lot of fun doing this and I think if you're not having a lot of fun doing stuff you probably shouldn't work on it. It's a privileged thing, obviously, and often a lot of people don't find work fun and they have to do it because it's economically necessary. But if you're going to build a company, try to have fun with it. There's a great quote in the Bhagavad Gita which I read recently again and it was like, "You know, you're not entitled to the fruits of your labor." I never think about the outcomes. I just try to figure out what I enjoy, how much fun I like to have, and the problems that I like thinking about. Like all this account that I have is just pure fun. I really enjoy talking and thinking about this and my brain prompts me to think about it. We were talking about how the real prompt is in your brain. That is where it originates and then you're translating that into some text that you're sending into AI, where we call that a prompt. But without the spark in your existence and your inner self, nothing would happen. There's some Rick Rubin shit, man. It's true! Like it's fucking so fucking true. Wish you'd get it together, Rick. This is why I think Rick Rubin... people made fun of him. No, he's amazing. He's incredible. And he's incredible because he was ahead of the curve. Oh, totally. And I think music is such a... I know Anish, you're a giant DJ and music person, but it's such a... you have to feel it. You have to feel it. And there's such an important element to it. I think when you're doing anything new, building a company, you have to feel it. So okay, I have a question for you. There's certain two different archetypes of great consumer founders. I'd say there's the modern archetype, somebody like Boris who's working on Claude Code, or of course Dario, or Sam and some of his co-founders who are so extraordinarily technical, they're like willing these things into existence that were unimaginable five years ago. And that's great. And ChatGPT is the fastest product to what I think is a billion users, etc., etc. There's another archetype from the Web 2.0 days, right? These are like the gentle sort of consumer philosophers whose canvas was technology. Think Ev, think Kevin Rose. All these people were just cut from a different cloth and they were perhaps more students of culture than technology. You've got two very different forms of types of companies. I don't quite know where I'd put Zuck, but let's set him aside for a moment. Do you think that there's a preferred model? Is the gentle builder more of a New York informed model and the technical builder is more of an SF informed model? Is it just something that matches with the product cycle? And then maybe talk a bit about what you think your strengths are and how it meets the moment. I think of most people as one type of artist or another. And they have brush strokes that they use. You know, I was at Monet and I loved going in and looking at the actual brush strokes of the painting. Then you get these like pixel level understanding of like, "Wow, he used this color for this brush stroke." Then you zoom out and you're like, "Oh my God, I see this wonderful little painting" and I'm deeply inspired by it. So it's just like I think people are utilizing different styles and different forms of a different type of... but it is just a sort of a canvas like the painting. Some people like hard-edge paintings and those Renaissance styles, and some people like modern art, and some people like the water lilies of Claude. I think just a different form. At least all the initial versions of it, and going back to the Web 2.0 era with Kevin Rose... it was a very different time. Building network products like that, whether it's Digg or Twitter, is fundamentally different than developing personalities of a model.
