Like holy crap, I was at OpenAI and we were discussing a bunch of things around how do you think about personality development of models, and the fact that you can't really easily change them, or how do you reduce the sycophancy of the models. These are really technically hard problems. I think every technology cycle to me is increasingly harder because you're probably going into a different part of how the human mind or the human body operates. Right now, we're like developing personality. That's insane. If you asked 10 years ago if we were going to build personalities for computers, you would have kind of been like, "Wait, what? What does that really mean?" And these guys with Kevin and Jack and whatnot, they were architecting I think delivery vehicles in some sense, right? They were developing architecture for humans to add payload and then send it to another human. Whether it's broadcast or one-to-one, Digg was the news and people posted and the comments, and Twitter was like another version of that. Now I think we're kind of designing this upper echelon of how a human personality works and how intelligence works. I think that's a grand... it no longer feels like a delivery vehicle, it feels like the actual thing and the payload. I don't know if that makes sense, but this is how my mind works. I don't know if you guys think about it that way, but in some sense, we've moved uplevel a lot and the complexity has increased drastically. Training a model and then reinforcement learning through human feedback... what's interesting is there's like multiple different types of ways that things engage. Talk a little bit more about what you've observed in terms of the personality differences between models or what you think particularly makes Claude so interesting. I think one of the things that they've focused on, going back to the Rick Rubin point, is that it feels artisan. It feels like it's got a soul. Whereas I think in some sense the other models feel a little bit more robotic and a little bit more utilitarian, if you will. If you think about what AGI is... there's a great Simpsons episode where Bart sells his soul for five dollars to Milhouse. It was one of the most profound, interesting episodes of The Simpsons. Okay, well, he wrote Bart's soul on a piece of paper and then handed it to him. And then he felt it. Like he felt like he didn't have a soul. I found that really interesting because it sort of explores the idea of what is a human and what are we doing here? How does technology help us in terms of understanding ourselves and the way that we exist? I felt like there's less sycophancy; there's this pushback. It's like talking to a real human being. It's personified. Like, it's called Claude. Claude is known as a person, so it feels very crafted, artisan, slash dare I say premium to a certain extent. And I think it's been really fun. One of my sisters is a doctor, and she randomly just... she was using ChatGPT for a few years and she canceled and she's like, "I'm using Claude now and I got a weird..." I was like, "What? How did you find out about this?" It's nuts! I think it just goes to show you the proliferation and the marketing and the storytelling of Claude has been aesthetically really next level. It's been really, really fun to watch. I like good products, I like talking about good products, and I like praising the people who make good products. That's what we're about here, right? Like, give credit where credit's due. They've made a beautiful little tool. When paired with a thing in your pocket that is also crafted and artisan, with Apple and iPhone, then you get this really magical intelligent experience on your device wherever you are. How do you think about what these product experiences might look like in a couple of years? How do you see the interface evolving? I think the most interesting to me is they seem like they are in a very infant state in some sense. I don't know. I mean, they're really powerful in one end of the dimension, but it's unclear to me. Experiencing intelligence through just conversation back and forth is one way, but the ambient layers are really fun and interesting to think about. We were building a fun little product that woke you up with AI, right? And it's like a very primitive thing. Everybody wakes up in the morning, God forbid otherwise. But how is it going to weave into your daily existence as if it's not a chatbot, but more as a sort of ethereal entity that exists? Obviously, movies have personified this and there's been "Her" and whatnot, but it is going to be fascinating to see how it weaves into your daily life, whether it's your home or work, and in a very ambient state. I know it's not about listening to you all the time potentially, but I think there's a lot of these explorations that have yet to be done at the interface layers. How does AI talk to you first? Today that's a push notification, roughly. Is that it? I don't know. How does Apple integrate AI into iOS and weave it into the operating system? How do we use specific types of things? Are they even necessary anymore? Do we even need an interface if we're just talking to it? I think those are really interesting questions. I personally like the ambient AI layer. I think you're seeing a little bit of this with Open Claude and whatnot, and sort of working agents in the background surfacing the right things at the right time. There's a great product a while ago that didn't work called Google Now. The whole purpose of Google Now was to kind of predict a search, right? It's like, "What are you going to search for next, Eric?" and it was ahead of its time in some sense. But when you marry it with context and intelligence, I think that is actually a huge vector for how to think about the future of AI and how the stuff will weave into our lives. I don't think there's going to be a single person who doesn't use this stuff. It's just a matter of when. On that note, just a quick story. I remember asking Balaji, I was like, "Balaji, how do you know so much about crypto and economics and bio and math and science? Give me all the books you read." He's like, "Books? I don't read books. I just get in fights with people on the internet and that's how I internalize all the information." It's real-time. He learns what he needs to know to win the argument. And he really remembers it. Conceptually, those are really fun ways to have these discussions. I mean, obviously, some of it's not coaching to possibly say or do out loud, but I think that's actually really cool. Learning from other people is what we do best. Monkeys or apes watch other people and they use tools because they learn how to use them. That's wonderful. Imagine if I'm using a tool wrong, like hammering it backwards, and somebody's like, "No, you're an idiot. This is how you use it." Wonderful. Now I've benefited. Maybe that other person got a dopamine hit because they proved me wrong. And I think in the end we all win. It's great. And I think that's how I treat my account in some ways. It's almost as if I'm not really trying to gain anything, it's just that I don't have anything to lose. What do I lose by being wrong? So I have a question for you. There's this study that came out a few weeks ago that generated a bunch of conversation, which is that in China AI is highly popular, and in the US AI is very unpopular. In fact, it's even less popular than ICE right now. The NPS of AI is not great in this country. How would you fix that? I always think about movements, right? When people create movements, all the movements are rooted in simple storytelling. In some ways we are in a fear-driven development. There's a lot of fear that's being generated as a result of this, and I think there's a positive framing to all this. Look, I think we're moving towards a world where hopefully there's highly abundant elements of everything. Right now, we all feel like we're fighting for resources, whether it's capital, labor, or whatever. People think of the world as a finite amount of things. In Silicon Valley, we have this classic thing where it's like "grow the pie." Positive sum. Everything is positive sum. Normal people don't really think about that. They don't really think about growing the pie. I'd love to get your perspective on this. Are we in some sense primitive in that we are competing for resources and finite things? But I think the framing has to be around like, hopefully, if we do our jobs well, we're moving towards a world that's highly abundant in everything that humans might actually need. I think that the number one way you change the NPS of AI is you make important things cheap quickly. Like, soon, okay? And we've all seen the famous chart that Marc's tweeted a thousand times, which is the diffusion of products' prices on a per product category basis. This is the famous one where it's 1970, everything is essentially referenced to that date, and then certain things get more expensive and certain things get cheaper. The number one thing that gets cheaper is flat-screen TVs. Flat-screen TVs are asymptoting to essentially zero dollars. The things that are getting expensive are healthcare, education, and housing. There's actually a little bit of math I did a few months ago to show how you can make education and healthcare cheaper with AI very, very quickly. And by cheaper I don't mean disinflation, I mean actual deflation. Cheaper than it was last year, okay? Education's actually the easiest one. If you restore student-administrator ratios to what they were 10 years ago and you make professors modestly more productive, then you can actually just have education and school getting cheaper every year. The explosion of administrators—not professors, not teachers, but administrators—is totally undiscussed and it's insane. So you can make education cheaper. We could do it right away. We already have all the technology, we just have to make a different set of choices. For healthcare, 45% of healthcare cost is administration. It's all this overhead. We've all done it: the revenue cycle management, all the back-office stuff, all the nurses phoning you to tell you what drugs to take the night before you get a procedure. All of that stuff is overhead and all that adds to cost. If you can take a bunch of the cost out of that with models... and by the way, the number one consumer of OpenAI models by category are healthcare companies and healthcare startups. You can make healthcare cheaper year over year. So I think we should set our moonshot as an industry to make these two things way cheaper in the next five years. Would you subsidize it? As in, should the model companies give it away for free to these industries? Maybe. I don't know how you subsidize. At cost, maybe. But it's very interesting because Marc Andreessen said this a while ago which really got me thinking. He's like, "How many of our problems in society are actually intelligence-bound versus being collective action problems?" That's why the third category I mentioned was housing. Housing has nothing to do with intelligence or technology. It's entirely collective action. We could just build skyscrapers in Marin tomorrow and it would be abundant cheap housing for everybody, but we have to decide to do that together. I wonder if giving stuff away or making it free... I wonder if people will realize it. Generally, the world has gotten cheaper and cheaper and cheaper. You can go to Walmart and buy a hairdryer for five bucks. That's ridiculous. I tweeted about this. The billionaires drink the same Coke as you are. They're using the same goddamn iPhone. They're using Claude and ChatGPT just like you are. The underlying essence of equality or access is incredibly similar. But they don't have the same healthcare you have, and we should fix that. That's true. If you get sick as a billionaire versus a normal person, what's the delta? Dude, you'd be surprised. Look at New York state right now, your beloved New York City and New York state. In New York state, they're about to make it illegal at the state level to give or receive health advice or financial advice via a model. Like, how fucked up is that? So what does that mean? People who have lawyers and doctors already are going to be unaffected, and people who use the models for lawyers and doctors are once again set back enormously. How can we be okay with that? So a lot of these are own goals. It's crazy. And if you look, the state of Massachusetts made it illegal to buy Apple stock because it was too speculative when Apple was going public. This is like the early '80s. The home of Elizabeth Warren. It's like, "We must protect the consumers from these enormous financial gains." The number of ridiculous things that are done in the name of protection is a fundamental underestimation of the average consumer. I think people are pretty smart, pretty savvy. They'll figure things out. If you don't prevent them from accessing the tools, they'll use those tools to make their lives better. I have a weird idea. Tell me. The fact that we don't allow normal people to have equity shares or stakes in OpenAI and Claude is insane. Imagine if normal people were like, "I own a piece of these things." Maybe that would feel much better and they would have this ownership mentality. Right now, all this concentration is happening in Silicon Valley in a few people. Give people access to create a sense of ownership earlier. Imagine if a billion people had stock in OpenAI in some way, shape, or form. Maybe that's a dumb idea, but would they be more bought in on AI? Would they have a positive view of AI? Or what if their kids did? What if you rolled it into their accounts? And it's like, "Look, I have my job and I think I'll be okay, and now I know that my kids will be okay too." Yeah, they have a stake in the future. Quite literally. You can market it that way. That to me has been a very weird development where I do think people perceive tech individuals as hoarding or concentrating resources and wealth. For example, we're all privileged in technology and it's wonderful. A lot of us are exposed to this whether it's via equity or whatnot or even just usage. But I think there's potentially a perception with all the power law outcomes that happen that there is a concentration or a hoarding. And that creates a weird dynamic. Like, "I'm going to get left behind while the guys in San Francisco are going to be enormously wealthy." And that is probably one... I've heard people feeling this way. Especially, there's potentially a negative sentiment in technology and the NPS score probably reflects that. That is something to be fixed and ownership might fix that. I think the power law dynamic that Peter introduced is really fascinating to me because it was like business outcomes, and the internet drives power law outcomes in a variety of other scenarios as well, not just business. And I think people are starting to catch up and people are seeing this sort of wealth discrepancy that exists. Technology is a crazy accelerator. Like we talked about this right at the beginning, this entity is accelerating returns. I think that's maybe something to look at. I don't know if it is the right answer, but I did see that tweet about the concentration of the inaccessible private companies. They are staying private longer. What does that really mean? That's crazy. Maybe we should have a law that says you have to go public at some point. Well, in some sort, we do have that with the way RSUs are structured and things like that. Anything you want to tease? Oh, that's a great question. We are building a fun little consumer product and I'm excited to kind of storytell on this. It's like a little bit different. I mean, we're three people having fun building a fun little interface for consumer AI and what we think might be really interesting for average, normal people to experience and use. And it works out of the box. So I'm very excited for that. That's been really fun. That's what we've been up to. One of the fundamental things is that I just don't want to talk the talk; I want to walk the walk. And I'm excited to be able to share what we're up to. I mean, it's small, it's fun, it's interesting, and we're gonna see how well it lands.

Netflix Just Keeps Printing Money, Gerber Says

Bloomberg Television(3.1M) ·10:19 ·9.9K views · 2026-04-16
Ross Gerber (Co-founder, President and CEO, Gerber Kawasaki)
Despite a post-earnings stock dip caused by conservative guidance, Netflix has cemented its status as a global entertainment monopoly with massive free cash flow and a weakened competitive field.
TL;DR Despite a post-earnings stock dip caused by conservative guidance, Netflix has cemented its status as a global entertainment monopoly with massive free cash flow and a weakened competitive field. Conservative Guidance: Gerber suggests Netflix is using the "Apple playbook"...

TL;DR

Despite a post-earnings stock dip caused by conservative guidance, Netflix has cemented its status as a global entertainment monopoly with massive free cash flow and a weakened competitive field.

Financial Performance & Market Strategy

  • Conservative Guidance: Gerber suggests Netflix is using the "Apple playbook" by keeping expectations low to avoid missing targets, despite "phenomenal" underlying numbers.
  • Cash Flow Management:
      • "Printing Money": The company maintains high free cash flow, bolstered by a $2.8 billion breakup fee related to industry merger activities.
      • Shareholder Returns: Gerber advocates for aggressive stock buybacks, noting the stock remains relatively cheap compared to its historical valuation.
  • Investment Strategy: Netflix is prioritizing reinvestment into the business over risky acquisitions, maintaining a lean balance sheet compared to debt-laden competitors.

Competitive Landscape: The "Masterful Gambit"

  • Weakened Rivals: By avoiding a merger with Paramount or Warner Brothers, Netflix allowed competitors to "stick themselves with a huge ball of debt."
      • Debt Burdens: Competitors are now facing high interest rates and inevitable layoffs, making them weaker and less able to compete on content production.
  • Market Consolidation: Gerber defines the current industry as a "two-horse game" between Netflix and Disney-Hulu.
  • The "Global Cable Company": Netflix has effectively become the primary utility for entertainment, acting as the modern equivalent of a global cable provider.

Leadership & Industry Dominance

  • Leadership Transition: With Reed Hastings exiting the board (potentially to pursue political ambitions), the company is now firmly under the control of Ted Sarandos and Greg Peters.
  • The "King of Hollywood": Gerber describes Co-CEO Ted Sarandos as having a level of dominance over the creative community not seen in a century.
  • Licensing Strategy: Netflix is successfully monetizing its competitors' content (e.g., licensing films like *Anaconda* or Warner Bros. titles), proving that rival content often performs better on Netflix than on the rivals' own platforms.

Future Growth Levers & Risk Profile

  • Video Gaming: Netflix is successfully attracting younger demographics to its gaming platform; the next major step is successfully monetizing its own intellectual property within this space.
  • Sports Strategy: The company aims to increase its sports presence without overpaying for traditional "ridiculous" broadcast rights.
  • Primary Risks:
      • Complacency: The main threat is "getting lazy" and lowering standards for content quality.
      • Market Saturation: While they have a "monopoly" position, they must continue to grind to maintain viewership among younger generations who only watch Netflix and YouTube.
"Netflix is in a phenomenal position, they continue to just basically print money. They have a ton of free cash flow to reinvest into the business or to distribute to shareholders." — Ross Gerber
"Ted [Sarandos] is the king of Hollywood in a way that we haven't seen since a hundred years ago... people know if they want to get a project done they should probably call Ted first." — Ross Gerber
"The way we look at Hollywood now is it's a two-horse game between Disney-Hulu and Netflix and that's basically it because everything else is either cut up or falling apart." — Ross Gerber
"Business is sort of like a contact sport... and Netflix is now a monopoly and that's where you want to be." — Ross Gerber
Take a look at shares after hours, down nearly 9%. A lot of disappointment clearly when it comes to the to the guide. Just to set the scene here, I'd love to hear your reaction. Well, nobody likes to guide high anymore because it doesn't make any economic sense for any CEO to be overly optimistic. So I think that they're playing the Apple playbook, which is keep expectations low. But I think these are great numbers; there's no parsing about it. I think what people wanted was even better, you know. They didn't really up their guidance for the year or other things that I think people were hoping for, and so we see a little pullback in the stock. But Netflix is in a phenomenal position; they continue to just basically print money. They have a ton of free cash flow to reinvest into the business or to, you know, distribute to shareholders one way or another. So this is just a core holding for us and I think they pulled a masterful gambit with the way they handled Paramount and Warner Brothers. So they're now the top player in the entertainment industry and I think that'll be forever. Well, you know, you mention what happened with Warner's, and we did see them boost their full year free cash flow out out outlook. A lot of that has to do to that breakup fee that they got of $2.8 billion dollars. I mean, as an investor in the stock, how would you like to see them sort of spend that breakup fee? Where would you like to see the money go to? Well, you know, after hours they could just buy back stock, you know. I think that the stock is actually relatively cheap compared to Netflix's historic valuation and I think the market price hasn't really fully recovered from all of the merger stuff. And so, you know, as an investor, returning capital to shareholders—that's what they should do. If they can't invest it in the business, they return it to shareholders. And Netflix is pretty aggressive about doing that. So this is a great shareholder-friendly company and that's why we like it. Yeah, and they point out too that cash flow, that big cash flow number, also accounts for the fact that they paused that buyback program. On the Warner Brothers side, I mean they kind of flick at it very quickly here, basically just kind of reiterating what they had in that letter from February where they basically said Warner Brothers would have been a great addition. It would have accelerated their strategy, but in their words, at the quote "the right price." When you look at sort of the idea of what Warner Brothers could have brought to the table and what Netflix has going forward here, should we be looking at Netflix maybe having the potential to outpace Warner Brothers in terms of performance going forward? And I don't just mean in terms of the stock, but I mean talking about in terms of viewership, subscribers, etc. Yeah, in fact, that's what's gonna happen is—and why we're so bullish is—what they actually did was they stuck Paramount Skydance with a huge ball of debt that they'll never be able to pay off. On top of the fact everybody in Hollywood hates the deal because they know it's gonna be layoffs and less production because they've got to pay ridiculous interest on all this debt. And so what they did was instead of having a bigger, stronger competitor, they forced that person to pay so much money that now it's a weaker company and has a lot of issues even just to complete this merger. And so they actually have less competition now. The way we look at Hollywood now is it's a two-horse game between Disney-Hulu and Netflix and that's basically it. Everything is either cut up or falling apart or not sure where it's gonna end up or how it's gonna be. So Netflix is in the driver's seat because Disney still hasn't fully articulated their streaming strategy in a way that would be competitive to Netflix. So what's happened now is they are the entertainment industry, or what we call they're the cable company for the world now, and they can just grow forever. Yeah, and I ask that question to kind of set up too this idea—we're having a debate with some of our colleagues about the impending departure of Reed Hastings, who's not going to stand for reelection to the board. I mean, this would effectively end his at least his formal role in any sort of company management. Obviously, he gave up the CEO chair to Ted Sarandos and Greg Peters a while ago here. But when we talk about the innovation that he brought, particularly that big pivot to streaming—I mean, obviously the mail-order business was a huge gamble, but even that pivot to streaming, a lot of people had no idea what he was doing. And then when I look at Ted Sarandos and I look at how he has basically become a Hollywood mogul to a certain extent, Greg Peters a little bit there. What is the leadership of this company going forward? How is this business going to be run? I mean, what do you think their vision—Sarandos and Peters, assuming they are going to be the shepherds for this for the next few years—what does that look like to you? Well, Ted is the king of Hollywood in a way that we haven't seen since a hundred years ago, you know, with just this kind of dominance. And also his relationship with so many in the creative community despite changing the format in a way that a lot of people didn't like. You know, people know if they want to get a project done they should probably call Ted first. You know, Reed really hasn't been that involved and I think exiting the board probably has something to do with his political ambitions, which may or may not be true. I'm hoping that he does actually spend his time trying to make this country a better place. And so exiting the board now makes a lot of sense for him and also to protect Netflix if there's any blowback for taking any political, you know, route, but he's talked about these kind of things in the past. So moving forward though, Netflix is about Ted and Greg; it's really about Ted, and he's just in his prime just dominating right now. So that's what it's all about. And what we see in the future is more of the same, which is: how do we make sports a bigger part of what we're doing but not paying ridiculous fees for sports rights? How do we continue to make the best content, whether it be shows... they're buying a lot of content actually from some of their competitors even. If you go on right now, Anaconda is one of the top movies on Netflix right now; they didn't make it, you know. And so they're still buying content from people who realize they can monetize their content better on a competitive streamer versus their own, which is like crazy to me. So I think they've got a lot of levers and they're doing well with video games right now. This is something a few people are starting to notice, but they really have now starting to attract younger people to their gaming platform. That is a huge opportunity if Netflix can monetize their IP in gaming. So we like the direction of the business and we're happy that they're focused on what they're doing and not trying to eat up a big merger that would ultimately kill them. Right, absolutely. And I do want to talk a little bit about, you know, you described this basically as a two-horse race. It's Disney-Hulu and then it's Netflix. You called Ted Sarandos the king of Hollywood, which is quite a crown to carry. And I just wonder, I mean, what are the risks here? Because, you know, the way that you're phrasing it, it sounds like this is Netflix's game to lose. So what would a misstep look like? Well, the risk to Netflix—I mean, the internet goes out? I just don't know what would hurt them now when this was the risk that they actually won the merger. See, if they would have bought Warner Brothers, Netflix would have been at great risk absorbing this dog. You know, Warner Brothers has killed every company that has bought it, and it's been five now, you know. And so it's gonna kill Paramount Skydance just like it killed the other four or whatever. So I was really worried about that and what we were betting on was that they were just trying to drive up the price and get out, and that's what ended up happening. And that's why we're more bullish than ever because they're gonna really be stuck with this. So it really is Netflix's game to lose right now. But when you think about losing in a generation that basically watches YouTube and Netflix, there is no losing for them. What they need to focus on is not getting lazy, continuing to make good content, having high standards of what the expectation for that content is, and not getting lazy and just throwing money at all these projects because they're the top. And so as long as they continue to make high-quality content people will come, just like there's some great Disney movies coming out this this year. They've got three great movies on tap. And if you put out the stuff, people will watch it. And so as long as Netflix keeps making good content, they'll continue to print money for forever, I think. So, you know, kids today, they watch YouTube and Netflix, and that's pretty much it. Yeah, it's it's pretty wild and in your view, Netflix just has to keep grinding here. But I do want to talk about the point you made that, you know, they buy a lot of content, it's not just Netflix content that's on Netflix. And they're also a buyer of Warner Brothers Discovery content as well. Yes, it's all over their streamer right now is Warner Brothers content. Well, I'm wondering then if you do see a situation, hypothetically, where, you know, Paramount-Warner Brothers Discovery—that is sort of an albatross and that's a company that's weighed down from here and not firing on all cylinders. I mean, I have to imagine at a certain extent, that would be bad news for Netflix if there were less people, less firms producing quality content. Yes, but you know, I live here, you know, in L.A., and there are so many people working on quality content that Netflix can find it on every corner here, you know what I mean? So it's really, I think having less competition in content is actually bad for Hollywood as a whole because I want to see all the studios survive and grow and prosper. It's good to have lots of competition and great content. You get better content with more competition, so there's something to that. But I don't look at it that way. I think business is sort of like a contact sport and you gotta play for your monopoly. That's the way you win is by having a monopoly and the biggest, the Mag 7, the biggest, most successful companies in America are all monopolies or duopolies. And Netflix is now a monopoly and that's where you want to be. And so anyway you look at it, if I'm a producer and I'm working on a project, I'm calling Netflix no matter what, and that's just the way it is.

The GPT Moment for Robotics Is Here

Y Combinator(2.2M) ·49:27 ·27.5K views · 2026-04-16
Quan Vuong (Co-founder, Physical Intelligence) Garry Tan (President and CEO, Y Combinator) Jared Friedman (Partner, Y Combinator)
Robotics has reached its "GPT-1 moment" as foundation models transition the field from specialized engineering to a scalable data-operations problem.
TL;DR Robotics has reached its "GPT-1 moment" as foundation models transition the field from specialized engineering to a scalable data-operations problem. Physical Intelligence (PI) has demonstrated that "cross-embodiment" models—trained across diverse robot hardware—perform...

TL;DR

Robotics has reached its "GPT-1 moment" as foundation models transition the field from specialized engineering to a scalable data-operations problem. Physical Intelligence (PI) has demonstrated that "cross-embodiment" models—trained across diverse robot hardware—perform 50% better than hardware-specific specialists and can execute complex tasks like laundry folding and warehouse logistics zero-shot.

The Technical Breakthrough: Cross-Embodiment Scaling

  • The "GPT-1 Moment": Robotics is moving away from deterministic, task-specific programming toward general-purpose models that can control any robot for any task it is physically capable of performing.
  • Open X-Embodiment Success: Research indicates that training a high-capacity model on data from multiple different robot platforms (rather than just one) yields a 50% performance improvement over hardware-specific specialist models.
      • Abstract Learning: By training on diverse fleets, the model learns a general notion of control rather than just memorizing a specific motor's quirks.
      • Hardware Drift Resilience: Specialist models are brittle and fail when hardware wears down or software updates occur; generalist models are robust to these "drifts."
  • From Semantics to Control: Early breakthroughs (SayCan, RT-2, PaLM-E) used Large Language Models (LLMs) to provide "common sense" and planning. PI is now extending this intelligence down to low-level, real-time physical control.

Infrastructure and Cloud-Based Control

  • Cloud-Hosted Intelligence: In a departure from traditional robotics, PI runs its models in the cloud rather than on-device. This allows robots to use massive foundation models without the high cost and power requirements of internal GPUs.
  • Latency Management: PI uses "action chunking" and predictive pipelining to maintain high-frequency control loops.
      • Pipelining: The robot executes a 100ms sequence of actions while simultaneously querying the cloud for the next set, burying inference time within the control loop.
      • Decoupling: This architecture allows PI to "parachute" into a partner’s existing hardware system via an API without needing to see the physical robot or understand its internal mechanics.
  • Data Scarcity Strategy: Since there is no "internet of robotics data," PI focuses on cross-embodiment data capture, making it possible to consume data from thousands of different robot types to accelerate the training process.

Real-World Applications and Demos

  • The "Turing Test" for Robotics: Folding laundry is considered a benchmark for robotics due to the infinite variety of deformable objects. PI and partner Weave demonstrated a robot folding diverse, unseen items in a public laundromat after only two weeks of model adaptation.
  • Warehouse Autonomy: Working with Ultra, PI deployed a model in an active e-commerce warehouse to package items into soft pouches—a task requiring precise "nudging" and reasoning about diverse objects.
  • Zero-Shot Capabilities: PI is seeing emergent properties where models can perform complex tasks zero-shot (with no task-specific training data) that previously required hundreds of hours of data collection.

The Playbook for the "Cambrian Explosion" of Vertical Robotics

  • Unbundling the Stack: Historically, robotics companies had to be vertically integrated (hardware, software, safety, sales). PI aims to provide the "seed of intelligence," allowing founders to focus solely on workflow and customer integration.
  • The New Startup Equation: The upfront cost of starting a robotics business is dropping rapidly because hardware is cheaper and autonomy can be "rented" or adapted from foundation models.
  • The Scaling Recipe for Founders:

1. Identify High-Value Workflows: Find tasks where labor shortages are acute (e.g., logistics).

2. Scrappy Hardware: Use affordable, off-the-shelf robots; reactive models can compensate for hardware inaccuracies.

3. Mixed Autonomy: Deploy a system where humans provide remote corrections when the robot fails.

4. Economic Break-even: Scale only once the system is profitable with a human-in-the-loop, then use that data to drive full autonomy.

***

Notable Quotes

"If you take data from 10 different platforms and absorb it into a model high-capacity enough to absorb that data... the generalist was 50 percent better than the specialist optimized for a particular embodiment." — Quan Vuong
"The equation for starting a robotic business has changed... it’s not about having your own proprietary autonomy stack anymore. It allows companies to focus on the components that actually differentiate them." — Quan Vuong
"Everyone is spending a lot of time in the digital world, but it feels like now is the time to start thinking about the world of atoms." — Garry Tan
"We think it's going to be like peeling an onion... you start from a strong base model, you deploy a mixed autonomy system that makes mistakes, and then by exposing it to the edge cases of the real world, it gets incrementally better until one day it is fully autonomous." — Quan Vuong
The equation I think for starting a robotic business has changed and will continue to change at an accelerating pace because the upfront cost is not that high anymore. Everyone's sort of spending a lot of time in the digital world, and it feels like now is the time to start thinking about the world of atoms. You literally just gave people the playbook for how to build a vertical robotics company. And this has really been our mission from the start is to create that Cambrian explosion. It still like blows my mind. I didn't know if this would exist even in my entire lifetime. Welcome back to another episode of The Lightcone. Today, we have a very special guest, Quan Vuong. He's one of the co-founders of Physical Intelligence, which we think might be the robotics AI lab that brings about the GPT-1 moment for all of robotics. Quan, thank you for joining us. Pleasure to be here. I have been a long-time admirer of YC. Our mission is to build a model that can control any robot to do any task that it is physically capable of and to do so at such a high level of performance that it's going to be useful to people in all walks of life. And so GPT-1 for robotics, you know, what is it? You know, is the ChatGPT moment for robotics real? Our perspective here is that we want to build a model that's really intelligent, and we want to build a platform that allows us to externalize that intelligence to the rest of the world and allow them to use it to build very interesting applications in all sorts of verticals in robotics. And we think that it's going to be more like a "peeling an onion" analogy where you start from a really strong base model that has all sorts of common sense knowledge and already works to some extent on your robot. You have then a mixed autonomy system very similar, for example, to an autonomous driving car today. And then you actually deploy that system to do a real job. That system might make a mistake; it's okay. And then over time, by actually exposing the system to the complexity and the edge cases of the real world, that system gets incrementally, even just slightly, better over time every day. And you know, one day you wake up and you suddenly have a system that is just fully autonomous and just provides tremendous value. It might be helpful to give the audience a bit of a mini history lesson on why robotics is so hard. There's been a lot of breakthroughs in the last two years. I mean, just to simplify, the robotics problem is three pillars: semantics, which I think we got a lot of unlocks from with language models that somehow we ported into robotics; then you have the planning; and then the last thing is control, which needs to be done in real time and interact with an environment that changes. Walk us through the seminal papers that a lot of the team of Pi Robotics published that gave you the inkling that the GPT-1 moment is near and that started in 2024. Yeah, so the dream to build general-purpose robots has been a long-time dream, I think, in humanity. Like, you know, we're not the first to say that our mission is to build a model that can work on any robot. We're really fortunate to be in this moment in time in history where we feel that it's possible. To kind of walk back a little bit a few years before, there was SayCan, which to me was the first demonstration of language models and how you can bring all of the common-sense knowledge into robotics. Therefore, that significantly kind of reduces the need to collect robot-specific data. So for example, if you have a task of, "Oh, I want to go to the YC office to record a podcast," you know, what are the steps I need to take? You can ask a language model, you know, "Just show me the steps and show me the plan," and that works incredibly well. And then the way kind of language models infiltrate, if you will, in robotics is it starts at the planning level, at the semantic level. But there is still the control problem. You know, at the end of the day, you still need a mechanism to convert the plan into low-level actions that can actually actuate the robot. And that brings us to PaLM-E and that brings us to RT-2, which stands for Robotic Transformer 2. And what these two works really show is that if you start from a vision language model that is really powerful and you kind of use robotic data to adapt this model to speak robot language, then you see a lot of transfer from the kind of knowledge that exists in the language model down to the low-level action. Like one of my favorite examples when we did the RT-2 project was you can have pictures of celebrities on the table. You have a picture of Taylor Swift, you have a picture of the Queen of England, and you can ask the robot, "You know, pick up the Coke can and move it to Taylor Swift," even though the concept of Taylor Swift doesn't exist in the robot data at all. And that works. You can do other examples, such as spatial reasoning that doesn't exist in the robot data at all. Like for example, "Move the dinosaur next to the like red car." And these are all just completely unseen objects in robot data. And so that was RT-2 and that was PaLM-E. Now, RT-2 and PaLM-E are single-embodiment exercises which, just for the audience, single-embodiment meaning it works for a very specific robot. It works for a very specific robot. In robotics, you can ask the question: how do you scale, especially how do you scale data collection? And one of the insights that we had back then was, you know, maybe the data from one robot is not that different from another robot anyway. If you have enough robots in your training data, maybe what the model learns isn't to control one specific robot, but something that's more abstract, which is, "How do I kind of learn a general notion of what it means to control any particular robotic platform?" Therefore, I would be better at controlling any particular platform, and that brings us to what we call Open X-Embodiment and Robotic Transformer X. That was a big paper because it was the first that showed potential scaling laws that apply to robotics because now you could start training all these models across multiple kinds of hardware, not just one, which has never been done in robotics ever before. Research labs would all train with a very specific set of sensor actuators or motors, and it was all very finicky with that particular hardware, right? Yeah, one of the really interesting results from Open X-Embodiment, and let me provide the context here, is that you can take let's say 10 different robot platforms, collect data from them, train a policy, and really optimize the policy to work well on that platform. So let's say you have that; you have 10 different platforms, 10 different policies. And now if you simply take the data and absorb it into a model that is high capacity enough to really absorb that data, and you can compare. You have this generalist, right, that learns to control all 10 different robots, and you can compare it to the specialist that has been optimized to work well on a particular embodiment. How does it compare? And the interesting result from Open X is it was 50 percent better. Wow. And that was really surprising because in robotics, it's hard enough to get your model to work on one particular robot platform. And one of the reasons why I say that we're really fortunate to be in this moment in time in robotics is because Open X was really only possible because of the support that we received from the broad robotic community. It was a huge collaboration across the robotic community. The reason why that's really important is there is this joke in robotic grad school that, you know, if you want to add two years to your PhD, just work on any robot platform. By that logic, if you want to have 10 robot platforms, that's 20 years. Why is that? It takes like a year or two to just get the platform up and running to even collect the data. Yeah. Is it fair to say that the data set that was created from embodiment X is similar to the scale and impact that ImageNet did for vision because it was huge and it was the first large data set across multiple hardware, huge collaboration. I still think that ImageNet was more impactful in the vision community, and the reason for that is a few. The first is that ImageNet also allowed for reproducible evaluation. Open X as an effort was more about making data available for people to use. And evaluation is a really difficult problem in robotics that Open X did not solve. And the second is I think Open X is a drop in the bucket at this point in the robotic community. If you measure in the kind of the scale and the volume and the diversity of data that the community is collecting, I think Open X at this point is a drop in the bucket. I mean, I guess we started talking about sort of GPT-1, but even GPT-1, you know, that was sort of this moment where you can prove you Alec Radford figured out that there was a neuron based on a very specific input and output, and then that allowed the scaling laws to sort of take hold. The biggest problem in robotics I've heard is basically exactly what we've been talking about, which is the data problem. You know language you could bootstrap off of, like the sum total of what you could get off the internet, which is actually quite a lot. Can you give us like a sense for scale? Is it like petabytes? Like, you know, what do you think is necessary as an input to, you know, the true GPT-1 of robotics. Yeah. So the data scarcity problem in robotics, there's a few ways to look at it. The first way is that it's really two problems in disguise. There is the data generation problem and there's the data capture problem. The difference is that the data capture is that there might already be lots of robotic data that is being generated, but there's just never been really an incentive to capture it to make it easy for digestion in training. And that's one of the goals that Open X was trying to solve, which is if you have robotic data, it's a really good idea to capture it and make it possible to train on. The second way to look at it is that robotics is very different from language models; there is not an internet of robotic data that you can use. And so you see this kind of very operationally heavy effort to collect data, and there's the question of is it going to scale. Well, the way that I look at it is let's take the US GDP: 24 trillion US dollars. Let's say if we actually solve robotics—a model that can control any robot to do any task. Napkin math, maybe you contribute 10 percent to US GDP. Well, that's already a massive number, and I think that promise is one of the reasons that warrants the investment into data collection in robotics. And the third way to look at it is we're very focused on cross-embodiment. And cross-embodiment, there is the data collection aspect as well, which is to really make sure that your model and your organizations and infrastructure are set up to consume data from many different sources of robots. And that actually allows you to scale easier. For example, if I were to contrast our approach compared to, let's say, a company that has a particular hardware platform that they optimize for and they scale, it's not an approach that has really allowed people to scale. Because it's just much harder to figure out how do you manufacture like a thousand units of something for now compared to making sure that you yourself are ready to absorb data from like a thousand different types of robots that are already in the community. I mean, it's a crazy problem, isn't it? I mean the hardware itself, even within the same design of embodiment, if there's a hardware run that goes awry or like one of the servos is slightly different, like you see it in the data, right? And then how do you control for that? Yeah. So I think we were doing kind of like an inventory of robots in the company and we were so shocked to find that there are no robots, no two robot platforms that are the same. If you ask people in the robot community, sometimes there is debate about multi-robot versus single-robot. And the argument is that single-robot is simpler to scale. And actually, that's not how it plays out in practice. How it plays out in practice is even if you have a single robot that you're optimizing for, over time that platform is going to drift. You know, maybe you want to make hardware changes or you have software changes. You end up in a situation where it's much harder for you to reuse old data because, in machine learning, if you want to generalize from a distribution, you would like many samples from that distribution. And if you just have one robot platform that has a major change every three months, maybe you have a few data points from that distribution. Whereas, if you start from the hypothesis that if you have many robot platforms in your fleet, your model is going to learn something more abstract—which is how do I control a robot, not any particular robot—then the model will be able to ingest data from, you know, a slightly different robot better. That's good news. We're doing work where you start to see interesting transfer between different data sources. For example, today it's possible to perform tasks "zero-shot." Zero-shot meaning you don't collect any data. And these are the tasks that last year might have required like hundreds and hundreds of hours. What are some examples? Yeah, do we have any videos we can see that show us? So, you know, I might get some flack when I come back because this is not a published result; hopefully this will come out soon. So you know, I want to reserve the excitement for that and I'm kind of like building up the excitement a little bit so hopefully this will come out soon. But these are not simple tasks. These are like actually difficult tasks that just last year required like hundreds of hours of data collection. You hear it here on Lightcone first that there's some emergent properties that are going to come out of Pi. Can you give us a sense of like the flavor of the tasks? It's really easy to fool yourself and so we wanted to test across different tasks of different flavors. Like tasks that require precision, tasks that require reasoning with multiple objects in the scene. It all seems to have this property that's really nice, so it does seem like that's something that's kind of a more general property that emerged rather than we just, you know, got lucky and suddenly the model started working on one particular task. Could you help us understand where we are now in terms of like what's working and how well it's working? Like we're not quite at the ChatGPT moment yet. Like, where are we? And I think you brought some videos that you were going to show us to like help everybody visualize what the current state of the art actually looks like. I think where we are is, I think if you have a task where it's okay for the robot to make a mistake and it's possible for you to set up a mixed-autonomy system where you have a person that takes over when the robot makes a mistake and provides corrections, it is possible to get to a level of performance where it starts to make sense to think about scaling robot deployment. And the example that I specifically want to highlight here is this blog post that we did with Weave and Ultra—and you know, it's great that these are also both YC companies. I want to provide a little bit of context here first. The context is that Pi is a primarily research organization. We want to focus on building the best model. But we also want to not be tunnel-visioned. We want to make sure that the model that we build actually is going to be useful and actually performs tasks that people in society care about. And one of the really good ways for us to do so is to partner really closely with companies that want to get robots out there today. And the way that these relationships work is that we treat each other like we're on the same team. Very free flow of information, and we design a system that tries to get the best possible performance for the tasks that these companies care about. So let me talk about Weave first. What you're seeing in this video is a system that we built together folding really diverse items of laundry in a real laundromat in the Mission. You can see people walking outside and why this task is difficult is because there's just infinite possibility in the observation space. Like, you know, clothing items are deformable and no two items of clothing here are the same. And these are also unseen. These are not clothing items that were seen in the training data. Yeah, I love this team. They are some of the most cracked people out of Apple I've ever met. Gary was the partner for Weave; maybe you want to like explain what Weave is and what their company is. Yeah, I mean they're actually shipping their first robots into the home. We sort of talked about it as being able to do household tasks like this, and I think they were very inspired by Physical Intelligence's first demos with laundry folding. So it's actually a total trip to hear about it; you know, a year ago we were talking about them doing it, and then now to see them do it working hand-in-hand with you is really awesome. I think this is a great example of like, you know, you need the model smarts, you need the data collection, and then the hardware and the sort of system integration all working together is just hard to nail. Yeah. And to get back to your question about why robotics is hard: it's really a hard system problem. Like, you need everything to work well and work well together to get this result. And like, Weave is such an incredible team for us to work with to get this result. And it actually didn't even take us that long to get this result. It was roughly—we set a goal, and then maybe it was like two weeks afterwards where we got a model and a system that was good enough at performing this task. It still like blows my mind to see a robot actually folding laundry because I remember until basically until ChatGPT, I didn't know if this would exist even in my entire lifetime. Because like folding laundry—I mean it's always been like the Turing test for robotics because there's no way to like deterministically program a system the way that you did like pre-AI to do this because the space is like so infinite. And like we've shown that it's possible for us to do like—basically if everyone can do this, like, robots will be able to do everything. It's only a matter of improving it from here. There was a funny story where when we first published Pi0, people thought of us as the laundry company because the demo was just focused on laundry. Actually, picking a home task, especially a task that has to do with deformable objects, was a very intentional choice on our end. We're not just after the home; we really want to make it broadly applicable. But picking a home task for us to start with has a few benefits. Like, one is relatable. You can see the laundry folding demo and you can kind of grok how this is going to be useful and you can get a sense of why it's hard. And the second is that it's really easy to set up to test generalization. You can talk about Ultra, which is your company, Jared, that demo of it. Yeah, this is Ultra. The thing that I love about this video is you see, you know, it's bright outside and you see this is 4x speed and it's 100 minutes. If I scroll to the end, the sun has set. Oh wow. That's cool. That was one of the big problems in robotics, where it would be so sensitive to the environment in lighting and mess up the vision system and the semantics part of it. Yeah. And the interesting thing here is that it is possible to get to the level of autonomy that the robot is just performing the task. This is autonomy at scale. Like, this is ready to be scaled. Quan, because this task is less familiar than laundry folding, do you want to explain what the robot is doing here and what Ultra is like doing as a company? Ultra is a company that wants to make it really easy to adapt robots to, you know, new tasks and right now they are focusing on the logistics space, which is really important because, you know, there's lots of labor shortages in logistics. The task that we focus on together here is: you know, if you order an item from Amazon, you sometimes get this soft pouch that the item gets shipped in. And the task here is you have a tray of these items here, and the robot is supposed to pick one of them at a time and place it inside this pouch. The machine will then close it and then pick up the pouch and put it on the left here to be ready for shipping. Now this is hard because there are many different types of objects that can be in this tray and the opening here is actually very narrow, so you see this interesting example of the robot kind of nudging the item to go into the pouch. And that's really hard. Like, that requires a very good understanding of the scene and like very precise motion to nudge the object into the pouch. The other thing that's hard about this task is the level of autonomy that's required. Like, this is running for an entire day. There is still human intervention, I want to say, in this full-day operation, but the level of intervention is actually quite minimal. This is not just like some like demo station, right? This is actually recorded in an actual e-commerce warehouse where they're actually shipping real products to real customers. This isn't just like a lab. This is packaging a real customer's real order for a customer to be shipped out in a real warehouse. So this is real operations. I think this is really cool because I think when people think about robots, they tend to think of the consumer use cases like Weave, because that's what we're familiar with in our daily life. What I find really interesting is that there's like a million applications like this Ultra thing that you wouldn't think of as obviously like, "Oh, who packs the soft pouch of things that you get from like Amazon?" Well, there's some person who does that, and this is like a job that we can now build a robot to do. The interesting thing about the approach is that you're converting it from a very difficult engineering problem into an operation problem of, "How do I identify the use case and how do I collect the right data?" which is in some sense more scalable because you can build a system that allows you to collect data for many different tasks. So it's now a problem of how do I scale data collection rather than, you know, for every new task, "How do I design a really difficult engineering system to solve it?" YC Startup School is back. We're hand-selecting the most promising builders in the world and flying them out to San Francisco for July 25th and 26th to discuss the cutting edge of tech. Apply now for a spot. Okay, back to the video. I think one thing that the audience may not know is that you have a very unique technical insight. In the past, robotics folks would have kind of gasped and been shocked because robots need to run in real time; a lot of times, all of the compute runs on-device. But you guys have done something very different. Can you tell us more about that so that this works in real-time with large models and really well? So the context here is that we talk to many companies that would like to deploy robots, and one of the first questions we get is, "What compute unit should we get on the robots?" You know, it's expensive, it's going to increase the BOM costs, and they're worried that it's going to go out of fashion very quickly because the model changes, the model gets bigger. How do I make sure that the hardware that I'm going to commit to today is going to be viable for, you know, a couple of years? It's a very difficult question. People are often really surprised when I tell them that almost all of the robot evaluations that we run at Pi today—including the really complicated demos that we have shown: making coffee, folding laundry, mobile robots navigating around—the model is actually hosted in the cloud. And you know, this is not like a cloud as in a server in the office; it's a real cloud. The model is hosted in a data center somewhere and within this high-frequency control loop that is controlling the robot, the robot is actually querying an API endpoint that hosts the model, sending it images and language commands, and getting back actions that then execute directly on the robot. And this is surprising because of precisely the reason that you mentioned: how do you actually make it work? This is why it's really important for Pi to couple system hardware and model development and research very tight together, because it allows us to solve for this problem. So for example, one of the insights that we have here is that you can actually bury the inference time within the robot control loop. Because you know, if I'm a robot, I have enough actions for me to execute for the next hundred milliseconds. Like, there's no reason for me to wait until I finish executing that action to ask my model for a different action. You know, I can do it as fast as inference, essentially. And so you know, maybe when I only have 50 milliseconds of action worth left, I can ask for the next set of actions. And when the current 50 milliseconds is over, like, I have something that's ready for me to continue with, you know, my next 100 milliseconds. So that's one of the insights. The other kind of algorithmic improvements—we refer to them as real-time chunking—design inference in such a way that you know there's going to be a delay in how long it takes to query the model on the cloud, basically. Like, the problem here, if I get a little bit more technical, is an action chunk is a sequence of actions that I can execute on the robot, so it's not just one action. And if I have an action chunk that I can execute for 100 milliseconds, and 50 milliseconds in I want to predict another action chunk, and I'm going to transition to that new action chunk after my current 50 milliseconds is over, how do I make sure the two are consistent? Like, you know, how do I make sure that if I'm moving this way, the next action chunk is going to continue to allow me to continue to be smoothly moving this way? You can pre-compute. Yeah, you can pre-compute, and like, that's one of the algorithmic improvements that we made to make inference using a model hosted in the cloud possible. I studied computer engineering, so I'm not really an algorithms person, but when it comes to systems like that—like pipelining—like, get me all over that. That sounds great; that's so interesting. I mean, this simplifies—it kind of is a brilliant choice because it simplifies so much of the system for the robots. You don't need all these clunky—I don't know, people have two operating systems at sometimes for robots, embedded RTOS and then the regular one, and all these complex giant compute and power requirements. This is what the initial versions of Waymo used to run: basically a server in the trunk. And you can't afford to do that with general-day robotics, which is brilliant that you figured out how to do it. Yeah, you don't have to. I mean, you can do things—some of it, there obviously has to be some compute there, but a lot of the compute can happen elsewhere. And then, there must be a video—like this thing that we're looking at in the top left—like, how much of that is sort of like video feedback? How much of it is like local processed? I mean... Yeah, is there any compute locally on this robot, or is it just like a dumb video camera that streams data to the cloud? For this, I am not 100 percent sure, but I am inclined to believe that it's just a dumb computer. Like for this specific video, I don't remember, but I'm just 100 percent confident that we can make this work with a dumb computer on the robot. And the one other interesting thing about our collaboration with Weave and Ultra is: one, I've never seen their robot in person. Oh wow. Two is I have very little idea about how their robot actually works. Interesting. And that's a very intentional choice. Like, I want to stay away from that as far as possible. I also don't know how they collect data. Like, I intentionally don't ask them these questions to understand whether it's possible for an organization like Pi to parachute into their existing system and to work really closely with them on the things that actually matter to get the system to work, and not have to learn about how they've set up their system because, in a way, that's like a more scalable recipe. Yeah, you completely decouple a lot of the hardware control loop choices from the semantics and planning, which just works. Which is brilliant. Yeah, it is. I mean, I'm really surprised. It works. And when we started the company, we thought that real deployment is going to be—it's only going to be in the conversation like five years into the life of the company, because the problem is just really hard. And we're two years in, and you know, this is the result that we have. Real deployment and scaling the number of robots is a really serious consideration today, and so the pace of progress has just been very pleasantly much faster than we expected originally. Often on this podcast, we talk about what all this means for startup founders. I think that might be an interesting question for us to explore here. So if you imagine someone was listening to this podcast and maybe they're like a college student that's studying computer science, and they think robots are really cool and they want to do something like this. How should they get started and what are the skills that they need? Do they need to be a mechanical engineer to be able to build a robot like this? Can they just buy an off-the-shelf robot arm and camera system and like load Pi and you're off and running in like a day? Yeah. Before I actually answer your question, let me provide a few more contexts. The first is that robotics is traditionally really hard because it's an extremely vertically integrated business. You need to have your own customer relationships, your own hardware, your own autonomy stack, your own safety certification, your own everything. And the barrier to entry is just really high because of that. And one of the things that we're trying to change is that we're trying to provide a foundation of physical intelligence that the community can build on top of. That allows them to onboard autonomy onto their robot and their tasks much quicker than before. So that's the first—you know, we want to provide that kind of seed of intelligence that allows people to move much faster so that they can, you know, focus on other problems. The second thing is that the recipe for starting a vertical robotics business today is: one, have a really good understanding of the existing workflow, because the robotic system needs to fit into the existing workflow. And the second is to be very meticulous about identifying where the opportunity is. You know, if there's a workflow that needs X number of work today, you know, where is the robot when you insert it going to make the biggest difference? And two is to really be scrappy when it comes to hardware and data collection. You don't need an incredibly expensive robot that is capable of very precise motion today to be able to do these tasks. And the reason why is that these models are really reactive, and so they can compensate for some of the inaccuracy in the actual robot movement. And to ensure that you have the ability to collect data and to run evaluation, especially evaluation in real deployment. The next step after that is to get a mixed autonomy system that allows you to get to the point where it's break-even. Break-even economically. Break-even economically. Because the reason why that's important is because it allows you to then scale the number of robots. And if you lose money on every robot, it's very hard to scale. That has been historically one of the biggest challenges for robotic companies as they go into growth stage; just the payback period just doesn't make sense. Yeah, so the equation, I think, for starting a robotic business has changed and will continue to change at an accelerating pace because the upfront cost is not that high anymore. And now, you know, what is the upfront cost? The upfront cost is much cheaper hardware, the ability to collect data, the ability to collect evaluation, and the ability to kind of like understand the use case to see where they should insert the robot. You know, it's not about having incredibly expensive hardware; it's not about having your own proprietary, I think, autonomy classical stack anymore to be able to do these tasks. And so it allows a company to focus on the components that will actually allow them to differentiate themselves from the rest of the space. Now that you sort of unbundled it and you no longer need to build this fully vertically integrated company in order to build a robotics company, are we on the precipice of a Cambrian explosion of vertical robotics companies? Where there's going to be like a thousand companies like Ultra going after, you know, every like menial job in the economy and like getting a deep understanding of the customer, building a robot that can solve that problem, doing a like mixed human-machine deployment until it can run fully autonomously and building a company in every sector. Is that the future that you see people building on top of Pi? It's funny that you mention "Cambrian explosion" because when we wrote this blog post, there was that term that's very kind of like hotly debated. We are, I think, academics at heart, and we want to be kind of very measured when we communicate. But you know, myself personally, I believe there's going to be a Cambrian explosion of robotic companies across the entire world and across many, many different verticals, just because it's just so much cheaper to build. It doesn't require, you know, someone with 20 years of experience in robotics to start anymore. You know, it requires someone that is really scrappy, that can move really quickly, can do the system integration, can understand customers and what they want to start the deployment. I mean, what's coming up for me is, obviously, we work with a lot of robotics companies and meet a lot of founders, and it feels like there's this continuum. One is to use an analogy to personal computing. You could argue that industrial robotics today is basically like mainframe or minicomputer level. Like, you know, if you look back in the '70s, huge public companies like Digital Equipment that just did like these sort of very, very expensive deployments, but like they were very, very specialized and it was all extreme enterprise. Like, you know, the idea of a personal computer was ridiculous, right? You know, it took the Altair and then Apple I and Apple II and then IBM PC XT to create personal computing. And then like, the traditional advice for robotics for many years is like, "Go after dirty and dangerous." And then of course those are sort of the industrial cases, like, you know, you have these giant Tesla robots in the Giga Factory and things like that. It feels like what you said around profitability is really, really big. So you know, does that mean that the people who do the vertical robot Cambrian explosion sort of moment, or the people who are sort of first in that—it sounds like they would be the first to be profitable and not dirty and dangerous. I think this is already happening today. I think we have the fortune of having lots of visibility into the robotic community because, you know, people would like to talk to us; people would like to learn what it's like to build a foundation model for robotics, and people would like to know, "How do I get the same level of autonomy?" And there's so many companies and businesses that we talk to that would love to put a robot into their space. That, you know, it's okay for the robot to make a mistake, and this is needed so much. I really believe that the recipe that I mentioned earlier of identifying where the robot can fit in, focusing on cheap hardware, collecting data, running evaluation, mixed autonomy, break-even, scaling robots—that will work across many different verticals. And I'm seeing it play out today, and it's just incredibly exciting to see. I think this is pretty cool that you literally just gave people the playbook for how to build a vertical robotics company. Like, this is a playbook that could possibly be followed successfully hundreds or thousands of times. And the reason why I want to mention it is because I do want to see that Cambrian explosion, and we want to help enable it. You know, for Pi, if we talk about why Pi is going to fail, it's probably going to be because the problem is just way too hard. You know, maybe it takes 50 more years to solve the robotic problem and you know, not a couple of years, five, ten. And so we want to enable the community; we want to accelerate progress, and that's why we're very open. Like, we publish our research, we open-source Pi0 and Pi0.5. And people also are shocked when they ask me, you know, "Is there any difference between Pi0 and Pi0.5 that you open-sourced versus the model that we use internally?" And the answer was actually no; it's the same model. Like, the pre-trained model weights that you're using that we open-sourced are also the pre-trained model weights that our research internally uses for Pi0 and Pi0.5. And so we really want to help accelerate progress in the community and to create that Cambrian explosion. Yeah, that's very inspiring. I mean, I feel like that's everyone's sort of spending a lot of time in the digital world, and it feels like, you know, now is the time to start thinking about the world of atoms. This is sort of the perfect mix of actually like, you know, how do you take electrons and turn it into abundance in the atoms world. And I think about Dario Amodei's essay, "All Watched Over by Machines of Loving Grace." When you really think about the perfect manifestation of that, it's not like, you know, perfect agents that look over you just like in the electronic world; it's you know, actually something a little bit more akin to what we're seeing here. Yeah. And this has really been our mission from the start is to create that Cambrian explosion. And you know, this is why we choose to focus on the model, because we believe that is the bottleneck to just really make robots useful across many different tasks in the world. And that's why we also focus on cross-embodiment. Success for us is not defined as only our model on our robot performing tasks that are useful. The surface area for success is actually much larger, which is our model performing really useful tasks on somebody else's robot out there—maybe that we don't even know what that robot is like—in a way that's useful to the end consumer. Could we maybe talk a little bit about the humans behind the robots here? Like, how did the company get started? Like, who are your co-founders? And how did you get together and what skills you each bring to such a complex problem? Sometimes the joke I make here is that the humans behind the robots are also robots—not really. Yeah, so Pi is a very, I would say, untraditional company. We have a larger than average founding team. And some of us worked really closely together when we were at the robotic team at Google. And the robotic team at Google was, I think, a really, really great environment for seeing the sign of life and creating the relationships and the community that allow the robotic community and these advances to flourish. There is Rocky, which we met when we were thinking about starting the company, and he's just been really instrumental in making sure that we're a good business. And there is Adnan, our hardware lead, that came over from Anduril. And Adnan has a really difficult job because if you want to work on cross-embodiment—you know, remember my joke about how if you want to add two years to your grad school, you bring on one more robot—the hardware problem and the operational problem for us is: how do we build, improve, and scale a fleet of heterogeneous robots? You know, it's just not one robot platform. And because we built the organization from scratch in the beginning to support that, I think we're able to do it, but it's just a really hard problem because there are just no two different robots in the fleet alike. How do you make sure that everything runs smoothly? We're really good at divide and conquer if you ask. But wait, how many co-founders are there in total? We have Brian, we have Chelsea, Sergey, myself, Rocky, and Adnan. Is it just necessary to have that many co-founders to solve a problem this big, or was it a case that you were already sort of like a unit together, you'd already worked together, and you just, whatever you started, you'd all have wanted to do it together? Yeah, one common question that we have is, "Why band together?" And you know, the first is that we really enjoy each other's company. We spend a lot of time at work and it, you know, in some sense gives meaning to life, and so we really want to enjoy the relationship we have at work. And the second is that, you know, any one of us could have started a company and be successful. But the problem is just so incredibly hard and the chances of success are just so much higher that we banded together and we can divide and conquer the problems. And you know, that's I think one of the main reasons why the progress has been much faster than we expected. What were the differences of you working before in either academia or a big industry, big company like Google, as opposed to now in a startup? Because this is the first time for a lot of you doing a startup, right? Yeah, this is the first time for a lot of us. One of the really surprising things that we learned when we started the company is that the infrastructure for supporting large-scale, general-purpose robots was just not there. And you know, this starts from the software itself. How do you collect data? What device do you use to collect data? How do you manage the data? How do you annotate the data? How you get visibility into the data? How you ensure data quality? How do you then make sure that you can easily train on that data? After you train on that data, how you run evaluation? Evaluation is a really hard problem in robotics because it scales super-linearly to model capability. Like, let's say you have a model that can perform a two-minute task; running evaluation for that is very different from running evaluation for a task that's 20 minutes.
