Like, it's not 10 times harder; it's more than 10 times harder. After you run evaluation, how can you distill the learning from that evaluation to know how to improve the model further? Like, one of the really side projects that I would love to take on is to build an automated robotic research scientist. Which is really one of the bottlenecks we have today because this is a really difficult skill set that requires intuition about the entire stack. So you know, I would love it if there is a model that can ingest multimodal data such as this and analyze failure modes. You know, understanding, "Oh, is the robot performing this way because of the data that was collected, or the way that it was annotated, or the way that we trained the model?" and then suggest ideas and actually try them to figure out if those hypotheses are correct. That's something that I would love to have and would like dramatically unlock us. Sometimes I make the joke in the company that we should record all of the meetings and then train a model to basically just make predictions about what is the next set of experiments. Oh you could. You totally could. What if it's OpenClaw and Obsidian and markdown files and like a brain.md with ontology that's custom to your use case, and what if it's a hundred OpenClaws in the background that you orchestrate? I think there's two sides to this. The first is that we already see a little bit of a sign of life where, for simple failure modes during evaluation, if you can describe the way that the robot failed in text very precisely and very clearly, then you know you can ask a language model to make very reasonable recommendations about what the next step is. But the flip side is that this only works for simple cases today. And the reason why that's the case is because I think it's a pretty fundamental limitation of the models that we have today, which is that they are not, at the core, models that take action in the world and see the consequences of its own action—especially actions that change the physical world. And so I think this kind of very fundamental understanding about how the physical world works is missing from the really large foundation models. I think that's one of the ingredients that's missing to be able to build this automated robot research scientist. What's interesting about OpenClaw—I don't know, I mean basically it can go in, it can just do things, which is interesting. And then at that point, it's on the research lab to provide, like, CLI MCP endpoints to the things that might control robots or reconfigure rooms. I mean, I think Karpathy feels like he's starting to talk a bunch about this where, you know, if you mix auto-research plus what he's been talking about with markdown files, like, it might just happen in the open. You know, there's this sort of sense that you have to make something much, much more complicated to make it work. But what if that's just wrong? What if we just have markdown files and agents and you know, you could make it yourself with literally Claude code and MCP today? What if it's not an algorithm problem, it's just literally an integration challenge? We have a version of this internally that I use a lot. There was a point when I was spending an embarrassingly large amount of money on API queries. And you know, my team was like, "Quan, what are you doing?" Oh, I'm that guy at Y Combinator right now. To give you an example, we have a Claude skill that is essentially serving the role of a "pre-training on-call" today. So you know, we have these pre-training runs that are really large. It's very, I think, a difficult exercise to keep them alive, to keep them continuing to churn, just because there's so many things that can go wrong. And I prototyped a pre-training on-call that kind of baby-sits the run and has the permission to take action to remedy errors that it sees. And one of the surprising outcomes of that exercise is that it leads to about a 50 percent improvement in compute usage—like, just overall compute utilization for that large pre-training run, which is huge for us. And you know, this was just a small simple prototype that I built, and I think like there's a lot more to be done. Quan, this is incredible. Thank you so much for everything. Thank you for making Physical Intelligence, thank you for showing us these incredible demos. And honestly, like, the thing that gives me the most hope is this idea that there's an entity, there's a research lab out there that is focused on giving this to the world to create this Cambrian explosion of robotics startups. So someone watching right now will be inspired by this and start playing with your model, and they might create a robot that touches billions of people's lives for the good. Thank you for having me. It's been a pleasure. To the listener, the one takeaway that I want you to have is: I think robotics has changed a lot, and the cost of building in robotics has decreased and I think will continue to dramatically decrease. And it also requires a very different kind of scrappy skill set that young startups like yours need. We hope to enable really an explosion of many, many different robotic use cases. And you know, always reach out to us if you want to collaborate. Thanks, man. Thanks, man. Thanks so much.

How SambaNova Is Challenging Nvidia With High Performance Inferencing Technology

Forbes(2.1M) ·29:53 ·337 views · 2026-04-16
Rodrigo Liang (CEO and Cofounder, SambaNova)
SambaNova is challenging NVIDIA’s GPU dominance by utilizing a proprietary "data flow" architecture optimized for high-speed, low-power AI inference, specifically targeting the shift toward autonomous agentic workflows and sovereign AI infrastructure.
TL;DR SambaNova is challenging NVIDIA’s GPU dominance by utilizing a proprietary "data flow" architecture optimized for high-speed, low-power AI inference, specifically targeting the shift toward autonomous agentic workflows and sovereign AI infrastructure. SambaNova CEO Rodrigo...

TL;DR

SambaNova is challenging NVIDIA’s GPU dominance by utilizing a proprietary "data flow" architecture optimized for high-speed, low-power AI inference, specifically targeting the shift toward autonomous agentic workflows and sovereign AI infrastructure.

Data Flow Architecture vs. Legacy GPUs

SambaNova CEO Rodrigo Liang argues that while GPUs were essential for the "training" phase of AI, they are fundamentally inefficient for the "inference" phase (production use).

  • Architectural Limitations: GPUs (Graphics Processing Units) are legacy chips designed for gaming and video; data flow chips are designed natively for how neural networks move data.
  • Performance Metrics: SambaNova’s technology achieves 5x to 10x faster inference performance than traditional GPUs.
  • Power Efficiency: A standard NVIDIA rack consumes roughly 140 kilowatts, necessitating nuclear-scale power support; SambaNova’s solution provides higher performance at 10 kilowatts per rack.
  • The "Slow Internet" Analogy: Liang posits that once users experience "fast inference," there will be zero market demand for "slow inference" provided by traditional hardware.

The Shift to "Agentic" Workflows

The hardware requirements for AI are evolving from supporting human-to-AI interactions to supporting complex, serial chains of AI agents.

  • Agent Latency Requirements: Humans typically have a two-second patience threshold for AI responses. In a "serial agent chain" (e.g., a voice agent passing data to a categorization agent, then to a search agent), each individual step must complete in ~0.2 seconds.
  • Beyond Human Reading Speeds: While critics argue SambaNova produces text faster than humans can read, Liang explains the speed is intended for "reading bots" that process vast amounts of data instantaneously to execute complex tasks.
  • Integrated Solutions: To support these agents, SambaNova is partnering with Intel to combine high-speed inference with necessary storage, networking, and systems of record.

Sovereign AI and Global Market Traction

SambaNova is seeing significant adoption among nations seeking "Sovereign AI"—the ability to run national-level AI models within their own borders without relying on American hyperscalers.

  • Infrastructure Compatibility: Many countries lack gigawatt-scale data centers. SambaNova’s chips can run in standard air-cooled data centers (which typically cap at 30 kilowatts per rack).
  • National Security and Culture: Sovereign AI allows countries to:
      • Keep sensitive government and healthcare data "air-gapped" from the public internet.
      • Train models on regional laws, history, and traditions rather than relying on US-centric datasets.
  • Key Partnerships: The company has already announced sovereign cloud deployments in the UK, Germany, Japan (via SoftBank), Australia, and France (via OVH Cloud).

Strategic Growth and Future Outlook

Despite raising $1.4 billion in capital, SambaNova is not aiming to become a cloud provider, but rather a technology enabler for service providers.

  • Commodity vs. Premium Tokens: Liang views NVIDIA chips as providers of "commodity tokens" where margins are thin. SambaNova aims to provide "premium tokens" for real-time, private, and secure AI services.
  • Capital Deployment: The recent $350 million funding round is dedicated to accelerating the chip roadmap; the company recently announced its fifth-generation chip.
  • The 6-Month Prediction: Liang predicts that within six months, the transition to personal agentic fleets—AI that books reservations, writes emails in a user's specific voice, and manages productivity—will be in the hands of everyday users.

***

Notable Quotes

"If there's one thing that they said is, in the world of inference, you need fast inference... there will be no market for slow inference." — Rodrigo Liang
"SambaNova shows up with a faster performance for inference at 10 kilowatts. [NVIDIA is] 140 kilowatts per rack. Now you're completely changing the economics of it." — Rodrigo Liang
"I’m not doing it for you. I’m doing it for the agents. The coming of these agents... you’re producing outcome from one agent to the next... that total has to be 2 seconds. Divided by 10 agents, that is 0.2 seconds. You can't be fast enough." — Rodrigo Liang
Hi, I'm Katharine Schwab. I'm an assistant managing editor here at Forbes covering tech, and I'm so excited to be here today with Rodrigo Liang. He is the CEO and co-founder of SambaNova, one of the big AI chip startups. Rodrigo, thanks for joining me. "Yeah, thanks for having us." "So I want to start with maybe very basic for you. Obviously, when anyone talks about chips and AI, the first thing that comes to mind is one of your biggest competitors, NVIDIA. Can you explain how your chips are distinct from NVIDIA's GPUs?" "Yeah, I mean NVIDIA's done a great job building up this market and building up this space, and yet as the market's evolving what you see is the challenges of scaling AI starting to become clear. You know, it's about cost, it's about power, it's about availability, right? It's about the real-time nature of inference." "And so, as we can discuss in a bit, the AI world is split between training models and inferencing models, right? Where training is creating that model that we all want to try; inferencing is the use of the model. And so, as this world's moving from training to inferencing, there are challenges there. At SambaNova, we've been focused on those exact issues." "When you get into a world where AI is in production and pervasive, and everybody wants to use it, you've got to get the cost down, you've got to get the performance up, and you've got to get the availability widespread. You've got to worry about power, because you can't continue to consume gigawatts and gigawatts of data center power." "And so those are real issues that get created once you go from lab and experimentation over to production, which is where we're entering—the phase of AI we're entering right now." "Okay, so talk to me a little bit about the chips that you offer. I know you've been working on this for many, many years, much predating ChatGPT. How do your chips work, and how do you address some of those challenges that you were just talking about?" "Right, so the base construct of the chip—if you look at NVIDIA's GPUs, GPU stands for graphics processing unit. The history of the GPU was really for graphics, you know, like videos, gaming, and things like that. That's the history of that architecture." "And so the attributes of the graphics processing were very similar to what AI needed for training, right? They're very similar. And so when people started training these models, the CPUs from Intel and AMD weren't good enough at the time, and so they started using these graphics chips as a proxy." "Well, fast forward a few years, we start realizing, you know what? Actually, these neural nets want something completely different than graphics chips, right? And if you look at what it's all about, it's about data. It's about data flowing through the machines." "And so one of the things that my co-founders, Stanford professors, did over years was research: well, if we weren't constrained by the legacy of graphics chips, or the legacies of CPUs, or legacies of other architectures, what is the native architecture that these machine learning models want? Well, it turns out it's this thing called data flow." "Okay, that's what it's called. It's basically all about what is the most efficient way for you to move a lot of data through a machine to get the best output. And so, we created this architecture that's around data flow." "Ultimately, we can go into the details of how we map these models because these models are complex, but the base premise is: move the data as efficiently as you can and don't burn power or time moving data into various different cores and memories and things like that. Those are traditional constructs that GPUs and CPUs have, but data flow allows you to avoid it." "And the net result, right? The net result, if you kind of take all that aside, what do we get on an inferencing machine? We can achieve 5 to 10X faster performance than the GPU at 1/10th the power. Right? 1/10th." "So, if you think about how much power we as a world are consuming per rack of NVIDIA GPUs, today it's 140 kilowatts per rack and you're stacking these racks up side by side. This is why you need nuclear power plants being built to actually support these data centers." "All of that's because the power per rack starts at 140 kilowatts per rack for NVIDIA. SambaNova, we show up with faster performance for inference at 10 kilowatts. 10. Right? So now you're completely changing the economics of it." "You're able to deploy a much faster solution for inference at a fraction of the power, which is now starting to solve the problem I mentioned. Once you go into production, you need to make it much lower cost, much more available, much lower power, and much easier to deploy. That's what we're seeing as the world's transitioning now, and they want to get into production." "Yeah, I would love to hear a little bit more about inference. This is kind of the big buzzword right now, but I think maybe some of our listeners and viewers might not fully understand what that means, and why that requires such a different architecture than training. Can you explain a little bit more about why inference is so important right now?" "Yeah. So, let me start with inference. Training is basically when you're inventing, right? A handful of researchers are working on those machines. And so, think of it in the world of search; there were a handful of people creating the search algorithm at Google, say, or at Yahoo. Those handful of people." "Inference is equivalent to the rest of us on the planet using Google Search or Yahoo Search, right? And so, the scale of inference is just going to be so much larger than the scale of training because there are only so many people that are going to go invent the next algorithm. The rest of us just want to use it." "And so, you look at ChatGPT—the millions and millions of people are using ChatGPT. A handful of people went and created ChatGPT, right? So the training of ChatGPT was localized to a small number of people; the inference part is now hundreds of millions of people, right?" "So, that's kind of what it is, but once you get into that, what are the attribute differences of the hardware architecture? When you train, you have a model that you've invented, you launch it, and you let it run for months. Right? You cluster these things, and you let it run for months because what is it trying to do?" "It's searching for a better pattern. It's searching for—is there a better combination of what we call weights in the world of AI, right? A better set of patterns that better predict what we want? And so that's what you're searching for for months. You don't have to touch it, cluster it together, launch it, and just let it go." "Well, in the real world, inference, the reality is we're an impatient world, right? When we type—" "We sure are." "Yeah, we sure are. That's for sure. Yeah. We want to see it right away. Right?" "If you type it into Chat or into Claude and this thing is not responding in a couple of seconds, you're starting to tap the phone, right? Like something happened. And so now you're not months away from a result, you're instantaneous. That's the world of inference." "And so if you don't have real-time, it closes off the largest portion of the use case for inference. Yeah, you'll have some cases where people can launch something and say, 'Hey, let me do a batch inference; overnight I'll create dozens of reports and they'll be fine.' There's some use case for that, but the large majority of the world of inference is going to be real-time." "We want it, we want it now, we want to see the response, and we want to interact with it. Maybe the response comes in and I want to iterate again and say, 'No, what if you add this?' And so that level of interactivity, that level of real-timeness is just not possible—or just very slow—on training-based machines." "And so, if there's one thing you could take away from GTC this week, it's that traditional GPUs are just not good for inference. That's why they went and made the Groq acquisition. Right? If there's one thing that they said is, in the world of inference, you need fast inference." "And here's what I'll say—it's kind of like the world of internet. When there was internet, when you see fast internet, who wants slow? Right? Who's the market for slow internet, when you saw fast internet? Same with inference." "When you now have the world of fast inference, of which there are only a couple players out there, SambaNova being one, who wants to go back to slow? And I think that that's effectively what you heard from GTC this week." "The one thing that it says is the world of inference is coming; it's here. It's the biggest part of the market, and a traditional GPU is not going to be good enough. It just won't be good enough. You need something more." "That's why they spent $20 billion to acquire Groq, and frankly, there are only a couple players in the market that produce fast inference, which to me is synonymous to just inference, right? Because there will be no market for slow inference." "So talking about NVIDIA again, they acquired the people and assets of Groq back in December—a huge, huge deal in your space. There were, of course, also whispers that you were in acquisition talks with Intel, and you've now announced a big partnership with Intel." "Talk to me about your big partnership with one of the other big chip giants, and how you see that helping you compete against NVIDIA." "Well, so the technology is the first piece. We've been at this—SambaNova has been around for 9 years. Many of the players are startups, right? Somebody gave me a hard time saying you can't consider yourself a startup. In my mind, we are, right? 9 years in." "But in the end, what you're trying to do is create new technology that otherwise would be hard to create in a much bigger established company. That's what we're trying to do. If you look at where SambaNova technology has come for inferencing, it's taken the performance level by an order of magnitude and driven down the cost and the power by a significant amount." "So, that's where we've landed. Now you have the tech. One of the things that you see in the infrastructure space, whether that's selling chips, selling racks, or building data centers, is you need scale. You need capital." "You need people that are able to go and invest and say, 'Look, we're going to pump in a huge amount of money, and we're going to go and deploy this not in hundreds and thousands at a time, but tens of thousands or hundreds of thousands at a time.'" "And at that level, you know, for startups like ours—and we're very well funded as a startup; we've raised 1.4 billion in capital in the duration of this company—you're going to take it to the next level where the scale that a partner like Intel brings is incredibly valuable." "These companies have established partnerships out there that can deploy and channel and support, and all of that is incredibly valuable. We've now gotten to the point where technology is so well understood for us. We just announced a couple weeks ago our fifth-generation chip." "So we're multiple generations in; we've evolved it, we've tuned it, and we've tweaked it. Now we know how good it can be, and it's attracting a lot of opportunities for us to partner with folks for whom this becomes an added piece into the portfolio of technologies necessary in order to build the next-generation cloud." "So why did you decide to partner and not join forces fully with Intel?" "Well, I mean, without going into all the details of what's happened or not happened, or what's rumor or not, let me talk about what the partnership really does. You know, if you think about it, there are three things that companies like ours really value in this space." "One, we already talked about, is the scale of a company. The number of people out there that can help promote the product and help get into data centers increases by an order of magnitude for us. But then you also need the capital, right? That upfront capital." "Once you go beyond that, now you start thinking about what's coming next. The next wave—we're talking about inference now—is production inference doing LLMs, whether that's Claude or Anthropic or these amazing open-source models, right?" "You look at a model like MiniMax for code agent; it's free. It's hard to compete with free when you're looking at open-source models. There's an attractiveness when people are thinking about heterogeneous setups and using multiple things. If one component is free, it reduces your average cost by a lot." "But we think about the broader technology landscape and what's coming next. What's coming next is the world of AgentTech. And to the extent that you or I are impatient people, these agents are incredibly impatient." "These agents don't care; they're constantly trying to create new traffic and constantly trying to engage with each other. When SambaNova first produced our inference results, it was number one in the world. In a single rack, we were generating thousands of tokens per second." "And people were complaining to me, it's like, 'Rodrigo, why? This is faster than I can read, you've overdone it, it's too fast.' Too fast? No such thing, right? Well, because with ChatGPT, we've streamed it and you can see the lines printed at a time. This was just a proof; it's just a big page." "People complain, 'You know, this is way beyond what's necessary.' And my answer was, 'Actually, I'm not doing it for you. I'm doing it for the agents. I'm doing it for the agents.' The coming of these agents means bots reading bots, and you're producing outcomes from one agent to the next." "That solution is very complicated, and that's why we need additional products that a partner like Intel might have. But you have to have it really, really fast. Think about this: maybe our patience is about two seconds. I type something in, and in two seconds I need to have something come back." "Well, these agents are going to get strung, 10 of them, serially. I have my voice agent that's going to interpret what I just said. That agent is going to pass it to a categorization agent that's going to decide who to distribute that work to. Then somebody else is going to pick it up and say, 'Oh, he wants to understand stock charts.'" "So that agent is going to go and fetch some charts, and then I'm going to go and interpret. You have 10 agents serially; that total has to be 2 seconds. 2 seconds divided by 10 is 0.2 seconds, right? When your latency per agent is not 2 seconds but 0.2 seconds, you can't be fast enough." "This is where you start getting into how we can partner with others to create faster agents that do this breadth of things. As we know, our core competence is: we do inference really fast. We just do it faster than anybody else, and we can do it across more agents than anybody else." "Now I need storage because I've got to go fetch some data; I need networking because I've got to go look up some things on the internet. As these agents get more and more sophisticated, they want to tie into systems of record and real-time news feeds. Now you start to need partners." "You need partnerships that give you access to all these other pieces so that you can create these complete solutions. At the core, we can run faster than anybody else and build better technology for inference, but now you're starting to have to tie into the rest of the world. This is where our partnership with Intel is incredibly helpful." "You just raised $350 million. What is the strategy for deploying that capital and getting to the next stage of the company?" "Well, look, there are many different ways that people are going to market. Today, one very popular one is people building out their own clouds. I build my own cloud, I put hardware in, and then I lease it to you. I charge by token or I charge by month. That's a very common way of doing this." "SambaNova—that's not our strategy. Our strategy today is actually to build technologies that service providers want to buy. So, for us, when we raised the money, it wasn't to build an additional cloud; we raised the money to accelerate our chip roadmap." "I can build better products for service providers, hyperscale cloud players, and sovereign cloud players to come in and say, 'I need a differentiated solution.' My entire footprint in my data center today is commodity. It's commodity. I paid a lot of money to NVIDIA and I'm serving the same models everyone else is serving." "The users are just looking for the cheapest place to get that. That's the reality of AI data centers today. You're caught in this race for who can offer NVIDIA chips the cheapest. In the meantime, of course, NVIDIA is very proud that they claim a 75% gross margin." "So they've got their pricing, and everybody else has to figure out how to commercialize within the remaining 25%, which is incredibly difficult. If you think about how that structure has to work, what we're trying to do is come in and say, 'Why don't I give you premium services?'" "I'm going to offer you premium services. If you want real-time, come to SambaNova. If you want custom checkpoints, come to SambaNova. If you want private and secure AI—because you have private data you want hosted in a place where you don't have to expose it—you come to SambaNova." "We then give that technology to enable these companies to offer those services. Now you have differentiated services: you have the NVIDIA commodity servers that produce the generic tokens, and you have SambaNova producing premium tokens. Our investment is going into accelerating those premium services." "How can I get you more services so you, as a service provider or cloud provider, can offer premium? That's the only way service providers will be able to make better margins on AI—by offering these premium services to blend into your commodity services." "So do you work then with the big hyperscalers, and where have your chips been deployed so far?" "We have engagements with all the major hyperscalers. If you're a customer that's sitting on AWS today, you can actually secure SambaNova cycles in order to operate your infrastructure. But today, we're mostly seeing traction in sovereign clouds." "Sovereign AI is becoming such an important aspect globally. Most people understand that I can use ChatGPT, OpenAI, or Anthropic. Most companies and most countries know this, and yet many countries have an initiative that says, 'No, we also need our own model and our own secure sovereign data center.'" "In some countries, the data cannot leave the borders, so we need a sovereign place where technology can sit and host all of our private national data without having to go outside the borders of the country. SambaNova has become incredibly valuable because many of these countries don't have gigawatt data centers." "What they do have are standard air-cooled data centers that are 30 kilowatts or less. 30 kilowatts is significantly less than NVIDIA's 140 kilowatts per rack. When SambaNova shows up at 10 kilowatts, it becomes an incredibly valuable asset for them." "They can say, 'Hey, I already have the data centers, it runs faster, and I can do sovereign private models locally. It looks the same as any other standard model, so why wouldn't I do that?' We're really excited by this Sovereign AI push that we see today." "Many countries are driving hard to have this heterogeneity. They understand they'll have the Microsofts and AWSs and Anthropics, but they also want something that's sovereign and of their own. They're looking for very energy-efficient, very high-performance solutions in order to operate this." "Can you share what countries you're working with?" "Yeah, we've announced a bunch of them recently. In the last 6 months or so, we have announced one in Australia—a Sovereign Cloud in Australia. This is all through partnerships. This is not SambaNova funding it; this is local entities building their business and then using SambaNova technology to power them." "We have one sovereign cloud in Australia, one in Germany, one in the UK. We have a partnership with OVHcloud in France to help build the premium services for the French Cloud, and we have a big one in Japan through our partners at SoftBank." "A number of them that we've already announced continue to build those out because they have significant national-level investment to build those types of models. They fund local entities and businesses in order for them to actually build out that infrastructure." "We're really proud of the fact that we've got partnerships with those national-level entities that are powering those sovereign missions. Is that a national security concern for these countries to have that totally secure data center that they control where nothing is leaving the borders? Is that really about national security and trust?" "Yeah, so a portion is. A big portion of that is if you look at their government use cases. We have some projects in the Middle East that are certainly like that, where we're building out these data centers for governments for national security purposes as on-prem." "It's in their own secure data center. It's air-gapped, right? Air-gapped means there's no in or out for internet traffic that's not controlled. That's certainly a very, very important part of this. There are lots of use cases in government and healthcare that have to be managed differently than putting it on an open domain." "Beyond the national security use cases, there's also a whole motion around the fact that they want to build their own models that are more relevant to their own country. For example, a lot of the legal opinions that our models represent are going to be more US-based because we've trained on US data and local laws." "If you are in other parts of the world, they may want to have models trained based on regional laws and traditions. So you're going to see this interest in creating more native models so that they can deploy something that is fitting for their country." "It's beyond just the security piece. If you think longer term, which countries do, the horizon is longer. They say, 'I want something that represents everything that we have—our history, our culture, our laws. It's us.' They want to capture that and have their citizens use that model versus using an American model." "Okay, one more question for you. Big picture: since you are right in the middle of all of these conversations right now—putting AI into production, the need for inference at such massive scale—where are we going? Five years from now, how do you think the world will be different?" "I think every single human on this planet is going to have a set of agents that they rely on—just a set of agents that are custom to you. Every single one of us has a set of internet tools and social media apps that we completely rely on, right?" "Prior to the internet, we weren't relying on Google Search for a lot of things, and now we do this every single day. Think of the next wave of technology as agents. These agents are going to help us do things, including, 'Hey, just write this document for me,' or 'Summarize all the things that happened this past week for me.'" "These agents are going to be, first and foremost, productivity agents that I think all of us are just going to be completely relying on. I think we're going to forget what the world looked like before I had that. You mean I had to sit down and compose an email that didn't sound like me? That’s crazy." "Now, I think you're going to get to the point where you have your own agent that will learn from the way you actually write, and it's going to say, 'Do you want to respond like this?' Those are the types of things that are going to be basics in our toolbox that we use every day, whether professionally or socially." "Think about how we book a reservation at a restaurant. Instead of calling and looking it up, you’ll just say, 'I need to meet with this person, somewhere around here, with this type of food.' It will figure it out and make the reservation. I think we're going to be so used to having these agents do stuff for us that there won't be any going back." "It’s just like how we used to pick up the phone and call a restaurant and check availability, whereas now we can go to OpenTable and just figure out what's there. Now you can just ask the agent, 'Hey, figure this out for me. I need a restaurant for 3 people.' And then it will just do it." "I think that wave is coming. I think all of us are going to use it and depend on it. I think it's going to be far more the scenario that productivity increases rather than the broad-based worry that this is going to limit all these jobs. People are going to be more free to create and connect." "They won't be bogged down by having to figure out how to transcribe a document because they need to read it. Those things, I think, will all be done for us." "Well, that's a lovely vision of the future. I look forward to having my own fleet of agents to make all sorts of restaurant reservations for me without me having to do it myself." "That will be in your hands in 6 months—this is what my prediction is. In 6 months, you’ll have an app that will do all things for you." "Amazing. Rodrigo, thanks so much for joining me." "Awesome. Thanks for having us."

Taiwan's Market Cap Overtakes UK's – April 16, 2026

TaiwanPlus Livestream(11.3K) ·27:01 ·395 views · 2026-04-16
> "Wei helped move the world from mobile-first to AI-first computing...
TL;DR: Driven by an AI-fueled 58% surge in TSMC’s quarterly profits, Taiwan’s market capitalization has surpassed US$4 trillion, overtaking the U.K. to become the world's seventh-largest market. However, the nation faces simultaneous challenges from U.S. trade investigations, a...

TL;DR: Driven by an AI-fueled 58% surge in TSMC’s quarterly profits, Taiwan’s market capitalization has surpassed US$4 trillion, overtaking the U.K. to become the world's seventh-largest market. However, the nation faces simultaneous challenges from U.S. trade investigations, a stalled US$40 billion special defense budget, and rising regional tensions in the South China Sea.

TSMC Record Performance and Global Market Expansion

  • Record Profits: TSMC reported a record first-quarter net profit of US$18.2 billion, a 58% year-on-year increase.
      • AI Demand: Growth is primarily driven by 3-nanometer technology and advanced packaging required for high-end AI chips.
      • Market Standing: The surge has pushed Taiwan’s total market capitalization past US$4 trillion, surpassing the United Kingdom.
  • Overseas Challenges: Despite financial success, TSMC faces rising costs related to its global expansion.
      • Capital Expenditure: The company plans to invest US$30 billion this year as part of a US$165 billion long-term commitment to U.S.-based production.
      • Margin Pressures: Analysts expressed concern regarding rising costs of semiconductor materials and the financial burden of the Arizona plant expansion.
  • Leadership Recognition: TSMC CEO C.C. Wei was named to the *Time 100* most influential people list, nominated by Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang for leading the shift from mobile-first to AI-first computing.

U.S. Trade Investigations and Diplomatic Response

  • Section 301 Probes: The U.S. has opened two investigations into Taiwan’s trade practices, focusing on the large trade surplus and allegations of imports involving forced labor.
  • Government Position: Former Trade Representative John Deng emphasized that Taiwan is treating these probes with "serious manner," arguing that the trade surplus is a result of complementary technology supply chains.
  • Agreement Stability: Officials hope to preserve recent trade and investment deals, arguing they are comprehensive enough to address the U.S. Trade Representative's (USTR) historical concerns regarding trade barriers.

Defense Budget and Regional Security

  • Legislative Standoff: While the 2026 general budget is moving forward, a US$40 billion special defense budget remains stalled in the legislature.
      • Opposition Concerns: The KMT and TPP parties have questioned the transparency of weapon procurement and the long-term strain on national finances.
      • Strategic Necessity: Defense Minister Wellington Koo maintains the budget is essential to counter Chinese aggression through a mix of domestic production and commercial procurement.
  • Balikatan Military Drills: The Philippines, U.S., and several allies—including Japan for the first time as a full participant—are launching major maritime exercises.
      • Taiwan Contingency: Experts note the drills focus on the Bashi Channel, a critical chokepoint for Taiwan’s southern security.
      • Regional Tensions: The drills coincide with reports of China building barriers at Scarborough Shoal and firing flares at Philippine aircraft.

National Security and Espionage

  • Infiltration Tactics: A Hong Kong national, surnamed Ding, recently died while standing trial for allegedly recruiting six Taiwanese military personnel under the cover of religious exchanges.
      • Financial Incentives: Prosecutors allege Ding was paid over US$350,000 by Beijing to build a spy network spanning the Army and Air Force.
  • Legislative Reform: Defense Minister Wellington Koo acknowledged that 90% of espionage cases are reported by service members themselves but noted the need for stiffer legal deterrents.
      • Sentence Disparity: Analysts point out that Taiwan’s average sentences for espionage are significantly lighter than those in the U.S. or Europe, leading to calls for the restoration of the military court system.

Notable Quotes

"Wei helped move the world from mobile-first to AI-first computing... under his leadership TSMC expanded into Japan and the U.S." — Jensen Huang, Nvidia CEO (via Time 100 nomination)
"U.S. now is the largest export market of Taiwan products. So we have to treat this investigation in a very serious manner." — John Deng, Taiwan's former trade representative
"If the U.S., Philippines, Japan and other countries can secure the South China Sea, Taiwan will face fewer threats from that direction." — Unnamed Security Expert (via TaiwanPlus report)
"Compared with other countries, sentences in Taiwan for military espionage are indeed relatively lighter... The defense ministry is currently studying restoring the military court system." — Wellington Koo, Taiwan Defense Minister
More record-breaking profits for TSMC, but how long can the Taiwanese chip giant ride the AI wave? Taiwan responds to U.S. investigations into its trade surplus and forced labor allegations. After months of disagreements, Taiwan's legislature moves forward with the 2026 general budget. Plus, tensions flare in the South China Sea ahead of joint military drills. A warm welcome to TaiwanPlus News. I'm Leslie Liao. Taiwanese chip giant TSMC is riding the global AI wave, posting record quarterly profits. But rising costs and massive overseas expansion are raising new questions. Lily LaMattina has more. 2026 is already shaping up to be a strong year for TSMC. The company posted record profits at its quarter one earnings call this Thursday, driven by huge demand for its most advanced AI chips. TSMC says net profit jumped 58% in the first quarter, beating expectations. It made about US$18.2 billion from January to March, its highest quarterly profit ever. That growth is coming from its most advanced chips, including 3-nanometer technology and packaging used in AI. Analysts say demand is now outpacing supply, with major clients like Nvidia and Apple relying on TSMC. But despite the strong results, some warn the company's stock may not keep rising. "Further growth potential is very limited and the stock price could easily experience a pullback. TSMC's earnings calls are always very impressive, but they're just one-offs. Usually, for about a week after an earnings call, the stock price generally remains in consolidation mode." So even as earnings surge, short-term momentum could slow. Still, TSMC's rally has helped lift Taiwan's broader market. Taiwan's total market capitalization has now topped US$4 trillion, overtaking the U.K. to become the world's seventh largest. At the same time, the company is ramping up spending overseas, raising concerns about rising costs. It plans to invest up to US$30 billion this year into its global operations, part of a wider US$165 billion effort to build up chip production in the United States. "This year, many semiconductor materials and industrial gases have seen significant price increases. The Arizona plant is also continuing to expand its production scale, which will put a financial burden on TSMC. I think everyone is concerned about whether TSMC's foundry prices will be adjusted upward this year." That raises questions about margins and profits going forward. So while demand for AI chips remains strong, rising costs and global expansion efforts will be key considerations to watch out for this year. Joseph Wu and Lily LaMattina in Taipei for TaiwanPlus. C.C. Wei, the head of Taiwanese chip giant TSMC, has made the Time 100 most influential people list. The magazine listed the CEO and chair in this year's "pioneer" category, citing his leadership as pushing tech innovation at the company. Wei was nominated by Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, who made the list last year. Huang says Wei helped move the world from mobile-first to AI-first computing, and under his leadership helped TSMC expand into Japan and the U.S. Taiwan has responded to U.S. investigations into its trade practices ahead of public hearings on them. The U.S. opened the two Section 301 probes last month. One looks into Taiwan's large trade surplus with the U.S., while the other is focused on imports of goods made with forced labor. Taipei says its surplus comes from its complementary trade ties with the U.S., pointing to the two countries' tech industries. And it says it's working with businesses to uphold international human rights standards on imports. Taiwan is hoping to maintain trade and investment agreements it signed with Washington earlier this year. For more on the government's response to these U.S. trade probes, our reporter Ai Chi spoke with John Deng, Taiwan's former trade representative. "The U.S. Trade Representative opened these Section 301 investigations in March. So what motivation might Taiwan have in providing its response now?" "So Taiwan government thinks this is a channel that we can use, present our side of stories, our side of arguments, so that they take advantage of this option, provide additional information that might be useful for the U.S. government. U.S. now is the largest export market of Taiwan products. So we have to treat this investigation in a very serious manner." "How far do you think this response will go in persuading the U.S. to refrain from penalizing Taiwan?" "It's difficult to say. It is up to U.S. government when they receive this information from Taiwan side. They then have to decide whether that the facts and the argument presented by Taiwan government is persuasive enough for them to decide that Taiwan is not engaged in unfair trade practice. Taiwan has agreed to do many, many measures to open up Taiwan's market and to change our regulations to meet the U.S. demands." "How might the investigations impact the trade investment deals Taiwan recently signed with the U.S.?" "President Trump and his senior officials, my reading and my sense is they want—they also want to keep the agreement. After reading this trade agreement, you will be surprised how comprehensive, it is a very, very detailed agreement. It almost covers all those so-called trade barriers that over the years that USTR collected from the U.S. private industries. So it is a very comprehensive, it serves the interest of the U.S. business, it also almost solves the trade tensions between U.S. and Taiwan." That was Taiwan's former trade representative John Deng. Funding for Taiwan's government could finally be on the way after the legislature moved forward on the 2026 general budget. But a separate special defense funding bill proposed by President Lai Ching-te isn't seeing much progress. Nearly halfway into the year, Taiwan's legislature is finally moving forward with the government's 2026 general budget. Ruling and opposition party lawmakers have agreed to move the budget to the next stage after Premier Cho Jung-tai gives a report on it next week. But a separate US$40 billion special defense budget put forth by President Lai Ching-te is still being debated in the legislature. For months, the United States, which sells most military equipment to Taiwan, has been pushing for the budget to pass. One retired U.S. admiral is in Taiwan meeting with legislative speaker Han Kuo-yu to make their case. "And I'm excited to be here with you because Jason tells me you're the man who will solve the defense bill problem." The ruling Democratic Progressive Party says the full budget is needed to fend off Chinese aggression. But the opposition Kuomintang and the Taiwan People's Party have both proposed alternative budgets, committing a lot less than Lai's version. They say Lai's proposal does not make it clear where the money would go and when Taiwan would receive the weapons. "Our future spending will go beyond this US$40 billion special budget. Will this put a long-term strain on the country's finances? There has to be some concrete figure, right?" But Defense Minister Wellington Koo says the entire budget is necessary for the country's security. "The KMT says arms purchases are necessary. In that case, we will propose a mix of domestically produced and commercially procured items and then I will present it to lawmakers." With the general budget now making progress, the question now is, what will come of the special budget? Howard Zhang and Leslie Liao for TaiwanPlus. Local media are reporting that the main opposition Kuomintang chair's recent visit to China was funded by a group sanctioned by Beijing. Mirror Media says the Taiwan Foundation for Democracy paid over US$150,000 for Cheng Li-wen's trip. The KMT says it "legally applied" to the foundation for the visit without confirming whether it received funding. The foundation is a government-funded nonprofit that supports democracy and human rights and provides funding to political parties for related activities. It says it has not provided funding for this trip. The foundation was sanctioned by the Chinese Communist Party in 2022 for supporting what they call pro-independence separatist forces. A group of six Nigerian journalists visited Taiwan for exchanges hosted by the foreign ministry talking misinformation and social media scams. "So we have to make the world know that we are different by fact-checking and not be interested in breaking the story. We should be interested in sending factual information out there to the public." The reporters met with local media professionals to learn about Taiwan's news environment. It's one of 12 groups of visiting foreign journalists sponsored by the ministry to share Taiwan's perspective with the world. During their stay in Taipei, they also met with the Taiwan-Africa Business Association and Foreign Minister Lin Chia-lung. Lin urged Nigeria's government to relocate Taiwan's trade office from the former capital Lagos to the current one in Abuja. Coming up after the break, China cracks down on Catholics trying to avoid its state-controlled churches. At Connected, we believe in the power of dialogue. It's not just about the headlines, it's about finding common ground. Join the conversation. Connected on TaiwanPlus. Welcome back. You're watching TaiwanPlus News. One person is missing after a Taiwanese fishing boat sank near Japan. Japan's Coast Guard says the boat caught fire early Thursday morning about 265 kilometers north of Ishigaki Island in Okinawa. A search operation is underway for the boat's missing Taiwanese captain while six foreign crew members were rescued. A Taiwanese Coast Guard patrol vessel is on its way to help with the search. Tensions are rising between Manila and Beijing over the disputed South China Sea. This comes as the Philippines, the U.S., and several other nations prepare for this year's Balikatan maritime drills. Lery Hiciano has more on what's going on. Things are getting hot in the South China Sea. In the past week alone, the Philippine Coast Guard has accused China of poisoning local fish and of firing flares at its planes. While Reuters says China is building barriers to block access to the Scarborough Shoal. Several countries have overlapping claims in the sea, including Taiwan, and incidents in the area are common. Beijing has militarized several islands to strengthen its own claims. This recent spike in tensions comes just before the Philippines, the U.S., and other countries hold joint military drills. Those start Monday and run until early May. Exercises will be held across the Philippines, with more of a focus on regional security. "It has moved from a focus on counterterrorism to now territorial integrity, i.e., South China Sea, Taiwan contingency. And I would say that they're very big in the sense of the multilateral aspect of it, but also some of the capabilities that they've tested out in the last few years, right? So they've done more related to long-range firing capability, wanting to maintain denial for things like the Bashi Channel or parts of the South China Sea/West Philippine Sea." Since it's not a participant, why should Taiwan care about these drills? Experts say what happens to the south of the country has major implications for its security. "Although Taiwan is not participating, it is crucial to Taiwan's security. Taiwan has no barrier to its north but to the south is the Bashi Channel." "If the U.S., Philippines, Japan and other countries can secure the South China Sea, Taiwan will face fewer threats from that direction. Though Taiwan is not participating, it can still coordinate with other countries." Taiwan is not the only country keeping a closer eye on the region. Australia, France, Canada, and others are also joining the drills. And for the first time, Japan is joining Balikatan as a full participant with combat troops, upping its involvement from just observing. With so many countries involved, Taiwan and China will both be watching the drills closely. Chris Ma and Lery Hiciano for TaiwanPlus. A new report from NGO Human Rights Watch says that China is persecuting Catholics who refuse to join its state-controlled church. The report says Chinese President Xi Jinping has curbed religious freedom in China under a decade-long campaign to stamp out foreign influences. Underground Catholic communities say they've been harassed and arrested for refusing to submit to the Chinese Communist Party. The churches have shrunk in number and influence after the Holy See and China signed a 2018 agreement allowing Beijing to nominate bishops. To learn more about the report, our reporter Irene Lin spoke with Yalkun Uluyol, a China researcher at Human Rights Watch and a co-author of the report. "How has Beijing's attitude towards religions evolved over time?" "Since Xi Jinping came to power, the increasing escalation of religious repression is more evident both from people's testimonies and also from the official documents that we have reviewed." "Especially this month marks the 10-year anniversary of the Sinicization campaign initiated by Xi Jinping in 2016, which basically calls for all religions to follow, you know, the Han-centric Chinese Communist Party ideology." "What is the current state of Catholic churches in China, particularly the communities that you've described as underground?" "Catholics who are part of the government-run Chinese Catholic Patriotic Association is the only recognized church in China. So if, you know, churches are not part of this Patriotic Association, then they are not recognized by Beijing and they are therefore subjected to persecution, repression, and intimidation and harassment because they pledge loyalty only to the Pope, not the Chinese Communist Party." "How has the 2018 agreement between the Vatican and Beijing affected Catholics in China?" "Underground communities are not going to have new bishops because according to the agreement, all bishops the Vatican is going to approve will be, you know, proposed by Beijing. If Beijing does not propose any candidacy, then the Vatican does not, you know, cannot appoint bishops to those churches. Therefore, underground communities feel betrayed by the Vatican." That was China researcher Yalkun Uluyol of Human Rights Watch. A Hong Kong national indicted for recruiting Taiwanese military personnel as spies has died during his trial. As Alan Lu reports, he was accused of using religious exchanges as cover for his activities. Taking his secrets to the grave, an alleged spy for China dies from illness while standing trial for espionage in Taiwan. Prosecutors say the Hong Kong national surnamed Ding used Mazu religious exchanges as a front to enter Taiwan in 2018. Acting under the direction of China, they say he was paid more than US$350,000 to recruit six current and former Taiwanese military personnel. This spy network spanned the Army and Air Force and was tasked with gathering intelligence and even persuading active-duty officers not to resist if war broke out. Ding died in February, but six other defendants were sentenced on similar charges on April 15th. "I am still pained that a small number of service members have violated their duty of loyalty. We also need to make it clear that infiltration is a serious concern but we have also seen that nearly 90 percent of cases are reported by service members themselves." Koo says the government will strengthen security awareness and vetting for service members and carry out damage control over any leaked information. But he didn't specify how. China claims Taiwan as part of its territory, threatens to use violence to seize the country, and uses spies to infiltrate its armed forces, sometimes like in this case, using shared cultural ties to gain access. Analysts say this kind of infiltration is a multi-stage process. "First, low-sensitivity contact such as religious or cultural exchange. In the second stage, the relationship building, including sustained communications or small financial interactions. And the third, tasking such as introducing specific individuals or requesting certain information." One reason why this is so effective, say others, is because of a lack of legal deterrent. They say compared to places like Europe and the U.S., penalties for national security cases in Taiwan are very light. "Compared with other countries, sentences in Taiwan for military espionage are indeed relatively lighter. The average sentence in other countries can reach around 19 years." "The defense ministry is currently studying restoring the military court system with the aim of ensuring that active-duty personnel are subject to the Armed Forces Penal Code so as to provide a more effective deterrent." While the state considers stiffer punishments, authorities have their hands full, with 10 other people facing prosecution in separate espionage cases in southern Taiwan. This time, the cases involve infiltrating the country's Coast Guard and drone units, showing that beyond legal reforms, there's also a need to step up prevention and awareness. Fu-hua Hung and Alan Lu for TaiwanPlus. A 12-year-old boy has made rare medical history in Taiwan following the successful transplants of two live adult organs. Now in recovery, his dream is to go traveling. "I want to travel overseas like go skiing in Japan." The boy was born with a rare disease and has struggled to live normally. In separate surgeries, he received his father's kidney and part of his mother's liver. His parents say the process was a physical, emotional, and financial challenge. The team of surgeons said the procedure was also difficult. Live organ transplants from an adult donor to a child are extremely challenging. "First, we had to consider the size of his abdomen and how large of a hernia mesh was needed. Next, we had to check whether his blood vessels were compatible. There's also the risk of infection and whether his heart could handle circulating blood with the new organs." The boy still requires some post-surgery monitoring but he has already returned back to school. The great Baishatun Mazu pilgrimage, a big moment in Taiwan's religious calendar, has reached Yunlin County in Central Taiwan. Nearly half a million people have registered for this eight-day trek, thankful for miracles they say they've received or praying for divine intervention. John Van Trieste reports. In Central Taiwan, the annual Baishatun Mazu procession marches southward across this bridge into Yunlin County. It is technically closed for repairs, but the sea goddess Mazu, beloved by so many, got special permission to cross. The route is decided through divination and is unpredictable. There are sprints, frequent unplanned detours, and sudden stops. Keeping up is tough, but the faithful are committed, many bringing stories of Mazu's miracles, including sick children cured through prayer. "I brought her to pray Mazu would protect her and keep her safe and that she could slowly get off the oxygen machine. At around one year old not long after I'd prayed to Mazu she slowly stopped needing the machine." There are stories of love matches at processions in years past. This couple met when this young woman walked the route carrying a "boyfriend wanted" sign. They're back this year to show their thanks. "I saw her carrying a sign and just took a picture. Then I kept walking. Once I got to Beigang and went home to rest I sent her a message." Many other believers are hoping for a miracle of their own. A mother prays for her paralyzed eight-year-old and a son prays for his father sick with cancer. And there is this woman, praying the goddess can help find her missing elderly mother-in-law where the police and missing person reports have failed. She gets a brief audience with the goddess after the carriage makes an unexpected U-turn in her direction. "I sincerely prayed to Mazu. She will definitely hear my heartfelt words. I really don't have any direction anymore. I really don't know what to do." As the goddess hears pleas like this, the believers look out for one another too. This truck following the crowd offers laundry service and hot showers for exhausted pilgrims on this multi-day trek, a roughly 400-kilometer loop through Central Taiwan. For those who believe in Mazu's mercy and miracles, there's a sense of community and togetherness as they offer prayers for well-being, love, and health amidst heartbreak. Luffy Li and John Van Trieste for TaiwanPlus. Thank you for watching TaiwanPlus News. You can visit the TaiwanPlus website or follow our social media for more stories from Taiwan and around the world. Finally, a corpse flower is in bloom at a U.S. greenhouse, drawing visitors eager to catch a whiff. Why would you ever? I'm Leslie Liao. Take care and see you next time.

U.S. Leadership in AI with Jensen Huang, Founder and CEO of NVIDIA, and Congressman Ro Khanna

Stanford Graduate School of Business(2.7M) ·58:39 ·13.9K views · 2026-04-16
Jensen Huang (Founder and CEO, NVIDIA) Ro Khanna (U.S. Congressman)
To maintain global leadership, the U.S.
TL;DR To maintain global leadership, the U.S. must foster a complete "five-layer" AI industrial stack—from energy to applications—while prioritizing widespread technology adoption and a "21st-century Marshall Plan" to reindustrialize the American workforce. Jensen Huang...

TL;DR

To maintain global leadership, the U.S. must foster a complete "five-layer" AI industrial stack—from energy to applications—while prioritizing widespread technology adoption and a "21st-century Marshall Plan" to reindustrialize the American workforce.

The Five-Layer AI Industrial Stack

Jensen Huang redefined AI as a shift from "retrieval-based" computing to "generative" computing, describing data centers as "AI factories" that turn electricity into tokens. He identified five critical layers where the U.S. must lead:

  • The Industrial Layers:
      • Energy and Chips: The foundational hardware and power requirements.
      • Infrastructure: Cloud-based "AI factories."
      • Models and Applications: The software and the specific industry use cases.
  • The Importance of Diffusion:
      • The Application Layer: Huang argued this is the most vital layer. If the U.S. regulates AI so heavily that industries cannot adopt it, the "flywheel" of innovation will fail.
      • Infrastructure Investment: NVIDIA plans to invest half a trillion dollars in domestic manufacturing, but this requires a thriving, unregulated business environment to be viable.

U.S. Competitive Advantages and Vulnerabilities

Congressman Ro Khanna and Jensen Huang debated the pillars of American dominance and the risks of global interdependence.

  • Human Capital and Research:
      • Global Talent: Immigrants have founded 60% of AI startups; 72% of top AI researchers in the U.S. received undergraduate degrees abroad.
      • Institutional Strength: The U.S. hosts 14 of the world’s top 20 research universities, supported by a unique "tech transfer" model and academic freedom.
  • The "National Treasure" Industries:
      • Huang noted that Computing and Finance are the two U.S. industries that lead so decisively they do not require subsidies or protection—unlike the automotive or energy sectors.
  • The Dependency Dilemma:
      • Supply Chain Realities: Huang called the idea of "decoupling" from China "insane" and "uninformed," noting that the U.S. energy and infrastructure sectors rely deeply on Chinese minerals and components.
      • Strategic Rebalancing: Khanna advocated for a "New Economic Patriotism," using strategic tariffs and an industrial development bank to end China’s monopoly on rare earths and pharmaceutical ingredients.

Workforce Transformation: Tasks vs. Jobs

Both speakers addressed the "AI doom" narrative regarding job displacement, suggesting that AI will change the nature of work rather than eliminate the need for workers.

  • The Radiologist Paradox:
      • Despite AI’s ability to automate scan readings, the demand for radiologists has increased. Huang argued that while tasks are automated, the purpose of the job remains, often expanding in scale (e.g., moving from a billion lines of code to a trillion).
  • The Adoption Gap:
      • Usage as Security: "It is unlikely most people will lose a job to AI; it is most likely that people will lose a job to someone who *uses* AI," said Huang.
  • Democratization of Opportunity:
      • Khanna emphasized that the benefits of AI must not be concentrated among billionaires. He proposed a federal "affirmative jobs agenda" to ensure the working class gains bargaining rights and ownership in the AI-driven economy.

Regulation and National Security

The discussion concluded with a focus on how to regulate AI without stifling the "head-start" the U.S. currently enjoys.

  • Application-Specific Regulation: Huang argued for regulating the *use* of AI (e.g., in healthcare or flight) rather than the underlying technology itself, warning against "premature regulation" that suffocates nascent industries.
  • Setting Global Standards: Khanna suggested that U.S. AI should be the global "gold standard" for safety and privacy. By keeping certain models open-source, the U.S. can ensure American values—rather than Chinese standards—govern the global AI landscape.
  • The "Middle Path": Both speakers rejected "polar extremes," advocating for a nuanced approach that allows the U.S. to compete fiercely with China without succumbing to "anti-Chinese" sentiment that could drive away the world's best talent.
"The concept to decouple [from China] is insane. It's deeply uninformed... In these interdependent worlds, having a big picture understanding of how the world and the systems work is helpful." — Jensen Huang, Founder and CEO of NVIDIA
"I don't view that as being an AI doomer... I’m an AI democratist. I want this technology to work for everyone... We need Silicon Valley asking what this region can do for America, not what America can do for Silicon Valley." — Ro Khanna, U.S. Congressman (CA-17)
"It is most likely that most people will lose a job to somebody who uses AI. And so we have to make sure that everybody uses AI." — Jensen Huang, Founder and CEO of NVIDIA
Well, good afternoon, everyone. I'm delighted to see such a full house and delighted to have the opportunity to kick off what I hope will be an absolutely fabulous discussion. We are gathering right now at a moment of extraordinary transformation in artificial intelligence. AI, of course, is not just reshaping business education, but it's raising important questions about US leadership, economic and national security, and the future of work itself. At the GSB, it is our responsibility to not only help our students to understand this technology, but to understand the broader context in which AI leaders, policymakers, and practitioners are making decisions. This is exactly why we are launching the Stanford Leadership Institute now. The institute brings together academics, policymakers, practitioners, and our students to help develop an understanding of the very context in which leadership takes place. My hope is that today's discussion will surface practical insights, raise important questions, and deepen all of our understanding of how we can responsibly harness the potential of AI while thoughtfully addressing its risks. We are thrilled to be joined today by our speakers. We have Jensen Huang, the founder and CEO of Nvidia; Congressman Ro Khanna, who represents California's 17th district; and General HR McMaster, former National Security Advisor and a senior fellow at the Hoover Institution and Freeman Spogli Institute for International Studies, who is going to moderate the discussion. Please join me in welcoming these wonderful speakers. Thank you so much. Hey, everybody. Thank you. Thank you. Sorry, I'd shake your hand except... Congressman, give him the... he can be in the center. They came here to see you. I think this might be the hot seat. I'll sit over here. I would shake your hand except I put some honey in my tea and now everything's sticky. My first thought was, "What, are you a child?" Hey, well, it is a great honor to be with both of you gentlemen and to be with all of you. Thanks for coming out for this panel discussion. It seems to be, this is kind of like a setup for a joke, you know: a washed-up general, a tech innovator and CEO, and a congressman from Silicon Valley walk into a bar. You know, what happens next? So, hey, why don't we just jump right into it? There's so much to talk about, obviously, and I really want to hear what's on your minds in each of these general areas. But first of all, I think we can all agree that artificial intelligence-related capabilities are extremely powerful. They have huge impact across society, they hold great promise, they're affecting the way that we wage warfare, and they determine, I think, whether or not we have a competitive advantage economically or militarily and from a national security perspective. Could I hear just both of your thoughts on how do we—if you agree, which I know you both do—maintain our competitive advantage? How do we do it? Jensen, why don't you start? First of all, I think it's helpful to take a step back and ask ourselves: what is it that we did? We have reinvented computing as we know it. How software is developed, how software is written, what software can do, and how software is processed. At its most fundamental level, in a lot of ways, that's all we've done. Now, of course, the nature of computing also changed in the sense that the way we did computing in the past is what was called retrieval-based computing. All of the content was pre-recorded. You know, you wrote a story, you designed something, or you recorded a video, you recorded a speech, and you stored it on cloud databases and data centers. Based on how you clicked something or whatever the recommendation systems are and whatever the algorithms are, it would present that pre-recorded content to you. The way that computing is done today is called generative. It takes all the context, the prompt, and your intentions, and it understands because it can now perceive. It understands, it reasons, and it could do something: write your story, summarize, or write software. And so the new type of computing is generative. It therefore seems intelligent. But when you look under the hood and you open the data center or you open up your computer, what you see is software running on top of computers. Okay? And so this is a new type of software. It is incredible in that sense, but it's no more incredible in that sense. It's not an alien, it didn't come from outer space, you know, it's not things like that. Okay? And so, I think that is number one. Now, because of the nature of this new computer, everything about the computer industry has changed because it's so capable and it could do such amazing things. Everything from the nature of companies, the position of companies, and the nature of data centers went from storage of files to now generation of tokens. And I call them factories. You turn electricity into tokens; it's manufacturing something. The classical data centers used to be a file server, and now you have basically token generators. Well, that takes a lot of computers, and so now the question is: what can it do? Well, I think that it's fairly clear to all of us that the latest rev of AI, which allowed us to go from perception to generative to now agentic systems, this next click of AI has proven to be incredibly capable. And we expected it to be, and it's doing real, real great things. Now, the implications to all the different industries we can go into a little bit later. So the first thing is to understand number one: what is the technology? From an industrial perspective, what is it? Well, from an industrial perspective, because of the way I described computing, AI is essentially a five-layer stack of an infrastructure. It's energy on the bottom, chips next, infrastructure like a cloud AI factory, AI models, and then most importantly, AI applications. And those applications could be enterprise software or consumer software or drug discovery or robotics or manufacturing, so on and so forth. These five layers, each one of them have industries and markets and lots of different companies. And I think the most important thing to take away is that if the United States wants to—and of course we do—want to stay in the lead, it is vital that we win in every single one of the five layers. Each of the layers has its own issues, each one of its layers has its own dynamics, and each one of its layers has different companies in it, but it's completely vital that we enable every one of those layers to succeed. And then finally, the single most important layer to succeed—and if this layer doesn't succeed, the flywheel will never happen—is the application layer. If the flywheel doesn't happen, the technology will never scale and the industry will never scale. The most important thing is that the application layer is diffused into the United States, into society, into our industries, and that AI is actually being used. If we cause ourselves, because of anything that we decide to say or that we decide to do, to be so fearful of AI that we resisted it, that we regulated it out of society, and we regulated it out of industry, we would slow ourselves down. It would be really quite unfortunate that this industrial revolution that we invented, that we started, and that we're in an across-the-board leadership position at, somehow we didn't take advantage of. So if I were to sum up what you said, it is to really work on removing barriers to adoption, to AI adoption, and think about how you can accelerate the development of the technology, but then the application of the technology. And Congressman Khanna, what do you think are the keys to maintaining our competitive advantage? What are you most concerned about in terms of how we stay ahead? First of all, let me just say it's an honor to be here, an honor to be here with Jensen. When I first met Jensen, his first question was, "Congressman, how do you understand Maxwell's Law?" and he gave me a pause, and then he proceeded to explain it very simply. And so he is someone who's an economic patriot, and I respect, even where we disagree, I've found him to be very thoughtful. General, I've appreciated you putting our country first, and thank you to Stanford GSB for hosting us. The first thing, in my view, that gives America a comparative advantage is that we have people from around the world who want to come here, study here, innovate here, and be part of the research universities here. If you look at the AI startups, 60% of them have been founded by immigrants. If you look at the AI researchers, 72% of them did not have an undergrad degree from the United States; they had an undergrad degree from other parts of the world. 38% of them are Chinese nationals who came to the United States. You're making me think of Wang An, the founder of Wang Computers. Yeah, and so I believe there's something not just about getting the world's talent, but having the world's talent interact with each other from diverse perspectives that creates magic and innovation. The second thing is our research universities, where we are. We have 14 of the world's top 20 research universities. You have Tsinghua and Peking University, two in China; we have 14. Now, Nature magazine says that they have nine out of the 10 top quantity, but we still have the top quality in research. That's not by accident; that's because we've funded research universities. Of course, people remember it was in Stanford that because of NSF funding in 1969, you had the ARPANET internet from UCLA to Stanford. We need to continue to fund our research universities. Third, I would say it's our academic freedom. You know, in our country, you could be—as I know as a politician—you could have any job and your view on war and peace or economics matters as much as a PhD, a congressman, or a president. And people are not afraid to say you're wrong, they're not afraid to question authority, they're not afraid to speak out, and they're not afraid to challenge convention. That is our comparative advantage; we need to keep that. And finally, which is here at Stanford, the tech transfer program. The idea that universities can collaborate with the private sector. We have a magic formula with the government, universities, and private sector working together. Those, in my view, are the fundamental principles that have allowed and will allow America to continue to lead, whether it's AI or other technology. Well, to maintain competitive advantage then, we're hearing remove barriers to application, which can be emotional or attitudinal, and you're emphasizing human capital, research funding, and academic freedom. I think the power of our free market is part of that, got to say that here at the home of Milton Friedman here at the Hoover Institution. But of course, there are certain actions that we have to take beyond even just funding research to maintain our competitive advantage that involve not only incredible innovators like Jensen and your team, but government policy. And so how do you view economic statecraft? What is the role of the tools of economic statecraft? I'm thinking in terms of an issue that you've had to confront directly: export controls, inbound and outbound investment screening, and creating the right incentives for investment by maybe countering elements of economic aggression like dumping of certain materials to maintain a grip on critical supply chains, for example. But also, you know, deregulation and removing barriers that are really impeding us maybe from maintaining that competitive advantage at all the levels you described, from energy all the way through to application. How do you view economic statecraft, really the role of government and government policy in maintaining this competitive advantage? Our situation is a little different than most industries. I would say that the computer industry, the computing technology industry, is one of America's national treasures. Unlike all of the other industries, this industry leads the world. Between computing technology—and of course I'm at the epicenter of computing technology education—this is one of our nation's national treasures, no doubt. The other one, of course, is our financial services industry, the backbone of the world's economy. Almost every other industry, we need subsidies. Almost every other industry, we need protection. These two we don't. These two industries we thrive in and lead at a level that is, you know, hard for most people to understand. There's no car company in history that has ever had 95% of the market. Nvidia's position in China was 95%. And so in our particular case, it's not... regulation is not about helping us succeed. This industry was very successful.
