And I had a great talk with both of them today. They're going to be having a ceasefire. That'll include Hezbollah and I think, I think it could be number 10 for me. Mr. President, do you have any news regarding peace in Ukraine? Do you have any news regarding peace in Ukraine? Ukraine is moving along. I wish they could get along, frankly. A lot of people are dying in Ukraine. We'll see what happens. There's things happening there. We're focused so much right now on Iran, seeing if we can get that completed. And I think, and I think we're going to be successful. And I think if you look, the stock market is good, the oil prices are coming down, and it's looking very good that we're going to make a deal with Iran and it's going to be a good deal. It's going to be a deal with no nuclear weapons. Mr. President, when is the next in-person round of negotiations on the war in Iran? When will that take place? They're all important. I mean, it's not a question of Iran; all of them are important. When will the next in-person meeting take place? Probably maybe over the weekend. Mr. President, will you send the Vice President? Will you send the Vice President? Just so you understand, the blockade has been incredible. It's held. They're not doing any business. They're unable to do any business because of the blockade. And so the combination of having no Navy, having no Air Force, having no anti-aircraft equipment—they have nothing. Everything is gone, including their leaders. Now they have a new set of leaders and we find them very reasonable. And Mr. President, on the 20-year minimum to stop enriching uranium, would that be acceptable to you? Would that be acceptable to you, sir? If that's the final sticking point, would you become— Well, we haven't agreed to any. We haven't agreed to any. What we have is a statement, a very powerful statement, that they will not have beyond 20 years, that they will not have nuclear weapons. That's beyond. There's no 20-year limit. Mr. President, on Todd Blanche, anyone interested, could you please give us more information on his go against the weaponization right? I think that Todd Blanche is doing a very good job, from what I'm seeing. All I can say is thus far I think Todd Blanche is doing a very good job. Mr. President, if there's no deal, would you be willing to extend the ceasefire or will the fighting resume, Mr. President? I would say the fighting—if there's no deal, fighting would resume. Okay. And on gas prices, how much longer will Americans continue to see these high gas prices? Well, they're not very high if you look at what they were supposed to be in order to get rid of a nuclear weapon, with the danger that entails. So the gas prices have come down very much over the last three, four days. Mr. President, gas is still— I know, you know, that's what ABC says. But the fact is that if you look, the stock market's up, everything's doing really well and the big thing we have to do is we have to make sure that Iran does not have a nuclear weapon. Because if they do, you want to talk about problems, you'd have problems. So very important is that Iran does not have a nuclear weapon. And they've agreed to that. Iran's agreed to that and they've agreed to it very powerfully. They've agreed to give us back the nuclear dust that's way underground because of the attack we made with the B-2 bombers. So we have a lot of agreement with Iran and I think something's going to happen very positive. Mr. President, what are you waiting for? Very complicated subject. I don't think we're waiting. I think we're moving very fast. It could happen pretty quickly. Regarding Lebanon, Hezbollah too, regarding Lebanon, maritime, how's the deadline? Say it. How long could you sustain the blockade on the Strait of Hormuz? The what? How long could you sustain, keep the blockade on the Strait of Hormuz? We're doing very well with the blockade. It's very routine for us. The Navy is incredible. And I think that the blockade is doing very well. No ship is even thinking about entering. No ship is going past our Navy. Mr. President, regarding you're fighting with the Pope, are you worried it's upsetting your Catholic voters? No, no, I have to do what's right. The Pope has to understand that, very simple. I've nothing against the Pope. His brother's MAGA all the way. I like his brother Lewis. I'm not fighting with him. The Pope made a statement. He says Iran can have a nuclear weapon. I say Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon. And if the Pope looked at the 42,000 people that were killed over the last two or three months, as a protester, with no weapons, no nothing—I mean, you take a look at that. So I can disagree with the Pope. I have a right to do it. I have a right to disagree with the Pope. What do you say to people who see that, who say your criticism of the— Nothing. I have—look, nothing. I have no disagreement with the fact the Pope can say what he wants, and I want him to say what he wants, but I can disagree. I think that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon. If they do, the whole world would be in jeopardy. The Middle East will be blown up and the whole world will be in jeopardy. We're very close to making a deal. That'll be a great thing. The Pope has to understand: Iran has killed more than 42,000 people over the last few months. Think of it. Protesters, without guns, without anything. They were totally unarmed protesters. The Pope has to understand that. This is the real world. It's a nasty world. But as far as the Pope and saying what he wants, he can do that. Now I know the Pope's brother. He is a big MAGA person. He's got MAGA all over his house. His name is Lewis. He's actually a great guy. And I'm sure the Pope is a great guy. I haven't met him. But I disagree with the Pope. If the Pope would allow Iran to have a nuclear weapon, you can't do that. Mr. President, Anthony Mortada from MTV Lebanon. What? Anthony Mortada from MTV Lebanon. I would like to ask you regarding congratulations for the 10th border agreement. Yeah, it's very exciting. With Lebanon, it's very exciting. I think we're going to have a deal. We're going to have a meeting, first time in 44 years. And Lebanon will be meeting with Israel and they're probably going to do it at the White House. When do you think— Mr. President, when do you think they would— Over the next week or two. Over the next week and do you think this agreement can reach without Hezbollah agreement? I do. I think they're going to— I think we will have an agreement between Lebanon and they're going to take care of Hezbollah. But they're going to be— they're going to be working on Hezbollah right now. But we'll have an agreement between Israel, very importantly, and Lebanon. Would you support our Lebanese army as well and work— I would. I would. Yes. I would. Iran's going to execute— Yeah, I would. Has Hezbollah agreed to this ceasefire? I mean, how do you even— They're all agreeing. It's a very nice little package for about a week. And we're not going to have lots of bombs dropping and we're going to see if we can make peace between Lebanon and Israel. And Mr. President, back on the Pope because there was a group of bishops that did put out a statement saying the Pope isn't merely exercising his opinion, he's preaching the gospel. I mean, what do you say to people that do have a hard time— Well, I want him to preach the gospel. I'm all about the gospel but I also know that you cannot let a certain country, which is a very mean-spirited country, have a nuclear weapon. If they did, they would use it and I think they'd use it quickly and they would kill many millions of people. So, you know, the Pope can disagree with me on that but certainly we're allowed to have that. I'm all about the gospel. I'm about it as much as anybody can be. But I can't allow as President of the United States of America, I can't allow Iran to have a nuclear weapon. And here's the story: they won't have. They've already agreed not to have. That's good news and I think the Pope will be very happy. Mr. President, Iran's going to execute four more people including a— Can the Federal Reserve wait and see about this Iran war before lowering interest rates? Do you agree with that? No, I don't agree. I think we should have lower interest rates. Iran's going to execute four more protesters reportedly including the first woman protester. What do you tell Iran? Well, tell that to the Pope. Mr. President, what, what do you say? Tell that to the Pope. That Iran is going to— Is that, did you just hear that? Iran is going to execute four people including a woman. President Trump, the Lebanese love you so much. Would you visit Lebanon? Would you extend the ceasefire? What makes you to extend the ceasefire? I don't know if we're going to have to but if, if we need to, I would do that. President, President Trump, wait, that's important, sorry. Airline. What are you telling the airlines and the oil companies about the way you're going to open up the trade? So if you look at the oil and if you look at the price we're paying, it's about half what people thought it would be if you did what I had to do. We had to take this journey to the Middle East in order to get rid of a nuclear weapon. There would have been— if we didn't hit them with the B-2 bombers, or if I didn't kill the Barack Hussein Obama deal, the nuclear deal which was a disaster, we would have had nuclear weapons exploding in the Middle East and beyond. I believe I've been able to stop that. I think we have a very successful negotiation going on right now and I think it'll be— if it, if it happens, it'll be announced fairly soon. And that'll give us free oil, free Hormuz Strait, everything will be nice and I think your oil price will go down to lower than what it was before. And I think a lot of good things will happen. President, the Lebanese people love you so much. Just for clarity, you're willing to extend the ceasefire through— Well, we'll see. I don't know that we'll have to. Ideally we wouldn't but if I needed to, I would. President, the Lebanese people love you so much. What's your message for them and do you— you will visit Lebanon after this deal? Yeah, I will do that. At the right time, I would certainly go there. Do you think that the war in Ukraine, you know, Putin doesn't want to stop it apparently. He keeps bombing Ukraine. Do you think this war has a potential to go on for years more? Well, it should have— it should have never started. If I were President, the war in Ukraine never would have started with Russia. But it's, it's going on. Hopefully it'll get over soon. Have you spoke with Putin? Melania came out last week and said she had no link to Jeffrey Epstein. Who did? The First Lady Melania. She had none. Why did she come out and say that last week? Because the fake news were saying she did and she had none and I think that's been proven. It bothered her that the fake news was being fake news, that's all. She just wanted to clarify. Mr. President, who's taking care of Hezbollah? Do you think that they are destroying the deal that you are trying to make? We're going to see how it all works out but they'll be working and coordinating if you know— you know Lebanon, right? So they'll be working with Hezbollah. If you fire— after— Mr. President, if you fire Jay Powell, are you still interested in acquiring Iranian oil after? Well, we'll see. I mean, we have a very good relationship with Iran right now, as hard as it is to believe. And I think it's a combination of about four weeks of bombing and a very powerful blockade. The blockade is maybe more powerful than the bombing if you want to know the truth. Are you, are you going to make the Arab countries pay a portion of all the costs? I think they'll make that— I think they're going to make that contribution. Mr. President, on the gospel. I think the Arab countries because we've done a lot, I think they'd make a contribution toward the cost. Lebanon, Hezbollah too. Mr. President, regarding the Prime Minister, on United Airlines and American Airlines, talked about— Not been involved. President Trump, as a— as a deal of Lebanon is a template with Iran? Iranian state without any condition if you make a deal? We're going to see what happens but I think we're very close to making a deal with Iran. You'll be the first to know but I think we have a chance. And if that happens, oil goes way down, prices go way down, inflation goes way down, and you're going to have much more importantly than even that, you won't have nuclear holocaust. Our talks are happening now? Mr. President, Bruce Blakeman— Will you extend that ceasefire? I think a deal could be reached possibly. I would be foolish not to. I mean, if we're close to a deal, would I extend? Yeah, I would do that. But we're close to a deal. We're getting along very well with the new Iranian leaders. It really is a regime change. They're people that are a lot different than we were dealing with at the beginning, as you know. They're no longer around. They're no longer with us. But we have a very good relationship and I think there's a very good chance we're going to make a deal. Also Israel and Lebanon, I think there's a good chance that they'll make a good deal so. Mr. President, when are they coming here? Any reaction to Russia's overnight attack on Kyiv? They killed 20 more in Ukraine. I think it's— I think it's terrible. Mr. President, are you going to send any cabinet member to China before your visit? Well, I have a great relationship, as you know, with President Xi and I look forward to being there. We have a very special relationship. So I look forward to being in China. Mr. President, thank you. Mr. President, you impose sanctions on Rwanda because they keep violating the Washington accord. But President Kagame just say he don't care about sanctions and he said he will not withdraw from Congo. His troops will continue in Congo. What is your reaction to that? We're going to look into it very strongly but they do have very big sanctions. Mr. President, there was a, there was a G20 meeting in Washington today. Why did you invite Poland instead of South Africa? Well, Poland's a great country. We like it a lot. We're friends with Poland. The President is doing a great job, so we invited Poland. Mr. President, Mr. President, I saw your Truth Social post about Bruce Blakeman in New York. He's being denied up to 7 million dollars of public campaign funds in New York by partisan Democrats. Have you seen that and what's your reaction to it? I can just tell you this: Bruce Blakeman is running for Governor. He will be a great Governor if he wins. I don't know that he wins or not because it's New York and you know, people cheat on elections and a lot of other things. But Bruce Blakeman is a great, great gentleman. As you know, he comes from Nassau County which is a Democrat area and he wins by a landslide all the time. And if Bruce Blakeman gets in, your taxes are going to go way down and a lot of good things are going to happen including cleanliness and all of the things that you want to see. I know him very well. He's been a great leader, a great executive. Even though it's largely Democrat, he wins in a landslide, everybody loves him. He's a very talented guy, very great politician. And I think he probably has a chance. If Bruce Blakeman gets in, your taxes are going to go way down and you know what else is going down? Crime. Mr. President, several people with access to classified stuff, nuclear material, aerospace, they've all gone missing or turned up dead in the last couple months. Based on what you've been briefed, what do you think is happening here and do you think that this is connected or totally random? Well, I hope it's random. But we're going to know in the next week and a half. I just left a meeting on that subject so, pretty serious stuff. But we're going to know. Hopefully— I don't know— coincidence, if you want to, whatever you want to call it. But some of them were very important people and we're going to look at it over the next short period. Is it possible that some boring adversary is in the United States to, to open these people up? Well, you know, Biden had open borders. It wasn't very hard to get here. But we've got many of them out. You know, we've caught many, many people, many, many very bad people including thousands of drug lords that we've taken out of our country. Hundreds of thousands of prisoners that were let into the US that we've taken out of our country. As far as the scientists are concerned, we'll probably have a pretty good answer over the next week. And with the peace talks in Iran, does it have to be a big deal all at once or would you accept a little deal to start? Well, I have a little deal. I can make a little— I don't want— I want to get it done and get back to keep, keep going with lower— you know, our pricing is doing great. As soon as the war is over, we're going to be lower than we were two or three years ago. So I want to get back to that. I want— we had to do something. We had to make sure that Iran never gets a nuclear weapon. And we're at that. They've agreed to that. They've totally agreed to that. They've agreed to almost everything. So maybe if they can get to the table, there's a difference. They agree, they're going to get to the table with a pen, we'll have that over with. And then we can focus on our great economy and also, you know, I inherited the highest prices in the history of our country, the worst inflation in the history of our country. I'll get it down to a very low number. We had it to a very low number and it's still low, but the most important thing right now is to make sure Iran can never have a nuclear weapon. Mr. President, would you ever go to Pakistan to seal the deal yourself? I would, I would go to Pakistan, yeah. Pakistan has been great. They've been so good. Islamabad, I'll be— I might go. Yeah. If the deal is signed in Islamabad, I might go. The Field Marshal has been great. The Prime Minister has been really great in Pakistan. So I may go. They want me. Mr. President, the First Lady last week called on Congress to further look into and uncover the truth on the Epstein files, the Epstein saga. Do you believe that there should be a public hearing for more of these Epstein survivors? I'm okay with it. I think we've had a lot of public hearings. I'm okay with it but I understand that the women didn't want to go under oath. That's what I heard. That the women, the victims or whatever, they refused to go under oath which was a little surprising. So Melania felt strongly about it because she was accused of that I met her through Epstein but it turns out to be totally false; it was a false story. Mr. President, does the ceasefire also include Iraq and the region? Are we talking about the one with Lebanon? Lebanon, Iraq and— hope it— So we just made a deal with Lebanon. As of two hours from now we have a ceasefire with Israel and Lebanon and that'll be great. And they'll be meeting, probably coming to the White House over the next four or five days. And that'll be the first time they've met in 44 years. Which is pretty unneighborly considering they're neighbors but there's a really good chance that that'll work out with Lebanon and Israel. I met through the telephone with the President of Lebanon who's a really good guy, highly respected, and with Bibi. And I think there's a chance we'll be meeting right here in the not-too-distant future. Mr. President, what's going to be the difference this time? Me. I'm the difference. Big difference. Me. Mr. President, on taxes— it looks like Virginia, North Carolina, and in New York, Democratic states are looking at raising taxes. Is that going to take away from the tax cuts that you have— No, because you know, No Tax on Tips. I'm going over No Tax on Tips today. That's why I'm going to Nevada; I'm going to Arizona. But this is sort of a No Tax on Tips. What's happening is people are finding out that in their tax return they're getting a big refund, much bigger than they thought. So it's No Tax on Tips, No Tax on Social Security, No Tax on Overtime. I think it's going to be amazing. But, but, if you look at what they're doing in New York and California, they're raising taxes and they're driving people out. It's a shame. Australia— Australia has just announced that it's increasing its defense spending to three percent. You've been asking for 3.5 percent. Are you satisfied— Well, we're going to see what happens but on defense spending, look, we built the greatest military anywhere in the world. There's nobody even close. All you have to do is take a look at the bombs. Where the bombs were powerful. And it weakened them. They have no Navy, they have no Air Force, they have no everything. They have no leaders. But their new leaders, the ones that have replaced them, it's a regime change. They are much more, I think frankly, more intelligent and more moderate. But we have a real chance to make a deal very soon with Iran. On Australia, Mr. President, you asked for three and a half percent increase like five percent you did with NATO. Australia has only gone to three percent. Are you satisfied with that decision? Would you consider— Well, I'm not happy with Australia because they were not— because they were not there when we asked them to be there. So Australia, Australia was another one. They were not there having to do with Hormuz, the Hormuz— What do you mean by that? I'm not happy. I'm not happy. But why specifically? Do you still have a good relationship with the Prime Minister? What message do you think the ceasefire in Lebanon sends to the UK and other European allies who chose not to get involved in the war? Well, you know, we, we spend trillions and trillions of dollars on NATO. And when I ask them to get involved on a much smaller situation, which is Hormuz and Iran, they weren't there for us. Remember what I say: they weren't there. None of them. They weren't there. So we were there on Ukraine, I mean, we should have been. It would have never happened if I were President. But nevertheless, when they've had problems over the years we were there. When we asked them to get involved on a very minor skirmish by comparison to what it could be, they weren't there. So I don't think they'd be there for a large skirmish and therefore I think they've got themselves a problem. Mr. President, do your policies like No Tax on Tips, No Tax on Overtime show that the Democrat party is no longer the party of the working class? And the Republican party under your leadership actually— I love this question. This is the greatest guy. Look how handsome he is; what a great guy he is. Yes. The Democrats don't know what they're doing. They want to tax everybody out of business. They want men playing in women's sports; they don't want voter ID. They don't know what they're doing. We've had an unbelievable run. And the people getting their tax returns and they're coming back and they're ending up getting five, six, seven thousand dollars more than they thought possible. Mr. President, do you think the Pope was being disrespectful to you? Back on the Pope. Back on the Pope. Do you think he was being disrespectful to you? I don't think about it that way. The Pope has to understand that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon. And the Pope has to understand that you can't let a country kill 42,000 protesters, who are totally unarmed protesters. You can't do that. So the Pope, you know, I know his brother. His brother's MAGA all the way. You know that. Lewis. He's a great guy. But the Pope has to understand: you're not allowed to kill 42,000 protesters who are totally unarmed. And you cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon or the entire world could be gone. And you know, when the Pope realizes that, I think we'll have a different understanding. Do you think he was being disrespectful? Mr. President, on energy— does energy dominance mean that Europeans should be buying more gas and oil from the United States? Well, they should be buying more from us and what they should be doing is using the North Sea. I can tell you UK, I've been telling that to your Prime Minister. UK should open up the North Sea. It's one of the greatest in the world. And they should use it instead of buying oil from Norway and other places that use the same source. They should use it. And they're not doing it. They're doing windmills and windmills. Windmills do one thing, you know what they do? Put you out of business. Mr. President, will you further speak to all the regional leaders about the peace deal or the peace talks about with Iran? Dealing with everybody. We're dealing with all of the leaders on many different subjects. We're very close to a deal with Iran. Thank you. Mr. President, stop telling us lies! Your actions are just telling us lies! Mr. President, stop telling us lies! Your actions are just telling us lies!

BREAKING: Warren Sounds Alarm About Kevin Warsh Post-Meeting, Says His Name Appears In Epstein Files

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M) ·11:15 ·3.3K views · 2026-04-16
Elizabeth Warren (U.S. Senator (D-MA)) Kevin Warsh (Fed Chair nominee)
Senator Elizabeth Warren is calling for the postponement of Kevin Warsh’s Federal Reserve chairmanship hearing, citing his failure to disclose $100 million in assets and unexplained appearances in the Jeffrey Epstein files.
TL;DR Senator Elizabeth Warren is calling for the postponement of Kevin Warsh’s Federal Reserve chairmanship hearing, citing his failure to disclose $100 million in assets and unexplained appearances in the Jeffrey Epstein files. Undisclosed Assets: Senator Warren alleges...

TL;DR

Senator Elizabeth Warren is calling for the postponement of Kevin Warsh’s Federal Reserve chairmanship hearing, citing his failure to disclose $100 million in assets and unexplained appearances in the Jeffrey Epstein files.

Financial Transparency and Ethics Concerns

  • Undisclosed Assets: Senator Warren alleges that Warsh has failed to disclose more than $100 million in assets, making it impossible to identify potential conflicts of interest.
      • Refusal to Comply: Warren claims Warsh was "explicit" in his refusal to provide additional documentation regarding these holdings.
      • Ethics Violations: Warren noted that Warsh is the first Fed nominee—and the first Trump nominee this term—to not be in compliance with standard ethics disclosure rules.
  • Divestment Issues: While Warsh claims he will divest his assets, Warren argues that without initial disclosure, the public and the Senate cannot verify if all "entanglements" have been properly unwound.
  • Specific Interests: Warren expressed concern over Warsh’s disclosed stake in Polymarket, suggesting it adds to a pattern of worrying financial interests.

Ties to Jeffrey Epstein

  • Presence in Files: Warren confirmed that Warsh’s name appears in publicly available Epstein files.
  • Lack of Explanation: During their meeting, Warsh reportedly claimed to have "zero knowledge" regarding why his name appears in the documents.
  • FBI Oversight: Warren criticized the FBI’s background investigation, stating the bureau made "zero investigation" into Warsh's financial holdings or the nature of his inclusion in the Epstein files.

Federal Reserve Independence

  • "Sock Puppet" Allegations: Warren expressed deep concern that Warsh would lack independence and instead act as a "sock puppet" for Donald Trump.
  • DOJ Interference: Warren highlighted the "irony" of the DOJ conducting what she termed "bogus" criminal investigations into current Fed Chair Jerome Powell and Governor Lisa Cook while simultaneously failing to investigate Warsh’s background thoroughly.
  • Bipartisan Resistance: The Senator suggested there is bipartisan appetite to halt the nomination process until the "bogus" investigations into current Fed leadership are dropped.

Critique of Economic Deregulation

  • Response to Scott Bessent: Warren rejected Treasury Secretary nominee Scott Bessent’s view that heavy regulation created the private credit market.
      • Market Risk: She compared current calls for deregulation to the era preceding the Great Depression, arguing that weak regulation leads to "hiding and deception" in financial books.
      • Private Credit: Warren argued that private credit markets are currently too weak and require more, not less, regulatory oversight to ensure honesty and transparency.

Notable Quotes

"Not telling about $100 million plus of assets means that it’s just not possible to understand about his entanglements... That’s not just a red flag. That’s a red flag surrounded by fireworks and sparklers." — Senator Elizabeth Warren
"I still have deep concerns that if he is confirmed, he will be Donald Trump’s sock puppet. And that means we will lose an independent Federal Reserve." — Senator Elizabeth Warren
"He claimed to have no knowledge [of the Epstein files]. None. Nothing. I got no information." — Senator Elizabeth Warren
"If he's not willing to make [his finances] public, he doesn't get to be Fed Chair." — Senator Elizabeth Warren
I just met with Kevin Warsh, who is Donald Trump’s nominee to be Chairman of the Federal Reserve. And I still have deep concerns that if he is confirmed, he will be Donald Trump’s sock puppet. But following this meeting, I have additional concerns. The first is that Mr. Warsh has not disclosed more than $100 million in assets, so that it’s not possible to know his financial entanglements and where conflicts might arise, and ascertain if he gets rid of them. The second concern I have is that his name appears in the Epstein files, and yet he claims to have zero knowledge of anything related to this. My meeting with Mr. Warsh was followed by a meeting with the White House briefer on the FBI investigation into Mr. Warsh’s background. And what I can say about that report is that I was told that the FBA, FBI made zero investigation into any of Mr. Warsh’s financial holdings, including those that he is refusing to disclose. They also made zero investigation into why Mr. Epstein appears in the publicly available Epstein files and whether he appears in other files that have not been made public. Senator, at this time, are—is the—is the next step that you see trying to get more information on these disclosures? How do you kind of move forward from here given that the hearing’s now scheduled for next week? Senator, we should not have his hearing next week. We should not have this hearing next week partly because Donald Trump is still out there trying to do, launch his takeover of the Fed by a bogus criminal investigation both against the Chairman of the Fed and one of the governors of the Fed. And there are many people, and I should say it’s bipartisan, who say we should not have this process going forward until that is resolved and those bogus charges have been dropped. But this hearing should not go forward now until these financial disclosures are resolved, until he is in compliance with ethics rules. I should note here: he is the first Fed nominee not to be in compliance with ethics rules and the first Trump nominee in this term not to be in compliance with ethics rules. Not telling about $100 million plus of assets means that it’s just not possible to understand about his entanglements. So my view on this is until we have full financial disclosure that has been fully investigated and full disclosure about Epstein ties and that has been fully disclosed, there should be no hearing. Have you spoken with anyone with the Office of Government Ethics on this to ask why they released these documents if they're not complete? I—we have the calls into them, but they have made it very clear that this is not a report, a financial report that is in compliance. Senator, did you ask his—what is his response to—I'm sorry, did you ask him about his inclusion in the Epstein files? What did he say? I mean what— He claimed to have no knowledge. None. Nothing. I got no information. Did he signal if he'd be willing to hand over more documents to make people more comfortable with his financial disclosures? No, in fact he was quite explicit that he would not hand over more documents, which frankly raises my concerns even higher. Are there any specific disclosures that you—you see or—or haven't seen that—that raise a question in your mind about a conflict of interest? Anything specific you want to point to within this very long document? Yes. There’s more than $100 million in assets to people or entities or outfits that are completely undisclosed and will remain undisclosed. That’s not just a red flag. That’s a red flag surrounded by fireworks and, and sparklers. This is a real problem. No one has gone forward in the Trump administration without disclosing fully their financial holdings and who they are financially entangled with. Did he express any discomfort in the DOJ investigation into Powell? No. Did he commit to divesting any of those assets? He claims that he will divest those assets and therefore he doesn't have to tell what they are. But I point out that that means you can’t check to make sure that they are all gone. The whole point of the disclosure is not only to know about his entanglements in the past, but to be able to check to see that they are unwound by the time he becomes a Fed governor. And remember here, the Fed has already suffered multiple ethics scandals because Fed governors have had conflicts of interest. So here they are making monetary policy and they have a financial interest in the outcome. That’s why it is absolutely crucial to know about someone’s financial holdings and that those holdings be eliminated if they create conflicts of interest. Do you have any indication of when the Justice Department investigation is going to be over? Senator Scott seemed to indicate it was going to wrap up soon. The President seems to have a different view. Do you have—have you gotten any word from Senator Scott? I mean obviously this hearing was scheduled I think with the idea that that was going to be wrapped up because it can't go forward if it isn't. Right. We’ve just seen another demonstration that the Justice Department does exactly what Donald Trump wants them to do. Remember in this Justice Department investigation of Jerome Powell and Lisa Cook, that courts independently for each of them have said there is nothing here. Zero. Not like, oh, there’s a little and here’s how you judge it, it’s there’s nothing. And yet Donald Trump wants his Department of Justice to go full bore and that’s exactly what they're doing. So this investigation evidently will last exactly as long as Donald Trump wants it to last. And you see any irony that you're saying that the FBI background investigation of Kevin Warsh was not sufficient while they're doing this investigation of Jerome Powell? Very worrisome. DOJ investigation aside, do you feel that all Democrats will be lined up against voting for Kevin Warsh, given what you've just told us? Look, I just want to emphasize right now: we cannot let this process go by. I know that, you know, process is always the—the hardest part to talk about, but there’s a reason that we ask for financial disclosures. There’s a reason we demand as a matter of law that these financial disclosures be completed. And we now have somebody who wants one of the most important economic positions in our nation to say he doesn't want to have to make those financial disclosures, but he expects votes from the United States Senate. That just doesn't work. We cannot let that pass. Senator, Kevin Warsh, he—he disclosed having a stake in Polymarket. Did you talk to him at that, about that at all? I'm sorry, we just didn't get to that one, but it certainly should be on the list. Is that concerning? Yes, it worries me. Of course. Even from what you know or don't know, should he withdraw? Well, he's the one who knows what that $100 million that he is determined to keep secret is all about. If I were in his shoes, I'd sure take a close look about why it is he doesn't want to make that public. But my position’s clear: if he's not willing to make it public, he doesn't get to be Fed Chair. Did he give you a timeline for divesting those assets? No. Secretary Scott Bessent actually gave you credit yesterday for the private credit market saying that it wouldn't exist if banks were not so heavily regulated. Just wanted to see what your response to that was. You know, I couldn't believe just how wrong the Secretary got it. The problem we've got is when financial institutions engage in either illegal or let’s just say very unethical behavior. And that’s how we got the run-up with the subprime mortgage market, right? That everyone was trading in all of these assets in the unregulated market and then they bled into the regulated market. Scott Bessent’s view of the world is if there’s a problem that the regulators have discovered, like assets that aren't worth what the paper says they're worth, then he thinks you need to get rid of the regulators. He thinks the banks and the private market should all be unregulated. We tried that prior to 1929 and we saw how it worked out. It not only crashed the stock market, it drove us into a country that had a 25% unemployment rate and a depression that landed, lasted for almost a decade. We have regulators so that our markets can work. And the problem we have right now is that the regulations over the banks are too weak, but they are also too weak over private credit. Private credit can engage in all kinds of hiding and deception and never get caught. This happens because they don't have a regulator who is there to make sure that, in effect, books and records are honest and regular reports are made publicly. All right, last two. Anybody? Yes. Well, on private—just to be clear Senator, if, if Warsh, you know, disclosed what you're asking and the investigation into Powell closed, would you then support his nomination? Look, we have problems. I started with the first problem and that is: Kevin Warsh has given every indication he will be Donald Trump’s sock puppet. And that means we will lose an independent Federal Reserve. That is not only not good for our economy, it’s not good for America. My second problem are the new concerns, and that is $100 million plus in assets that he flatly refuses to disclose in violation of the legal disclosure requirements. He needs to explain that, and I need to see what’s in them. And the third is an investigation into why he's in the Jeffrey Epstein files. That needs to be fully investigated and discovered. Until he can explain how he's going to be independent, and he can explain his finances, and he can explain his ties to Jeffrey Epstein: no, I would not support him for Federal Reserve Chair.

Bootstrapped To $1 Billion: How Arizona-Based Lectric eBikes Is Dominating The D2C Market

Forbes(2.1M) ·40:36 ·949 views · 2026-04-16
Levi Conlow (Co-founder and CEO, Lectric eBikes) Alex York (Reporter, Forbes)
By prioritizing immediate profitability and operational leverage over the traditional venture capital "growth-at-all-costs" model, Levi Conlow has scaled Lectric eBikes to over $1 billion in sales and 700,000 units, securing its position as North America's largest e-bike brand.
TL;DR By prioritizing immediate profitability and operational leverage over the traditional venture capital "growth-at-all-costs" model, Levi Conlow has scaled Lectric eBikes to over $1 billion in sales and 700,000 units, securing its position as North America's largest e-bike...

TL;DR

By prioritizing immediate profitability and operational leverage over the traditional venture capital "growth-at-all-costs" model, Levi Conlow has scaled Lectric eBikes to over $1 billion in sales and 700,000 units, securing its position as North America's largest e-bike brand.

The Bootstrapped Business Model vs. VC Failure

  • Profitable Growth: Unlike competitors that raised hundreds of millions at billion-dollar valuations and subsequently failed, Lectric has been profitable every year since its 2019 launch.
  • The "Venture Capital Formula" Trap: Conlow attributes the failure of many e-bike brands to a reliance on achieving future "operational leverage" to become profitable. Lectric, conversely, ensures every bike designed and sold is profitable from day one.
  • Margin Management: The company operates with a strict "margin ceiling" and "margin floor." When operational efficiencies or component prices improve, the savings are reinvested into product quality or price reductions for the consumer rather than padding bottom-line profits.
  • Direct-to-Consumer (D2C) Advantage: While expanded into 300 retail locations like Best Buy, the core D2C model provides a wealth of data used to identify market gaps and customer performance needs.

Supply Chain Complexity and the Tariff Landscape

  • Global Sourcing: Lectric manages a complex supply chain utilizing components from China, Cambodia, Vietnam, Taiwan, Thailand, Germany, and the US (Kentucky).
  • The Tariffs Challenge: The company has faced significant friction from Section 301, 122, and 232 tariffs. Recently, Lectric moved manufacturing from China to Cambodia, only to face new trade headwinds shortly after.
  • Absorbing Costs: Rather than passing price fluctuations to customers, Lectric has "taken it on the chin," absorbing short-term tariff costs to maintain market stability while waiting for a clearer long-term trade climate.
  • The Onshoring Reality: Conlow states there are currently no plans to reshore manufacturing to the US, noting that onshoring would increase costs four to five times and introduce high quality-control risks compared to established Asian manufacturing partners with decades of expertise.

Market Expansion and Strategic Leverage

  • Aggressive Acquisitions: Lectric is deploying $10 million toward two non-Lectric e-bike businesses, including the acquisition of a bankrupt competitor. These brands will target market segments (performance and aesthetics) that the core Lectric brand does not currently serve.
  • Negotiation Power: Due to its scale (selling nearly 100,000 units of a single model annually), Lectric commands significant leverage with suppliers like LG. This allows them to include premium components—such as two-piston hydraulic brakes—at price points that competitors cannot match.
  • Customer Service as Marketing: Lectric maintains an in-house customer service team of over 75 people—a force larger than the entire US operations of some of its largest competitors. Conlow views high-quality service as a primary driver for lowering Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) through referrals.

Corporate Culture and Philanthropy

  • Philanthropy as a Core Value: The company has funded desalination systems in Kenya, schools in Cameroon, and donated over $600,000 to St. Jude.
  • Community Impact: In 2025, the founders announced a program to provide 30 full-ride college scholarships (including housing and meals) for Arizona foster children at Grand Canyon University.
  • High-Touch Leadership: Conlow prioritizes employee retention through unconventional methods, such as taking every employee to high-end dinners and hosting "Banner Ceremonies" (modeled after the NBA) to celebrate becoming the industry’s #1 seller.

Future Outlook: The US E-Bike Market

  • Market Penetration: US e-bike adoption has grown from 7% in 2023 to approximately 12% today. However, this remains far behind European markets like Germany and the Netherlands, where penetration exceeds 50%.
  • Correction vs. Shrinkage: While the broader market experienced a "right-sizing" after the 2020-2021 boom, Conlow remains bullish, believing that the exit of "undeserving" competitors has created a massive opportunity for market leaders to capture share.
"A lot of our competitors... their whole US team is smaller than just my customer service team. Why not apply pressure on everyone to have such a high standard placed in the industry?" — Levi Conlow
"If the customer says your product doesn't sell well, the worst thing you could do is try to focus on getting people to believe in that version of the product. You should listen to every single customer... even if that means completely ripping away everything that you cared so much about." — Levi Conlow
"It would be incredibly naive for me to think that I could do it better than [partners in Asia] by onshoring it here. It’s a very, very difficult product." — Levi Conlow
Well Levi Conlow, thank you so much for joining me today. Super stoked. Thanks for having me. So you are the co-founder of a company called Lectric, which is an e-bike company. Can you kind of give me like a little bit of a rundown of for someone who hasn't heard of you, what Lectric is today? Yeah, so Lectric is technically North America's largest e-bike company. I started it back in 2019 with my childhood best friend and we sell e-bikes direct to consumer, but we are also in some dealers and Best Buy as well. Between Best Buy and our dealers we are in probably 300 locations. You know, we were one of the newest players onto the scene, but now we sell more e-bikes than anybody else in North America. All time, we now have the record with over 700,000 e-bikes sold in really just the US alone. Mhm. The last time you spoke with Forbes was in 2024. So can you kind of give me a rundown of like what your 2025 looked like? Any major wins over the last year? Any major challenges you guys were up against? What's the last 12 months looked like? The last 12 months have been dramatic. You know, we've continued to grow the company. We've grown the company every single year since we've started it. We've always been profitable every single year, so we've continued to keep those same standards and expectations. But with an evolving trade climate and tariff situation, it certainly complicated the business. You know, we've had to move manufacturing around and change some sourcing decisions. And yeah, so there's been a ton of work and friction over the last year with trying to best optimize the business to deliver the best pricing and product for our riders. So I think the last 12 months have been the most challenging 12 months that I've ever experienced, you know, running this company. But it's also been a 12 months that has really helped weed out the competition. Some of our competitors who raised hundreds of millions of dollars at, you know, billion-plus valuations have, you know, kind of fallen to the wayside and some gone through bankruptcy. And so it's as difficult as it's been for the past 12 months, it's also just been a tremendous opportunity to take market share and better situate ourselves as the market leader. So you know, yeah, there's a pro and con to it all. I was going to ask about that because I think that there has, and I want to talk about like the tariffs and all these sorts of things that have impacted you as well. But first I want to touch on a little bit of that competition and you know, especially in around 2020, a little bit before and in that kind of era, there was such a massive boom in e-bikes. There was a boom in a lot of things that were like either sustainability focused or kind of changing the ways that things have been done in the past. And to your point, following those that, you know, kind of couple years of boom, it really has kind of come in the other direction where companies have shut down, adoption has maybe slowed, and I kind of want to know a little bit more about that. Like contextually, can you paint the picture of what your market looks like today compared to a couple of years ago? Yeah, the e-bike category as a whole has experienced a bit of a shrinkage from 2021 to where we are today. I do believe that it's probably more appropriately sized. You know, we are bigger than what it was in 2019 and 2020. So I think there was a lot of demand that was pulled forward and there was a bit of a right-sizing of the industry over the last couple years. You know, I'm a bit of a believer that it's not so much the market, because the tool itself and e-bikes are freaking awesome. So like, I'm of the belief that it's the lack of the right product and competition that has led to a shrinkage in the market. You know, if we rewind to 2020, 2021, there was a lot of players in the field who I would say maybe didn't even deserve to be in the game, and now those players are out. But as they exited, you know, some of them lost their market share and some of us were able to pick it up. But you know, Lectric has grown every single year. So throughout all this like turmoil and heartache and all the headlines saying the e-bike market is shrinking, we've still managed to grow every single year, done so at a very profitable degree. So I'm a bit of a believer of I just don't think the right companies and products were here to be able to weather the storms. You know, as people became more price sensitive, you know, having a product mix that can deliver to those needs was critical and a lot of our competition didn't pay attention to that. So yeah, the market has certainly evolved. I'm actually more bullish on this category than I've ever been. You know, so you gotta despite how challenging tariffs were in 2025, you know we've experienced tremendous growth. We acquired one of our competitors out of bankruptcy in 2025 that we're relaunching this year. We have a group that we're investing into in Minnesota that is also an e-bike player. And so between those two things, you know we're deploying about $10 million towards these two businesses that are not Lectric, right? They're going to be direct competitors of Lectric. And I wouldn't be doing that if I didn't believe in the market potential and have I'm just extremely bullish on e-bikes as a solution. So yeah I'm I'm very excited still. As you continue to then make these acquisitions and invest in other components of this market, what's going to be kind of the differentiator between those products you're building out versus Lectric? How are you going to continue to like, you know, compete almost with yourself in some ways as you're kind of bringing in new folds to your kind of overarching company? Yeah, well so we've sold what was cool recently we I was on my Shopify and I had seen that we crossed over our billionth dollar in online sales since the time we started this company in 2019. And so we do a couple hundred millions of dollars a year now at this point. And one of the really cool things that being a D2C online company is we have a wealth of knowledge, right? So much data on where we're selling the customers, everything like that. And we can overlay that with legacy bike sales and everything like that to try to figure out where are we falling short? Where are the parts of the market where we're dominating and where are the, you know, parts of the market that are still an opportunity? And that's really what these additional investments are about is you know, we cater to a customer at Lectric currently today extremely well, but there's some customers who are not interested in buying a Lectric. Whether it's not hitting the performance needs that they have or the aesthetic, you know, there's a massive segment of the market that we're simply missing out on because of, you know, our product. So first and foremost we need to solve that to be able to go after those customers. And as you do that, maybe different big box retailers like a Best Buy may want to carry those products or a different dealer may want to carry those products as well. So I expect a lot of opportunity to come from launching those two products and their product lines. You mentioned too a minute ago that kind of in this like the early stages of building this company compared to now, like a lot of the companies that have fallen off or have not been able to sustain themselves, you feel like they were just like missing what they needed for the market. What were they missing? Like what were they doing wrong that allowed that you guys were doing right that allowed you guys to continue to grow throughout then and and now into the future that those other companies were maybe missing the mark on? Yeah, you know, I think first and foremost product is everything, right? So you need to nail that first. And then once you do that, you need to nail the customer experience and servicing them. And I think I think a lot of competitors run their businesses in a different way where you know they may have venture capital funding, right? They may go out, get the venture capital funding and the economics of the business or the product itself are not all that sound. But they have an idea of hey once I get to scale, I'm going to be able to get operational leverage, economies of scale on my negotiations and then I'll bring my price down or, excuse me, my cost down and then I will become profitable, right? This is a formula we see so many times. And it's a hard consumer good though, you know the e-bike category and and you need to be aware of that and appropriately build your business. So every single bike we've ever sold has been profitable, every bike we design is profitable. And so as we scale, right, when we were first on on Forbes 30 Under 30, we were doing 75,000 bikes a year maybe at that point. Well, now we're doing well over double that and expect significant growth in 2026. Well, that comes with better pricing from our component makers, we have better pricing from our logistics partners, fulfillment, our warehousing efficiencies. You know, the just operational leverage of headcount all gets better. And so what happens as the company gets more profitable, the bike gets more profitable. And the way I run the company is, you know, with a margin ceiling and a margin floor. So we take that savings and we just reinvest it into the bike to make the bike better or we reduce price. But what's incredibly difficult for the competition is they're fundamentally operating on this venture capital formula of we need to get scale and then we'll be profitable. They're chasing now the category leader that is doing the exact same thing as them, just in perfect stride, except passing the savings or the performance enhancements to the customer rather than focusing it on profitability. We've been profitable since day one. We don't need to be any more profitable than we are today. We like our margin profiles, so we just need to stick to it. And so as the business experiences wins, you know, it's our duty to then pass those wins onto the customer and, you know, make better bikes. So I think that to me is one of the most crucial differences, but also we've invested heavily into you know customer service and everything like that. So many of our competitors even to this day do not care about that the way we do. We view it as just an extension of marketing. This is, you know, helping drive down a lower CAC for us, customer acquisition cost, is by having exceptional customer service. A lot of them will outsource it. Actually, I would say the vast majority or the normalized operation is outsourcing it and we have it all in-house. Our customer service team is probably now around 75 people, which is bigger than our next biggest e-bike competitor's entire US operation. Their whole US team is smaller than just my customer service team. And so it's as you grow the business you have to care critically about these you know opportunities for enhanced leverage. There is pressure against competition where if I can now afford to have a customer service team of 75 people and it makes sense with my economic structure, then why not do it? Why not apply pressure on everyone to have such a high standard placed in the industry of the customer service that they should expect. Mhm. And so I want to talk too about 'cause you mentioned a lot of this there about like pricing for your models, the cost that it, you know, that you guys are required to put up front for for these models and how you've kind of measured that with what you're selling them for. Can you tell me one, just a little bit about the manufacturing process for Lectric bikes? Like where are you making this? Where are the components coming from? And how to your point with tariffs has this changed over the last couple of years? Yeah, so it's a complicated supply chain, right? So we need to use componentry from China, Cambodia, Vietnam, Taiwan, Thailand, Germany, Kentucky, you know and then we do all of that and and get it over to Cambodia and then we make the bikes and then we ship them over here and it's a very complicated supply chain. And you know, we had spent a while working on moving our manufacturing from China, but then once we had made the move then the IEPA tariffs had been announced and that was a fun wrench. I think I was operating for like less than one full week out of the Cambodia location by the time they had announced IEPA tariffs. Now it's still a complete unknown. We don't know what tariffs are going to actually wind up at. You know, right now we have the Section 122 tariff and that's for the short term. You know, we know Section 301 is being worked on, Section 232 is also in in process. So there's all this complexity and cost that is really burdening the business and cost profile. What we've tried to do is just take it all on the chin. So for the last year we basically have, you know, bodied all those blows and decided that we don't want to pass these costs onto the customer. We don't know what prices are ultimately going to end up needing to be at for the long term, right? It's been a a longer short-term pain than what I was anticipating. But you know, we're we're monitoring the situation and maybe we find that we need to once again move manufacturing, but for now it's a wait and see and learn more. Any plans to like reshore some of that production? Bring these things to like Arizona where you're at or somewhere else across the US or are you pretty set on these kind of international components? No, I would say no plans for that. It's not economical to bring it here. We've seen, you know, I really just only one e-bike company, or excuse me one bike company, bring it stateside and their costs are four to five times the price. It is a very expensive manufacturing process. And it's also in my mind extremely high risk to go away from manufacturing partners who have been doing this for 30 years who are the best in the industry, have the best quality standards and practices in place. It would be incredibly naive for me to think that I could do it better than them right by onshoring it here. It's a very, very difficult product. And you also have the, you know, whole supply chain is for the most part very centralized in Asia and so there's efficiencies by having a location there. So you know, there's a solid 40 plus parts that make up one of our bikes and you need to be efficient with how you bring all that together. Mhm. How does your focus on keeping these bikes affordable and accessible to more consumers kind of impact like the quality here? Like what are you having to think about? Are there things that you're either sacrificing in order to keep it cheaper and more affordable or things that you know you've really had to kind of like, you know, weigh, what are we going to focus on, where are we going to have the money, where are we not? What does that look like for you guys? Yeah, I would say because of our size, we do not have to make those sacrifices. I would say most of the sacrifice is just on EBITDA or profitability. Like we could make way more money. If you were to take our our flagship model the XP 4, it's the number one selling e-bike model in the US. We sell close to 100,000 of just that design a year and so that one bike is bigger than the next biggest competitor's whole business. That bike should not be $1,000 by any respect. If you were to just take the logo off of it and go component by component, there is no reason it shouldn't be priced at you know 1,500 to $1,600. But I have built a business so efficient that I can afford to do it. I have such operational leverage that I can afford to do it, my negotiating power you know. So I think there's that one thing. From a quality standpoint, we are by far the category leader. We have built out our whole in-house quality team and we pulled people from, you know, Amazon and what have you to build this all in-house. I am extremely confident that you can even stack us up against like a Trek or specialized that we have the best quality team in the entire industry. But you know three years ago I probably couldn't have said that. But as we've been able to grow the business, capital opens up so we're able to invest into that. And now we're at the point right where if you look at we're D2C company first and foremost, we have over 700,000 riders out there and we've grown every single year. That's not possible, right, unless you're dumb with money and spending a bunch of money on marketing and dragging, you know, and operating at a loss. It's not possible to do all those things without having an exceptional product and packaged together with exceptional service because what you're going to lose out on is customer referrals, right? Your NPS score is not going to be exciting and that's just not how we run the business. So we run it with very high standards and because of that we've been able to be the clear category leader and winner. Mhm. And then in terms of then you know that you mentioned there like your negotiation tactics and how you've been able to kind of like create this supply chain system. What kind from behind the scenes did that look like compared to how other electric bike manufacturers are you know acquiring these parts and components and putting them together? How does your like pricing model, what you guys are putting up towards these manufacturers differ from others? How is your negotiation impacted that? Yeah, I think I have an incredible team around me, some who have been working in the industry for you know 30 plus years and then I personally play a very involved role in all the negotiations. So you know I'm over in Asia every couple months. I'm probably there four or five times a year. And there's tremendous weight in Asia when you're you're negotiating as the CEO, as the co-founder. You can just make a lot more more progress. But we also sit in a position where people really, really want our business. We recently did a deal with LG and you know there's not a ton of groups out there that are actually buying 10 million battery cells at once right, or making contracts for 10 million plus cells. And so it allows us to get attention from people that you know otherwise wouldn't care about any other e-bike company. But by the way we design our products throughout all of the brands, it allows us to have a more important role to these manufacturers and partners. And there's some people who offer us insanely good componentry, componentry that should not be on bikes that are priced at this price point just because they want it to be on the number one seller in the industry. And I'll give an example. So we have this one group who, you know, in testing have proven that they have like the best two-piston hydraulic brakes in the entire industry, right? And they really wanted them on our bikes and economically no way we were going to make the math you know make sense. Yet they came to us and said all right we're going to give it to you for the exact same price you're currently paying for this other brake set because they knew that if we started to carry that as the industry leader, as the one that's most affordable as well, how is the group that's $500 or $1,000 more expensive going to get away with not carrying that type of componentry. And so we sit in a position where it demands a response from the industry whenever we make a component decision. We went to those two-piston hydraulic brakes, and there has not been a single bike launch over the last two years that has not come out with hydraulic brakes. And it is because we were the biggest, the most affordable, we went there and now it demands everybody else to. And that's a really powerful position to be in when working with the supply chain and component makers because every single person we have some wicked componentry coming out this year and it's going to continue to surprise people. How on earth are they putting that bike or that component on that bike at that price? And it's pretty simple. It's just everybody wants their components on our bikes. Yeah, that totally makes sense to me. I want to touch on too your kind of building this company in Phoenix, Arizona. How has this location impacted one, like your kind of just process as a founder and as a builder, but also in terms of like who your customers are, who your target demographics are? How have you thought about like who's buying Lectric, who's riding Lectric bikes while you guys are building in Arizona? Well, 300 days of sunshine is pretty awesome. It's over than that. So like product testing can happen every single day. And we're always on our bikes learning, crafting, improving, so that is a very material benefit from it. The the people here are incredible, right? So I run this office here in Phoenix. We probably have 150 people across our three locations here in Phoenix. We have our warehouse, our secondary site, and then our primary HQ. And yeah, actually more than 150 employees here in Phoenix. And the quality of people is incredible because they're they're very driven. But it's a melting pot. It seems like every person here in Arizona is not from Arizona. We've chosen to be here, right? And when you do that there's a certain joy and appreciation for the location that you're in. So yeah, we've been very fortunate to build a the best in class team because of the location we're in. You know, it's actually incredibly easy, I'm from Minnesota, right? And it's been very easy to recruit or poach people from competitors to come move to Arizona. You fly someone out in the month of February from the Midwest to come tour and see the operation, you know, it's a very compelling pitch. And so we've had tremendous success with recruiting because of where we're located. You know, just in terms of location also, it's opposite to what the traditional seasonality of a bike business is. Usually those sales are heavier in the summer. Here in Phoenix it's in the winter. And so that really helps just with our own exposure to seasonality where we can perform well kind of all 12 months of the year. So yeah, I absolutely love Phoenix. I've been out here for 10 years. I originally moved here for college. I went to Grand Canyon University. I got a degree in business entrepreneurship and then I got a master's in business leadership before starting Lectric eBikes. And actually what's kind of a cool full story thing with the university is Robbie, my co-founder and I, we both turn 30 in December. But what one of our goals that we wanted to do was by the time we turned 30 we wanted to be able to pay for the full rides of 30 Arizona foster children. And so we did that in May of 2025. We announced that program, so there's on campus there's a Lectric lounge, there's a Lectric lecture hall and it's it's really cool full circle moment. But it's it's good to support those students and yeah, it's complete full ride, four years of education, their their housing, their meals, their equipment whatever it is. We're helping them out and and that's been a really cool thing. I know you've done so much philanthropy work through Lectric and kind of you know used this business for good. I would love to know just briefly some of those other initiatives that you guys have worked on whether that's like where you've been deploying your bikes or how they're being used. I know there are some fun stories here just in terms of how this can be used for good. I'd love to know more about that from you. Yeah, philanthropy is like a cornerstone of Lectric's ethos, right? You know Robbie and I have been very fortunate to build a great company and and you need to figure out a why you know and this is one of our whys is to use this company to do good in the world beyond just selling e-bikes and changing transportation. And so we've done projects like orphanage in Khayelitsha South Africa where I've been extremely involved in that project and continue to invest in it over the years, visiting it once a year and doing additional fun activities with them. Another orphanage in Zambia, a school in Cameroon, a desalination system in Mombasa Kenya. That was really cool, so that provides clean drinking water for about 20,000 people but then we shipped our e-bikes there, about 30 of them. They use the e-bikes to then distribute the water to everyone in the surrounding areas. That's pretty sick. And then like even stateside here we've done a ton with the foster care community and trying to be a resource there. But we've also invested not only in the foster care but just those that are underserved here in Arizona where it's getting access to e-bikes, right? Some of them maybe you're a refugee and you've come here and and you can't get a driver's license but you don't want to have a small radius of where you can work or where you can grocery shop or anything like that. Well you know what we need to do is provide them with a solution to expand their radius and I make e-bikes. This is perfect. I can just give them an e-bike. And so we've given away literally thousands, millions of millions of dollars of cost of e-bikes over the years. You know, and a lot of those people who have benefited from that have been right here in Arizona, across the US. But again we're even doing it in places where we don't even sell bikes. Like think of all the bikes or like the Kenya example, we don't sell any bikes there. We get no benefit from building a desalinization system and providing e-bikes as distribution, but like that's the right thing to do, right? And and we want to be able to look at this company and say it is doing good in the world not to our own self-benefit but just for the benefit of people. And that is something we take to heart. So those are some of them you know we we do so many other things. We partnered with St. Jude and donated over $600,000 last summer to them, have more projects coming this year as well which I'm super stoked. So yeah that's probably one of the greatest joys about this business is as a business has scaled and become this success our giving has scaled proportionally, right? And that's what's so important and what I encourage people to really think about and do you know. And you don't always have to start with the big orphanage, right? You can start at smaller scale giving and contributing to the world and as your business succeeds or yourself become more successful scale up your giving in proportion to it. I think that's incredibly important is it's not too early to ever get involved with giving. Yeah, no I think it's such a special you know part of you and and your company. I want to touch too briefly on your Master's degree in leadership, business leadership. What does that look like in practice and how have you taken that into your leadership style as a founder, as a CEO today? So when I graduated with my entrepreneurship program, I did so extremely fast. I graduated in like two and a half years and I'm sitting there twiddling my thumbs like shoot I'm 20 years old, not sure I'm ready for the real world. So I enrolled in the master's program to kind of just buy myself some time before entering the real world. So I joined that program and I was the youngest person in it. I was 20 years old and everyone was probably for the most part in their 40s or some in their 50s, but a lot in their 30s 40s. And I had never had a job that you know I didn't create for myself. My whole life I was an entrepreneur. So I'm sitting there and I'm learning from everybody about kind of the pains of their their leaders and the management styles and everything like that. It was incredibly insightful to just sit there through the program and hear about the frustrations that people have with their leaders. And I would just take note and be like all right I do not want to be that person, right? And then I would also try to pick up oh it really stood out to this person how their boss came to their kid's soccer game. I should try to be that type of boss. I should try to invest into these people. Leadership is something I take incredibly seriously. I aspire to to make my team proud. I want them to be proud of the giving and philanthropy that we do. That's at the decision of myself and Robbie. There's no one else on the board or anything that is telling us that we should do that other than Rob and I arriving at that decision. I want them to be proud of who they work for. I want them to enjoy and value the people they work with. So one of the things I've been doing is there's this one restaurant out here in the valley and it's you know it's a your typical overpriced steakhouse and it's amazing meal and you know it's 5 600 dollars a person. And I've been working my way taking every single employee to this restaurant, right? I've been to this restaurant probably 40 times now and I take a handful of them at once and I want them to look back even if they're only at the company for a couple years. For them to look back in their career one day and say man it was so special you know I was an entry level CSR and the CEO of the company took me to this incredible dinner and invested time into me and asked me you know. I try to memorize before I go to a company party. I go and I write down and I have every person's I know every person's name but their spouse's name, their kids' names, was there a recent trip? And I sit there and I'll study it for hours so then when I go to the company party I can say hello to their spouse without even needing to say anything to them. And ask them hey how was that trip to Duluth I knew you were going on. And it it really surprises people where they're like how on earth does he remember all this stuff? And it's not because I have that good of memory, it's just because I care that much. I care to invest and spend hours on my team you know and so yeah and because of it here's the wicked thing right if you're ever bored you know come to Lectric's big day. So when we this is super fun so when we are the number one seller in the e-bike company I drop a banner from the rafters. And so we have a banner ceremony 'cause I don't own an NBA team right I own an e-bike company but I the NBA is cool to me so I would like to you know take from that culture. So we have in our rafters banners for each year we're number one. So like this May we have our banner ceremony, the banner drops down, it's a big celebration, we show everybody the new bikes and get everyone excited for the new year. But my team has such buy-in, the culture is incredible where people are literally face painted, right? They have thundersticks we have championship shirts that say, you know, last year's was "back to back." You know, it's just like such a cool culture and it's it's because of this shared mission and vision. We like who we work with, we we want to succeed together. And yeah I've just I've been very fortunate to build a an amazing team and they've helped build an incredible culture and I I refuse to give up on the fun stuff too. I will still at times you know it's couple times a year I will kind of shut down half my company. I'll rent out a movie theater and go see whatever new DC or Marvel movie and then I come back and I grab the other half and then I take them to the movies. I get to see a movie twice which is awesome and they all get to like have a a fun experience. Or when the new Call of Dutys come out I have like literally like 20 Xboxes set up and just everybody's playing them. Like, you know, I want to enjoy where I go for eight or 10 hours a day and I want to enjoy the people I'm around and I've unbelievably lucky that I've actually pulled it off and have built something like that. Yeah, no it's super special and going back that investing in people, I want to take a step back a little bit just briefly to the kind of early phases of your career. I think that this is something that obviously you were invested in by your dad, $40,000 he put into this company to to start off. But you kind of blew that that money right away and it and it took a second iteration to like turn that investment into something that you guys were proud of that you could continue to build on. What what is your best piece of advice for someone when they're trying to iterate? So like when that first chunk of money comes in it doesn't go so well, what should people do to kind of make it work on that second try? So two things, you gotta have the right attitude, right? You you need to understand if the math told you that this was going to work and it didn't work, right, you gotta have the right attitude and belief that all right there there is a way and do not shy away from the learnings. And you know, it's easy when you're a founder and you created something yourself that you don't want to change the product or the customer doesn't quite understand. You know that's foolish. If the customer says if your math says e-bikes are going to be incredibly popular in the US and there's demand for it and you create a product that doesn't sell well, the worst thing you could do is just try to focus on getting people to believe in that version of the product. You should listen to every single customer, figure out all the reasons why they were saying no and then redesign your bike. If that means completely ripping away everything that you you cared so much about then you should do that, right? It is just too painful to fight the downstream opinions of consumer. But so you gotta have the right attitude, you gotta be confident and believe in yourself and and the math, you know math and data is just so important when justifying your business. And just accept what the changes are and don't fight it and that will that will bring tremendous success for your business. Mhm. 'Cause I think so many founders go through that where it's like they believe in something they put something out there it doesn't quite work and sometimes it can you know be hard to admit to yourself I didn't have it totally right to begin with. I got to make a change here to to move forward. Yeah, right, totally. My last question for you, when we spoke with you in 2023, you said that 7% of the bike market in the US was going towards purchasing e-bikes. Where are we at right now? Where are we going in the future? How are you guys hoping to shape that? I think we're at about 12% right now, which is an improvement but incredibly low by Europe standards and everything like that. So you know if you look at the European market places like Germany and the Netherlands they're both over 50% of all bikes sold are are electric. And so you know for Germany from the time they went from 10% to 50% it was only about a a 10 year time difference. So that to me is what I would love to see. I think Lectric and and our other companies that we're we're moving forward with are going to help accelerate it there. If you look at our part of the market, the adoption of the e-bike to that category to that price point to that style is actually over 20%.
