So we're helping carry forward the adoption, it's the parts of the market that we currently don't serve with our price point and strategy and customer service are the ones that are actually falling behind in terms of the penetration of the electric bike to that bike market. And we're we're looking to change that. We're going at a couple parts of the market and I assume that within two years from now the the picture will look very different. Yeah. Well thank you so much Levi for taking the time today. I hope that you guys can drop a three-peat banner this year for another number one product. I already ordered the three-peat banner. Now it's on to the four. Okay, love it. Well thank you so much again for taking the time, can't wait to see all you guys do and see you in Arizona. Awesome. Thank you.

What Founders Can Learn About Excellence From MIT President Sally Kornbluth

Sequoia Capital(145.0K) ·44:29 ·4.6K views · 2026-04-16
Sally Kornbluth (President, MIT)
Sustaining organizational excellence requires a ruthless refusal to compromise on merit—avoiding "the lollipop of mediocrity"—and surviving high-stakes crises depends entirely on maintaining external composure and securing a board that prioritizes governance over management.
TL;DR Sustaining organizational excellence requires a ruthless refusal to compromise on merit—avoiding "the lollipop of mediocrity"—and surviving high-stakes crises depends entirely on maintaining external composure and securing a board that prioritizes governance over...

TL;DR

Sustaining organizational excellence requires a ruthless refusal to compromise on merit—avoiding "the lollipop of mediocrity"—and surviving high-stakes crises depends entirely on maintaining external composure and securing a board that prioritizes governance over management.

Sustaining Radical Meritocracy

  • The "Lollipop of Mediocrity": Sally Kornbluth emphasizes that excellence is fragile; if a leader takes even one "lick" of mediocrity by lowering standards, the organization’s culture is permanently compromised.
  • Pure Merit in Admissions: MIT maintains its brand of excellence by strictly adhering to quantifiable merit.
      • No "Back Doors": Unlike many peers, MIT avoids legacy admissions or "development" admissions (donors).
      • Standardized Testing: MIT was the first major institution to reinstate SAT/ACT requirements post-COVID, viewing them as essential tools for identifying talent regardless of background.
      • Financial Accessibility: The institution remains "need-blind" and provides free tuition for families earning under $200,000.
  • Consistent Messaging: Meritocracy only scales if the message is consistent across all "doors" (faculty, staff, and students). If one department or entry point allows a lower bar, the entire system regresses to the mean.

Scaling Leadership and Culture

  • Transitioning from 150 to 500+ Employees: Leaders often struggle when they stop personally interviewing every hire.
      • Structure of Lieutenants: To scale, a CEO must build a structure of trusted "lieutenants" who deeply understand the standard of excellence.
      • High-Touch Communication: Leadership emails often have low engagement (e.g., a 30% open rate at MIT regarding reopening). Effective messaging must be "fanned out" personally through the management chain rather than relying on mass broadcasts.
  • The Praise-to-Correction Ratio: Kornbluth supports the "5:1" trope—giving five pieces of praise for every one correction.
      • Authenticity: Praise costs nothing but is only effective if the leader genuinely believes it; "temporizing" or giving fake praise to keep people happy eventually erodes trust.
      • Direct Feedback: Performance issues should be addressed immediately before personal relationships (knowledge of families, pets, etc.) make difficult conversations harder.
  • Managing "Prima Donnas": In high-performance environments, brilliant but difficult individuals are common. Kornbluth argues they are manageable as long as they "have the goods" (e.g., Nobel-level output). The real danger is "low-quality" individuals with the same overbearing attitude.

Crisis Playbook: Lessons from Congress

  • External Composure vs. Internal Reality: During the high-profile Congressional testimony on antisemitism, Kornbluth’s survival was attributed to remaining calm and factual on the outside, even when feeling the process was "political theater" rather than genuine dialogue.
  • The Pivotal Role of the Board: Kornbluth identifies board support as the single most important variable in surviving a crisis.
      • Standing Firm: While Harvard and UPenn’s leadership fell, MIT’s board publicly backed Kornbluth, telling critics to "go away."
      • Propagating the Truth: Board members did more than issue statements; they read the full transcripts and personally corrected misinformation among their own networks.
  • Handling Personal Attacks: Kornbluth admits to "transiently" getting down when attacks were personal (e.g., being called an antisemite while being Jewish), but manages stress by "wearing it on her sleeve" and talking through issues incessantly with her team to get them out of her system.

Strategic Governance and Response

  • The "Thanks but No Thanks" Approach: MIT was the first of 11 institutions to reject the federal government’s "Compact for Academic Excellence."
      • Bedrock of Merit: The rejection was based on the compact’s shift toward preferential funding based on "commitments" rather than scientific merit.
      • Decisiveness: Kornbluth moved before the deadline to tell MIT's story on its own terms, rather than waiting for a collective university response.
  • Building a Functional Board:
      • Governance vs. Management: A great board understands its role is to oversee, not to micromanage the CEO.
      • Hiring for Integrity: Look for board members who are "open to arguments." Avoid those with rigid, pre-formed views who are unwilling to consider different perspectives.
      • Crisis Experience: Ideally, a board should include current or former CEOs who have navigated high-stakes situations and understand the necessity of staying calm under fire.

Notable Quotes

"If you take a lick of the lollipop of mediocrity, you will suck forever." — Sally Kornbluth (quoting a former colleague)
"People want their leaders to be calm and measured. You have to be calm and measured on the outside... even if you are screaming inside." — Sally Kornbluth
"The bedrock of scientific excellence in this country is scientific merit... MIT has a great story to tell in terms of merit, and I didn’t have any hesitancy. The story sort of told itself." — Sally Kornbluth
"Bringing in just people [instead of excellent people]—it’s better to have nobody." — Sally Kornbluth
The way you maintain meritocracy and excellence is to make sure that each person you bring in—and for us, this means all of our faculty, all of our staff, and all of our students—we have to consistently focus on excellence. There was a colleague of mine at Duke who had a sign in his office that said, "If you take a lick of the lollipop of mediocrity, you will suck forever." And I, I always liked that. Hey everybody, we have Sally Kornbluth on the pod today. She's president of MIT, and a real gem. She's had a really interesting journey. Within a year of being hired as president of MIT, she was summoned to Congress and spoke alongside the president of Harvard and UPenn about all manner of things. You probably all remember Liz Magill really asking her some tough questions, and all three presidents not really nailing that. So, we get into that and her response to it, and I think her response was excellent. Neither the Harvard president nor the UPenn president made it through that crisis. She not only survived, but she thrived in it. She also is having more recent run-ins with the Compact for Excellence in Higher Education that the federal government gave her, which was pretty interesting, and her reaction to that I thought was really interesting and showed terrific leadership. There were only 11 or 12 institutions that got it, and I thought MIT shined on that. So, she is good in a crisis, and talks a bunch about that. Sally and I talked a lot about what I refer to as "sustaining meritocracy." She's got a giant organization and over 150 years, you know, they are synonymous with excellence and synonymous with meritocracy. The natural state of things is you kind of regress to the mean, and most companies do that, so we talked about how they keep the standards high and how they sustain it over long periods of time. I think it's super applicable to scaling CEOs. She's got a great quote in there that stuck in my head: "If you take a lick of the lollipop of mediocrity, you suck forever." I think she hits the nail on the head in that one. And then she gets into just some blocking and tackling of how to be a CEO of a large organization, some things that I need a reminder on, and hopefully, they're useful to you. And lastly, we talked a bunch about how to manage, how to hire a board, and how to manage a board and how to get the most out of a board. I hope you like it; she's a gem. My nephew Jack is applying to business schools. He's a wonderful kid, and I really want him to go to Sloan. I went to Sloan, I teach at Sloan, and I've worked hard to influence the process. I went through the front door, I went through the back door, I went through the basement and the attic. Every door I go to, they're like, "We don't care who you are. You're not going to influence the process." 100%. I love that. I love that we were the first place to bring back SATs and ACTs after COVID. I love that we're need-blind. Can you talk about that? And it's like, it's easy for companies to be meritocracies like we are; it's hard to scale it. Like, how do we—how do you scale that? Well, you know, first of all, I should say, I had the same experience when I got here because someone said, "Oh, can you, you know, speak to someone about getting my kid into MIT?" And I was like, "Well, you know, I can just tell them to take a closer look." No. No, I cannot. I was new here, and they were like, "We do not do that at MIT. We just don't." So, it is, it is pure merit. You know, it's interesting. I think the way to scale meritocracy, if you want to extrapolate, is: how do you scale excellence? And the thing is that when you start a company, or when you start a new team or a new job, you're probably the best person out there at it, right? And there's a temptation to just hire people. And honestly, bringing in just people, it's better to have nobody. The way you maintain meritocracy and excellence is to make sure that each person you bring in—and for us, this means all of our faculty, all of our staff, all of our students—we have to consistently focus on excellence. There was a colleague of mine at Duke who had a sign in his office that said, "If you take a lick of the lollipop of mediocrity, you will suck forever." And I, I always liked that. And, you know, so we really pay attention that each hire is excellent so that when people collaborate across MIT, they know it's not just them that are going to do good work; they know that the people next door are also going to do good work, or the people down the hall. So, I think that is a very big part of it, is just having a mindset that consistency is really important. And to your point about going from one door to another door to another door, it has to be a consistent message across the whole institution. Because if one of those doors had been open, then you could bet people would have flooded through that door. I definitely would have gotten through if there was a door open. Exactly. Exactly. He did get in, obviously. I had nothing to do with it, and I feel bad for him because everyone assumes I got him in. I had nothing to do with it. Well, you know, the other thing is that merit can be defined in a number of different ways. I mean, I think people think that, "Sure, you got to get high grades to get into MIT. You got to have great test scores to get into MIT." But there's so many dimensions, and I would define it in this way: we want to bring in people who can benefit from the fantastic environment we have and who will contribute to that fantastic environment. And there can be a lot of dimensions on which this is true. They're not all completely quantifiable. A lot of them come through in the intangible. Is it cultural or is it written down? It's not exactly written down because it's a little bit like the "if you know it, you see it." And that obviously could be subject to a lot of objectivity. So, I don't mean that it's not written down in the sense that we don't have certain criteria in mind; I just mean there's some flexibility to it, to take into account a wide variety of what you or I might call excellent, fantastic people. Got it. Okay. I coach all these CEOs, they're terrific, and like something happens around 150 employees where the founder no longer interviews everyone. Right. And the bar drops a little bit, and they wake up at 500 employees, and you're like, "What the hell happened to my company?" You're that CEO, you drop the bar, and then you try to pick it up at 500. What would you do? What advice would you give that CEO? Well, I would say this. First of all, again, coming to the fact that in your head or in reality, you may be the best person and you've held up that standard. It is a lot easier to stop that slide than to recover from it, as you say. So, the most important thing when someone's starting is to build a strong team to whom they can delegate and who they can trust. In other words, there's a little bit sometimes of trust-but-verify at the beginning; you want to see if someone's really good. But after that, micromanagement is really problematic and it's easy to criticize, but you really have to let people run a little bit. And I think to your point, you have to build a strong structure of lieutenants along the way when you're getting to 150 to 500, etc. And if you can't convey that message down through the troops, you're never going to be able to get control of it. I have to say, even at a place like MIT, we do have trouble though sometimes communicating those messages, even if we have a hierarchy. My best example of this was actually from my previous institution, okay? Everyone was bugging us, "When are we going to reopen?" This was after COVID. "When are we going to reopen? When are we going to reopen?" And, you know, we sent out an email to everybody about when we're going to reopen. It had a 30% opening rate. Okay. So, no one really cared that much. No, they cared, but they still didn't want to read leadership emails. So, I really think that high touch, series of people who can fan out and carry your message personally becomes really important. Sally, I have trust issues, and I've found almost all the CEOs I work with have trust issues, and it's a very, very small amount of employees they deeply trust to hire and do things cross-functionally. Do you have trust issues? I would say no in the sense that I try to, I try to nip that in the bud up front, which is again—you know, people criticized me a little bit at first when I came in, like, "Well, why aren't you turning over these positions?" I think I needed time to assess the capabilities of every person. And so when I make a decision, I really feel good about it and have a gut feeling. Now, that said, if someone does violate the trust, or if someone is really doing a bad job, I think it behooves you to tell them in a very straightforward, clear way right away. Don't wait. Don't wait, because once time has gone on it becomes harder and harder and harder. You know their kids, you know their family, you know their dogs, you know. "Sorry, you're doing a really bad job." See, you can't do that. And so, I find that really just being very frank when someone's doing less than I want—to be very clear about what it is. But also, the flip side is to tell people when they're doing well, and I think that is sometimes hard to remember. You know, it's very easy to kind of think, "Oh, they're doing a great job and I'm doing such a great job, of course I'm leading such a great team." I think people really, really like to hear it when they're doing well. And if you can do that, then you can tell people when things aren't going well. No, there's an old CEO trope that you should give praise five times—you should give praise to every one sort of correction. Do you buy into that ratio or is it a bunch of BS? Oh, I totally buy into that ratio. I think that's absolutely right. And you know what? It doesn't cost you anything. No, it doesn't. You know, if you're showing gratitude toward the people who are doing things, it doesn't cost you anything. Now, if you tell people they're good when they're not really that good just to kind of temporize, that's where you really run into trouble because you can't blame them for thinking they're doing a great job when you're just kind of saying that to keep them happy and keep things moving along. So, I think that ratio is good if you really believe it, but if you don't believe it, don't propagate that message. Okay. I spoke with a bunch of people who work for you to prepare. I did my homework. None of it's true. They said wonderful things. Okay, maybe someone said something that was echoed a bunch: that you're bold but not overbearing. And when I was a CEO, I was bold and overbearing. What advice do you give to someone like me? I'm like most CEOs; how do you scale that? Well, you know, I have to say that the training of a scientist who was training graduate students taught me a lot. Being a graduate student can be fabulous—you're doing wonderful things, it's so exciting, it's so interesting. It can also be—one of my students called it—the "dark tea time of the soul," you know? You're slogging away, it's like horrible, and but you have to keep pushing. That's mostly when your experiments aren't working. When things are working, it's like super exciting and super fun. And so what I learned was that you want things from the people who are working, particularly when you're starting a lab; their experiments have to work because you're not doing the experiments every day. And so you have to think about ways to motivate them, and sometimes it feels a little bit Machiavellian. You know? But what it really is about is thinking, "What do they need?" What can you give them that they need, so that you get what you need from it? And I learned a lot about individually catering to people's motivations, trying to figure out what makes them tick and makes them really productive and what makes them happy. Now, that doesn't scale infinitely, but for your immediate team, there really is a huge amount of individuality in terms of what makes people do good work, and I think really getting to know the people individually becomes extremely important in terms of motivating them. And sometimes you're like, "Why do they think that's fun or interesting?" But you have to indulge that. Especially here. Okay. That's very helpful. There's a columnist named George Will. He described it, I think accurately, as inexplicably ebullient. And I just, again, I ask from my own perspective: like, my day is having meetings and reading my inbox, it's mostly bad news. You know, it's a lot of bad news and you get really, really, really annoyed. I would get down, and all CEOs get down. Why are you so ebullient in the face of, I'm sure, there's a shit show going on behind you? So yeah, well, you know, in part I think I'm inexplicably ebullient, which is—you know, there's some unbelievable things going on here. Every day I hear a discovery that makes my jaw drop. So for me to be able to enable the discoveries of the people around me, I am, you know, essentially helping science move forward that's way better than the science I did myself. So that's pretty fun. The other thing is, you know, two things about it. One is you got to find humor in some of this, like you really can't make some of this stuff up. So that kind of keeps me, you know, a little bit up. The other thing is this phenomenon in biology called "receptor down regulation." What that means is if you have a receptor for a drug or for any molecule, if you keep stimulating it over and over again, eventually it kind of like goes away and down regulates. So, the last few years have been just like a constant incoming. And so eventually you're like, "Okay, well, I could live miserable all the time or, you know, I can rise above it and find, you know, pleasure in all the cool things we're doing." It doesn't mean I don't get pissed off every day about something, because I do, but you can do that without becoming depressed about it if you ignore the fact that a lot of it is pretty annoying. Okay. You've been through some stuff. You get down? I get down when it's personal. So, one thing about the last few years—some of the attacks—so for instance, after I testified in Congress on antisemitism, I got a lot of incoming that was very personal, and I'm Jewish. To be called an antisemite over and over again, I did not find appropriate. And so when it's personal, when people say negative things about me, some of the emails people sent to me, I was sort of like, "Go wash your mouth out, folks." But would you get down? Transiently. I would get a little bit. Now, I don't get down about all the other junk that's coming into everybody in our sector, but yes, I would get transiently down. But the way I deal with it is to just, you know, talk to people incessantly. Like, I'm a little bit of a "wear it on my sleeve, get it out of my system" person. It may wear down the other people a little bit, but that's definitely how I operate. Okay. You brought that up. You just started, you barely know your way around the Infinite Corridor, and you get summoned to Congress. I assume you did not see that coming. I did not see that coming. When you got the summons, what was going on inside of your head? Who did you call? Well, first of all, it wasn't a choice. People ask me, "Why did you go?" And I went because it said, "If you don't go, it's a summons and you will be subpoenaed if you don't come." Okay, that's the first thing. Look, you know, the first people I obviously talked to were the team, including people like Alfred Ironside, our VP for communications. We talk—I talked to a lot of other friends in universities. I was naive. I think the biggest disappointment to me of that whole thing was not that it was hard to deal with personally, it was that it was theater. It was theater. And you know, I was a political science major in college, focusing on American political institutions, and I was so disappointed in the quality of the dialogue and the questioning. I know that sounds really naive, but I thought we were really having an interchange, but it wasn't. It was political theater. And I was ill—well, not that I was ill-equipped; I actually think I was prepared pretty well. And if you listen to the full testimony, the full four hours or five hours or whatever it is, or read the transcript, it was basically fine until sort of the gotcha moment at the end. But it reminded me a lot—you know, I should have—it reminded me of the Michael Dukakis moment with the helmet. Well, no, when they were asking about his wife being attacked and he was very factual. I wish I—and so I sort of did that. I maintained a very factual tone. What I should have said was, "Oh my God, nobody would say that on our campus." But, you know, hindsight is 2020. Okay. So you wish you had a do-over? Not really, because I don't want to do it again. But I would have handled it differently. Okay. So I assume you were prepped by your lawyers and everybody for that, and you're sitting there listening to the president of Harvard and the president of UPenn, you're watching the whole thing, and then you answer the question kind of similarly to them, a little bit better. No, I was first on that question. That was the problem. They'd been going Harvard, Penn, MIT for every question. Oh, interesting. On the press, Harvard was first. Yeah, they'd been going Harvard, Penn, MIT for every question, until that last question. Did you know you had messed up that second? No, I didn't. When did you find out that your 15 minutes of fame was right now? Well, well before Saturday Night Live. But I, you know, I was naive. Most people who talked to me were like, "Yeah, you did pretty well." They didn't realize that that moment would become viral. You know, as soon as I read the newspaper, I saw it, and you know, then I started whenever I start getting a lot of texts from friends from my old days at Duke, "How you doing?" I know there's something really bad out there. When I get like 10 texts in a row like, "Oh my God." But you know, survived it. Some of these CEOs I talked to in an interview go through major crises. This is a whopper. What was it like? So first of all, I think you—people want their leaders to be calm and measured. And so, first of all, I think no matter what for me, no matter what I was thinking inside, and a lot of it was like, "Okay." You know, fine. I'm happy to hear that because you were calm and measured on the outside. But you have to be calm and measured on the outside. I mean, you have a human reaction, but I felt it was so unfair. And it's interesting because one of my biggest—it's sort of almost a joke in my family—one of my biggest sort of Achilles heels is the notion that I don't want to be accused of something I didn't do. And you know, I even watch lots of crime shows, etc., and the ones that really, like, I can barely watch are the people who are falsely accused of crimes. So I felt during that whole incident that I was being accused of having certain views or thinking certain things, and people extrapolated from that, and that to me was the crux of it. I felt it was unfair. But you know, we have a lot to do around here. So I had to move past it. I thought you handled the aftermath, and I think this is a lesson for every CEO through crises: you clarified your remarks and you got it focused back on the course. That was one good thing. The second thing that happened is the board publicly came out and backed you. That's what I was going to say. Neither the other two stepped down shortly after those remarks. That's right. So talk about both those things. Let's start with the remarks, the clarification. Who did you call? How did you think about that? It was very quick like, "That was excellent. That was excellent leadership." Well, you know, I tried to not create another news cycle. And I think one thing we did for quite a long time was to be very quiet about it. I mean, you know, I wasn't about to start like showing up on TV shows and I was just very clear with our community; I think that's the first thing. But the other thing in terms of our board or corporation being supportive, that is actually the pivotal moment in terms of me surviving at MIT because they were like, "We just hired her. It was fine. Go away." And that really created an environment where I felt I had a lot of support; I could keep moving on with what we were doing at MIT. And, you know, to this day when I do something, I don't look over my shoulder and think, "Oh my God, are they going to fire me over this?" Because you know, we have a great relationship. Now that doesn't mean they won't fire me for something someday, but at the moment I will say, I feel like transparency and great interchange—I knew right away they were supportive. The other thing is many, many people on our board did listen to the whole thing, did read the whole transcript, and when people would come to them and say, "Oh my God, how could you not fire her?" they would say, "Did you listen to the whole thing?" And then they would distribute it or talk to people about it, and so it wasn't only that they were being supportive in voice; they actually were propagating the correct message. So I think that made a huge difference. My takeaways were: you were calm—you were in a crisis, you were calm. You had a good board that backed you, which is invaluable. And you clarified your statements in a thoughtful way. And I think that's—I think that's a best practice right there that all CEOs can kind of learn from. Okay, there's been some similar—I wouldn't say crisis-like, actually, a crisis averted. The federal government sent you the Compact for Academic Excellence and Higher Ed; they sent it to 11 institutions including us. You were the first to respond, which was interesting, way before the deadline. And you basically, very politely said, "No," and here is why. Why were you first? Well, so a couple of reasons. First of all, when I received the compact, I looked at it and I said, "There's no way I'm signing this thing." Right away. And I called Mark Gorenberg, the chair of the corporation; I said, "Mark, I'm not signing this thing." And he goes, "Who's going to make you sign this thing?" So right away, I knew I had the chair's support and we went to the executive committee, everyone was supportive, and then eventually the whole board, so that was the first thing. Part of the reason I think we came out quickly rather than collectively is because this has been true throughout all these crises: every school has a little bit of a different story to tell. And the crux of this coming back to your first question was about merit. You know, I didn't care what the particulars of the letter were; they talked about giving preferential funding based on behaviors or commitments, not on scientific merit. And to me, the bedrock of scientific excellence in this country is scientific merit. Now, MIT has a great story to tell in terms of merit. No legacy admissions, no development admissions, under 200k family income free tuition. We reinstated the SATs before other schools, etc. We had such a great story to tell in terms of merit, I didn't really have any hesitancy. We didn't have to think, "Well, how can we couch this?" The story sort of told itself. So I wasn't trying to, you know, make a huge statement or speak for anyone else. I just felt like we had a good story to tell and we're going to tell it. The other thing is I didn't want to poke anyone in the eye. I thought the tone that we were striving for was "Thanks but no thanks." Like, we'll compete with other people. The letter was extremely well written. Who wrote that? Okay. So we have a fantastic writer, Martha Eddison. She's on the PR team. Someone should have a great writer to help them, I'll tell you that. But what Martha did was generate a long draft on all these points, and then four or five of us iterated for multiple days around the clock. You know, so I would not have written everything she wrote by scratch, but there were pieces of that that I did write myself; there were pieces of that that various members of the team wrote. And actually, we came down to a very few words at the end that actually took us a couple extra days, and we were sitting with the executive committee of our board discussing the letter, and we wanted to get some of the words exactly right. And so we were arguing about different things and I remember our EVP, Glenn Shore, who is the financial person, finally like came up with a phrase and everyone was like, "Okay, we're done here." So there was a lot of group think thinking about this, and I think we came to a common message because we hang together well as a team, and we were able to be blunt. You know, Martha will write something, I'm like, "That is not what we want to say." She's like, "Okay, tell me what you want to say." You brought up the board a bunch, more than I thought you would. And all my CEOs have to build boards before they go public. Advice on building a board and managing a board? Yeah, so I obviously didn't build this board, but I do have input via our governance committee now in terms of who joins the board. You know, first of all, we have a very large board, 75 people, so that is not like most companies. But you must have a core group. We do, the executive committee. Look, you want a range of expertise, but fundamentally, you want people who are smart, with integrity, and open to arguments. You know, if people come in with very strong views and they're unwilling to think about other viewpoints, it's going to be incredibly frustrating. Part of the reason I brought it up honestly is because it's unusual to have a board that you sync with so easily. I have friends all over the country and some of them have great boards, some of them don't, and they have to always kind of be maneuvering. What I really like is their governance, but not management. And so they let me sort of do my job. And so I've been, you know, I've been pretty lucky that way. That doesn't mean we agree on everything; in fact, some of the things in the compact letter, I had a lot of phone calls with people and said, "No, I'm not going to do that," or "No, I'm not going to say that." But it's kind of an open exchange. I don't really feel like I have to parse my words and I don't think they do either. That's what I think's critical: people that you can be honest with, that will be honest with you, and it's not existential. Sounds like they were all also calm in a crisis, and boards all deal with crises. They were calm. They were calm and many of them are CEOs or have been CEOs. It's not like you want to fill your whole board with CEOs, but you want people who have navigated crises, been on other boards, been in high-stakes situations, and understand that staying calm is a huge piece of managing to navigate the crisis. Okay, kind of bringing it back to MIT, it's a pretty decentralized place, like really decentralized. How do you decide when it's like, "No, that thing that's on the edge, I'm going to bring that in the center and we're going to centralize that?" And then how do you decide—like you get some big initiatives you're pushing out there—how do you decide? Yeah, so the centralization thing is a lot thornier than what initiatives you're going to do. Everybody wants one of their own everything. And when you're in a budget crisis or you really have to reduce, getting rid of duplication is incredibly difficult. Everyone views their own research program, their own team, as like their personal babies, right? And so it's hard. So we try to do it when it's absolutely necessary, either for efficiency or financial reasons, or because the function will just work way better. And then you kind of navigate the noise until people realize like, "Yeah, actually this is kind of better." In terms of the initiatives at a place like MIT, a lot of it is from the bottom up, in that we look around, we see what great work is going on and we try to capture it. I see. So what I wanted to do with these initiatives—we have a suite of presidential initiatives now—I wanted to encourage interdisciplinarity. And that comes back to everybody being at a high level, so whoever you collaborate with, you're going to get good partners. Can I interrupt you on that? Every CEO I talked to asked me, "What do I do with the prima donna, the brilliant asshole?" Sorry for the... every place has them. I, we have a lot of prima donnas at MIT. How do you deal with the prima donnas? So you know, I've encountered prima donnas in my career. Most of the prima donnas that I've seen in MIT—and there's not that many compared to some places—at least they have the goods. Okay. The worst thing is like someone who thinks they're great when they're not. Like, if someone wants to come to me and say, "My program's really important, this is really critical and fantastic," and like you know, they've just won a Nobel Prize, well, I'm not going to argue with them. I mean, the stuff here is really amazing. I really haven't had someone come to defense of something that I thought was really like low quality. Okay. But that does make it harder to take things away. How do you take things away? Like, CEOs all the time, they're like, "We're decentralized, decentralized," and it's like, "No, no, no, we're going to start to..." Yeah, it's hard. Particularly now there's a thing called "founder mode," if you've ever heard of it. And like you look at Jack Dorsey, for example, he just laid off 40% of the employees. Lots of people were decentralized; micromanagement's in style. What's your take on all this? Well, first of all, remember that a big part of our workforce is tenured. So that's a very different environment to navigate in. You know what I found all along in these roles is like, you want to say yes a bunch of times, but you have to say no a huge amount of your time. And I found if you engage someone and really give them a good rationale—you've really thought through it, it's not just like "I don't like you" or "I don't want to do this"—most people will ultimately accept it. Not all people; I'm sure there are people out there that hate me because I said no or took something away. But in general, I think people understand that you have to make these decisions. Okay. You've been through some stuff. You get down? I get down when it's personal. So, one thing about the last few years—some of the attacks—so for instance, after I testified in Congress on antisemitism, I got a lot of incoming that was very personal, and I'm Jewish. To be called an antisemite over and over again, I did not find appropriate. And so when it's personal, when people say negative things about me, some of the emails people sent to me, I was sort of like, "Go wash your mouth out, folks." But would you get down? Transiently. I would get a little bit. Now, I don't get down about all the other junk that's coming into everybody in our sector, but yes, I would get transiently down. But the way I deal with it is to just like, you know, talk to people incessantly. Like, I'm a little bit of a "wear it on my sleeve, get it out of my system" person. It may wear down the other people a little bit, but that's definitely how I operate. Okay. You brought that up. You just started, you barely know your way around the Infinite Corridor, and you get summoned to Congress. What was your—you did not see that coming, I assume. I did not see that coming. When you got the summons, what was going on inside of your head? Who did you call? Well, first of all, it wasn't a choice. People ask me, "Why did you go?" And I went because it said, "If you don't go, it's a summons and you will be subpoenaed if you don't come." Okay, that's the first thing. Look, you know, the first people I obviously talked to were the team, including people like Alfred Ironside, you know, our VP for communications. We talk—I talk to a lot of other friends in universities. I did not—I was naive. I think the biggest disappointment to me of that whole thing was not that it was hard to deal with personally, it was that it was theater. It was theater. It was and, you know, I was a political science major in college, focusing on American political institutions, and I was so disappointed in the quality of the dialogue and the questioning. I know that sounds really naive, but I thought we were really having an interchange, but it wasn't. It was political theater. And I was ill—well, not that I was ill-equipped, I actually think I was prepared pretty well. If you listen to the full testimony—the full four hours or five hours or whatever it is—or read the transcript, it was basically fine until sort of the gotcha moment at the end. But it reminded me a lot—you know, I should have done—it reminded me of the Michael Dukakis moment when the helmet... yeah, well no, when they were asking about his wife being attacked and he was very factual. I wish I—and so I sort of did that. I maintained a very factual tone. What I should have said was, "Oh my God, nobody would say that on our campus." But, you know, hindsight is 20/20. Okay. So you wish you had a do-over? Not really, because I don't want to do it again. But I would have handled it differently. Okay. So I assume you were prepped by your lawyers and everybody for that, and you're sitting there listening to the president of Harvard and the president of UPenn; you're watching the whole thing, and then you answer the question kind of similarly to them, a little bit better. No, I was first on that question. That was the problem. They'd been going Harvard, Penn, MIT for every question. Oh, interesting. On the press, Harvard was first. Yeah, they'd been going Harvard, Penn, MIT for every question until that last question. Did you know you had messed up that second? No. I didn't. When did you find out that your 15 minutes of fame was right now? Well, well before Saturday Night Live, but I... you know, I was naive. Most people who talked to me were like, "Yeah, you did pretty well." They didn't realize that that moment would become viral. You know, as soon as I read the newspaper, I saw it. And you know, then I started—whenever I start getting a lot of texts from friends from my old days at Duke, "How you doing?"—I know there's something really bad out there. When I get like 10 texts in a row like, "Oh my God," but you know, I survived it. Some of these CEOs I talked to in an interview go through major crises. This is a whopper. What was it like? So first of all, I think people want their leaders to be calm and measured. And so first of all, I think no matter what for me—no matter what I was thinking inside and a lot of it was like, "Okay"—you know, fine, I'm happy to hear that because you were calm and measured on the outside. But you have to be calm and measured on the outside. I mean, you have a human reaction, but I felt it was so unfair. And it's interesting because one of my biggest—it's sort of almost a joke in my family—one of my biggest Achilles' heels is the notion that I don't want to be accused of something I didn't do. And you know, I even watch lots of crime shows etc., and the ones that really—I can barely watch—are the people who are falsely accused of crimes. So I felt during that whole incident that I was being accused of having certain views or thinking certain things, and people extrapolated from that. That to me was the crux of it. I felt it was unfair. But you know, we have a lot to do around here. So I had to move past it. I thought you handled the aftermath—and I think this is a lesson for every CEO through crises—like, you clarified your remarks and you got it focused back on the course. That was one good thing. The second thing that happened is the board publicly came out and backed you. That's what I was going to say. And the other two stepped down shortly after those remarks. That's right. So talk about both those things. Let's start with the remarks, the clarification. Who did you call? How did you think about that? It was very quick like, "That was excellent. That was excellent leadership." Well, you know, I tried to not create another news cycle. And I think one thing we did for quite a long time was to be very quiet about it. I mean, you know, I wasn't about to start like showing up on TV shows and I was just very clear with our community; I think that's the first thing. But the other thing in terms of our board or corporation being supportive, that is actually the pivotal moment in terms of me surviving at MIT. Because they were like, "We just hired her. It was fine. Go away." And that really created an environment where I felt I had a lot of support; I could keep moving on with what we were doing at MIT. And, you know, to this day when I do something, I don't look over my shoulder and think, "Oh my God, are they going to fire me over this?" because you know, we have a great relationship. Now that doesn't mean they won't fire me for something someday, but at the moment I will say, I feel like transparency, great interchange, and I knew right away they were supportive. The other thing is many, many people on our board did listen to the whole thing, did read the whole transcript, and when people would come to them and say, "Oh my God, how could you not fire her?" they would say, "Did you listen to the whole thing?" And then they would distribute it or talk to people about it. So it wasn't only that they were being supportive in voice; they actually were propagating the correct message. So I think that made a huge difference. My takeaways were: you were calm—you were in a crisis, you were calm. You had a good board that backed you, which is invaluable. And you clarified your statements in a thoughtful way. And I think that's a best practice right there that all CEOs can kind of learn from. Okay, there's been some similar—I wouldn't say crisis-like, actually, a crisis averted. The federal government sent you the Compact for Academic Excellence and Higher Ed; they sent it to 11 institutions including us. You were the first to respond, which was interesting, way before the deadline. And you basically, very politely said, "No," and here is why. Why were you first? Well, so a couple of reasons. First of all, when I received the compact, I looked at it and I said, "There's no way I'm signing this thing." "Okay, great." "Right away." And I called Mark Gorenberg, the chair of the corporation; I said, "Mark, I'm not signing this thing." And he goes, "Who's going to make you sign this thing?" And the crux of this, coming back to your first question, was about merit. You know, I didn't care what the particulars of the letter were; they talked about giving preferential funding based on behaviors or commitments, not on scientific merit. And to me, the bedrock of scientific excellence in this country is scientific merit. Now, MIT has a great story to tell in terms of merit. You know, an awesome story. The awesome story to tell: no legacy admissions, no development admissions, free tuition for family income under 200k. We reinstated the SATs before other schools, etc. We had such a great story to tell that, in terms of merit, I didn't really have any hesitancy. We didn't have to think, "Well, how can we couch this?" The story sort of told itself. So I wasn't trying to, you know, make a huge statement or speak for anyone else. I just felt like we had a good story to tell and we're going to tell it. The other thing is I didn't want to poke anyone in the eye. I thought the tone that we were striving for was "Thanks, but no thanks." Like, we'll compete with other people. The letter was extremely well written. Who wrote that? Okay. And actually we came down to a very few words at the end that actually took us a couple extra days. You know, Martha will write something, I'm like, "That is not what we want to say." Okay, she's like, "Okay, tell me what you want to say." So I felt during that whole incident that I was being accused of having certain views or thinking certain things, and people extrapolated from that, and that to me was the crux of it. I felt it was unfair. But you know, we have a lot to do around here. So I had to move past it. I thought you handled the aftermath—and I think this is a lesson for every CEO through crises—like, you clarified your remarks and you got it focused back on the course. That was one good thing. The second thing that happened is the board publicly came out and backed you. That's what I was going to say. And the other two stepped down shortly after those remarks. That's right. So talk about both those things. Let's start with the remarks, the clarification. Who did you call? How did you think about that? It was very quick like, "That was excellent. That was excellent leadership." Well, you know, I tried to not create another news cycle. And I think one thing we did for quite a long time was to be very quiet about it. I mean, you know, I wasn't about to start like showing up on TV shows and I was just very clear with our community; I think that's the first thing. But the other thing in terms of our board or corporation being supportive, that is actually the pivotal moment in terms of me surviving at MIT. Because they were like, "We just hired her. It was fine. Go away." And that really created an environment where I felt I had a lot of support; I could keep moving on with what we were doing at MIT. And, you know, to this day when I do something, I don't look over my shoulder and think, "Oh my God, are they going to fire me over this?" because you know, we have a great relationship. Now that doesn't mean they won't fire me for something someday, but at the moment I will say, I feel like transparency, great interchange, and I knew right away they were supportive. The other thing is many, many people on our board did listen to the whole thing, did read the whole transcript, and when people would come to them and say, "Oh my God, how could you not fire her?" they would say, "Did you listen to the whole thing?" And then they would distribute it or talk to people about it. And so it wasn't only that they were being supportive in voice, they actually were propagating the correct message. So I think that made a huge difference. My takeaways were: you were calm, you were in a crisis, you were calm. You had a good board that backed you, which is invaluable. And you clarified your statements in a thoughtful way. And I think that's a best practice right there that all CEOs can kind of learn from. Okay, there's been some similar—I wouldn't say crisis-like, actually, a crisis averted. The federal government sent you the Compact for Academic Excellence and Higher Ed; they sent it to 11 institutions including us. You were the first to respond, which was interesting, way before the deadline. And you basically, very politely said, "No," and here is why. Why were you first? Well, so a couple of reasons. First of all, when I received the compact, I looked at it and I said, "There's no way I'm signing this thing." "Okay, great. Right away." And I called Mark Gorenberg, the chair of the corporation; I said, "Mark, I'm not signing this thing." And he goes, "Who's going to make you sign this thing?" So right away, I knew I had the chair's support and we went to the executive committee; everyone was supportive and then eventually the whole board. So that was the first thing. Okay. Part of the reason I think we came out quickly rather than collectively is because—this has been true throughout all these crises—every school has a little bit of a different story to tell. And the crux of this, coming back to your first question, was about merit. You know, I didn't care what the particulars of the letter were; they talked about giving preferential funding based on behaviors or commitments, not on scientific merit. And to me, the bedrock of scientific excellence in this country is scientific merit. Now, MIT has a great story to tell in terms of merit. You know, an awesome story. The awesome story to tell: no legacy admissions, no development admissions, free tuition for family income under 200k. We reinstated the SATs before other schools, etc. We had such a great story to tell that, in terms of merit, I didn't really have any hesitancy. We didn't have to think, "Well, how can we couch this?" The story sort of told itself. So I wasn't trying to, you know, make a huge statement or speak for anyone else. I just felt like we had a good story to tell and we're going to tell it. The other thing is I didn't want to poke anyone in the eye. I thought the tone that we were striving for was "Thanks, but no thanks." Like, we'll compete with other people. The letter was extremely well written. Who wrote that? Okay. So we have a fantastic writer, Martha Eddison. And she's on the PR team. Should all CEOs have one of these? Well, someone should have a great writer to help them, I'll tell you that. But what Martha did was generate a long draft on all these points, and then four or five of us iterated for multiple days around the clock. You know, so I would not have written everything she wrote from scratch, but there were pieces of that that I did write myself, and there were pieces of that that various members of the team wrote. And actually we came down to a very few words at the end that actually took us a couple extra days. We were sitting with the executive committee of our board discussing the letter and we wanted to get some of the words exactly right. And so we were arguing about different things. I remember our EVP, Glenn Shore, who is the financial person, finally came up with a phrase and everyone was like, "Okay, we're done here." So there was a lot of groupthink thinking about this, and I think we came to a common message because we hang together well as a team, and we were able to be blunt. You know, Martha will write something, I'm like, "That is not what we want to say." Okay, she's like, "Okay, tell me what you want to say." You brought up the board a bunch, more than I thought you would. And all my CEOs have to build boards before they go public. Yes. Advice on building a board and managing a board? Yeah, so I obviously didn't build this board, but I do have input via our governance committee now in terms of who joins the board. You know, first of all, we have a very large board—75 people—so that is not like most companies. But you must have a core group. We do, the executive committee. Yeah. Look, you want a range of expertise, but fundamentally, you want people who are smart, with integrity, and open to arguments. Okay. You know, if people come in with very strong views and they're unwilling to think about other viewpoints, it's going to be incredibly frustrating. Part of the reason I brought it up, honestly, is because it's unusual to have a board that you sync with so easily. I have friends all over the country and some of them have great boards, some of them don't, and have to always kind of be maneuvering. What I really like is their focus on governance, but not management. And so they let me sort of do my job. And so I've been, you know, I've been pretty lucky that way. That doesn't mean we agree on everything; in fact, on some of the things in the compact letter, I had a lot of phone calls with people and said, "No, I'm not going to do that, or no, I'm not going to say that." But it's kind of an open exchange; I don't really feel like I have to parse my words and I don't think they do either. That's what I think's critical: people that you can be honest with, that will be honest with you, and it's not existential. Like if someone is really feeling like... Sounds like they were all also calm in a crisis, and boards all deal with crises. They were calm. They were calm and many of them are CEOs, have been CEOs or are CEOs. It's not like you want to fill your whole board with CEOs, but you want people who have navigated crises, been on other boards, been in high-stakes situations, and understand that staying calm is a huge piece of managing to navigate the crisis. Okay, kind of bringing it back to MIT—it's a pretty decentralized place, like really decentralized. How do you decide when it's like, "No, that thing that's on the edge I'm going to bring that into the center and we're going to centralize that?" And then how do you decide—like you get some big initiatives you're pushing out there—how do you decide? Yeah, so the centralization thing is a lot thornier than what initiatives you're going to do. Everybody wants one of their own everything. And when you're in a budget crisis or you really have to reduce, it's getting rid of duplication is incredibly difficult; everyone views their own research program, their own team as their personal babies, right? And so it's hard. So we try to do it when it's absolutely necessary, either for efficiency or financial reasons, or because the function will just work way better. And then you kind of navigate the noise until people realize like, "Yeah, actually this is kind of better." In terms of the initiatives at a place like MIT, a lot of it is from the bottom up, in that we look around, we see what great work is going on and we try to capture it. I see. So, what I wanted to do with these initiatives—we have a suite of presidential initiatives now—I wanted to encourage interdisciplinarity. And that comes back to everybody being at a high level so whoever you collaborate with, you're going to get good partners. Can I interrupt you on that? Yes. Every CEO I talked to asked me, "What do I do with the prima donna, the brilliant asshole?" Sorry for the... every place has them. I know, we have a lot of primadonnas at MIT. How do you deal with the primadonnas? So you know, most of—I've looked, I've encountered primadonnas in my career. Most of the primadonnas that I've seen at MIT—and there's not that many compared to some places—at least they have the goods. Okay. The worst thing is like someone who thinks they're great when they're not. Like, if someone wants to come to me and say, "My program's really important, this is really, you know, critical and fantastic," and like you know, they've just won a Nobel Prize, well I'm not going to argue with them. I mean, the stuff here is really amazing. I really haven't had someone come to defense of something that I thought was really like low quality. Okay. But that does make it harder to take things away. How do you take things away? Like, CEOs all the time, they're like, "We're decentralized, decentralized," and it's like, "No, no, no, we're going to start to..." Yeah, it's hard. Particularly now there's a thing called "founder mode," you've ever heard of it? Yeah. And like you look at Jack Dorsey, for example, he just laid off 40% of the employees. Lots of people were decentralised; micromanagement's in style. What's your take on all this? Well, first of all, remember that a big part of our workforce is tenured. So that's a very different environment to navigate in. You know what I found all along in these roles: you want to say yes a bunch of times, but you have to say no a huge amount of your time. And I found if you engage someone and really give them a good rationale—you've really thought through it, it's not just like I don't like you or I don't want to do this—most people will ultimately accept it. Not all people; I'm sure there are people out there that hate me because I said no or took something away. But in general, I think people understand that you have to make these decisions. Okay. You've been through some stuff. You get down? I get down when it's personal. So, one thing about the last few years, some of the attacks... so for instance, after I testified in Congress on antisemitism, I got a lot of incoming that was very personal. And I'm Jewish. To be called an antisemite over and over again, I did not find appropriate. And so when it's personal, when people say negative things about me—some of the emails people people sent to me—I was sort of like, "Wash your mouth out, folks." But would you get down? Transiently. My—I would get a little bit. Now I don't get down about all the other junk that's coming into everybody in our sector, but yes, I would get transiently down. But the way I deal with it is to just, you know, talk to people incessantly. Like, I'm a little bit of a "wear it on my sleeve, get it out of my system" person. It may wear down the other people a little bit, but that's definitely how I operate. Okay. You brought that up. You just started, you barely know your way around the Infinite Corridor, and you get summoned to Congress. Yes. What was your—you did not see that coming, I assume. I did not see that coming. When you got the summons, what was going on inside of your head? Who did you call? Well, first of all, it wasn't a choice. People ask me, "Why did you go?" And I went because it said, "If you don't go, it's a summons and you will be subpoenaed if you don't come." Okay, that's the first thing. Look, you know, the first people I obviously talked to were the team, including people like Alfred Ironside, our VP for communications. We talked to a lot of other friends in universities. I did not—I was naive. I think the biggest disappointment to me of that whole thing was not that it was hard to deal with personally, it was that it was theater. It was, and you know, I was a political science major in college, focusing on American political institutions, and I was so disappointed in the quality of the dialogue and the questioning. I know that sounds really naive, but I thought we were really having an interchange, but it wasn't. It was political theater. And I was ill—well, not that I was ill-equipped, I actually think I was prepared pretty well. And if you listen to the full testimony—the full four hours or five hours or whatever it is—or read the transcript, it was basically fine until sort of the gotcha moment at the end. But it reminded me a lot—it reminded me of the Michael Dukakis moment when the helmet... yeah, well no, when they were asking about his wife being attacked and he was very factual. I wish I—and so I sort of did that. I maintained a very factual tone. What I should have said was, "Oh my God, nobody would say that on our campus." But, you know, hindsight is 20/20. Okay. So you wish you had a do-over? Not really, because I don't want to do it again. But I would have handled it differently. Okay. So I assume you were prepped by your lawyers and everybody for that, and you're sitting there listening to the president of Harvard and the president of UPenn, you're watching the whole thing, and then you answer the question kind of similarly to them, a little bit better. No, I was first on that question. That was the problem. They'd been going Harvard, Penn, MIT for every question. Oh, interesting. On the press, Harvard was first. Yeah, they'd been going Harvard, Penn, MIT for every question until that last question. Did you know you had messed up that second? No. I didn't. When did you find out that your 15 minutes of fame was right now? Well, well before Saturday Night Live, but I—you know, I was naive. Most people who talked to me were like, "Yeah, you did pretty well." They didn't realize that that moment would become viral. You know, as soon as I read the newspaper, I saw it. And you know, then I started whenever I start getting a lot of texts from friends from my old days at Duke, "How you doing?" I know there's something really bad out there. When I get like 10 texts in a row like, "Oh my God," but you know, survived it. Some of these CEOs I talked to in an interview go through major crises. This is a whopper. What was it like? So first of all, I think people want their leaders to be calm and measured. And so first of all, I think no matter what for me, no matter what I was thinking inside and a lot of it was like, "Okay." Fine, I'm happy to hear that because you were calm and measured on the outside. But you have to be calm and measured on the outside. I mean, you have a human reaction, but I felt it was so unfair. And it's interesting because one of my biggest—it's sort of almost a joke in my family—one of my biggest Achilles' heels is the notion that I don't want to be accused of something I didn't do. And you know, I even watch lots of crime shows etc. and the ones that really like—I can barely watch—are the people who are falsely accused of crimes. So I felt during that whole incident that I was being accused of having certain views or thinking certain things, and people extrapolated from that. That to me was the crux of it. I felt it was unfair. But you know, we have a lot to do around here. So I had to move past it. I thought you handled the aftermath—and I think this is a lesson for every CEO through crises—like, you clarified your remarks and you got it focused back on the course. That was one good thing. The second thing that happened is the board publicly came out and backed you. That's what I was going to say. And the other two stepped down shortly after those remarks. That's right. So talk about both those things. Let's start with the remarks, the clarification. Who did you call? How did you think about that? It was very quick like, "That was excellent. That was excellent leadership." Well, you know, I tried to not create another news cycle. And I think one thing we did for quite a long time was to be very quiet about it. I mean, you know, I wasn't about to start like showing up on TV shows. I was just very clear with our community; I think that's the first thing. But the other thing in terms of our board or corporation being supportive, that is actually the pivotal moment in terms of me surviving at MIT. Because they were like, "We just hired her. It was fine. Go away." And that really created an environment that I felt I had a lot of support; I could keep moving on with what we were doing at MIT. And, you know, to this day when I do something, I don't look over my shoulder and think, "Oh my God, are they going to fire me over this?" Because you know, we have a great relationship. Now that doesn't mean they won't fire me for something someday, but at the moment I will say, I feel like transparency, great interchange, and I knew right away they were supportive. The other thing is many, many people on our board did listen to the whole thing, did read the whole transcript. And when people would come to them and say, "Oh my God, how could you not fire her?" they would say, "Did you listen to the whole thing?" And then they would distribute it or talk to people about it. And so it wasn't only that they were being supportive in voice; they actually were propagating the correct message. So I think that made a huge difference. My takeaways were you were calm—you were in a crisis, you were calm. You had a good board that backed you, which is invaluable. And you clarified your statements in a thoughtful way. And I think that's a best practice right there that all CEOs can kind of learn from. Okay, there's been some similar—I wouldn't say crisis-like, actually, a crisis averted. And I found if you engage someone and really give them a good rationale—you've really thought through it, it's not just like "I don't like you" or "I don't want to do this"—most people will ultimately accept it. Not all people; I'm sure there are people out there that hate me because I said no or took something away. But in general, I think people understand that you have to make these decisions. Okay. You've been through some stuff. You get down? I get down when it's personal. So, one thing about the last few years, some of the attacks—so for instance, after I testified in Congress on antisemitism, I got a lot of incoming that was very personal, and I'm Jewish. To be called an antisemite over and over again, I did not find appropriate. And so when it's personal, when people say negative things about me—some of the emails people sent to me, I was sort of like, "Wash your mouth out folks." But would you get down? Transiently. And I went because it said, "If you don't go, it's a summons and you will be subpoenaed if you don't come." Okay, that's the first thing. Look, you know, the first people I obviously talked to were the team, including people like Alfred Ironside, our VP for communications. We talked to a lot of other friends in universities. I did not—I was naive. I think the biggest disappointment to me of that whole thing was not that it was hard to deal with personally, it was that it was theater. It was, and you know, I was a political science major in college, focusing on American political institutions, and I was so disappointed in the quality of the dialogue and the questioning. I know that sounds really naive, but I thought we were really having an interchange, but it wasn't. It was political theater. And I was ill—well, not that I was ill-equipped, I actually think I was prepared pretty well. And if you listen to the full testimony—the full four hours or five hours or whatever it is—or read the transcript, it was basically fine until sort of the gotcha moment at the end. But it reminded me a lot—it reminded me of the Michael Dukakis moment when the helmet... yeah, well no, when they were asking about his wife being attacked and he was very factual. I wish I—and so I sort of did that. I maintained a very factual tone. What I should have said was, "Oh my God, nobody would say that on our campus." But, you know, hindsight is 20/20. Okay. So you wish you had a do-over? Not really, because I don't want to do it again. But I would have handled it differently. Okay. So I assume you were prepped by your lawyers and everybody for that, and you're sitting there listening to the president of Harvard and the president of UPenn, you're watching the whole thing, and then you answer the question kind of similarly to them, a little bit better.
