He'd say, "Okay, yeah, that's the price in the ballpark, but the price here in the store is $7,000." And it was, let me tell you, it was some funny shit. Okay, I just have to tell you that and, by the way, the guy ends up paying $6,000 for the bike and leaving. So this probably doesn't reflect well on me, my family, or some of the shenanigans that we did in the shop, but, you know, that's what we did. By the way, if you pissed us off in the shop and you had to use the bathroom, we would hook a DieHard car battery to the men's room door. And so we would clip it on with the two jumper cable clamps, and you're trying to get out of the bathroom—bzzz, bzzz—you know, you would be electrocuted by us. So I don't know, probably shouldn't be telling you all this, but that was life in the 1970s at Ghost Motorcycle. And I got a lot of my street education at Ghost, so I'm very happy about all that. All right, let's keep going here. We've gone from breastfeeding to electrocuting customers coming out of the men's room, okay? This is why I love doing these Q&A sessions. YouTube, Scott Kunda LH7VW. As someone who is very new to Bitcoin, how is Bitcoin useful now and in the future for a common shlub like me? So listen, it's complicated. If you own Bitcoin, I recommend you stay in Bitcoin. Bitcoin is a very cyclical, hard-to-understand piece of technology right now, but I do think it's continuing its adoption and expansion. And I think it's going to be an operating system to the future of money. And I want you to hold on to it and learn more about it. And the more you learn about it, I think the more you'll like it. I think that's been my position for a long period of time related to Bitcoin. Maxx Giuliana from Instagram. How would you suggest a young person go about finding a job they can learn and grow in? You know, this is the best question of all time about careers. And I would just tell you something you really like. I don't know what—I don't know what it is, but there's something you really like. For me as a kid, I loved business. Wall Street is the business of understanding other businesses. I also like books, which is why I'm doing this podcast. But remember, I started this podcast in my late 50s, and I started my Wall Street career in my mid-20s. So find something you really like and just go do that. Whatever the hell that is, go do it. Okay, and lo and behold, before you know it, you're never working a day in your life and you'll be successful. Okay, but you gotta have passion in what you're doing, and you gotta get up early and hit things hard, and when you get a setback, you gotta dust yourself off. Those are the number one things. Okay, LinkedIn, Kate Doak, I believe, I hope. What was Deidre's response to your April Fools' Day prank? Next question. Going right to the next question. No, I'm just kidding. You know, she was like perturbed slightly, but also amused. But I was—look at this guy, I was pretty flattered by the whole thing. You know, I—I don't know, some haters on there. I mean, there was one person that said to me, my hubris has no bounds. And I'm thinking, well, maybe that's true, but your IQ obviously does have some bounds. But you know what doesn't have any bounds? My sense of humor. Okay, so let's keep things real, but, you know, Deidre wasn't in love with the idea. Let's just put it that way. Okay, Instagram, Jen Moress. How do you stay looking so young? I thought you were maybe 50. All right, Jen, blowing kisses, even if Deidre's pissed off about that. Flattery will get you everywhere with me. And I think you gotta moisturize from the inside. And I don't mind telling people that I dye my hair and I probably have enough Botox in my—are we seeing any—any movement there? I sort of feel like we have a little bit of movement, but I have a lot of Botox on my forehead. Okay, so Botox, hair dye, lots of water. Those are the three things. Stay out of the sun and probably avoid alcohol and you'll stay pretty young. But also you have to not give a shit. Okay, just remember that too, okay? Like, do you think I give a shit what other people think of me? I do not. Do you think I give a shit about the short-term market gyrations? I do not. And so you have to not give a shit. And if you don't give a shit, you can sleep well. And then remember the immortal words of Mel Brooks: "Relax, none of us are getting out of here alive." So not giving a shit will also help you stay young, Jen. But those are my tips there. Okay, Instagram, Samantha JBen. Behind the scenes—is there anything human or normal about Trump? Sure, of course. Of course, that's the complexity of human life. You know, he—he looks like a caricature, he acts like a caricature, he mocks himself with his own caricature-like behavior. But of course there's some human stuff. I'll tell you something, you know, I think I've shared this before and if I have, forgive me, but I'll share it again because it was meaningful to me. I'm in the Oval Office. It's probably my first or second day on the job. I'm looking up—he's got everything painted gold now—but if you looked up on the ceiling, there's this presidential seal on the ceiling of the Oval Office. I looked up, looked at it with him. And I say, "Hey, we're in the Oval Office." I say, "You know, I mean, come on, we're in the Oval Office." I said, "Did that ever intimidate you?" And he very honestly looked at me and said, "Oh yeah, the first time I was in here I was unnerved, and then I got a call from White House protocol and they said that I'm gonna meet Theresa May. She's gonna be my first head of state visit, and they told me to go to the North Portico. And I'm like, where the hell is the North Portico?" And I thought that was very human of Trump. So listen, you know, I don't like him. I think he's been a danger to society and so forth. But let's not underestimate the fact that he's human. Okay, @ Instagram WSFORD, Sean Ford, very good friend of mine. How important are real relationships in business? Well, yeah, I think, I think it's everything, Sean. I think you know that, I know that. I take everything personally. I know you take everything personally. And some people say, oh, it's—it's not personal, it's business. They're full of shit. Of course it's personal. Take everything personally. And make your relationships as real as possible. It's a great question from Sean. Okay, Instagram, The_Sub_2003. What did you dislike the most about working in the Trump White House? You know, a lot of shots fired. You know, people shooting at each other. You know, they weren't on a team. Everyone was out for themselves. Very, very grabby, very weird. A lot of backstabbing, no frontstabbing. If someone hated my guts, they would be like, "golly gee" to my face while they were trying to plunge the knife in my back or drop a dime on me to a journalist saying something that isn't true about me that would hurt my reputation. But, you know, it's a shitty place to be honest. Okay, Marie Colannino, hopefully I'm saying it right, Marie. It's Marie Colannino, Instagram. What do you get more enjoyment from—your day job or podcasting? So I like both. You know, I also like traveling. I do some public speeches, and I like getting out on the road and meeting people. I think the thing I get the most enjoyment out of, Marie, is meeting new people. How about that? So if you see me somewhere and I'm—you know, I'm a big packer, everyone likes to quote-unquote "pack light," not me. I like to bring like a—almost like a walk-in closet with me. So if I'm waiting somewhere at baggage claim, come over and say hello. Okay, I like meeting the people. All right, here we go. Nadia S. Gill. This is from Instagram. Why do you still consider yourself a Republican? Okay, it's—it's such a great question. You know, if you are supporting Manchester United and they win some championships and now they suck, do you give up the team? Or let's say you're a Manchester City fan. Or let's say, look at—I am a Jet—I mean, just look about how, you know, sad my life is from a sports perspective. I'm a Jet, Met, Ranger, Nick fan. So my whole life has been like a house of pain, and my whole life has just been waiting for the collapse. But what are you gonna do? Are you now gonna switch to like be a Dodger fan or something like that because they're winning? I mean, come on. You know, we gotta fix this party. The system is not gonna survive with this high level of function. So yes, I'm not a Republican in terms of being a MAGA Republican, but I'm a Republican where, you know, I'm more of a socially inclusive Republican. I don't want to get involved in people's lives or—or make their personal decisions for them and all that nonsense. But I also, you know, I believed in certain tenets of free business, policy-free thinking about business and growth. So gotta fix the Republican Party, Nadia. So that's why I'm still a Republican. Instagram, Stan.Wall.5. I mean, where do you guys come up with these names? What is one thing everyone can do to make this world a nicer and better place to live? Well, you know the answer to that. Okay, yeah, there's a group of people that you're hanging out with. Be nice. Okay, don't be arrogant. Don't snap at the waiter. Don't—you know, I mean, I'm sure that you're pissed off about one of your family members, of course you have to be. I mean, they're family members. Forgive them, move on. You know, I think the big thing for me—and I will say this to people about making the world a nicer or better place—starts with you. Just be nice. You know, there's a butterfly effect to that stuff. You're a little bit nicer and who the hell—and who the hell knows what's going on in somebody else's life? You know, you don't know the baggage that somebody else is sharing in their life. There's a—there's a word called "Sonder," S-O-N-D-E-R. You guys should look up that word. What does Sonder mean? You know, I'm living in my own world with my own level of self-importance and my own friends and my own frenetic life, and—and maybe that's all important and all-consuming to me. But guess what? The person that I'm walking by on the street also has their Sonder, has their complexity, has their life, their health issues, their people that they're worried about. And when you start thinking in that way, you become way more psychologically minded and, by the way, you become way more open to the idea that they're human just like you. How about that? How about them apples? Okay, and if you remember that reference, "how about them apples," that came from Good Will Hunting. Remember that? Matt Damon said, "How you like those apples?" All right, so that's a wrap on today's Q&A episode of Open Book. Remember I'm reading all these comments, and I love listening to what's on your mind. Keep sending the questions, please. Okay, and listen, subscribe, there's a button on YouTube somewhere, subscribe. And if any of you were offended by me electrocuting somebody in the 1970s, get over it. Okay, and if you didn't like the fact that we told somebody that there was a ballpark price, actually it was a ballpark price, we just happened to throw the price into the ballpark. So get over that too. All right, but in the meantime enjoy Open Book, and I really do appreciate you tuning in. We're going to be back next month with this question and answer session again, something I love doing. And so please continue to send your questions and we're really looking forward to doing our best to answer them.

Rollins hosts press conference

The National Desk(758.0K) ·42:09 ·1.4K views · 2026-04-16
Brooke L. Rollins (Secretary of Agriculture) Howard Lutnick (U.S. Secretary of Commerce)
Secretary of Agriculture Brooke L.
TL;DR Secretary of Agriculture Brooke L. Rollins announced the launch of the first-ever USDA Office of Seafood while highlighting the broad economic impact of the "Working Families Tax Cuts" on rural agriculture and coastal fishing communities. Direct Tax Relief: Secretary...

TL;DR

Secretary of Agriculture Brooke L. Rollins announced the launch of the first-ever USDA Office of Seafood while highlighting the broad economic impact of the "Working Families Tax Cuts" on rural agriculture and coastal fishing communities.

The Working Families Tax Cut Act

  • Direct Tax Relief: Secretary Rollins characterized the current filing season as the first under new "Working Families" tax cuts, noting significant participation across the U.S. workforce.
      • Filers and Refunds: More than 53 million filers claimed at least one new tax cut, with the average refund reaching over $3,400.
      • Targeted Exemptions: Over 6 million filers claimed "no tax on tips," and over 25 million claimed "no tax on overtime."
      • Standard Deduction: More than 100 million filers utilized the now-permanent double standard deduction.
  • Social Security Benefits: Secretary Doug Burgum noted that 51 million seniors (88% of those on Social Security) will now pay no tax on their benefits.

Support for Rural America and Family Farms

  • Estate and Capital Gains Tax Reform: The administration moved to avert the "death tax" exemption sunset, protecting approximately 2 million family farms from the tax.
      • Land Sales: New provisions allow farmers to defer capital gains taxes over four years when selling land to another farmer, incentivizing the next generation of producers.
  • Investment and Infrastructure: The act drives $100 billion into rural investment through Opportunity Zones and commits $50 billion to the Rural Health Transformation Program.
  • Commodity Price Protection: Reference prices for major commodities—including corn, soybeans, and wheat—were increased by 10% to 21%, providing up to $13 billion in additional revenue protection starting this October.
  • Trade Deficit Reduction: Rollins reported that through 18 new trade deals, the administration reduced the $50 billion agriculture trade deficit inherited by 40% in a single year.

Launch of the USDA Office of Seafood

  • New Institutional Support: The USDA officially stood up its first-ever Office of Seafood to advocate for "farmers of the sea" and integrate the industry into existing USDA programs.
      • Director Appointment: The office will be led by a 20-year USDA career veteran to ensure institutional continuity.
      • Program Access: Fishermen will gain access to USDA marketing, risk management tools, and financing previously reserved for traditional land-based farmers.
  • Import Safety and Competition: Rollins highlighted the need to combat "toxic" seafood imports from adversaries like Russia.
      • Inspection Gaps: Current data suggests only 0.1% of incoming seafood is inspected; of that tiny fraction, 33% fails to pass safety standards.
  • Industry Protections: The speakers celebrated the defunding of offshore wind subsidies, which fishermen like Jason (an 8th-generation fisherman) and Dustin Delano argued threaten the commercial fishing industry.

Deregulation and Government Efficiency

  • Permitting Reform: Secretary Burgum emphasized a massive reduction in bureaucracy, claiming the administration completed an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) in 24 days that typically takes four years.
      • Regulatory "Bloat": The USDA is pivoting away from what Rollins described as "wasteful" research studies—such as those involving transgender mice or sociology in the pest business—to focus strictly on core agricultural production.
  • Fertilizer and Energy Independence: The administration is addressing high fertilizer costs by leveraging domestic natural gas.
      • Manufacturing Shift: Burgum noted that since the U.S. has the world's lowest natural gas prices (under $3), the goal is to shift from being a fertilizer importer to a major manufacturer and exporter.

Notable Quotes

"For the first time in a long time, commercial fishermen are being recognized for what we truly are: food producers... This is not just about policy folks. It’s about people. It’s about preserving a way of life."

— Dustin Delano, Fourth-Generation Fisherman

"We’ve taken federal employees and we’ve basically created mind-numbing soul-sucking jobs where all they do is follow a bunch of regulations that aren’t literally something that Congress passed... getting rid of the red tape... helps the federal team members. It brings people back to life."

— Doug Burgum, Secretary of the Interior

"Instead of funding the studies of the menstruation cycle of transgender mice... we are refocusing the US Department of Agriculture to actually focusing on agriculture."

— Brooke L. Rollins, Secretary of Agriculture

Very grateful to have my partner from the cabinet, the incredible Secretary Doug Burgum is here. Thank you, sir, so much. Along with Congresswoman Kat Cammack and again some of our great fishermen. Our Deputy Secretary Stephen Vaden is here and others. So grateful. So a couple of quick top lines before I turn it over to Secretary Burgum and we move through the program. This is the first Tax Day since the working families tax cuts went into effect. Tax cuts that President Trump promised, that Republicans in Congress delivered, and that all of us across the administration, including here at USDA, are implementing. The working families tax cuts are the largest tax cuts in history for working and middle-class Americans, and Americans are already seeing real relief. More than 53 million filers claimed at least one of the new tax cuts. 53 million Americans. The average refund this filing season is more than $3,400. The Trump administration has delivered more refunds and larger refunds than in any one year in American history. Over 6 million filers have claimed no tax on tips and over 25 million filers have claimed no tax on overtime. Over 100 million filers have claimed the now permanent double standard deduction. This is promises made and promises kept. So thank you, Mr. President. The working families tax cuts are also the largest investment in rural America in history. And they are breathing new life and new opportunity into the heartland and into rural fishing communities for our fishermen here by giving them the relief and the support that they deserve. There are so many wins for rural America in that Working Families Tax Cut Act that it would take me a really long time to go through them all. But to just name a few, number one: it averted the death tax exemption sunset by exempting 2 million family farms in America from the death tax. The act made it easier to invest in the family farm by making permanent 100% immediate expensing for capital-intensive business investments. It allowed farmers to defer capital gains taxes over a four-year period instead of all at once when selling qualifying land if they sell to another farmer, supporting a new generation of farmers. That Working Family Tax Cut Act also committed $50 billion in the Rural Health Transformation Program. Dr. Oz is running that for generations to come. It also drives $100 billion in rural investment. Again, y'all, this is unprecedented. Never before has there been an effort focused on rural America and rural communities in our agriculture communities as was this last year with this tax cut act. The 100 billion moving into opportunity zones in rural America is no longer just an urban America project, and it will change everything. For our farmers who had been waiting more than a decade for an increase in reference prices from 10 to 21% for many and most of our commodities—corn, soybeans, wheat—changes that provide up to $13 billion in additional revenue and price protection starting this October is a game-changer for a struggling rural economy. And finally, building on what I believe is one of the most important and impressive wins of this president and our administration over the last year: trade and reopening the markets around the world for our great American agriculture products. This bill invested at a time, I remind people, that a big focus was to shrink government, return power to the people, and to cut taxes. At the same time, this administration with our partners on the Hill invested significant funds in rural America, including $280 million a year in addition to what we were already spending to support opening the markets in international agriculture trade. In just one year, President Trump took what we inherited—which was a $50 billion agriculture trade deficit—and cut it by 40%. No one thought that was possible, but the 18 new trade deals have made it possible thanks to President Trump's leadership. And finally, I'll say, in addition to Tax Day, this week also represents the 50th anniversary of the Magnuson-Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act, the primary law governing our fisheries in US federal waters. There are so many representatives, as I mentioned, here today from all over this country representing their seafood industry. In fact, I think one behind me is an eighth-generation fisherman from Maine. From Maine! And I asked in our conversation what year does that mean so we can put it into context, and he said his seventh-generation grandfather began fishing in America in 1806 off the coast of Maine. Y'all, this was revolutionary times for our country. As we celebrate the 250th birthday of the greatest country in the history of the world that preserved and ignited the fire for freedom and for liberty, it was the families of these fishermen standing behind me and the families of the farmers that we know today that were the heart of that effort. That is what we fight for every single day. So again, what a joy to have you all here. We just announced the launch of the first-ever USDA Office of Seafood to support our farmers of the sea. For the first time ever, the US Department of Agriculture will have an office and a team fully dedicated to advancing, honoring, elevating, and supporting our great farmers of the sea. And what a joy it is to be able to lead on that. Again, thank you everyone. I'm going to turn it over to my great partner Doug Burgum for some thoughts, and then you'll get to hear a little bit from some of our fishermen. But what a joy to be here and then we'll open it up for some questions from our press in the back. Thank you so much. Secretary Burgum: Thank you, Brooke. Great to be here with all of you and great to be here with our incredible fishermen from around the country. We just got done with a round table with folks from Alaska to Florida and from Maine to the Gulf Coast. And what a day. Congratulations Brooke. Congratulations everybody at USDA. Congratulations to Secretary Howard Lutnick who was here with us working together. Again, commerce of course has got the National Marine Fisheries and NOAA, and the idea that we're going to be bringing ag programs to support our fishermen of the sea is really incredible. It's an achievement. Senator Dan Sullivan was here. He's had this idea for over 10 years, but you're going to hear from all of them. Let's give one more round of applause. This is really an historic day for the thing. Way to go, Brooke. And it is Tax Day and of course as Brooke said, I mean, it is kind of odd that we're celebrating. This used to be the day that was the black day on the calendar, the mark that you'd put through and you dreaded this day would come. But here we are and it's actually a day of celebration and it's a day of gratitude. The gratitude goes out to everybody in Congress, both the House and the Senate, who voted for this bill. I think all of you know who those folks were. And you know, it passed very narrowly but it passed. By doing that thing, this is part of again President Trump's agenda which was going to cut red tape, lower taxes, and reinvigorate the American Dream. We're going to make sure that if there was trade going on, it was truly fair trade and that the country stopped getting ripped off. All of those things have happened. And so what's happened in a short 15 months: our economy, the foundation of having lower taxes, less regulation, and a record amount of investment coming into our economy is going to be an explosion—an explosion of jobs and capital investment. It's an exciting day for our country because when people are looking at their refunds this year on average, the ones that have come in so far have been an average of $3,500. People are getting a refund today of $3,500 because of that. That's a boost for all of them. And of course Brooke talked about this, how fantastic for our seniors. Seniors paying into Social Security their whole life only then have to pay taxes on their earnings coming back to them. But now 51 million seniors—88% of all the seniors on Social Security—will pay no tax on their Social Security. How about that? And of course on the no taxes on tips, I mean this is a huge thing and President Trump can take full credit for this. I mean he shared the story, we've all heard it, but he was talking to a person that was waiting tables at a restaurant he was at, asking about what he could do when he was campaigning. They said, "I have to pay taxes on my hard-earned tips." And he came up with this idea, drove it through, and got it passed. Sampling of this going on right now: the people that work professionally or part-time are seeing anywhere between $3,000 and $10,000 of savings of their hard-earned money staying in their own pockets. I think the economists are saying on average it's about $7,000. But you know, what a gift to America for people that work hard and the no tax on overtime. You know, many of you I'm sure did—when I was a kid man I couldn't wait to get past 40 hours because that's when you finally started making a little bit of money getting time and a half. But now to have no tax on overtime, the incentive for people to work harder and begin their own steps towards the American Dream. So again, common sense policies. But I do want to say in terms of gratitude, I want to say thank you to President Trump. Thank you to the fellow cabinet secretaries like Brooke Rollins that are here today. The team of people that we are working for—we're working for a president that works 20 hours a day, gets up every day fighting for the American people. It's so inspiring and so motivating to see the policies actually in action coming together. And so with that and with Brooke my great partner, as it relates to the fishermen that are up here, people like why is the Secretary of Interior talking about oceans? I mean this is the Interior. But let's all remember a couple things: between Brooke and I, between Agriculture and Interior, we have 700 million acres of land and 700 million acres of subsurface. But when you throw in the Interior where we've got responsibility for federal offshore, we have 3 billion acres of offshore. And I can tell you what we've turned around in the last 15 months: the prior administration had strangled every industry that you could imagine that depended on public lands. Whether it was timber, whether it was mining, whether it was critical minerals, whether it was oil and gas, or whether it was fishing in federal waters—all of those industries were being choked to death by regulation. Listening to those people that are out there, getting ideas from the people that are trying to... you know, again, you'll hear it's not just eighth generation, third, fourth, fifth generation—and eighth generation of course was the first one I've met whose family was actually fishing during the War of 1812. You don't get to meet that every day. But it's incredible that now we're listening to the people that are actually the thing. That's why the Working Families Tax Cut is so important, because if you're talking to farmers or ranchers or timber or miner, they're working families. These are the people of America that build things. And now that we're supporting them with the tax cuts, but also supporting them with like the new Office of Seafood, it is a great day for working families in America. And again thank you all. Thank you Brooke. Thank you USDA and thank you everybody up here that's making it happen. Brooke Rollins: Beautiful, beautiful. Thank you. Beautiful. Love it. Very quickly, our incredible Congresswoman who represents a lot of fisheries but certainly represents the Make America Great Again tax cuts and has just been such a warrior on all of it: Kat Cammack. Kat Cammack: Well, I just want to echo the comments of what has been said here already. As the lone representative for the free state of Florida on House Agriculture with a whole heck of a lot of coastline including the Gulf of Mexico, this is an amazing day for our fishermen. We have, yes, yes. Now, it was said in the round table earlier: we know the juggernaut that Alaska is in this space, but as the representative of the lower 48 of the tremendous oystermen, clammers, crabbers, shrimpers, you name it, this is a fantastic step forward because we know that food security is national security. And this has been an issue that we have been pushing for, fighting for. To have a team of cabinet secretaries who not only get along and genuinely like each other but take action instead of just talking about an issue—they deliver. And not only have they delivered today but they have delivered previously, will continue to do so, and on Tax Day today we are here as living proof that President Trump is very, very serious when he says we're going to make America great again and put America first. Thank you. Thank you, thank you, Secretary Rollins. Thank you, Secretary Burgum to our amazing fishermen that are here today representing an industry that has been a little overlooked, but no longer will be. Thank you for everything that you're doing today. It's a new day as we look forward to continuing to make America great again. Thank you guys. Brooke Rollins: That was beautiful, beautiful. You sure? Alright, a fourth-generation fisherman and also the fisherman in residence at NOAA, Dustin Delano from Maine to talk a little bit about the tax cuts. Dustin, thank you. Dustin Delano: Thank you, Madam Secretary. It's an honor to be here and a privilege. Again, my name is Dustin Delano and I serve as a fisherman in residence at NOAA. But before that title means anything, before Washington, before policy, I'm a fourth-generation lobsterman. I grew up in a small fishing village that depends almost entirely on commercial fishing. It's not just what we do, it's who we are. I grew up watching my dad, a single father, work all day on his lobster boat, hauling traps, battling weather, chasing a living that most people will never understand. And then he would come in, he'd grab a quick bite and head back out to catch the evening tide to dig clams. That is what shaped me. I understand hard work as a way of life. I'm a living example of the grit and resilience that comes from making a living on the ocean as a farmer of the sea. And that is why today matters. Because for the first time in a long time, commercial fishermen are being recognized for what we truly are: food producers. So today is Tax Day. And for generations, fishermen have carried the weight of taxes, regulations, and rising costs while continuing to show up every day to feed this country. Now we are seeing meaningful change. Because of the working families tax cuts and broader reforms, fishermen are getting real relief. We can now fully deduct meals on vessels and expense equipment, engines, gear, and even vessels, making it possible to reinvest and stay competitive. We are also seeing stronger support for small, family-run operations through expanded business income deductions. And I can't forget to mention one of my favorites: eliminating subsidies for offshore wind that was slated to kill our fishing industry. These are long overdue steps toward fairness. But what matters even more is what comes next. Bringing commercial fishermen and the seafood industry into the USDA is a milestone. Because access matters. Access to marketing, to risk management tools, and to financing that can strengthen this industry and support coastal communities across America. This did not happen by accident. It happened because of leadership. Because President Trump made seafood competitiveness a priority through his seafood executive order. Because leaders like Secretary Rollins, Secretary Burgum, Secretary Lutnick, and others, they didn't just listen, they took action. And for the first time in decades, fishermen are feeling something we have not felt in a long time: hope. And many of you in this audience fought for this long before me. You refused to let this industry be forgotten. So yes, today is a milestone, but it's also a starting point. For me, this moment means a great deal. But what matters more is what it means for the future, for the next generation. For the boy in Sitka learning to mend twine with his grandfather. For the girl in Gloucester watching her father haul traps before sunrise. For the kid in Galveston stepping onto a shrimp boat for the first time. For the young fisherman in Kodiak wondering if there is still a future in this life. This is not just about policy folks; it's about people. It's about preserving a way of life. It's about ensuring that the American fisherman, the farmer of the sea, has a future in this country. And today, that future feels just a little bit closer. Thank you. Brooke Rollins: Well, maybe we just finish there. No, I'm just kidding. That was incredible, how inspiring, Dustin, thank you. Another great fisherman with us from Alaska, Sterling, if you wouldn't mind joining us. We added them at the end but they are—he and Jason represent just so many remarkable, again, patriots. And what a joy Sterling to have you here. So thank you for joining us and tell us what you think. He said, "I'm going to pass it to Jason." This is Jason again like I said, our eighth-generation fisherman from Maine. He has been such a stalwart on this issue and the fight in front of us and the righteousness of this work. Talked about how Peter in the Bible was a fisherman and this goes back really, really long way. So Jason thank you so much for being here. Jason: Thank you. Wow, this is amazing. Sterling's a good speaker. I just met him about 15 minutes ago and he can talk up a storm. Wow and Dustin, proud of him. Man, what a nice young man. He grew up in a fishing family and I'm very proud of him. To be able to have this opportunity to be here today, thank our president. I mentioned to them in there, I showed up six years ago to speak at the RNC to endorse our president, and six years later I'm here witnessing a change and creation of a seafood office at the USDA. It's amazing and we cannot thank him enough. As a lobsterman, I don't know where I'm going to go with this because no one told me I was going to speak. I would say to the American people: if you ever hear anything about ropeless fishing with lobstering, stay away from it. That's bad stuff. It is so dangerous. It is so dangerous. And to, you know, within the—the one big beautiful bill there to take away that funding from all of that offshore wind, oh those people are so corrupt. They are so corrupt and we're so happy as fishermen from the East Coast to the West Coast to see that stuff defunded. But, and I don't know how long do you want me to talk? So I'm probably good right now. So thank you. Thank you for being here and thank you to our president. Thank you. Brooke Rollins: You sure? Okay, okay. Alright, well what an encouraging, incredible day. We are going to open it up for some questions from the press. But as we move into this portion, again I just want to thank—I want to thank the amazing USDA team that is here, all the work that's been done on this. I just couldn't ask for more incredible people to work with every single day. So I think we've got some of our USDAers here. I just want to thank you all. It's my honor to serve you every day, every day. Alright, Alec we'll take some questions. Yes ma'am. Grace (Politico): Hi Madam Secretary. Grace from Politico. Hopefully you can hear me okay. Thank you for taking my question. I wanted to ask about your message to farmers who are seeing higher prices of fertilizer and fuel recently. I know you've said you've been in contact with fertilizer companies and you've posted that any sleight of hand from fertilizer companies will not be tolerated. How are those conversations going and how is the administration prepared to act in response to any price gouging, and what kinds of solutions are on the table there? Brooke Rollins: Well, thank you. Could we just first ask one quick question about seafood? Just so we can check that box and then we'll talk fertilizer. Okay. Grace (Politico): Well, could you walk me through, now that the office is open, what's priority number one for you guys at USDA? Brooke Rollins: Oh, thank you so much. Way to go, Grace from Politico! What a great question. Listen, here's the beautiful thing. I don't have the list in front of me but everything from crop protection tools to prioritizing seafood on the international stage. You know, you think about—and probably a lot from the press or maybe any of you don't fully understand—our seafood industry has been crushed by what we call the communist fish from Russia and other parts around the world where a lot of it is toxic and it doesn't get inspected enough, if at all. One of the numbers we talked about, I think Jason brought it up in our round table we just finished, is that currently incoming seafood only 1/10th of 1% is inspected and of that 1/10th of 1%, 1/3rd of it doesn't pass the inspection. So these numbers are crazy. We've got to refocus on how we're supporting American agriculture and how we're supporting American seafood. When you think about it in the context of the Make America Healthy Again movement and what this particular industry means for making America healthy again, all of those come into play. And so for me this seemed like such a no-brainer. I was a little startled that USDA has never had an office for seafood. We've never had an advocate for seafood for all of the programs that are at USDA, all of them—from beginning farmers and ranchers to risk mitigation to the various programs when there's weather incidents etc. Now all of that is on the table. We have a lot of work to do to get from point A to point B but that's very important. The second thing I'll add is it's got to be more than just the next two years and nine months and a couple of days. Most of this, if not all, has to be codified. And I think I speak for Secretary Burgum and our administration: we will work day in and day out with this industry to get this codified so that they don't have to go through another fight in another couple of years to get this done. So what a joy. So thank you, Grace, for that question. Thank you. Good to see you. Steve Davies (Agri-Pulse): Yeah, Steve Davies with Agri-Pulse. Thank you for doing this Madam Secretary. If I can start off with the seafood question and then ask some—I follow the rules. Good job, Steve. How big will the Office of Seafood be and when will you stand it up? Brooke Rollins: Well, thank you for that question. It stood up as of today. We have been working on it over the last almost eight months and wanted to get all of our t's crossed and our i's dotted, make sure that when we announced it, it wasn't going to be another year before it opened. It is now open today, which is really, really cool. Is Nick our... where are you? Right there behind you. There he is! Okay, there he is, right in the back. Yes, that is our new director, a 20-year employee of the US Department of Agriculture. Which means he was here long before I got here and he'll probably be here long after I go, but that's the consistency that we really wanted to put into place. A great career employee of the US Department of Agriculture. We're so grateful to you as you build this team out and fight for these incredible people. So thank you for being here. Thank you. Mike Hellenberg: Mike Hellenberg. I forgive me for asking this, but the President's budget called USDA a bloated bureaucracy. What are you going to do in the next year to reduce the bloat? Brooke Rollins: Well, this President has basically called all of the federal government a bloated bureaucracy and again Doug may want to lean into this a little bit too. I'll let him offer some thoughts. But there's no doubt that when this President first came down the escalator in 2015—and there were 17 people running in the Republican primary that year, including several Texans that I know and were friends with and so many others, including some that are in the cabinet right now—that the President really connected with the American people. We—and I saw it firsthand—was the first president to really come down that escalator and said, you know, "I'm going to go and fix Washington." I mean, I'm going to bring my own programs, but instead saying I'm going to cut government in Washington that has grown too big. Our founders envisioned under the 10th Amendment—our 250th year this year—they, George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, James Madison, John Adams, Alexander Hamilton, they envisioned a very small federal government where the government closest to the people under the 10th Amendment, the states and the locals, they should be the ones making the decision for the American people, and that had gotten so big and so out of control. And so here at USDA, part of that recalibrating the US Department of Agriculture is to actually focus on agriculture, to ensuring that instead of funding the studies of the menstruation cycle—the menstrual cycles of transgender mice, which was actually a funded study by the USDA until we got here. Instead of funding the racism in the pest business, which was actually a study that was funded until we got here. Studying the habits of "queer and BIPOC farmers," the name of the study, of San Francisco, which was actually a taxpayer-funded study until we got here. So that's what the President is talking about: to ensure that we have every tool in our toolkit to support the American farmer, the American rancher, to support our rural communities, and to change the trajectory of agriculture in this country so it's no longer going to be purchased and consolidated, especially by foreign adversaries and foreign countries. No longer selling our farmland to foreign adversaries like China, which has been happening for a long time. These are all existential issues for the future of America. And so when the President talks about the bloated USDA budget or the bloated Interior budget, we have such a short period of time to fix it while we're here but to make the first things first and a priority. Doug, I know you may have some thoughts on Interior and the budget process. We're going through that right now. But thank you for that question. Doug Burgum: I just want to agree with everything that Brooke said and then add on. But it's fascinating when you dig in. Of course, I was in the private sector before I became governor, and then I was governor for eight years of a state and I had a chance to, you know, dig into the programs that are there. Many of these things just grow and grow and grow and they get outside of not just their core mission, but they actually get outside the guidelines of what the actual law is. Recently, of course, NEPA has been weaponized to stop infrastructure in this country. If you want to slow something down use NEPA, if you want to kill it use the Endangered Species Act. I mean, we've got $1.5 trillion of capital spending that's been approved in our country that's waiting for a permit. And it's often waiting for a permit because it's being challenged and litigated around some of these rules that were not passed by Congress. 80% of NEPA was not passed by Congress. At Interior, we've just reduced the amount of things that people have to do to get to the—actually, between getting to the 20% that meant the law. The other 80% is in an addendum. You can look at it and go, "Oh, those are interesting ideas," but that's not actually the law. And the same thing, we're now going through that with ESA. So part of getting rid of the bloat is to save taxpayer moneys. But actually, I know Brooke cares about the people that work here at USDA; I care about all the people that work at Interior. But we've taken federal employees and we've basically created mind-numbing, soul-sucking jobs where all they do is follow a bunch of regulations that aren't literally something that Congress passed. And so if people get into government because they want to make a difference in people's lives, these roles done right can actually make a difference in supporting, like we saw today, a group of cabinet leaders and all of our teams supporting an industry to have an opportunity to flourish and not be buried under red tape. But when you get rid of the red tape, it not only helps the industry, it helps the federal team members. It brings people back to life to say, "Hey, I can actually do a job where I can make a difference in people's lives," and that they can be a participant in helping, you know, create the American Dream for America. So there are so many ways—I mean, I feel like the jobs that Brooke and I have are like get up in the morning and it's a joy because you can attack in any direction and there's some, you know, waste, fraud, abuse, IT. You know, on the IT side, man, we've got not just old technology, we have ancient technology. We have stuff that should be moved out of our departments and put in a museum. It is—and things that just add cost and add time to... I mean, permits. We had permits that were taking years and we said, "What if a team of people just did that permit?" We did an environmental assessment in 11 days. They normally take one to two years. We did an EIS in 24 days. It would normally—people didn't think anything at all if it took four years, four years, an entire administration. We did it in 24 days and we followed every aspect of the law to do it. If I gave a judge the 24-day EIS and I gave him the one that took four years and asked them to tell the difference, they would probably say, "Wow, this one looks like it's really well thought through." That was the 24-day one because a group of people just had a chance to work on it. So there is an unlimited opportunity to reduce costs and improve the services we deliver to the citizens in these jobs. Brooke Rollins: Yes, beautifully said. Thank you, Doug. We'll do last question. Elizabeth Elkin (Bloomberg News): Hi, Secretary. Elizabeth Elkin, Bloomberg News. Good to see you. I'm also going to ask a fish question and then a fertilizer question. We're just going to go on theme here. Brooke Rollins: I love it, I love it. Elizabeth Elkin (Bloomberg News): I wonder how demand for seafood fits into the overall demand for protein that we're seeing? Like, how do you see fish demand as fitting into the protein craze? Brooke Rollins: Yeah, there—no, that's right. And it actually ties back to Steve's question, too. So as we work to make America healthy again, a report came out last week that said for the first time in American history, the real food purchases were up 7 and a half percent. We've been trending away from quote "real food," our kind of dietary guideline that Bobby and I put out a few weeks ago. And so Americans are buying more real food as it becomes very apparent what has been happening over the last number of decades with what we're eating in this country and the chronic health problems that come with that. So as we're looking to make America healthy again and looking to have proteins—right, eggs, beef, chicken, today seafood—as part of that effort, it's just an incredibly encouraging and exciting time. I've said it before but I'll repeat it: USDA alone spends $400 million a day across 16 nutrition programs—that's SNAP, WIC, school lunches etc. The opportunity to pivot toward more real food, toward seafood and beef and chicken etc., is just limitless from my perspective. And then what that eventually means for our agriculture producers and rebuilding these smaller, mid-sized family farms and being able to pass to the ninth and tenth generation continues to be a priority. So I'm sorry Bobby couldn't be here; he was really sad to miss because he was very excited about this, too. He's getting ready for a hearing or he may be in a hearing right now. But this is an across-the-cabinet effort and one that I think is going to be so important to seafood. So thank you. Thank you for the question, Elizabeth. Thank you. Elizabeth Elkin (Bloomberg News): And my fertilizer question: if Mosaic is unwilling to step back those countervailing duties that we've been talking about, what are next steps to bring relief for farmers? Brooke Rollins: You know, we have so many different options that we're looking at. As I mentioned earlier today, we had Secretary Burgum, we had Secretary Lutnick, we had Ambassador Jamieson Greer, and several other key folks from the administration sitting down with some of these companies. Mosaic was not one of them; they did not come. But understanding what it is that we can do together, hopefully to work to keep this fertilizer crisis in check. We're looking at additional more short-term—as I mentioned, the long-term is coming too, but more short-term steps. Nothing I can announce right now because we're still working through that. But know that it is an across-the-cabinet effort. I've talked to the President himself about it; he truthed about it on Saturday, ensuring that there was no price gouging. The Department of Justice is involved. We're watching this very, very closely. But the silver lining is it has shined a light that has long needed to be shined on this particular input and how difficult it has been for farmers as it has continually increased year over year over year. In the last 30 days is again just one more, you know, check on the checkmarks and what's happening. And we're going to get our arms around it and do everything we can to help solve for it. Doug Burgum: Can I just say one thing about that? And then we're going to wrap. I just want to add again, adding on everything Brooke said. But wow, what an opportunity for America. When we have conflicts abroad or if you have disruptions even like COVID, you want to understand that supply chain, the supply chain risk. What do you make nitrogen? It's the number one component of fertilizer. What's the primary component? Natural gas. 20—you know, 20 years ago the US was the world's largest importer of natural gas. Under President Trump, we're the world's largest exporter of natural gas. Natural gas... everyone's... I'm not... I'm talking about natural gas which comes out of the ground when you're producing oil and gas. The price of that at the beginning of this conflict was $3. The price of it now is $2.60. In the US, we have the most abundant, lowest cost natural gas. That's what you turn—that's what you make nitrogen out of. And whether it's a gaseous form like ammonia, whether it's urea which is solid, or whether it's a liquid form, farmers can apply all three in different applications. We don't have an issue with the resource. We are blessed as a country with enormous resources and natural gas which can be converted—you know, heat, light, or fertilizer. It's amazing. It's the superpower of energy. And we've got it, we've got so much of it. But what we need is we need to manufacture those products here as opposed to import them. So when you go through a situation like this, what's going to come out the back side? And the companies that were meeting with the Secretary, we're going to see companies that stand up and start making investments in developing more fertilizer here. Just like with natural gas, instead of being an importer now we're the world's largest exporter, we have the same opportunity in fertilizer. There's no reason why we have to be paying high prices when we could have the lowest prices for fertilizer because we have the lowest prices for natural gas in the world. Under $3. I mean, in other places around the world right now, including Europe, it's $12 or $15 for natural gas—so five times higher. So incredible business opportunity for America. I know that with Brooke and with Howard and the team that they meet with today, we're going to see solutions on this thing. So again, I look at the future, these are all solvable. American innovation, American greatness. We're going to have the lowest priced—we do have the lowest priced energy in the world because of President Trump's energy dominance agenda—and we're going to have the lowest priced fertilizer in the world under the same agenda. So fantastic future for our farmers and for everybody else. It'll lower the cost of food and improve food security. Brooke Rollins: Amen. Thank you. Amen. Thank you. Alright everybody. Happy working families tax cut Tax Day. So grateful to be fighting every day for all of our Americans, returning the American Dream to every corner of this country. Of course, here at USDA to our farmers of the sea, to our farmers of the land, and we will never stop battling. God bless you all. Thank you. And thanks to the press for being here.

Ronan Farrow on Sam Altman's strained relationship with the truth | Decoder

Decoder with Nilay Patel(12.0K) ·56:40 ·15.0K views · 2026-04-16
Ronan Farrow (Investigative Reporter) Nilay Patel (Host, Decoder) Sam Altman (CEO, OpenAI)
Investigative reporter Ronan Farrow characterizes Sam Altman as a leader "unconstrained by the truth," driven by a paradoxical combination of extreme people-pleasing and a sociopathic disregard for the consequences of deception, which has led to a "race to the bottom" regarding AI safety.
TL;DR Investigative reporter Ronan Farrow characterizes Sam Altman as a leader "unconstrained by the truth," driven by a paradoxical combination of extreme people-pleasing and a sociopathic disregard for the consequences of deception, which has led to a "race to the bottom"...

TL;DR

Investigative reporter Ronan Farrow characterizes Sam Altman as a leader "unconstrained by the truth," driven by a paradoxical combination of extreme people-pleasing and a sociopathic disregard for the consequences of deception, which has led to a "race to the bottom" regarding AI safety.

The Personality Paradox and "Unconstrained" Truth

  • The Thesis of Deception: Farrow identifies a dual personality trait in Altman that is rarely seen in one person:
      • A desperate need to be liked and to please people in any given interaction.
      • A chilling lack of concern for the fallout once those people realize they have been deceived.
  • Founder Culture as a Shield: Altman’s tendency to tell different groups conflicting things is often viewed in Silicon Valley as a "feature, not a bug" of successful founders, but Farrow argues Altman is an extraordinary case that has exhausted the patience of even seasoned insiders.
  • The "Salesman" Archetype: Former board members describe Altman as someone who "believes the shifting reality of his sales pitches," allowing him to bluster through contradictions without meaningful self-doubt.

Lack of Documentation and the WilmerHale Investigation

  • Oral Briefings Over Paper Trails: Farrow reveals that the pivotal WilmerHale investigation into Altman’s firing—demanded by the board that initially ousted him—was deliberately kept out of writing.
      • Only an 800-word press release was ever published, while the substantive findings were delivered via oral briefings to avoid creating a discoverable paper trail.
  • Legal Implications: Legal experts suggest this lack of documentation is "less than professional" for a company of OpenAI's stature. Under Delaware corporate law, if OpenAI moves toward an IPO, this lack of transparency could trigger shareholder lawsuits and Section 220 demands for underlying records.
  • Board Mismanagement: The original board that fired Altman is described as "JV" (Junior Varsity), failing to communicate the nuances of Altman's "lack of candor" to major backers like Microsoft’s Satya Nadella.

Safety Risks and the "Race to the Bottom"

  • Speed Over Security: Farrow argues that OpenAI has shifted from a safety-focused non-profit research lab to a trillion-dollar entity where "speed is trumping everything else."
  • Whistleblower Vulnerability: There are currently no federal statutes protecting AI employees who disclose safety concerns. Farrow cites the departure of senior safety figures like Jan Leike, who warned the board that the company was "going off the rails" regarding its mission.
  • Utopian Hype vs. Reality: Farrow notes that Altman frequently makes grand promises (e.g., curing all cancer, reaching superintelligence "around the corner") that technologists find "way off" and potentially market-destabilizing if the "AI bubble" punctures.

Strained Corporate and Political Relationships

  • The Microsoft Friction: Despite their partnership, Microsoft executives are increasingly "burned" by Altman’s business maneuvers.
      • Exclusivity Conflicts: On the same day OpenAI reaffirmed exclusivity with Microsoft, it announced a deal with Amazon for "stateful" AI agents, which Microsoft insiders believe is impossible without violating their underlying agreement.
  • Regulatory "Bait and Switch": Altman is accused of posturing for regulation during the Biden administration while simultaneously funding PACs to quash meaningful state and federal oversight.
      • Farrow notes that Altman pivoted quickly toward a "no holds barred" acceleration posture following political shifts, including plans for massive data centers in Abu Dhabi.
  • Investor Regret: While investors "went to war" to bring Altman back after his firing, some now admit they acted on partial information and fear he was not sufficiently "taken out behind the woodshed" to reform his deceptive habits.

Notable Quotes

"Sam Altman is unconstrained by the truth... he has two traits that are almost never seen in the same person. The first is a strong desire to please people... and the second is an almost sociopathic lack of concern for the consequences that may come from deceiving someone." — Ronan Farrow, Investigative Reporter
"So many other people around it who had the concerns and voiced them urgently just folded like napkins and changed their tune the moment they saw the wind was blowing the other way and they wanted in on the profit train." — Ronan Farrow, Investigative Reporter
"I think there's a small but real chance that he winds up being a Madoff-level level scammer... not that Sam is being accused of those specific types of fraud, but that the degree of dissembling and deception from Sam may have a chance of ultimately being remembered at that scale." — Nilay Patel, quoting a senior Microsoft executive (paraphrased by Farrow)
"This is a culture where even the people saying 'this could literally kill us all' have nothing but incentive to go fast and ignore those risks." — Ronan Farrow, Investigative Reporter
There's a line in the story that to me feels like the thesis of the story: Sam Altman is unconstrained by the truth and that he has two traits that are almost never seen in the same person. The first is a strong desire to please people, to be liked in any given interaction, and the second is an almost sociopathic lack of concern for the consequences that may come from deceiving someone. So many other people around it who had the concerns and voiced them urgently just folded like napkins and changed their tune the moment they saw the wind was blowing the other way and they wanted in on the profit train. It's pretty dark, honestly, from my standpoint as a reporter. But then I think back to Sam getting fired. I took a source call at the Bronx Zoo at 8:00 PM on a Friday and it was someone saying they're going to try to get Sam back. Well, first of all, sorry to your daughter and my partner and all the other people around journalists. It was quite a weekend for everyone. Yeah, no, it does take over one's life. Hello and welcome to Decoder. I'm Nilay Patel, editor-in-chief of The Verge, and Decoder is my show about big ideas and other problems. Today, I'm talking with Ronan Farrow, one of the biggest stars of investigative reporting working today. Ronan broke the Harvey Weinstein story among many, many others. And just last week, he and co-author Andrew Marantz published an incredible deep dive feature in the New Yorker about OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, his trustworthiness, and the rise of OpenAI itself. One note before we go any further here: The New Yorker published that story and Ronan and I had this conversation before the attacks on Sam Altman's home. So you won't hear us talk about them directly. But just to say it, I think violence of any kind is unacceptable. These attacks on Sam were unacceptable. And the kind of helplessness that people feel, which leads to this kind of violence, is also unacceptable. And it's worth more scrutiny from both the industry and our political leaders. I hope that's clear. All that said, there is a lot of swirl around Sam Altman that's fair game for rigorous reporting. The kind of reporting that Ronan set out to do. Thanks to the popularity of ChatGPT, Altman has emerged as the most visible figurehead of the AI industry, having turned a once non-profit research lab into an almost trillion-dollar private company in just a few years. But the myth of Sam Altman is deeply conflicted, defined equally by both his obvious deal-making ability and the tendency, which Ronan reported, to, well, lie to everyone around him. Ronan talked to Altman many times over the 18 months he spent reporting the story. And so one of the main things I was curious about was whether Ronan sensed any change in Altman over that time. After all, a lot has happened in AI, in tech, and in the world over the past year and a half. You'll hear Ronan talk about that very directly, as well as his sense that people have become much more willing to talk about Altman's ability to stretch the truth. Okay, Ronan Farrow on Sam Altman, AI, and the truth. Here we go. Ronan Farrow, you are an investigative reporter and contributor to The New Yorker. Welcome to Decoder. Glad to be here. Thanks for having me. I am very excited to talk to you. You just wrote a big piece for The New Yorker, it's a profile of Sam Altman and sort of with it, OpenAI. My read of it is it, as all great features do, it, with rigorous reporting, validates a lot of feelings people have had about Sam Altman for a very long time. You've obviously published it, you've gotten reactions to it. How are you feeling about it right now? I've been heartened, actually, by the extent to which it's broken through in a time where the attention economy is so kind of schizophrenic and shallow. This is a story that in my view affects all of us. And when I spend a year and a half of my life, and my co-author Andrew Marantz also spent that time of his, really trying to do something forensic and meticulous, it's always because I feel like there are bigger structural issues that affect people, you know, beyond the individual at the heart of the story, beyond the company at the heart of the story. Sam Altman against the backdrop of Silicon Valley hype culture, right, and startups that balloon to massive valuations based on promises that may or may not come to pass in the future, and an increasing embrace of a founder culture that thinks of as a feature, not a bug, telling different groups different conflicting things. Even against that backdrop, Sam Altman is an extraordinary case where everyone in Silicon Valley who expects those things can't stop talking about this question of his trustworthiness and his honesty. And you know, we knew already that he had been fired over some version of allegations of dishonesty or serial alleged lying. But extraordinarily, despite the fact that there's been wonderful reporting, you know, Keach Hagey has done great work on this, Karen Hao has done great work on this, there really wasn't a definitive understanding of the actual alleged proof points and the reasons why those have stayed out of the public eye. Point number one is I feel heartened by the fact that some of those gaps in our public knowledge and even in the knowledge of Silicon Valley insiders have now been filled a little bit more. Some of the reasons that they were gaps have been filled a little bit more. You know, we report on cases where people inside this company really felt like things were covered up or deliberately not documented. One of the new things in this story is that a pivotal law firm investigation by WilmerHale, you know, which is obviously a fancy, credible, big law firm that did investigations of Enron and WorldCom, which by the way were all voluminous, like hundreds of pages published. They did this investigation that was demanded by board members that had fired Altman as a condition of their departure when he got rid of them and he came back. And extraordinarily in the eyes of many legal experts I spoke to and shockingly in the eyes of many people in this company, they kept it out of writing. All that ever emerged from that was an 800-word press release from OpenAI that described what happened as a breakdown in trust. And we, you know, confirm that this was kept to oral briefings. There's cases we talk about where, like for instance, a board member seemingly wants to vote against the conversion from OpenAI's original nonprofit form into a for-profit entity and it's recorded as an abstention. And there's like a lawyer in the meeting saying, well that could trigger too much scrutiny, and the person wants to vote against and it gets recorded as an abstention. To all appearances, you know there's factual dispute, OpenAI claims otherwise as you might imagine. But these are all cases essentially where you have a company that by their own account holds our future in their hands, right? The safety stakes are so acute, they have not gone away. This is the reason this company was founded as a nonprofit focused on safety. And where things were being obscured in a way that like credible people around this found less than professional. And you couple that with a backdrop where there's so little political appetite for meaningful regulation, and I think it's a very combustible situation. The point for me is not just that Sam Altman, you know, deserves these questions so acutely. It's that any of these guys in this field and many of the key figures exhibit like if not this particular idiosyncratic alleged lying all the time trait, certainly like some degree of a race to the bottom mentality, right? Where the people who were safest have watered down those commitments and everyone is in a race posture. I think the point as we look at like even recent leaks at Anthropic is there's a person who poses the question of who should have their finger on the button in this piece. The answer is like if we don't have meaningful oversight, I think we gotta be asking serious questions and trying to surface as much information as we can about all of these guys. So I've been heartened by what feels like a meaningful conversation about that or the beginnings of one. The reason I asked it that way is you worked on this for a year and a half, you talked to I believe a hundred people with your co-author Andrew. That's a long time for a story to cook. And I think about the last year and a half in AI in particular and boy have the attitudes and values of all these characters shifted very quickly. Maybe none more so than Sam Altman who started off as the default winner because they had released ChatGPT and everyone thought that would just take over for Google. And then Google responded, which seemed to surprise them, that Google would try to protect its business, maybe one of the best businesses in tech history if not business history. Anthropic decided that it would focus on the enterprise, it seems to be taking a commanding lead there because the enterprise uses of AI are so high. And OpenAI's product they're now refocusing away from "we're going to take on Google" to Codex and they're going to take on the enterprise. And I just can't quite tell over the course of reporting over the last year and a half, did it feel like the characters you were talking to changed? Like their attitudes and their values, did those change? Yes, I think first of all that the critique that is explored in this piece coming from many people inside these companies at this point, that this is an industry that despite the existential stakes is descending into something of a race to the bottom on safety, and where speed is trumping everything else. That concern has grown more acute. And like I think those concerns have been more validated as the past year and a half has transpired. Simultaneously attitudes about Sam Altman specifically have changed. You know, when we started talking to sources for this, people really really were leery of being quoted about this, going on the record about this. And then by the end of the reporting, you know you have a body of reporting where people are talking about this very openly and explicitly and you have like you know board members saying like he's a pathological liar, he's a sociopath. There is a range of perspectives from "this is dangerous given the safety stakes" that we need leaders of this tech that have elevated integrity, all the way up to "forget the safety stakes, this is behavior that is untenable for any executive of any major company, that it just creates too much dysfunction." So the conversation has become much more explicit, in a way that feels maybe belated but is heartening in one sense. And Sam Altman to his credit, the piece is very fair and even generous I would say to Sam. You know, this is not the kind of piece where there was a lot of gotcha stuff, this is like I spent many many hours on the phone with him as we were finishing this up and really heard him out. And as you can imagine in a piece like this not everything makes it in. And some of those cases in this one were because like I was listening sincerely and if Sam was actually making an argument that I felt carried water that something even if it were true could be sensationalist, you know I really erred on the side of keeping this like forensic and measured. I think that is being received rightly and I just hope this factual record now that's accumulated over this period of time can trigger a more bracing conversation about the need for oversight. That's actually my next question. I think you talked to Sam a dozen times over the course of reporting this story. Again, that's a lot of conversations over a long period of time. Did you think Sam changed over the course of the reporting over the past year and a half? Yeah, I think one of the most interesting subplots in this is that Sam Altman is also talking about this trait more explicitly than he has in the past. The posture of Sam in this piece is not like "there's nothing there," you know "this is not true, I don't know what you're talking about." The posture he has is he says that this is attributable to a people-pleasing tendency and a kind of conflict aversion. He's acknowledging that it caused problems for him, particularly earlier in his career. He is saying, "Well, I am moving past that or have to some extent moved past that." I think what's really interesting to me is the contingent of people we talked to who were not just sort of safety advocates, not just the underlying technical researchers who very often tend to have these acute safety concerns, but like pragmatic big-time investors. They are backers of Sam's, who in some cases look at this question and talk about even having played a key role in him coming back after his firing and now say on this question of "has he reformed, to what extent is that change meaningful?" Well, we gave him the benefit of the doubt at the time. And I'm thinking of, you know, one prominent investor in particular who said, "But since then like it seems clear he wasn't taken out behind the woodshed," was the phrase that this one used, to the extent that was necessary, and as a result it seems like this is now like a stable trait. Like we're seeing this in an ongoing way. And you can look at some of OpenAI's biggest business relationships and the way they kind of carry the weight of that mistrust in an ongoing way. Like Microsoft, you talk to executives over there, there's really acute and like recently catalyzed concerns. There's this instance where in the same day OpenAI is reaffirming their exclusivity with Microsoft with respect to underlying stateless AI models and then announcing a new deal with Amazon. That is to do with selling enterprise solutions for building AI agents that are stateful, meaning they have memory. And you talk to Microsoft people and they're like, "That's not possible to do without interacting with the underlying stuff that we have an exclusivity deal on." So that's just like one of many small examples where this trait has tendrils into ongoing business activity all the time and is a subject of active concern, you know within OpenAI's board, within OpenAI's executive suite, and in the wider tech community. You keep saying that trait. There's a line in the story that to me feels like the thesis of the story. And it's a description of the trait you're describing. It's that Sam Altman is unconstrained by the truth and that he has two traits that are almost never seen in the same person. The first is a strong desire to please people, to be liked in any given interaction, and the second is an almost sociopathic lack of concern for the consequences that may come from deceiving someone. I have to tell you, I read that sentence 500 times and I tried to imagine always saying what people wanted to be liked and then not being upset when they felt lied to. And I could not make like my emotional state understand how those things can exist in the same person. How, as you've talked to Sam a lot, you've talked to people that have experienced these traits, how does he do it? Yeah, you know it's interesting on a human level because I do approach bodies of reporting like this with a real focus on humanizing whoever's at the heart of it and like seeking deep understanding, right, and empathy. When I kind of tried to approach this from a more human standpoint and say, "Hey like this would be devastating for me if so many people that I've worked with said I'm a pathological liar, how do you carry that weight? Like how do you talk about that in therapy? What is the story you tell yourself about that?" You know, I got some sort of in my view maybe like West Coast platitudes about like, "Yeah I like breathwork," but not a lot of the kind of bracing sense of deep self-confrontation that I think a lot of us would probably have if we were if we were seeing this kind of feedback about our behavior and our treatment of people. And I think that that actually goes to the broader answer to the question too. Sam asserts basically that this trait has caused problems but also that it's part of what's empowered him to accelerate OpenAI's growth so much. That, you know, he is able to unite and please essentially different groups of people. He's constantly convincing all of these conflicting constituencies that what they care about is what he cares about. And that can be a really useful skill for a founder. You know, I've talked to investors who then say, "Well maybe it's a less useful skill for actually running a company because it sows so much discord." But on the Sam personal side, you know I think the thing that I pick up on when I try to connect on a human level on this, the apparent lack of like deeper confrontation and reflection and self-accountability also informs that whether you want to call it a superpower or a liability for a company preparing for an IPO. He is someone who, in the words of one former board member—there's this former board member named Sue Yoon who's on the record in the piece—saying that to the point of fecklessness is the phrase she uses. He really believes the shifting reality of his sales pitches or is able to convince himself of them or at least if he doesn't believe them is able to bluster through them without like meaningful self-doubt. I think the thing that you're talking about where you or I might, as we're saying the thing and realizing that it conflicts with the other assurance we've made, kind of have a moment of freezing up or checking ourselves. I think that that doesn't happen with him. And you know there's a wider Silicon Valley hype culture and like founder culture that kind of embraces that. It's funny, The Verge is built on what amounts to a product reviews program. Like it's the heart of what we do here is we hold a trillion dollars of Apple R&D once a year and say "this phone is a seven" and it sort of legitimizes all of our reporting and our opinions elsewhere, right? We have an evaluative function. And we spend so much time just looking at the AI products and saying do they work? And that feels missing from a lot of the conversation about AI as it is today. There's endless conversation about what it might be able to do, how dangerous it might be. And then you drill down and say, does it actually do the thing it's supposed to do today? And in some cases the answer is yes, but in many, many cases the answer is no. And that feels like it connects to the hype culture you're describing and also to the sense that well if you say it's going to do something and it doesn't and someone feels bad, that's fine because we're on to the next thing. Like that's in the past. And in AI in particular, Sam is so good at making the grand promises. Right? Just this week, I think the same day as your story published, OpenAI released a policy document that said we have to rethink the social contract and have AI efficiency stipends from the government. And this is a grand promise about how some technology might shape the future of the world and how we live. And all of that relies on the technology working in exactly the way that maybe it's promised to work or it should work. Did you ever find Sam doubting AI turning into AGI or superintelligence or getting to the finish line? Because that's the thing that I wonder about the most. Is there any reflection about whether this core technology can do all of the things that they say it can do? It's exactly the right set of questions. There are credible technologists that we spoke to in this body of reporting and obviously Sam Altman is not one, right? He's a business person. Who say the way that Sam talks about the timeline for this tech is just way off. You know, there's blog posts going back a few years where Sam is saying we've already reached the event horizon, AGI is like basically here, superintelligence is around the corner, we're going to be on other planets, we're going to be curing all forms of cancer, like truly I'm not embellishing here. The cancer one is actually really interesting. There Sam is hyping up the person who theoretically cured their dog's cancer with ChatGPT and that simply did not happen. They talked to ChatGPT and that helped them guide some researchers that actually did the work but the one-to-one "this tool cured this dog" is not actually the story. I'm glad you raised that because I want to go on to this bigger point about when is the both the potential and the risk of the technology really going to vest. But it's worth mentioning these like little asides constantly that happen from Sam Altman where he seems to embody this trait all over again.
