I mean to use the example of the WilmerHale report, where we had this information that it had been kept out of writing and wanted to know, you know, was the brief—the oral brief along the way—given to anyone other than the two board members Sam helped install to oversee it. And he said like, "Yeah, yeah no I believe it was given to everyone who joined the board after." And that is we have you know a person with direct knowledge of that saying that is simply a lie. And like that really does appear to be the case that that is untrue, you know if we want to be generous perhaps he was misinformed. So there's a lot of these casual assurances and I use that example in part because that's a great example of dissembling, let's call it, that can have real consequences legally. You know I don't need to tell you like under Delaware corporate law if this company IPOs, shareholders could under Section 220 like complain about this and demand underlying documentation. There's already board members saying like "well wait a minute that briefing should have happened." So these things that seem to jump out of his mouth all the time they can have like real market-moving effects, real effects for this company and—bringing it back to the kind of utopian hype language that's resurfaced I think not coincidentally on the day this piece came out—also effects for all of us. Because the dangers are so acute, you know, with respect to the way it's being deployed in weaponry, with respect to the way it's being used to identify chemical warfare agents, the disinformation potential, you know because of the way in which the utopian hype does seem to be prompting a lot of credible economists to say this has all the signs of a bubble. Even Sam Altman has said, you know, someone's going to lose a lot of money here. That could really like crater a lot of American and global economic growth if there's like a true puncturing of a bubble involving all of these companies doing deals with each other, going all in on AI while borrowing so heavily. So what Sam Altman says matters. And I think the preponderance of people around him—you mentioned we talked to more than 100, it was actually well over 100. We had a conversation at the finish line where it's like "would it be too petty to say it's like this much higher number" and we were like "nah let's downplay it, we'll play it cool." But it was so many people and such a significant majority of them saying this is a concern and I think that's all why. Let me ask you about that number and as you mentioned people got more and more open with the concerns as time went on. It feels like the pressure around the bubble, the race to win, to pay off all this investment, to emerge as the winner, to IPO, that has changed a lot of attitudes. It certainly created more pressure on Sam and OpenAI. We published a story this week just about the vibes at OpenAI. Your story is a part of it, but massive staffing changes in the executive ranks at OpenAI. People are coming and going. The researchers are all headed away, largely to Anthropic, which I think is really interesting. You can just see this company is feeling the pressure and it is responding to that pressure in some way. But then I think back to Sam getting fired. And very memorable—this is just memorable for me, it's memorable for no one else—but I took a source call at the Bronx Zoo at 8:00 PM on a Friday and it was someone saying they're going to try to get Sam back. And then we spent the weekend chasing that story down. And I was just like, "I'm at the zoo like what do you want me to do here?" And the answer was stay on the phone while my daughter is on a—get off the phone. And like that's what I did. It was ride or die to get Sam back. That company was like, "No, you—we're not letting the board fire Sam Altman." The investors, they're quoted in your piece, "we went to war" I think is the Thrive Capital position to get Sam back. Microsoft went to war to get Sam back. It's later and now everyone's like, "We're very—we're going to IPO, we got to the finish line, we got our guy back and he's going to get us to the finish line. We're concerned he's a liar." Why was it war to get him back then? Because it doesn't seem like anything has actually changed, right? You talk about the memos that Ilya Sutskever and Dario Amodei kept while they were contemporaries of Sam Altman. Ilya's number one concern was Sam is a liar. None of that has changed. So why was it war to bring him back then and now that we're at the finish line it seems like all the concerns are out in the open? Well first of all, sorry to your daughter and my partner and all the other people around journalists. It was quite a weekend for everyone. Yeah no, it does take over one's life and this story definitely has mine over the last period of time. It actually relates to this theme of journalism and access to information, I think. You know the investors that went to war for Sam and all played roles in making sure he came back, and the board that had been specifically designed to protect a nonprofit's mission to put safety over growth and to fire an executive they couldn't be trusted with that, them going away. That that was all because, yes the market incentives were there, right? You know Sam was able to convince people, "Well the company's just going to fall apart," but the reason he had support was lack of information. Those investors in many cases now say, "You know I look back and and I think I should have had more concerns if I had known fully what the claims were and what the concerns were." Not all of them, opinions vary and we quote a range of opinions but there are significant ones who were acting on very partial info. The board that fired Sam was in the words of one person who used to be on the board, you know, very JV. And they fumbled the ball hard. And you know we document the underlying complaints and people can decide for themselves whether it accumulates into the kind of urgent concern they felt it was. But that argument and that information was not being presented. They received what some of them now acknowledge as bad legal advice, to describe it—you'll remember the quote probably, a lot of your listeners and viewers will remember the quote—you know "a lack of candor" was what it was reduced to. And then they like essentially wouldn't take calls. So you have, forget journalists, like Satya Nadella saying like "what the hell happened? I can't get anyone to explain to me." And that's the company's major financial backer. And then you have like Satya calling Reid Hoffman and Reid calling around and saying, "I don't know what the fuck happened." And they're like understandably in that void of information looking for, you know, the traditional non-AI indicators that would justify such an urgent sudden firing, like "okay did—was it sex crimes? Was it embezzlement?" And the entire subtle but I think meaningful argument that this tech is different and that this kind of a steady accumulation of smaller betrayals actually could have meaningful stakes both for this business and maybe for the world, that was really largely lost. And so, you know capitalist incentives won out but also the people who made it win out were not always operating with complete information. I want to just ask about the "whatever everyone thought it was" aspect for one moment. Because I certainly saw the news and I said, oh something bad must have happened. You've done a lot of MeToo reporting, famously you broke the Harvey Weinstein story. You spent a lot of time reporting on these claims that I think you decided were ultimately unfounded that Altman had sexually assaulted minors or hired sex workers or even murdered an OpenAI whistleblower. Did you—I mean you are the person who can report this stuff the most rigorously. Did you decide that came to nothing? Well look, I'm not in the business of saying something has come to nothing. What I can say is I spent months looking at these claims and did not find corroboration for them. And it was striking to me that, you know, these guys—these companies who have so much power over our futures—truly are spending a disproportionate amount of their time and resources in a childish mudfight. You know, one executive describes it as Shakespearean. It's like the amount of, you know, private investigator money, the opposition dossiers being compiled, it's relentless. And the unfortunate thing is that the kind of salacious stuff, which gets parodied by Sam's competitors as just assumed fact, right? There's this allegation that he, you know, pursues underage boys and at many cocktail parties in Silicon Valley you hear this and on the conference circuit I've heard it repeated by like credible prominent executives. Like everybody knows this is a fact. You know I talk about where this comes from, right? The various vectors by which it's transmitted—Elon Musk and his associates seemingly pushing like really hardcore dossiers that kind of amount to nothing, they're vaporous when you actually start to look at the underlying claims. The sad thing is that it really obscures the more evidence-based critiques here that I think really deserve urgent oversight and consideration. The other theme that really comes through in the story is an almost sense of fear. That Sam has so many friends he's invested in so many companies from his previous role as CEO of Y Combinator, just to his personal investing, some of which is in direct conflict with his role as CEO of OpenAI, that there's silence around him. There's one line that really struck me, you describe Ilya Sutskever's memos. And they're just out in Silicon Valley and everyone calls them the "Ilya Memos." But there's even silence around that, right? They're passed around but they're not discussed. Where do you think that comes from? Is it fear, is it a desire to get angel investment? Where does that come from? I think it's a lot of cowardice I'll be honest. Having reported on national security stories where the sources are, you know, whistleblowers who stand to lose everything and like face prosecution and they still do the right thing and talk about things to create accountability. I've worked on, you know, the sex crimes related stories that you mentioned where sources are deeply traumatized and fear like a very personal kind of retribution. In many cases around this beat you're dealing with people with their own profile and power, right? You know they're like either famous people themselves or they're surrounded by famous people, they have robust business lives. And in my view it is actually like very low exposure for them to talk about this stuff. And thankfully like the needle is moving as we talked about earlier and people are now talking more. But for such a long time people really just shut up about it because I think the Silicon Valley culture is just so kind of ruthlessly self-interested and ruthlessly business and growth-oriented. So, you know I think this afflicts even like some of the people who were involved in firing Sam where you saw in the days after, yes one factor that led to him coming back and the firing of old board members was that he rallied investors who were confused to his cause. But another is that so many other people around it who had the concerns and voiced them urgently just folded like napkins and changed their tune the moment they saw the wind was blowing the other way and they wanted in on the profit train. It's pretty dark, honestly, from my standpoint as a reporter. Even though they think they're building digital God that will somehow either eliminate all labor or create more labor or something will happen. Well that's the thing. So it's the culture of not standing by your convictions and all ethical concerns falling by the wayside the moment there's any heat or anything that could threaten your own standing in the business. It is, you know, maybe all well and good to some extent for "business as usual" companies that are making whatever kind of widget. But these are also the same people who are saying this could literally kill us all. And you know again you don't have to go to the Terminator Skynet extreme, like that is a set of risks that are already materializing. It is real, they are right to warn about that. But, you know, you'd have to have someone else armchair psychologize how those two things can live in the same people where they're sounding the urgent warnings they're maybe like putting a toe in and trying to do something and then they're just folding and falling silent. And that is precisely why you can have these kind of instances of things being kept out of writing and things being swept under the rug and no one talking about it this openly for years after the fact. The natural responsible party here would would not be the CEOs of these companies, it would be governments. In the United States maybe it's state governments, maybe it's federal government. Certainly these companies all want to be global, there's lots of global implications here. I watched OpenAI and Google and Anthropic all sort of goad the Biden administration into releasing an AI executive order. It was pretty toothless in the end. It just said they had to talk about what their models were capable of and release some safety testing. And then they all kind of backtracked and Trump came in and wiped all that out and said "we have to be competitive, it's a free-for-all, go for it." At the same time that they're all trying to raise money from Middle Eastern countries that have lots of oil money that want to change their economies. Those are politicians. I feel like politicians should definitely understand someone is talking out of both sides of their mouth and they're not going to be too upset if someone's disappointed in the end. But the politicians are getting taken for a ride too. Why do you think that is? This is really I think why the piece matters in my view and why it was worth spending all this time and detail on. We are in an environment where the systems that as you say should be providing oversight are just hollowed out. And that's a post-Citizens United America where the flow of money is so unfettered. And it's a particular concentration of that problem around AI, right? Where there's these PACs that are like proliferating and flooding money into quashing meaningful regulation at both a state and a federal level. You have Greg Brockman, you know Sam's second in command, directly contributing in a major way to a couple of those. And it leads to a situation where there's just really capture of legislators and potential regulators. And that is a hard spiral to get out of. The sad thing is I think that there are simple policy moves, some of which are being trialed elsewhere in the world, that would help with some of these accountability problems. You could have more mandatory pre-deployment safety testing, which is something that is already happening in Europe, you know for frontier models. You could have more stringent written public record requirements for the kinds of internal investigations, you know, where we saw things being kept out of writing in this case. You could have a more robust set of national security review mechanisms for the kinds of like Middle Eastern infrastructure ambitions that Sam Altman was pushing. And as you say, where he was kind of doing this bait and switch of with the Biden administration saying "regulate us, regulate us" and helping them craft an executive order and then the moment Trump gets in—like truly in the very first days—just going no holds barred, "let's accelerate and let's build a massive data center campus in Abu Dhabi." You could have—this is a really simple one—like whistleblower protections. There is no federal statute protecting AI company employees who disclose these kinds of safety concerns that are being aired in this piece. You know we have cases where, like Jan Leike who was a senior safety guy at the time leading superalignment at the company, writes to the board essentially whistleblower material saying the company is going off the rails on its safety mission. Like those are the kinds of people who should actually have an oversight body they can go to and they should have explicit statutory protections of the kind we see in other sectors, right? Like this is simple to replicate a kind of a Sarbanes-Oxley style regime. I think that despite how acute the problem is of Silicon Valley assuming control of all of the levers of power, and despite how hollowed out some of these institutions that might provide oversight and guardrails are, I still do believe in the basic math of democracy and of self-interested politicians. And there is more and more polling data emerging that a majority of Americans think that the concerns or questions or risks of AI currently outweigh the benefits. And so I think the flood of money into politics from AI—it's within all of our power to make that a source of a question mark with respect to politicians. You know, when Americans go to vote they should be scrutinizing whether the people they vote for, especially if they are you know uncritical and anti-regulation given all these concerns, are bankrolled by Big Tech special interests. So I think like if people can read pieces like this and listen to podcasts like this and care enough to think critically about their decisions as voters, there is a real opportunity to generate a constituency in Washington of representatives who do keep an eye and do force oversight. That might be one of the most optimistic things I've ever heard anyone say about the current AI industry and I appreciate it. I'm obsessed with the polling that you're talking about. There's a lot of it now, it's all pretty consistent. And it kind of looks like the more young people in particular are exposed to AI, the more distrustful and angry they are about it. That's kind of the valence of all the polling. And I look at that and I think, well yeah smart politicians would just run against that. They would just say we're going to hold Big Tech accountable. And then I think about the past 20 years of politicians saying they're going to hold Big Tech accountable and I'm struggling to find even one moment of Big Tech being held accountable. And the only thing that makes me think this might be different is, well, you actually have to build the data centers. And you can vote against that and you can petition against that and you can protest against that. You know I think a politician who just had their house shot at because they voted for a data center—the tension is reaching I would call a fever pitch. You've described the insularity of Silicon Valley, right? This is a closed ecosystem. It feels like they think they can run the world, right? They're putting a ton of money into politics and they are running up against the reality of people don't love the products, which doesn't give them a lot of cover, right? The more they use the products, the more upset they are. And the politicians are beginning to see there are real consequences to supporting the tech industry over the people they represent. You talked to so many people. Do you think it is possible for the tech industry to learn the lesson that is right in front of them? You say it feels like they think they can run the world without accountability, I don't even think that needs the "feels like" qualifier. I mean you look at like the language Peter Thiel is using, it's explicit, right? And of course that's an extreme example and, you know, Sam Altman—though he is like close with and informed by Thiel's ideology to some extent—is a very different kind of person who might sound you know different and more measured up to a point. But I do think like the wider ideology that you get from Thiel, which is basically like we're done with democracy, we don't need it anymore, we have so much that we kind of just want to build our own little bunkers. We're not dealing with the Carnegies anymore or the Rockefellers anymore where they're bad guys but they feel a need to participate in a social contract and build things for people. There's a real nihilism that's set in. And I do think it's just been like a mutually reinforcing spiral in recent American history of moguls and private companies acquiring super-governmental power while democratic institutions that might hold them accountable are hollowed out. And I do not feel optimistic about the idea that those guys might just wake up one day and think like, "Huh actually maybe we do need to participate in society and help build things for people." I mean you look at like the little microcosm example of the giving pledge. Where there was a moment where it was seemly to be charitable and that moment is now like past and even kind of ridiculed. That is a problem, the broader problem of lack of accountability that I think can only be solved extrinsically. That has to be voters mobilizing and resurrecting the power of government oversight. And you're exactly right to say that the main vector through which people could maybe achieve that is it's local, it's to do with where infrastructure is being built. You know and you mentioned some of the like white-hot tension around this that's leading to violence and threats and obviously nobody should be violent or threatening. But I and I'm also not here to make specific policy recommendations other than to just present like these are some of the policy steps that seem basic and are working elsewhere in the world, right, or that have worked in other sectors. I'm not here to say which of those should be executed and how. I do think something needs to happen and it needs to be external, not just trusting these companies. Because right now we have a situation where the companies that are developing the tech and are equipped best to understand the risks and in fact are the ones warning us of the risks are also the ones with nothing but incentive to go fast and ignore those risks. And you just don't have anything to counterbalance that. So whatever form reforms might take in terms of specifics, something has to run up against that and I do still return to that optimism that the people still matter. I generally buy your argument. Let me just make the one tiny counter-argument that I think I can articulate. The other thing that could happen outside of the ballot box is that the bubble pops. Right? That not all these companies get to the finish line, that there isn't product market fit for consumer AI applications and again I don't quite see it yet, but I'm a consumer tech reviewer and maybe I just have higher standards than everybody else. There is product market fit in the business world, right? Having a bunch of AI agents write a bunch of software seems to be a real market for these tools. You can read the arguments from these companies saying we've solved coding and that means we can solve anything. If we can make software we can solve any problem. I think there are real limits to the things software can do. That that's great in the business world, software can't solve every problem in reality. But they gotta get there. They gotta finish the job and maybe not everybody makes it to the finish line. And there is a crash and this bubble pops and maybe OpenAI or Anthropic or xAI, one of these companies does crater and all this investment goes away. OpenAI is right on the cusp of an IPO. There's a lot of doubts about Sam as a leader. Do you think they're going to make it to the finish line? I'm not going to prognosticate. But I think you raise an important point, which is market incentives do matter internally to Silicon Valley. And the precariousness of the current maybe potentially allegedly bubble dynamics do stand to interrupt the—you know again potential according to critics—race to the bottom on safety. I would also add to that if you look at historical precedents where there's a similarly seemingly impenetrable set of market incentives and potentially deleterious effects for the public, there's impact litigation. And you see that as an area of concern lately. Like Sam Altman is out there this week endorsing legislation that would shield AI companies from some of the types of liability that OpenAI has been exposed to, right, in wrongful death suits for instance. Of course there's a desire to have that shield from liability. I think that the courts can still be a meaningful mechanism. And it'll be really interesting to see how these suits shape up. You know you already saw for instance the class action suit—actually of which I and many many other authors I know are members—against Anthropic for their use of books that were under copyright. If there are, you know, smart legal minds and plaintiffs who care, we just have seen historically in cases from big tobacco to big energy that you can also get some guardrails and some incentives to slow down or be careful or protect people that way. It does feel like the entire cost structure of the AI industry hangs on a very, very charitable interpretation of fair use. Doesn't come up enough, right? The cost structure of these companies could spiral out of control if they have to pay you and everyone else whose work they've taken, but that it's inconvenient to think about so we just don't think about it. Right next to that is all of these products are now running at a loss. Like currently today they're all running at a loss. They're burning more money than they can make. At some point they have to flip the switch. Sam as a businessman, right, as you've mentioned several times he's not a technologist, he's a business person. Do you think he's ready to flip the switch and say we're going to make a dollar? Because when I ask do you think OpenAI is going to make it, it's they gotta make a dollar. And so far Sam has made all of his dollars by asking other people for their money instead of having his companies make money. Well that's a big lingering question, you know for Silicon Valley, for investors, for the public. You see some statements and moves out of OpenAI that seem to evince a kind of panic about that. You know, shutting down Sora, shutting down some ancillary projects, trying to zero in on the core product. But then on the other hand you still see at the same time tons of mission creep. Right, you know even like the—as a small example it's obviously not core to their business—but like the TVPN acquisition. By the way, right as we were reaching the finish line in fact-checking, the company facing this kind of journalistic scrutiny acquires a platform where they can you know have more direct control over the conversation. I think that there's a lot of investors that are concerned based on the conversations I've had that this problem of promising all things to all people also extends to this lack of focus in the core business model. And I mean you're closer to the kind of prognosticating and watching the market than I am probably, I'll leave you and I'll leave the listeners to be the judge of whether they think OpenAI can flip the switch. Well I asked the question because you've got a quote in the piece from a senior Microsoft executive and it is that Sam's legacy might end up more similar to Bernie Madoff or Sam Bankman-Fried than Steve Jobs. That is quite a comparison. What did you make of that comparison? I think that's a paraphrase, the Steve Jobs thing isn't part of that quote. But yes there is this extraordinary comparison to—I think it's actually there's an interesting sort of sobriety to it because it's phrased as like, "I think there's a small but real chance that he winds up being a you know an SBF or a Madoff-level level scammer." Meaning, in my mind, not that Sam is being accused of those specific types of fraud or crimes, but that the degree of dissembling and deception from Sam may have a chance of ultimately being remembered at that scale. Yeah, I think what's most striking about that quote honestly is that you call around at Microsoft and and you don't get a "that's crazy, we've never heard that," you get a lot of like "yep, a lot of people here do think that." Which is remarkable and and I think it does go to these nuts and bolts business questions. That there are investors who say like—one told me for instance, "Look in light of the way in which this trait has persisted in the years after the firing, this was also like I thought an interesting sober thought, that it's not necessarily that Sam should be at the absolute bottom of the list, should be like the lowest of the low in terms of the people that absolutely must not build this technology." For what it's worth there's several people who say Elon Musk is that person. But that this trait puts him maybe at the bottom of the list of people that should build AGI, you know and beneath several other leading figures in this field. So I thought that was an interesting appraisal, you know, and that's the kind of thinking I think that you get from the real pragmatists who maybe aren't buying into the safety concerns as much. They're just growth-oriented, and they think that OpenAI now has a problem with Sam Altman. The Microsoft piece of it is really interesting. That company thought they were on top of the world, that they had made this investment and they were going to leapfrog everyone, especially and most importantly Google, back into consumer good graces. And the level to which they feel burned by this adventure, for this very soberly run company, I don't think can be overstated. You mentioned the characters and the personality traits, and I want to end here with a question from our listeners. I said on our other show, The Vergecast, that I was going to be talking to you, and I said if you have questions for Ronan about the story, let me know. So we have one here that I think ties in neatly with what you're describing. I'm just going to read it to you: "How do the justifications for bad behavior, cutthroat actions of Altman and other AI leaders differ from the justifications Ronan has heard from other high-profile leaders in politics and media? Don't they all justify their actions by saying this is how the world gets changed, if I don't do this someone else will?" Yeah, there's a lot of that going around. I would say what is distinctive to AI is that the existential stakes being so uniquely high means both the statements of risk are extreme, right? You have Sam Altman saying this could be lights out for all of us, and also the kind of mania, as the questioner is referring to, is extreme. The thing that Sam accused Elon of on the record, you know, that maybe he wants to save humanity but only if it's him. The kind of ego component of wanting to win, which is a framing Sam uses all the time, that this is one for the history books, this could change everything, and therefore even above and beyond the "you've got to break a few eggs" mindset of most Silicon Valley enterprises, there is in the minds of some figures leading AI, I think, a complete rationalization for any and all fallout. And you know, forget breaking eggs, I think a lot of the underlying safety researchers would say like potentially risking breaking the country, breaking the world, breaking, you know, millions of people whose jobs and safety hang in the balance. That's what's unique about it. And that's where I close, reflecting on this body of reporting, really believing this is about more than Sam Altman. This is about an industry that is unconstrained and a spiraling problem of America being unable to constrain it. Well, we had some optimism in there, but I think that's a good place to leave it, although on a downbeat. Of course, that's every great story really. The Musk-Altman trial is upcoming, I think we're going to learn a lot more here, and I suspect I will want to talk to you again. Ronan Farrow, thank you so much for being on Decoder. Thank you. I'd like to thank Ronan Farrow for taking the time to join Decoder, and thank you for listening. I hope you enjoyed it. If you'd like to let us know what you thought about this episode or really anything else at all, drop us a line. You can email us at decoder@theverge.com or you can hit me up directly on Threads and Bluesky. If you liked Decoder, please share it with your friends and subscribe wherever you get your podcasts. Decoder is a production of The Verge and part of the Vox Media Podcast Network. We'll see you next time.

Rollins, Lutnick, Hassett/Tax Day News Conference

KMPH FOX26 NEWS(317.0K) ·43:15 ·369 views · 2026-04-16
Brooke L. Rollins (Secretary of Agriculture) Howard Lutnick (U.S. Secretary of Commerce) Kevin Hassett (National Economic Council)
The Trump administration marked Tax Day by celebrating the "Working Families Tax Cuts" and announcing the historic launch of the first-ever USDA Office of Seafood to support American "farmers of the sea.
TL;DR The Trump administration marked Tax Day by celebrating the "Working Families Tax Cuts" and announcing the historic launch of the first-ever USDA Office of Seafood to support American "farmers of the sea." Key priorities include leveraging domestic energy to lower...

TL;DR

The Trump administration marked Tax Day by celebrating the "Working Families Tax Cuts" and announcing the historic launch of the first-ever USDA Office of Seafood to support American "farmers of the sea." Key priorities include leveraging domestic energy to lower fertilizer costs, slashing regulatory "bloat," and protecting rural economies from foreign trade deficits.

Working Families Tax Cut Act Impacts

  • Direct Financial Relief: The administration reported that 53 million filers claimed at least one new tax cut, with an average refund this season exceeding $3,400.
      • No Tax on Tips: Over 6 million filers have benefited from the elimination of taxes on tips.
      • Overtime & Senior Relief: 25 million filers claimed no tax on overtime, and 88% of seniors on Social Security (51 million people) now pay no tax on those benefits.
  • Rural Economic Investment: The act includes $100 billion for rural Opportunity Zones and $50 billion for a rural health transformation program.
  • Farm Preservation:
      • Death Tax: Averted the sunset of the death tax exemption, protecting two million family farms.
      • Capital Gains: Farmers can now defer capital gains taxes over four years when selling land to other farmers.
      • Expensing: Made permanent 100% immediate expensing for capital-intensive business investments.

Launch of the USDA Office of Seafood

  • Historic Designation: For the first time, the USDA will host a dedicated office for commercial fishermen, officially recognizing them as "farmers of the sea."
      • Director Appointment: The office is led by Nick, a 20-year career USDA veteran, to ensure long-term consistency.
  • Competitive Protections: The office aims to combat "communist fish" imports (specifically from Russia) that are often uninspected or toxic.
      • Inspection Gaps: Only 0.1% of incoming seafood is currently inspected; of that tiny fraction, one-third fails inspection.
  • Policy Wins for Fishermen:
      • Subsidies: The administration celebrated the elimination of subsidies for offshore wind projects that fishermen argued would destroy their industry.
      • Tax Deductions: Fishermen can now fully deduct vessel meals and expense gear, engines, and vessels.

Addressing the Fertilizer and Energy Crisis

  • Fertilizer Price Spikes: Fertilizer prices have risen 40% in the last 40 days due to global conflict.
  • Short-Term Actions: The administration waived the Jones Act and opened fertilizer lines from Venezuela to provide immediate relief.
  • Energy Dominance Strategy: Secretary Burgum emphasized using the U.S. "superpower" of low-cost natural gas ($2.60 in the U.S. vs. $12–$15 in Europe) to reshore nitrogen production.
      • The Goal: Transitioning the U.S. from a fertilizer importer to a global exporter by building domestic infrastructure.

Deregulation and Government Reform

  • Cutting "Bloat": Secretary Rollins cited the elimination of "taxpayer-funded studies" on topics she deemed irrelevant to agriculture, such as "menstrual cycles of transgender mice" and "racism in the pest business."
  • Permitting Speed: Secretary Burgum highlighted reforms to NEPA and the Endangered Species Act.
      • Efficiency: Claimed the department completed an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) in 24 days that typically takes four years.
  • Trade Deficit Reduction: The administration cut the inherited $50 billion agricultural trade deficit by 40% through 18 new trade deals.

Notable Quotes

"For the first time in a long time, commercial fishermen are being recognized for what we truly are: food producers... This is not just about policy, folks. It's about people. It's about preserving a way of life." — Dustin Delano, Fourth-Generation Lobsterman and Fisherman in Residence at NOAA
"We’ve taken federal employees and we’ve basically created mind-numbing, soul-sucking jobs where all they do is follow a bunch of regulations that are not literally something that Congress passed... When you get rid of the red tape, it not only helps the industry, it helps the federal team members." — Doug Burgum, U.S. Secretary of the Interior
"I will not leave this building in two years and nine months if we have not made significant progress to build out that [fertilizer] infrastructure... we’re going to get our arms around it and do everything we can to help solve for it." — Brooke L. Rollins, U.S. Secretary of Agriculture
Alaska, from Maine, from North Carolina, from the Gulf of America, Texas, and some others. So, we're going to talk a little bit about seafood too in the announcement we just had. But really we're here today to talk about again the incredible, the incredible work that has been done and as we're moving forward to celebrate a new day in America. Very grateful to have my partner from the cabinet, the incredible Secretary Doug Burgum is here. Thank you, sir, so much. Along with Congresswoman Kat Cammack and again some of our great fishermen. Our Deputy Secretary Stephen Vaden is here and others. So grateful. So a couple of quick top lines before I turn it over to Secretary Burgum and we move through the program. This is the first tax day since the working families tax cuts went into effect. Tax cuts that President Trump promised, that Republicans in Congress delivered, and that all of us across the administration, including here at USDA, are implementing. The working families tax cuts are the largest tax cuts in history for working and middle-class Americans, and Americans are already seeing real relief. More than 53 million filers claimed at least one of the new tax cuts. 53 million Americans. The average refund this filing season is more than $3,400. The Trump administration has delivered more refunds and larger refunds than in any one year in American history. Over six million—these are really important—over six million filers have claimed no tax on tips, and over 25 million filers have claimed no tax on overtime. Over 100 million filers have claimed the now permanent double standard deduction. This is promises made and promises kept. So, thank you, Mr. President. The working families tax cuts are also the largest investment in rural America in history. They are breathing new life and new opportunity into the heartland and into rural fishing communities for our fishermen here by giving them the relief and the support that they deserve. There are so many wins for rural America in that Working Families Tax Cut Act that it would take me a really long time to go through them all. But to just name a few, number one: we averted the death tax exemption sunset by exempting two million family farms in America from the death tax. The act made it easier to invest in the family farm by making permanent 100% immediate expensing for capital-intensive business investments. It allowed farmers to defer capital gains taxes over a four-year period instead of all at once when selling qualifying land if they sell to another farmer, supporting a new generation of farmers. That Working Families Tax Cut Act also committed $50 billion in the Rural Health Transformation Program. Dr. Oz is running that for generations to come. It also drives $100 billion in rural investment. Again, y'all, this is unprecedented. Never before has there been an effort focused on rural America and rural communities in our agriculture communities as was this last year with this tax cut act. The $100 billion moving into opportunity zones in rural America is no longer just an urban America project. It will change everything for our farmers who had been waiting more than a decade for an increase in reference prices from 10 to 21% for many and most of our commodities: corn, soybeans, wheat. Changes that provide up to $13 billion in additional revenue and price protection for our farmers starting this October is a game changer for a struggling rural economy. And finally, building on, I believe, one of the most important and impressive wins of this President and our administration over the last year, and that is in trade and re-opening the markets around the world for our great American agriculture products. This bill invested at a time I remind people that a big focus was to shrink government, return power to the people, and to cut taxes. At the same time, this administration with our partners on the hill invested significant funds in rural America, including $280 million a year in addition to what we were already spending to support opening the markets in international agricultural trade. In just one year, President Trump brought what we inherited, which was a $50 billion agricultural trade deficit, and cut it by 40%. No one thought that was possible, but the 18 new trade deals have made it possible thanks to President Trump's leadership. Finally, I'll say in addition to tax day, this week also represents the 50th anniversary of the Magnuson-Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act, the primary law governing our fisheries in U.S. federal waters. There are so many representatives, as I mentioned, here today from all over this country representing their seafood industry. In fact, I think one behind me is an eighth-generation fisherman from Maine. From Maine! And I asked in our conversation what year does that mean so we can put it into context and he said his seventh generation ago grandfather began fishing in America in 1806 off the coast of Maine. Y'all, this was revolutionary times for our country. And as we celebrate the 250th birthday of the greatest country in the history of the world that preserved and ignited the fire for freedom and for liberty, it was the families of these fishermen standing behind me and the families of the farmers that we know today that were the heart of that effort. That is what we fight for every single day. So again, what a joy to have you all here. We just announced the launch of the first-ever USDA Office of Seafood to support our farmers of the sea. For the first time ever, the U.S. Department of Agriculture will have an office and a team fully dedicated to advancing, honoring, elevating, and supporting our great farmers of the sea, and what a joy it is to be able to lead on that. Again, thank you everyone. I'm going to turn it over to my great partner Doug Burgum for some thoughts and then you'll get to hear a little bit from some of our fishermen, but what a joy to be here. And then we will open it up for some questions from our press in the back. Thank you so much. Secretary Burgum. Thank you, Brooke. Great to be here with all of you and great to be here with our incredible fishermen from around the country. We just got done with a roundtable with folks from Alaska to Florida and from Maine to the Gulf Coast. And what a day. Congratulations, Brooke. Congratulations everybody at USDA. Congratulations to Secretary Howard Lutnick who was here with us working together. Again, Commerce of course has got the National Marine Fisheries and NOAA, and the idea that we're going to be bringing Ag programs to support our fishermen of the sea is really incredible. It's an achievement. Senator Dan Sullivan was here. He's had this idea for over 10 years, but I think you're going to hear from all of them. Let's give one more round of applause. This is a really historic day. Way to go, Brooke. And it is tax day. And of course, as Brooke said, I mean kind of odd that we're celebrating. This used to be the day that was the black day on the calendar, the mark that you'd put through and like you dreaded this day would come. But here we are and it's actually a day of celebration and it's a day of gratitude. And the gratitude goes out to everybody in Congress, both the House and the Senate, who voted for this bill. I think all of you know who those folks were. And you know, it passed very narrowly, but it passed. But by doing that thing—this is part of, again, President Trump's agenda, which was going to cut red tape, lower taxes, and reinvigorate the American dream. We're going to make sure that if there was trade going on that it was truly fair trade and that the country stopped getting ripped off. All of those things have happened. And so what's happening in a short 15 months? Our economy, the foundation of having lower taxes, less regulation, and a record amount of investment coming into our economy is going to be an explosion of jobs and capital investment. It's an exciting, exciting, exciting day for our country because when people are looking at their refunds this year, on average, the ones that have come in so far have been an average of $3,500. People are getting a refund today of $3,500 because of that. That's a boost for all of them. And of course, Brooke talked about this, how fantastic our seniors. Seniors paying into Social Security their whole life, only then have to pay taxes on their earnings coming back to them. But now 51 million seniors, 88% of all the seniors on Social Security, will pay no tax on their Social Security. How about that? And of course, on the no taxes on tips, I mean this is a huge thing and President Trump can take full credit for this. I mean he shared the story, we've all heard it, but he was talking to a person that was waiting tables at a restaurant that he was at, asking them about what he could do when he was campaigning and they said I have to pay taxes on my hard-earned tips. And he came up with this idea, drove it through, got it passed, and a sampling of this is going out right now. The people that work professionally or part-time are seeing anywhere between $3,000 and $10,000 of savings. Their own money is staying in their own pockets. I think the economists are saying on average it's about $7,000. But what a gift to America for people that work hard, and the no tax on overtime. You know, many of you, I'm sure did, when I was a kid, man, I couldn't wait to get past 40 hours because that's when you finally started making a little bit of money, getting time and a half. But now to have no tax on overtime, it's the incentive for people to work harder and begin their own steps towards the American dream. So again, common-sense policies. But I do want to say in terms of gratitude, I want to say thank you to President Trump. Thank you to the fellow cabinet secretaries like Brooke Rollins that are here today. The team of people that we're working for, we're working for a President that works 20 hours a day, gets up every day fighting for the American people. It's so inspiring and so motivating to see the policies actually in action coming together. And so with that and with Brooke, my great partner as it relates to the fishermen that are up here, you'd be like, why is the Secretary of Interior talking about, you know, oceans? I mean this is the interior. But let's all remember a couple things. Between Agriculture and Interior, there are 700 million acres of land and 700 million acres of subsurface. But when you throw in the Interior where we've got responsibility for federal offshore, there are three billion acres of offshore. And I can tell you what we've turned around in the last 15 months. The prior administration had strangled every industry that you could imagine that depended on public lands, whether it was timber, whether it was mining, whether it was critical minerals, whether it was oil and gas, or whether it was fishing in federal waters. All of those industries were being choked to death by regulation. Listening to those people that are out there, getting ideas from the people that are trying to—again, you'll hear. It's not just eighth generation, third, fourth, fifth generation, and eighth generation. Of course, he was the first one I've met whose family was actually fishing during the War of 1812. You don't get to meet that every day. But it's incredible that now we're listening to the people that are actually doing the thing. That's why it's so important, the working families tax cut, because if you're talking to farmers or ranchers or timber or miners, they're working families. These are the people of America that build things. Now that we're supporting them with the tax cuts and supporting them with the new Office of Seafood, it's a great day for working families in America. And again, thank you. Thank you all. Thank you, Brooke. Thank you, USDA, and thank you everybody up here that's making it happen. That was beautiful. Muah! Beautiful. Got me a little emotional. Very quickly, our incredible congresswoman who represents a lot of fisheries, but certainly represents the Make America Great Again tax cuts and has just been such a warrior on all of it, Kat Cammack. Thank you. Well, I just want to echo the comments of what has been said here already. As the lone representative for the free state of Florida on House Agriculture, with a whole heck of a lot of coastline, including the Gulf of America, this is an amazing day for our fishermen. Now it was said in the roundtable earlier, we know the juggernaut that Alaska is in this space, but as the representative of the lower 48, of the tremendous osterman, clamors, crabbers, shrimpers, you name it, this is a fantastic step forward because we know that food security is national security. This has been an issue that we have been pushing for, fighting for, and to have a team of cabinet secretaries who not only get along and genuinely like each other, but take action instead of just talking about an issue, they deliver. And not only have they delivered today, but they have delivered previously and will continue to do so. On tax day today we are here, living proof that President Trump is very, very serious when he says we're going to make America great again and put America first. Thank you. Thank you, thank you, Secretary Rollins. Thank you, Secretary Burgum to our amazing fishermen that are here today representing an industry that has been a little overlooked, but no longer will be. Thank you for everything that you're doing. Today is a new day as we look forward to continuing to make America great again. Thank you guys. That was beautiful. Beautiful. All right, a fourth-generation fisherman and also the fisherman in residence at NOAA, Dustin Delano from Maine, to talk a little bit about the tax cuts. Dustin, thank you. Thank you, Madam Secretary. It's an honor to be here and a privilege. Again, my name is Dustin Delano and I serve as a fisherman in residence at NOAA. But before that title means anything, before Washington, before policy, I'm a fourth-generation lobsterman. I grew up in a small fishing village that depends almost entirely on commercial fishing. It's not just what we do, it's who we are. I grew up watching my dad, a single father, work all day on his lobster boat hauling traps, battling weather, chasing a living that most people will never understand. And then he would come in, he'd grab a quick bite and head back out to catch the evening tide to dig clams. That is what shaped me. I understand hard work as a way of life. I'm a living example of the grit and resilience that comes from making a living on the ocean as a farmer of the sea. And that is why today matters. Because for the first time in a long time, commercial fishermen are being recognized for what we truly are: food producers. So today is tax day, and for generations, fishermen have carried the weight of taxes, regulations, and rising costs while continuing to show up every day to feed this country. Now we are seeing meaningful change. Because of the working families tax cuts and broader reforms, fishermen are getting real relief. We can now fully deduct meals on vessels and expense equipment, engines, gear, and even vessels, making it possible to reinvest and stay competitive. We are also seeing stronger support for small, family-run operations through expanded business income deductions. And I can't forget to mention one of my favorites: eliminating subsidies for offshore wind that was slated to kill our fishing industry. These are long-overdue steps toward fairness. But what matters even more is what comes next. Bringing commercial fishermen and the seafood industry into the USDA is a milestone because access matters. Access to marketing, to risk management tools, and to financing can strengthen this industry and support coastal communities across America. This did not happen by accident. It happened because of leadership. Because President Trump made seafood competitiveness a priority through his seafood executive order. Because leaders like Secretary Rollins, Secretary Burgum, Secretary Lutnick, and others, they didn't just listen, they took action. And for the first time in decades, fishermen are feeling something we have not felt in a long time: hope. And many of you in this audience fought for this long before me. You refused to let this industry be forgotten. So yes, today is a milestone. But it's also a starting point. For me, this moment means a great deal. But what matters more is what it means for the future, for the next generation. For the boy in Sitka learning to mend twine with his grandfather. For the girl in Gloucester watching her father haul traps before sunrise. For the kid in Galveston stepping onto a shrimp boat for the first time. For the young fisherman in Kodiak wondering if there's still a future in this life. This is not just about policy, folks. It's about people. It's about preserving a way of life. It's about ensuring that the American fisherman, the farmer of the sea, has a future in this country. And today, that future feels just a little bit closer. Thank you. Well, maybe we just finish there. No, I'm just kidding. That was incredible, how inspiring. Dustin, thank you. Another great fisherman with us from Alaska, Sterling, if you wouldn't mind joining us. We added them at the end, but they are—he and Jason represent just so many remarkable patriots. What a joy, Sterling, to have you here. So thank you for joining us and tell us what you think. He said I'm going to pass it to Jason. This is Jason, again, like I said, our eighth-generation fisherman from Maine. He has been such a stalwart on this issue and the fight in front of us and the righteousness of this work. He talked about how Peter in the Bible was a fisherman, and this goes back really, really long way. So Jason, thank you so much for being here. Wow, this is amazing. Sterling's a good speaker. I just met him about 15 minutes ago and he can talk up a storm. Wow. And Dustin, proud of him. Man, what a nice young man. He grows up in a fishing family and I'm very proud of him. To be able to have this opportunity to be here today, thank our President. I mentioned to them in there, I showed up six years ago to speak at the RNC to endorse our President. And six years later, I'm here witnessing a change and the creation of a seafood office at the USDA. It's amazing. And we can't thank him enough. As a lobsterman, I don't know where I'm going with this because no one told me I was going to speak. I would say to the American people, if you ever hear anything about ropless fishing with lobstering, stay away from it. That's bad stuff. It is so dangerous. It is so dangerous. And to, you know, within the bill that—the one big beautiful bill there—to take away that funding from all that offshore wind, oh, those people are so corrupt. They are so corrupt. And we're so happy as fishermen from the east coast to the west coast to see that stuff defunded. I don't know, how long do you want me to talk? So I'm probably good right now. So thank you. Thank you for being here and thank you to our President. You sure? Okay, okay. All right, well, what an encouraging, incredible day. We are going to open it up for some questions from the press. But as we move into this portion, again, I just want to thank the amazing USDA team that is here. All the work that's been done on this. I just couldn't ask for more incredible people to work with every single day. So I think we've got some of our USDAers here. I just want to thank you all. It's my honor to serve you every day. Every day. All right, Alec, we'll take some questions. Yes, ma'am. Hi, Madam Secretary. Hello. Grace from Politico. Hopefully you can hear me okay. Thank you for taking my question. I wanted to ask about your message to farmers who are seeing higher prices of fertilizer and fuel recently. I know you've said you've been in contact with fertilizer companies and you posted that any sleight of hand from fertilizer companies will not be tolerated. How are those conversations going and how is the administration prepared to act in response to any price gouging? What kinds of solutions are on the table there? Well, thank you. Could we just first ask one quick question about seafood just so we can check that box and then we'll talk fertilizer. Okay. Well, could you walk me through, now that the office is open, what's priority number one for you guys? Oh, thank you so much. Way to go, Grace from Politico. What a great question. Listen, here's the beautiful thing. I don't have the list in front of me, but everything from crop protection tools to prioritizing seafood on the international stage. You know, you think about it and probably a lot from the press or maybe any of you don't fully understand, our seafood industry has been crushed by what we call the communist fish from Russia and other parts around the world where a lot of it is toxic and it doesn't get inspected enough, if at all. One of the numbers we talked about—I think Jason brought it up in our roundtable we just finished—is that currently incoming seafood, only one tenth of one percent is inspected and of that one tenth of one percent, one third of it doesn't pass the inspection. These numbers are crazy. We've got to refocus on how we're supporting American agriculture and how we're supporting American seafood. When you think about it in the context of the Make America Healthy Again movement and what this particular industry means for making America healthy again, all of those come into play. For me, this seemed like such a no-brainer. I was a little startled that USDA has never had an office for seafood. We've never had an advocate for seafood for all of the programs that are at USDA, from beginning farmers and ranchers to risk mitigation to the various programs when there's weather incidents, etc. Now all of that is on the table. We have a lot of work to do to get from point A to point B, but that's very important. The second thing I'll add is it's got to be more than just the next two years and nine months and a couple of days. Most of this, if not all, has to be codified. And I, and I think I speak for Secretary Burgum and our administration, will work day in and day out with this industry to get this codified so that they don't have to go through another fight in another couple of years to get this done. So what a joy. So thank you, Grace, for that question. Thank you. Now we'll talk fertilizer. Clearly the current conflict happening across the world has had significant negative impact on the price of fertilizer just in the last 35, 40 days since the conflict began. Fertilizer has increased 40% on average. That is in addition to the four years under the Biden administration where fertilizer increased on average 40%. One day I think it increased 99%. So we've got a big short-term problem for our farmers. We also have a long-term structural problem for our farmers that has to be solved. In the short term, this administration waived the Jones Act and we opened up lines of fertilizer from Venezuela. We entered an MOU on some critical minerals thanks to Secretary Burgum. We're doing everything we can on the short term. But the bigger question is the long term, and to make a little news, two hours ago I had a handful of CEOs from some of the world's largest fertilizer companies sit right up there in my office, which is right over my left shoulder. And we had a very frank discussion. The challenge is only a handful of companies in the world supply fertilizer to the world. It is a worldwide market. The good news is, especially with nitrogen, which is really the key issue right now as we're moving into planting season, in America we have enough LNG thanks to President Trump's energy independence agenda. We've gone from a net importer just a decade ago to a net exporter on nitrogen, but we don't have the infrastructure or the plants here. But it is a new day. And again, this is more long term. We're focused on the short term, but I will not leave this building in two years and nine months if we have not made significant progress to build out that infrastructure. The final thing I'll say is part of that meeting was our incredible Secretary of Commerce, Howard Lutnick. Clearly with this President's focus on reshoring and friendshoring—Canada is a big partner in this by the way based on some of their natural minerals—we have to ensure that we're working across the cabinet. Doug Burgum has a really big role in that as well. So that has become a significant priority recently and we're going to stay on it. So thank you for the question, Grace. Thank you. Good to see you. Yeah, Steve Davies with Agri-Pulse. Thank you for doing this, Madam Secretary. If I can start off with a seafood question—I follow the rules. How big will the Office of Seafood be and when will you stand it up? Well, thank you for that question. It is stood up as of today. We have been working on it over the last almost eight months and wanted to get all of our t's crossed and our i's dotted to make sure that when we announced it, it wasn't going to be another year before it opened. It is now open today, which is really, really cool. Is Nick our director? Right there he is in the back. That's our new director, a 20-year employee of the U.S. Department of Agriculture, which means he was here long before I got here and he'll probably be here long after I go. But that's the consistency that we really wanted to put into place. A great career employee of the U.S. Department of Agriculture. We're so grateful to you as you build this team out and fight for these incredible people. So thank you for being here. Thank you. Michael, forgive me for asking this, but the President's budget called USDA a bloated bureaucracy. What are you going to do in the next year to reduce the bloat? Well, this President has basically called all of the federal government a bloated bureaucracy, and again Doug may want to lean into this a little bit too. But there's no doubt that when this President first came down the escalator in 2015, there were 17 people running in the Republican primary that year, including several Texans that I know and are friends with. The President really connected with the American people. We saw it firsthand. He was the first President to really come down that escalator and say, "I'm going to go and fix Washington," meaning instead of bringing new programs, he was going to cut government in Washington that had grown too big. Our founders envisioned under the 10th Amendment a very small federal government where the government closest to the people—the states and the locals—should be the ones making the decision for the American people. It had gotten so big and so out of control. So here at USDA, part of that is recalibrating the U.S. Department of Agriculture to actually focus on agriculture. We are ensuring that instead of funding the studies of the menstruation cycle of transgender mice—which was actually a funded study by the USDA until we got here—we focus on our mission. Instead of funding studies on racism in the pest business—which was actually a study that was funded until we got here—or studying the habits of "queer and bipoc farmers" of San Francisco, which was actually a taxpayer funded study until we got here, we are making priorities first. That's what the President is talking about to ensure that we have every tool in our toolkit to support the American farmer and rancher, to support our rural communities, and to change the trajectory of agriculture so it's no longer being purchased and consolidated by foreign adversaries like China. These are all existential issues for the future of America. So when the President talks about the bloated USDA budget or the bloated Interior budget, we have such a short period of time to fix it while we're here and to make the first things first. Doug, I know you may have some thoughts on Interior. I just want to agree with everything that Brooke said and then add on. It's fascinating when you dig in. Of course, I was in the private sector before I became governor, and I was governor for eight years. I had a chance to dig into the programs that are there. Many of these things just grow and grow and grow, and they get outside of not just their core mission, but they actually get outside the guidelines of what the actual law is. Recently, of course, NEPA has been weaponized to stop infrastructure in this country. If you want to slow something down, use NEPA. If you want to kill it, use the Endangered Species Act. We've got a 1.5 trillion dollars of capital spending that's been approved in our country that's waiting for a permit, and it's often waiting for a permit because it's being challenged and litigated around some of these rules that were not passed by Congress. 80% of NEPA was not passed by Congress. In Interior, we've just reduced the amount of things that people have to do to actually get to the 20% that meant the law. The other 80% is in an addendum. You can look at it and go, "oh those are interesting ideas," but that's not actually the law. And the same thing is now happening as we go through that with the ESA. Part of getting rid of the bloat is to save taxpayer moneys. I know Brooke cares about the people that work here at USDA and I care about all the people that work at Interior. But we've taken federal employees and we've basically created mind-numbing, soul-sucking jobs where all they do is follow a bunch of regulations that are not literally something that Congress passed. If people get into government because they want to make a difference in people's lives, these roles done right can actually make a difference. We saw today a group of cabinet leaders and all of our team supporting an industry to have an opportunity to flourish and not be buried under red tape. When you get rid of the red tape, it not only helps the industry, it helps the federal team members. It brings people back to life. They can actually do a job where they can make a difference in people's lives and be a participant in helping create the American dream. There is an unlimited opportunity to reduce costs and improve the services we deliver to the citizens in these jobs. Yes, beautifully said. Thank you, Doug. We'll do last question. Hi Secretary, Elizabeth Alkin, Bloomberg News. Good to see you. I'm also going to ask a fish question and then a fertilizer question. I wonder how demand for seafood fits into the overall demand for protein that we're seeing. Like how do you see fish demand as fitting into the protein craze? Yeah, that's right. And it actually ties back to Steve's question too. So as we work to make America healthy again, a report came out last week that said for the first time in American history, real food purchases were up 7.5%. We've been trending away from quote "real food," which is the kind of dietary guideline that Bobby and I put out a few weeks ago. Americans are buying more real food as it becomes very apparent what has been happening over the last number of decades with what we're eating in this country and the chronic health problems that come with that. As we're looking to make America healthy again and looking to have proteins—eggs, beef, chicken, today seafood—as part of that effort, it's just an incredibly encouraging and exciting time. I've said it before but I'll repeat it: USDA alone spends $400 million a day across 16 nutrition programs. The opportunity to pivot toward more real food—toward seafood and beef and chicken—is just limitless from my perspective. What that eventually means for our agriculture producers and rebuilding these smaller mid-size family farms and being able to pass to the ninth and tenth generation continues to be a priority. I'm sorry Bobby couldn't be here. He was really sad to miss because he was very excited about this too. He was getting ready for a hearing or he may be in a hearing right now. But this is an across-the-cabinet effort and one that I think is going to be so important to seafood. So thank you for the question. And my fertilizer question: if Mosaic is unwilling to step back those countervailing duties that we've been talking about, what are next steps to bring relief for farmers? You know, we have so many different options that we're looking at. As I mentioned earlier today, we had Secretary Burgum, we had Secretary Lutnick, we had Ambassador Jameson Greer, and several other key folks from the administration sitting down with some of these companies. Mosaic was not one of them; they did not come. But we are understanding what it is that we can do together, hopefully to work to keep this fertilizer crisis in check. We're looking at additional short-term steps—nothing I can announce right now because we're still working through that. But know that it is an across-the-cabinet effort. I've talked to the President himself about it. He Truthed about it on Saturday, ensuring that there was no price gouging. The Department of Justice is involved. We are watching this very, very closely. The silver lining is it has shined a light that has long needed to be shined on this particular input. It has been difficult for farmers as it has continually increased year over year over year. In the last 30 days is again just one more check mark on what's happening. We're going to get our arms around it and do everything we can to help solve for it. Can I say one thing? Yeah, please. And then we're going to wrap. I just want to add, again adding on everything Brooke said, but wow what an opportunity for America. When we have conflicts abroad or if you have disruptions even like COVID, you understand that supply chain and the supply chain risk. What do you make nitrogen out of? It's the number one component of fertilizer. What's the primary component? Natural gas. 20 years ago the U.S. was the world's largest importer of natural gas. Under President Trump we're the world's largest exporter of natural gas. I'm talking about natural gas which comes out of the ground when you're producing oil and gas. The price of that at the beginning of this conflict was $3. The price of it now is $2.60. In the U.S., we have the most abundant, lowest cost natural gas. That's what you make nitrogen out of. Whether it's a gaseous form like ammonia, whether it's urea which is solid, or whether it's a liquid form, farmers can apply all three in different applications. We don't have an issue with the resource. We are blessed as a country with enormous resources in natural gas which can be converted to heat, light, or fertilizer. It's the superpower of energy. We've got it and we've got so much of it, but what we need is to manufacture those products here as opposed to importing them. When you go through a situation like this, what's going to come out the back side? We're going to see companies that stand up and start making investments in developing more fertilizer here. Just like with natural gas, instead of being an importer, now we're the world's largest exporter. We have the same opportunity in fertilizer. There's no reason why we have to be paying high prices when we could have the lowest prices for fertilizer because we have the lowest prices for natural gas in the world. In other places right now, including Europe, it's $12 or $15 for natural gas, so five times higher. It's an incredible business opportunity for America. I know that with Brooke and with Howard and the team that they meet with today, we're going to see solutions on this thing. I look at the future; these are all solvable. American innovation and American greatness mean we're going to have the lowest priced energy in the world because of President Trump's energy dominance agenda. We're going to have the lowest priced fertilizer in the world under the same agenda. It will lower the cost of food and improve food security. Thank you. All right everybody, happy working families tax cut tax day. So grateful to be fighting every day for all of our Americans and returning the American dream to every corner of this country. Of course here at USDA, to our farmers of the sea and to our farmers of the land, we will never stop battling. God bless you all, thank you and thanks to the press for being here. Thank y'all.

Can the WTO Survive a New World Order?–Conversation w/ Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala | The Futures Summit

Center for Strategic & International Studies(388.0K) ·44:20 ·559 views · 2026-04-16
Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala (Director-General, World Trade Organization)
> "The purpose of the WTO written into its founding document is to enhance living standards, to help create jobs, and to support sustainable development.
TL;DR: While the global trading system faces significant geopolitical disruptions and supply chain "overdependencies," it remains remarkably resilient; however, the WTO must reform its consensus-based decision-making to better integrate digital trade, green initiatives, and...

TL;DR: While the global trading system faces significant geopolitical disruptions and supply chain "overdependencies," it remains remarkably resilient; however, the WTO must reform its consensus-based decision-making to better integrate digital trade, green initiatives, and South-South cooperation to move from mere adaptability to long-term robustness.

Navigating Global Disruption and Overdependence

  • Current Economic Outlook: Despite global instability, trade has shown surprising resilience.
      • Growth Statistics: Global goods trade grew by 4.6% last year (surpassing the projected 2.4%), driven largely by AI investment and frontloading ahead of tariffs.
      • 2024 Projections: Growth is projected at 1.9%, though ongoing conflicts (such as in the Middle East) could cause a 0.5% drop to 1.4%.
  • The "Choke Point" Problem: Dr. Okonjo-Iweala highlighted dangerous overdependencies on specific markets and geographic transit points.
      • Market Concentrations: Over-reliance on the U.S. for markets, China for critical supplies, and Taiwan for semiconductors.
      • Geographic Fragility: Vital transit routes including the Malacca Strait, Panama Canal, and the Suez Canal are increasingly vulnerable.
  • Resilience vs. Robustness: While global trade has adapted to shocks (e.g., finding new wheat sources in Latin America when Black Sea routes were blocked), Dr. Okonjo-Iweala argues that "resilience is not robustness." The system needs proactive de-concentration to ensure stability.

WTO Reform and the "Consensus" Dilemma

  • The Turning Point Ministerial (MC14): The recent ministerial in Yaoundé was intended as a pivot toward institutional modernization.
      • Reform Pathway: Ministers successfully agreed on a program of reforms with specific modalities, timelines, and six-monthly progress reports.
      • The E-Commerce Moratorium: A critical 28-year-old agreement to not charge customs duties on digital transmissions reached a stalemate. While 164 of 166 members reached a "landing zone" for agreement, the WTO's requirement for unanimity blocked the final gavel.
  • Redefining Consensus: The Director General emphasized that "consensus" at the WTO is currently practiced as "unanimity," which allows a single country to block the majority.
      • Proposed Shift: Reform efforts are looking at how to maintain consensus as a core value while practicing it in a way that allows the organization to remain agile and responsive to a fast-changing world.

Trade as the Primary Engine of Development

  • Integration vs. Separation: Dr. Okonjo-Iweala strongly disagreed with the premise that trade and development are separate fields.
      • Means to an End: Trade is an essential instrument for economic growth, which is the necessary precursor to development.
      • Beyond Technical Assistance: Developing nations should focus less on "Special and Differential Treatment" (SDT) and more on how specific trade agreements can fundamentally drive their internal growth.
  • The Rise of South-South Trade: Southern countries are increasingly trading with one another, rising from 10% of global trade in 1995 to 25% today.
      • CELAC-Africa Cooperation: A landmark meeting between Latin American/Caribbean (CELAC) and African trade ministers occurred at MC14 to diversify trade and reduce dependence on traditional hubs.
      • AfCFTA: The African Continental Free Trade Area is viewed as a vital instrument, though Dr. Okonjo-Iweala urged for faster implementation.

The Future: Digital, Green, and AI-Driven Trade

  • The Digital Economy: Digitally delivered services are the fastest-growing segment of global trade, increasing at 6% annually and currently valued at $5.3 trillion.
      • Plurilateral Agreements: 66 members recently launched an interim agreement on basic rules for e-commerce to provide business certainty.
  • Climate and Carbon Pricing: There is a critical need for a global "methodological approach" to carbon pricing to prevent trade fragmentation.
      • CBAM Concerns: Many members view Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanisms as restrictive trade measures.
      • Institutional Silos: Dr. Okonjo-Iweala noted a disconnect between environment ministers and trade ministers, urging for better communication to ensure climate goals don't accidentally stifle trade.
  • AI’s Impact: Investment in AI goods was a massive driver of trade performance last year, accounting for 42% of the total growth in goods trade.
"The purpose of the WTO written into its founding document is to enhance living standards, to help create jobs, and to support sustainable development. Can you believe that? So it’s about people. It’s not just about globalization." — Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala
"At the WTO, consensus is practiced as unanimity. And that’s one of the things we are looking at in the reform. It’s great—the smallest country has the same power as the largest. The only place where you have that. But it also holds you back in your ability to make decisions." — Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala
